item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1451期】加息争议再起，市场等待方向！财报展望✨20260712#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、隐藏的"角色互换"：市场正在替美联储做它不敢做的事

这期节目里最出人意料的观察，其实藏在小陶那句被一带而过的话里——"市场已经通过两年期十年期利率的上涨，帮凯文沃什去做事了"。

多数分析师习惯把美联储视为"投手"、市场视为"接球手"。但过去两个月发生的事，其实是投手和接球手悄悄换了位置：债券市场自己把长短端收益率抬上去，等于替美联储完成了一次"影子加息"。这意味着如果 9 月美联储不跟随市场定价加息，就会出现一个反直觉的局面——不加息本身反而构成"背信"，因为市场已经先行紧缩了，货币当局如果不确认，就等于在事后否定市场做的紧缩工作。

这里有个跨领域连接值得玩味：这个结构其实和"预期自我实现的语言学现象"（比如自证预言 self-fulfilling prophecy）非常像。市场用价格"说了一句话"，如果说话人（美联储）不呼应，语言系统就崩塌了——不是加息或不加息本身重要，而是信号一致性本身构成了新的政策资产。

二、"银行财报—消费者健康度"这条链，正在被一条更隐秘的链取代

节目里小陶依然把美国银行财报当作"消费者体温计"。但如果你把他自己后面无意提到的几个碎片拼起来，会发现一个他没有明说、但更前瞻的指标已经浮现：

  • 3 月战争 → 油价上涨 → 消费者几乎无感
  • Memory 涨价 → 3C 硬件涨价 → 消费者依然消化
  • 劳动力市场"完全没有被 AI 冲击"（超出他年初预期）

真正的隐藏变量不是"消费者还能不能撑住"，而是"AI 的资本开支正在替代传统消费周期充当经济发动机"。这也解释了为什么 GDP 增速在紧缩周期末端反而"高于历史"——多数美联储成员归因于 AI 投资。

这意味着分析师们盯错了仪表盘：过去十年看信用卡消费，现在应该看数据中心投产节奏。芯片板块 YTD 涨近 100%、IT 板块贡献了全部 EPS 上调，其实是同一现象的两个投影——AI Capex 正在成为一种新型宏观变量，它既不是消费也不是投资，而更像一种"数字基建战时经济"。

三、一个反常识：这轮上涨最真实的驱动，其实是"市场年初集体误判"

小陶提到一个几乎所有人忽视的细节：年初市场对 2026 年标普 500 营收增速的预期是 15%，现在被下调到 10.7%——但 EPS 增速反而被上调到 24.2%。

营收放缓、盈利加速——这是一个教科书式的"利润率跃迁"信号。它通常只在两种情况下出现： 1. 技术革命带来的成本崩塌（比如互联网早期、云计算早期）； 2. 大规模劳动力替代。

而这两点，恰好都指向 AI。也就是说，华尔街不是在给"业绩"定价，是在悄悄给"劳动生产率的结构性跳变"定价——只是没人敢把话说出来。

这里可以做一个非常有趣的跨领域连接：这个形态和 19 世纪末电力普及初期的美股结构极为相似。当年铁路公司营收增速在放缓，但盈利能力却在扩张——因为电力这个"外部变量"让整个工业成本函数发生了平移。历史上每一次这样的错位，都会在 3–5 年后被追认为一次"生产率革命"。当下芯片股的估值溢价，本质上不是泡沫，而是市场在无意识地对一次尚未官宣的技术拐点押注。

番外洞见：小陶自己没意识到的"决策系统"矛盾

他说自己"年初判断降息两次，现在改成有可能加息"，并强调"我不坚持年初的判断"。听起来是理性更新，但如果你留意——他同时坚持"UNH 到 23 倍就一定卖"，这是完全反向的决策模式：宏观允许自己被数据推翻，个股却预设固定退出估值。

这其实揭示了一个多数投资者共有的认知非对称：我们对宏观愿意谦虚，对个股却常常固执。而历史证明，恰恰相反的姿态更赚钱——宏观趋势具有强惯性，个股估值天花板反而经常被重构。

📝 转录文本

开场

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分析员小陶。

过去两周，其实市场比较的平淡。严格来说，从 6 月初市场开始调整之后，在过去这段时间，基本上纳指也好、标普也好，就在一个区间里面横盘。那接下去精彩的来了——财报季度，那也是我们所投资的公司将要交出他们一个季度的一个成绩表。

所以今天这些内容： - 先快速回顾一下过去一周资产价格的走势 - 看一下上一周公布的美联储纪要 - 看一下市场对于 7 月份和 9 月份加息的这个预期，现在是多少百分比 - 然后我们就去展望未来的一周，有 CPI 数据、有不同公司发布财报，对于每家公司我稍微快速地做一下点评 - 然后我们再往更远的去看，展望一下这一个季度的财报季

好吗？那我就废话不多说，马上开始。在开始之前，大家记得留言、点赞、加转发。

一、过去一周资产价格走势回顾

美债市场

美债市场这边，2 年、10 年期分别上涨了 7 和 8 个基点，TLT 下跌了 1.2%，YTD TLT 来到负的 3.1%。

那么阶段性的我们可以看到，TLT 出现了一些反弹，但是我们看到起码相对比较长的一个周期去看——我讲这句话两年了，不要去碰 TLT 97 这一块，因为我们将要面临的是财政的这个挑战，包括债务的这个规模，所以对于偿债这一块的一个回报并不是非常的好，除非我们未来会有一个衰退，但短期之内并看不到这个衰退的一个风险。

三大指数与板块表现

三大指数这边过去一周，标普和纳指都出现了反弹；道指相对会弱一些，下跌了 0.5%；罗素也相对会弱一些。

标普和纳指的强势主要是来自于前期大科技股的这个反弹，那么上涨了 4%。芯片板块在上一周也出现了反弹。

因为前期大科技股的这个反弹，把 Mag Seven 和标普 500 等权指数的一个回报差拉近了一些，现在是 12% 减 3.8，差不多 9% 左右吧，而最大的时候这两者之间的一个回报差是超过了 12%。

所以这个市场里边基本上很多时候就这样：如果一个标的短期之内跌得比较多了，我们首先考虑一下这个标的本身，基本面有没有问题，如果没有问题的话，就果断买入；而在中间可能大多数的时候，我们就是处在一个等待的阶段或者持股的一个阶段。

银行板块

银行板块这边上涨了 1.7%，YTD 来到了 14%。因为整个财报季度通常是银行板块先发布财报。

而且我特地去看了一下 KBWB 银行板块 ETF 的周 K 线，现在已经来到了超买的这个位置。通常来说，银行板块过去几个季度我们所看到的一个习惯是：在财报之前会走得比较好，然后财报之后会出现 sell the news，然后在下一次财报之前又开始出现反弹。银行板块基本是这样，因为不可能连续上涨嘛。银行反馈也相对会比较稳定一些。

所以因为现在技术面已经来到了一个超买的阶段，那接下去银行板块发布财报，稍微小心一下发布完财报之后的走势。

二、市场对加息的定价

来看一下市场对于加息的这个定价。

  • 7 月底：有一次美联储会议，市场预计有 40% 的可能性要加息。7 月份的会议我认为加息的概率比较低，所以接下去市场可能会把这 40% 的概率给交易掉，到会议之前的话，这个概率应该要低于 10%。
  • 9 月份：现在将近 100% 的可能性会加息 25 个基点。9 月份的 96.7% 是建立在 7 月份加息 38% 可能性的基础上，所以它是一个累计的概率。

本周将会有凯文·沃什的听证会。这一次听证会可能也会透露：他当然绝对不会透露接下去美联储政策的路径，但是他会透露更多他自己个人的一些性格、看待数据的一些方法和角度。

到 2026 年年底之前，市场现在预期 1.6 次加息的概率。

6 月美联储会议纪要

6 月份的美联储会议，我们在上一周看到了那一次会议的纪要。那纪要本身已经是非常大的一个后视镜了，主要是看一下当时整个会议里边内部有没有一些争论或者其他美联储成员的一些看法。

内部争论非常的激烈，整体来说是偏鹰派的一次美联储会议。

  • 三位美联储成员认为那一次会议就要加息的，那几乎是不可能在那一次就要加息的。
  • 多数的美联储成员认为 GDP 的增速比历史要强，为什么要强？因为是 AI 的这个投资。
  • 有 4 位到 5 位美联储成员认为通胀在扩张，所以他们对于通胀是产生一定的担心的。
  • 同时多数的美联储成员认为，经过几年高于 2% 的这个通胀——你想从 2022 年到现在已经四年的时间了——那么通胀在 2% 以上，有一定的风险让通胀预期成为习惯，这是比较危险的一个信号。

因为不断的有些事件进来：22 年的俄乌战争，然后到今年又再次有个战争，去年有关税，今年又一次战争，所以对于通胀这一块，起码在油价这一块不断的反复。

但是在这个基础上，多数的美联储成员仍然认为中期通胀的预期符合美联储 2% 的目标。当然了，这个预期还是市场所交易出来的结果。所以短期之内的确在通胀这一块有一些噪音在背后。

通胀高于 2% 这样的时间跨度现在已经来到了第 4 年多了，如果你算上 21 年的话就已经是 5 年，到明年的话要 6 年了。市场还是相信美联储能够把通胀带回 2%——从很多市场所交易出来未来通胀预期的这些指标去看的话，现在仍然是维持在 2% 左右。

华尔街观点与个人看法

彭博他们认为今年年底之前是不会加息的。他们的看法是：工作小组（凯文·沃什所成立的 5 个工作小组）是用来拖延时间的。这和我在上周看到的一家机构所持有的是同样的观点。

我看到不少华尔街正统机构，包括彭博这个团队，都认为凯文·沃什会利用这个工作小组去拖延时间，接下去不会进行任何货币政策上的调整。但是市场却不是这么说的。

那我个人的看法和这些机构有一些不一样：

我认为，市场现在已经在帮美联储做加一次这个行为了。市场已经通过两年期美债收益率的这个上涨，帮债券市场这一块进行紧缩了——紧缩的行为是已经发生了。并不是因为接下去美联储会加息或者不加息，市场才会进行紧缩，并不是这样。市场现在已经通过两年期、十年期利率的上涨出现了这个紧缩，已经在帮凯文·沃什去做事了。

所以在这种情况下，我认为美联储要跟随市场的判断：市场认为 9 月份要加息的话，美联储就应该要加息。而如果凯文·沃什不去这么做的话，那接下去市场对于他所释放的一些信号、所讲的一些话可能会失信。如果市场和美联储之间存在这个误解的话，对市场来说并不是一件好事。

三、市场参与度与情绪

参与度这边，三大指数纳指相对会弱一些，现在还处在一个中性的位置：20 均线、50 均线都在 49%。罗素 2000 相对会乐观一些，57% 和 65%。然后标普 500 相对更加乐观一些，62% 和 68%，但是是处在超买的、处在乐观的位置。

如果去看 CNN 的恐慌贪婪指数，现在在 49，处在一个完全中性的位置。所以和纳指这边市场参与度的结果是完全一样。

Safe Haven Demand 指标

如果去看里边那些数据的话，有一图数据我稍微挑出来聊一下，叫做 Safe Haven Demand（市场的避风港需求）。

这个数据主要衡量的是股市和债市之间过去 20 天的一个回报差： - 当数据在比较高的位置时，就是股市跑赢了债市。当股市大幅跑赢债市在 20 天之内，就代表股市可能走得太乐观了，那短期之内就会出现回调。 - 反之也是一样。比如在两周之前，你看到股市和债市之间的回报差是负的——股市跑输了债市，而且负得比较多，负 4% 左右。接下去在过去一周，我们就看到大科技出现了反弹，把 QQQ 开始带回来。

这是一个类似于超卖和超买的指标：在超卖位置小心一些，在超卖位置可以激进一些。

当下处在什么位置？当下可能处在一个略接近于超买的位置。过去 20 天股市跑赢了债市将近 5% 不到，其实是在过去这一段时间一个相对比较厂白的位置。也就是说，在接下去的一段时间，这条线可能要往回走，代表股市这边要跑输债市。

QQQ 技术形态

最后在展望之前稍微看一下 QQQ 这边的走势，画了一个区间，非常的明显：QQQ 现在是在高位进行一个横盘，而且是在走一个收缩。

当然了，也不是说收缩就一定不能突破。只要还是在这个区间里边横盘，就代表市场是在进行盘整。那么盘整也是对于筹码的消耗，对于比较高的情绪面的消耗。

特别是，虽然 QQQ 现在还处在一个横盘的阶段，但是和芯片相关的一些个股从高位回调的幅度，有些标的已经比较多了，30% 甚至 30% 以上。

当下操作建议

把我们前面所有的信息、对于当下市场的一些描述等等全部综合在一起，我认为当下就是一个比较明显的等机会的时候，就持股等机会的时候：

  • 现在并不适合空仓，也不适合满仓
  • 稍微留一些仓位出来等待个股的一些标的就可以了
  • 大多数的仓位应该在市场里边

我现在几乎没有太多现金——可能是 3% 不到左右的现金，然后其他所有的仓位还是在市场里边。

四、展望：本周 CPI 与财报

CPI 数据

首先是本周的宏观数据 CPI，老样子在周二的早上美东时间 8 点 30。基本上没有太大的意外——彭博这边的预期与市场预期基本符合：

  • 广义同比增速比市场高了 0.1%
  • 核心环比增速 0.2
  • 广义环比负的 0.1（因为油价的下跌）

这样的数据本身我认是 OK 的。

本周财报安排

那本周就拉开了第二季度的财报期了。

周二盘前：银行板块

大多数的银行都会在周二盘前发布财报。我比较关注的就是美国银行——从美国银行这个角度看一下消费者的消费水平。

其实和年初对比，有一点比我想象中的要好很多——就是劳动力市场。在年初的时候，我认为今年的劳动力市场会放缓，主要是因为 AI 的关系。但是到现在为止，so far 我们完全没有看到。

3 月份的战争事件，在 4 月份发布财报的时候，我们从银行的财报里边看到——3 月份战争事件对于消费者（因为油价的上涨嘛）几乎没有任何的影响。所以把这些东西全部再结合在一起，再加上最近这段时间 memory 价格的上涨，使得很多 3C 产品硬件方面价格的上涨，对于消费者的影响：

使得我在年初认为今年美联储会降息两次，现在我是赞同市场的看法，我认为今年美联储是有可能加息的。

所以我自己的看法，随着不同的数据进来之后，是会发生改变的。我并不会一直坚持"我年初是这么判断，现在还是这么判断"，而去忽略中间数据上的更新。

回到银行财报这边，我一直跟大家强调，美国银行的财报每一个季度都是我用来衡量消费者健康状况的一个水平。有一些数据可以关注：消费者的同比增速到底是多少，只要停在 4.5% 至 5% 之间，就代表整个消费没有任何的问题。

周三盘前：ASML 和强生

周四盘前：多家公司

  • UNH（我持有）：截止到周五收盘，估值差不多是在 20.1 倍到 21.5 倍吧，22 倍不到一些。我上次和大家分享过，再往上的话，23 倍就绝对是一个卖点。23 倍离 22 倍差不多还有 5% 的空间。所以如果再涨 5%，财报出来之后——比如说盘后出现了 5%、6% 上涨，那我就直接就卖了，可能会清仓。但如果财报之前市场继续上涨的话，那在发布财报之前就可以卖，因为估值到了 23 倍我认为就是一个卖点。这其实也是我前面说的，你就等等待市场给你机会。
  • 台积电：财报通常不会有太大的波动，因为台积电每一个月都公布数据。
  • GE（我持有）：过去这段时间走得也不错。市场在定价什么？上一个季度发布财报的时候，因为还在战争的状态之下，公司管理层给了一个相对没有那么乐观、比较悲观的指引。现在因为战争的迷雾开始慢慢消失，股价开始出现反弹——这就是和我上一个季度发布完财报之后所说的完全一样。

周四盘后：

  • Netflix：最近这段时间有很多人问我关于 Netflix 这个标的本身，平时我没有关注，但每一个季度财报我可以去看一下，听一下管理层的一些指引，包括财报会议。
  • Intuitive Surgical（ISRG）：我另外一家最近刚开仓的公司。

虽然只是财报季度的第一周，但是已经有 3 家我持有的公司发布财报。然后银行的财报可以看消费者的健康状况；ASML 和台积电都是在整个芯片板块的上游，你可以看到上游的需求；然后还有医疗板块的一些公司发布财报。

五、Q2 财报季展望（FactSet 数据）

EPS 增速预期

首先，现在市场预计第二季度标普 500 EPS 同比增速将会达到 23.6%。如果最终是超过 20% 的话，将会连续第二个季度标普 500 EPS 同比增速超过 20%。

在 3 月 31 日（第一季度结束的时候），市场对于第二季度的 EPS 增速指引预期是在 18.8%，19% 不到一些。也就是在过去三个月的时间里边，随着市场公布了第一季度的财报，市场反而对于第二季度变得更加的乐观。

但通常来说，在这一个季度里面，市场会把 EPS 增速往下调。为什么要往下调？ 调完之后，当最终公司发布财报的时候，容易去超过原本市场的预期——这是华尔街惯用的这一套：在这个季度里边先下调，下调完之后就容易去 beat。

但是在过去的一个季度里面反而是上调了。在一季度的时候，我们也看到了，即使在战争的情况下，EPS 增速还是往上调；往上调完之后，最终公布结果的时候，比调完之后的结果还要好。

所以这个季度也是一样：你每一个季度回过头去看的话，70% 到 80% 的公司都会比市场预期好，这是一个习惯、一个结果。但是我经常和大家说，财报本身好于市场预期不太重要——因为 80% 的公司最后都会比市场预期好，难道 80% 的公司发布完财报之后价格就都一定要上涨吗？肯定不是这样。但起码这一组数据显示了这一个季度整个标普 500 的公司会有多么的赚钱。

估值水平

从估值角度去看，FactSet 里面也是有数据的： - 当下估值：标普 500 是 25.5 倍 - 5 年平均：19.9 倍 - 10 年平均：19 倍

其实 20.5 和 19.9、20 倍几乎没有太大差别，也就是告诉你现在标普 500 的估值在 5 年的平均水平——高不高？绝对不算高。

板块贡献

标普 EPS 增速 23.6%，哪些板块会比平均数要好？哪些板块会差呢？主要是来自于三个板块，是非常非常的集中：

  1. Material（材料板块）
  2. 能源板块
  3. Technology（IT 板块）——同比增速超过 60%

前两个板块和战争有关系。为什么 IT 那么高？因为整个 IT 板块里边有很多的科技股，很多科技股主要里面有很多芯片。

注意：像我们通常所说的这些大科技，并不是在科技板块（IT）里面： - Consumer Discretionary 里边有两家大科技：亚马逊、特斯拉。如果把亚马逊给去掉的话，整个这一个板块的 margin 其实完全没有扩张。 - Consumer Services 里边也有两家公司：Meta、谷歌。

所以并不是所有的大科技全部在 IT 里面。而 IT 里边主要的增速非常高，是来自于芯片板块。

全年展望

最后是全年这边的预期：2026 年市场预计标普 500 EPS 增速能够达到 24.2%，Top line（营收增速）10.7%。

在年初的时候，大家可能忘了——这组数据是 15%，我记得非常的清楚。是因为我在年初的时候和大家说"年初降 Beta"，我的一个期待是把 Beta 降到 1，我的期待是今年标普 500 能够涨 15%（EPS 增速 15%、PE 不变，那整体价格就涨 15%）。所以我期待今年整体的投资回报就是 15%。

然后到了 3 月份，因为价格跌下去之后、估值跌下去之后开始加 Beta，慢慢地把 Beta 给加回来。

但是其实我们把战争的事件放在一边去，因为战争的事件并不会影响到这些公司的 EPS 增速。而只是和年初去做对比的话，我们应该这么说：市场其实没有预料到芯片板块的公司可以那么的赚钱——主要的这些 EPS 增速来自于芯片板块。

当然了，也有战争的事件使得能源板块和材料板块 EPS 的增速出现了上调，但是这两个板块因为权重的关系，所以对于整体变化的影响没有那么大，而 IT 板块的影响会大一些。

所以这 24.2% 主要现在市场的低估其实都是来自于芯片板块。那芯片板块 YTD 出现 90% 以上、将近 100% 的涨幅其实也是合理的——因为年初的时候市场是低估了。

六、Seeking Alpha 活动

最后是 Seeking Alpha 的活动。他们的量化分析首席分析师将会在 7 月 14 日中午美东时间 12 点至下午 1 点 30 和大家分享对于下半年的展望，当然会分享很多的标的。

这个量化策略本身： - YTD 跑赢标普 500 大幅跑赢 - 参加这次活动的门槛只需要订阅他们的 Premium - 现在有 7 天的免费试用，也就是说你可以现在订阅、免费试用 7 天、参加完这次活动直接取消——可以撸这一次的羊毛 - 当然如果喜欢的话，你可以持续订阅。现在首年有 9 折的优惠

他们现在还在推另外一个 Seeking Alpha Pro： - 价格会贵一些，一年是 1920 美金，原价是 2400，每个月要 200 美金了 - Pro 包含以前我聊的所有的服务 - 同时还有 Weekly 的一些 idea，每一周他们都会推一些做多和做空的标的 - 每一天都会有一些分享，包括标的本身是上调还是下调

对于一些交易者，包括对 Seeking Alpha 有比较信任程度的，可以考虑他们 Pro 的服务，因为价格相对会高一些。

无论是 Pro 还是其他的订阅，我会放在置顶的留言里面，大家感兴趣的话可以在那边找到。

那今天视频内容就到这边，我们下期见。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、隐藏的"角色互换"：市场正在替美联储做它不敢做的事
这期节目里最出人意料的观察，其实藏在小陶那句被一带而过的话里——**"市场已经通过两年期十年期利率的上涨，帮凯文沃什去做事了"**。
多数分析师习惯把美联储视为"投手"、市场视为"接球手"。但过去两个月发生的事，其实是**投手和接球手悄悄换了位置**：债券市场自己把长短端收益率抬上去，等于替美联储完成了一次"影子加息"。这意味着如果 9 月美联储不跟随市场定价加息，就会出现一个反直觉的局面——**不加息本身反而构成"背信"**，因为市场已经先行紧缩了，货币当局如果不确认，就等于在事后否定市场做的紧缩工作。
这里有个跨领域连接值得玩味：这个结构其实和**"预期自我实现的语言学现象"**（比如自证预言 self-fulfilling prophecy）非常像。市场用价格"说了一句话"，如果说话人（美联储）不呼应，语言系统就崩塌了——不是加息或不加息本身重要，而是**信号一致性**本身构成了新的政策资产。
## 二、"银行财报—消费者健康度"这条链，正在被一条更隐秘的链取代
节目里小陶依然把美国银行财报当作"消费者体温计

item.title: [Business of Sport] The Deal that Saved Silverstone
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一场被"三重锁"逼出来的商业觉醒

最反直觉的一点是：Silverstone 之所以变成一门年入 1.1 亿英镑的生意，恰恰是因为它差点破产。 2015 年那份"5% 逐年递增"的托管费合约，其实是英国养老金里那套 "triple lock" 机制的镜像——一种在低通胀时代反而具有毁灭性的复利结构。BRDC 长期把自己定位为"俱乐部"而非"企业"，是这个被 Bernie Ecclestone 逼到墙角的时刻，才第一次真正激活了他们作为经营者的身份。危机不是打断了它的传统，而是替它完成了本来永远不会自己启动的组织转型。

从"寻租经济"到"IP 经济"的路径切换

这一段真正的隐藏叙事，是 F1 商业模型的一次范式跃迁：Bernie 时代运行的是寻租型（rent-extraction）逻辑——把 Grand Prix 当稀缺牌照卖给主权国家，谁付得多谁办；Liberty 时代运行的是IP 复利型逻辑——观众密度、情绪浓度、镜头美学本身就是资产，因此 20 万人山人海的 Silverstone 比一场空荡荡的沙漠夜赛更值钱。这几乎和迪士尼在 2010 年代对乐园的重估如出一辙：从"门票收入中心"重新定义为"品牌情绪引擎"。Silverstone 被救，不是因为它便宜，而是因为 Liberty 发现观众的尖叫也是一种可折现的知识产权。

一个跨领域连接：BRDC 与"学院制"的隐秘同构

BRDC 的 850 人会员制、必须被提名和附议、必须"在赛车运动中有卓越成就"——它的社会学结构和牛津剑桥的学院（college）制度、英国皇家学会乃至法国法兰西学院几乎是同构的：一个不以盈利为目的、以"卓越认证"为货币的封闭网络。有趣之处在于：这类结构在现代资本主义里通常被视为低效、僵化、无法商业化的化石——但 Silverstone 的案例恰好证明，当外部环境（Liberty 的新契约）为它托底后，"学院制"反而变成一种极强的品牌护城河：它做不到别人做得到的敏捷，但它拥有别人永远无法伪造的血统。Vegas 和 Miami 由 F1 自己掏钱办赛，本质上是在购买 Silverstone 天然就拥有的东西——历史认证。

一个让人意外的观察

真正微妙的是：Silverstone 的胜利，是"最不像现代企业"的那部分——它的会员制、它的祖传俱乐部结构、它的"Bentley Boys 起源神话"——反而成了它在 Liberty 新商业逻辑下的核心资产。 十年前它是包袱，十年后它是无形资产。这几乎是一个反 DEI、反 KPI、反现代治理框架的"活化石"，靠着从不试图现代化的那一部分自己，完成了最漂亮的现代化转身。Charles Leclerc 赢了比赛，但真正的赢家是那个 1928 年成立、连爱尔兰籍 Eddie Jordan 都要单独开例外条款的老俱乐部。

📝 转录文本

开场：不被注意的英国大奖赛

So, moving on. So, another massive sporting event that happened last weekend, Charlie. Um, and almost like just for once outside of Petrol Headland, a little bit under the radar because you had the World Cup going at full pelt right here, including the buildup to the Big England game. You had Wimbledon building towards its its its finale here. Um, and then the little matter of the British Grand Prix comes along.

Now, the race itself, did you watch it?

No.

Okay. I was at a one-year-old's birthday party, which is the social events now that really keep you going.

Indeed. I I I did watch it, or at least I watched parts of it. Um, and it ended very unsatisfactorily. And that would be a whole conversation for a different day and probably not on this podcast about why um a major sporting event like that can finish behind a safety car, meaning that you don't get any any excitement or any um sort of, you know, fulfillment even towards the end of the race, right? Basically, it was a

Are you calling for a rerun of the Lewis Hamilton Max Vistappen disaster?

No, I'm I'm, as I said, it's a very complex topic. I don't want to go into it right now. It was an unsatisfactory race. Let's just put it that way.

Charlotte Clair won, didn't he?

Charlotte Clair won. Um, and

but then it was just a run of British drivers.

Loads of them. Loads of them.

事件规模：F1 历史最大盛会

Anyway, we got a lot to talk about here, so let's [laughter] not talk about the race, right? The race was the race and it was unsatisfactory and it and it was what it was. Um, but the event the event was absolutely massive. Do you know how many people turned up at Silverstone over the weekend?

564,000 people.

564,000 people over 3 days.

The biggest F1 event in history.

So you're talking about almost 200,000 a day Charles.

4 days.

Oh, it was four days.

Four days. Yeah, cuz they include the um Thursday as well.

So it's 150,000 on average a day, right? 150,000 people on average a day over a 4-day period. It was an absolute beast of an of an event and I thought it'd be quite a useful moment to examine both the British Grand Prix itself but also um to have a think about this area of the Formula 1 industry which is those countries and tracks that hosts events and the economics of that.

We've been very focused since Liberty Media took over Formula 1. We've been very focused on the top end i.e. what Liberty are doing and increasingly focused on the teams of course and we've discussed usefully over the last few months how the economics and the valuations of the teams have been moving dramatically and meanwhile the economics of the hosting side have dropped down the agenda where 20 years ago this was at the at the top of the Formula 1 sporting agenda.

BRDC：一个 1928 年成立的私人俱乐部

So um where to start? I think perhaps

do you want to do you want to really start? I think the first thing just based on how it's going to how we're going to develop this is to really look at who actually is responsible for putting on Silverstone itself.

Indeed.

That's right. So, um, Silverstone is owned and operated by something called the British Racing Drivers Club, the BRDC. Um, were you aware of this body until I brought it up with you?

I was. You were.

Yes. Okay.

But only I was only relatively recently, right? because I've been spammed by Jake Humphrey's social media accounts as he has just recently been made a member of the BRDC which he was absolutely ecstatic

about because it's because it's very meaningful in within petrol head world there are 850 members and to become a member you have to have achieved something of excellence within motorsport this isn't something which is okay well you know I'm rich I can just pay to get in this is an invitation thing you you're proposed and seconded and accepted you you know your proposal and second I have to explain this is why this person has has achieved something significant in motorsport and you have to be either British or from the Commonwealth right now they made a little exception to let Eddie Jordan in the late Eddie Jordan where they said okay well if you were born in Ireland before it before the whole thing officially became a fully separated if you're in the Irish free state or something like that basically it was we want Eddie in and we'll create a sort of exemption there for him because we like

Um, but it's 850 members. So, it was founded in 1928. So, we're coming up to the the centinery of the BRDC. And it was founded by a man called Dr. Dudley Benerfield. Um, and he was one of what was called the Bentley Boys. And these were the early motor racing drivers. These are the ones who back when, you know, just cars themselves were a relatively recent innovation, were racing some of the early faster types of cars, including obviously Bentley's, hence why they're known as the Bentley Boys.

And the BRDC was based at Brooklyn's, um, which was a track in in Surrey. Um, and it was, you know, an upand cominging sport. And during the during the Second World War, actually a lot of BRDC members died. A lot of them were involved in flying. Um so you have battle of Britain you know sort of um people who died in the battle of Britain etc.

从 Silverstone 到英国大奖赛

So it was still a relatively young organization as we come out the back of the second world war and this is when we start the sort of the move towards Silverstone. So they take they took over the lease of Silverstone in 1952, the BRDC did um as a motor racing track. Um and then in 1966, right? So this is within some people's not mine before you mention it. You cheeky sod this is within living memory for some people who might be listening to this show. Um so the BRDC um opened a subsidiary um which is called Silverstone Circuit Limited which was specifically for the development of the British Grand Prix. Right? So that's where we're going back to.

Bernie Ecclestone 与托管费的诞生

So over the following 20 years, the British Grand Prix develops and grows as do other Grand Pris around the world, but in slightly chaotic fashion. The whole thing wasn't sort of properly put together until, of course, famously Bernie Eckleston, who had been both a driver himself and then a team boss came along and said to the other team owners, "We need to organize this properly. We need to sell the rights properly. We need to do this whole thing in a much more structured

way."

And of course by this point the British Grand Prix is one of the established Grand Prix one the legacy the historic Grand Pris which Bernie's new F1 circuit effectively circus inherits the British Grand Prix at Silverston as already being a major event. This is the early 19 late 1970s early 1980s. So it's already a a major sporting event.

Um and uh um Bernie's business model then um starts to develop during the 1980s. And one of the ways in which Bernie starts to make money where previously they would say, "Oh well, you know, the British RA the British Racing Drivers Club is having a Grand Prix and that's that's great. Let's all go and race there and happy days." Bernie then starts saying to circuits, "If you want your Grand Prix to be in my circuits, if I'm going to bring all of these teams to race at your circuit, you need to pay me money. You need to pay Formula 1 management from money."

It's what called a hosting fee, right? So that starts off quite small, but as Bernie then starts to access Grand Pris and different parts of the world where they're very happy to pay very large sums of money, particularly the the push into the Middle East that started to happen 20 25 years ago, um some of the historic circuits start to drop out because they can't afford to pay these hosting fees.

挤压：托管费失控与 2015 年破产边缘

Okay? And eventually the hosting fees started to get so big that Silverson started to struggle to pay them as well. So this is around about 10 [snorts] to 15 years ago. They've gone from 5 million to 10 million to 15 million a year, right? And by this point, what Bernie's done within Formula 1 is he's basically said, right, I keep pretty much all the commercial revenues for Formula 1 management. Um, obviously we've taken the TV rights revenues. So as a host or as a host of the race, you don't get any TV rights revenue. You don't get any commercial revenue. Um, the one thing you do get is ticketing. Congratulations. Right.

So, you're starting to be confronted with quite a stark thing, which is we pay Formula 1 management £15 million a year. We then need to get £15 million worth of ticketing just to cover our costs.

So, the chairman John Grant, I was I was just reading this. I mean, he very he effortlessly put it is the costs are more than the revenues. [laughter]

[gasps] Yes.

Great. I mean that's the kind of [laughter]

So what happened was by 2015 they're on the verge of bankruptcy. They were being bankrupted by Bernie Eckleston and Bernie Eckle's viewers well guys if you don't want to have a Grand Prix don't have a Grand Prix. Right. Really interesting.

Yeah. Which he he used to terrible circuit anyway. Terrible facilities. He was always slagging it off. He was always slagging off the BRDC.

Right. I think was there an element of sort of chip on shoulder about the whole thing that there was this rather sort of you know grand you know club which included lots of jukes and ss and there was Bernie the southeast London car salesman who wasn't really part of that whole set. Was there a bit of that going on paying off old scores from what he saw as maybe he was felt slighted in the 1970s by not being invited to be a member back then? all of maybe there was a bit of all that going on but the bottom line is is that by 2015 which is just before when Liberty took over.

They're tied into this contract with Bernie by which basically the amount they have to pay to host the race increases by a like it's the equivalent of the pension triple lock increases ahead of inflation every single year.

Well, it was on 5% escalators, weren't they?

Exactly. So each year it went up 5%.

Yeah.

And that this is during a period when inflation was very very low. But it's also interesting here because it's during a period where Formula 1 itself is struggling more than it has done in a very long time. And that was important. That's very important on both sides.

Right? So on Bernie's side, the fact that Formula 1 was struggling in general meant that the hosting fees had become an absolutely critical [snorts] part of the entire financial ecosystem. Extracting money from tracks was becoming the main revenue line, right? Because they were going to track in the Middle East. Bernie was saying, "Right, do you want to do you want to have a Grand Prix? Well, it's 40 million, right? Do you want to have a Grand Prix? It's 45 million." And then to Silverson saying, "Well, look, if you're not even willing to pay me 15, 20 million, then I'll just go and take another one somewhere else. You know, I'm not particularly interested in you."

Which of course was disastrous for the sport and and you lost some historic Grand Prix like the one in South Africa and the one in Argentina during this 20 25 year period because Bernie knew that they wouldn't pay the hosting fees and he'd rather take it somewhere else. But of course those were the places where you get massive crowds in place in big countries like South Africa and Argentina whereas some of the places where Bernie then took Formula 1 2 was was a little bit more marginal.

破约条款与 Liberty Media 的救援

So John Grant in effect he activated the break law clause didn't he for the BRDC. So he basically said

exactly right we're going to fine we're out.

We're out.

So the just before Bernie sold or CBC and Bernie sold to Liberty Media which 2017 I think 2017 2018 just before then Silverstone the the British racing drivers club had activated a break clause in the contract and they basically said we can't afford this right so we were looking at the end of the British Grand Prix at that point and that was much discussed in the media at the time um and then Liberty come along and Liberty take a totally different view of the industry and the sport and say no the British Grand Prix is a crucial bit of IP which lends into the value of the entire thing having a race where there are 200,000 people there and everyone's going absolutely mad this is like the core of fandom and we're trying to build fandom so they then went and cut a better deal with the BRDC which meant the BRDC DC could then see right we are now going to carry on hold hosting this Grand Prix for a significant period of time but we have much greater certainty over it potentially affordable.

Stuart Pringle 与 BRDC 的自我改革

But at the same time, the BRDC decided, okay, well, we also do need to get our own house in order, right? We need to become a more diversified business so that we can, you know, be well, a proper business rather than a club that holds a Grand Prix. We actually need to be a motor racing business as a whole.

And there's been a guy there sort of unsung hero of sports business guy there called called Stuart Pringle who's been the CEO or I think originally was the secretary of the British Racing Drivers Club but since then the title's been changed to CEO since 2006. So we're talking about a 20year um regime stability there. The first 10 of which was extremely difficult when they were having to spend money on repairing facilities and when the hosting fees were going up and up and when Formula 1 as a whole was going down and down leading to the verge of bankruptcy in 2015.

十年翻身：从破产边缘到 1.1 亿英镑营收

Lo and behold 10 years later through both the efforts of Silverstone themselves but also of course the efforts of Liberty Media in terms of the way in which they run Formula 1. Silverstone now has an annual turnover of 110 million pounds 50% of which is from the British Grand Prix. So you can work it out. Okay, it's about 50 to 60 million they get from from the Brit from the British Grand Prix and 50% from the rest of the year from the stuff that they do during the rest of the year.

And during that period they have invested heavily in a heritage center, conference center, hotel, increased capacity hospitality all of this type of all this type of stuff. And Liberty have got a much more progressive model in terms of working with tracks where these days the tracks do get to keep past the hospitality, right? So it's not the old days of the paddock club where that was all kept in house. These days tracks are incentivized to drive hospitality and to actually sort of become sort of part of the whole mix.

The bottom line is they're now making pre-tax profit of 14 million quid and they've got cash reserves of almost 50 million quid. I mean it's the most astonishing sports business turnaround.

10 years.

Yeah. In less than 10 years you've gone from basically going we can't afford this to now just this week gone by hence why we're talking about it. The biggest event in Formula 1 history.

Yes, that is actually most importantly financially valuable,

profitable and massively valuable. So if you think so ju let's just have a think about this right just as a business as a sports industry business annual turnover of 110 million quid with a profit margin of getting on for 10%. No but more than 10% getting on for 15%. Right? So you're looking at probably a business which the BRDC if it were a private company and was now to you know sell itself criy I don't know what would it be worth um you're probably talking about certainly the thick end of 500 million right so you've gone from something on the verge of bankruptcy to something that's worth 500 odd million in the space of 10 years so it's been a great

一个古怪但成功的组织

ju just give people just quickly to give people a bit of a flavor of how idiosyncratic this organization is so um the president is David Koultard, the former Formula 1 driver. And the vice presidents are Martin Brundle, former Formula 1 driver media pundit. Ron Dennis, the former McLaren boss, Damon Hill, the former Formula 1 world champion, Nigel Manel, the former Formula 1 world champion, Jackie Stewart,

Derek Warrick, the former [laughter] the former um Formula 1.

So basically,

their diversity is very strong.

So basically this

the Lancaster cricket board test, they're not passing that.

They're not. But they're actually doing a great job. [laughter]

They're actually doing a great job.

They might not be ticking too many DEI boxes here, but the BRDC are doing a great job. And for all that, sometimes um on this show we do pick apart things that aren't working. I think sometimes we have to take a step back and say that it wasn't Silverson's fault that this race was a mess. That was Formula 1's problem. In terms of the event as a whole, obviously stalking weather as well, which helped. This was an absolutely epic sports business event.

横向对比：全球托管模式

Yeah. And most interestingly as I was going through it as well, it was looking at this compared to the other hosting organizations around. And this is so this is the only private um members club hosting. I think actually I'm going to say now because I think the Dutch Grand Prix was also similar, but they're losing their race. So it will now be the only independent. The rest of the uh models generally are funded by state. So then thereabar Abu Dhabi

Bahrain actually Australia part for the Melbourne Grand Prix um and and really for those fees you know

I'm not sure how it works in Miami.

So I do. Yeah. So but but the fees attached on the state naturally, you know, Aabaijan reportedly are paying $57 million a year. Qatar and Saudi 55 10year agreements locked in with Qatar. So that's 550 million. Abu Dhabi 42, Australia, Singapore 3540. You then have kind of a mixture of promoter and state, which is the Monaco and Monza type models and they pay about 15 to$20 million. And that's interestingly they both recently doubled uh in the new extensions so

prices going up.

Then you have silvers and a private members club.

Then you have Miami and Vegas. F1 itself pays the hosting fee and it's not hard to understand why the value for Formula 1

breaking the US market

breaking the US market and having Vegas.

So those are generally the different ways in which um Formula 1 makes its hosting fees and it's it totals you you mentioned it was a big part. I mean, it's $700 million a year

in hosting fees

in hosting fees for

F1.

That's the amount of money that the holding company of Formula 1 rakes in from places paying them for the privilege of having a Grand Prix.

It's a huge business.

Yeah. It's about 20 to 25% of Formula 1's revenue,

which is extraordinarily high.

巴林视角：为什么国家愿意付钱

It is. Yeah. It's a key part of of the pie and you can sort of understand it like I I I advised the Bahin Grand Prix for many many years. um criy 8 n years and I would often ask you know is is this worth it right absolutely it's worth it right this is one week of the year when the attention of a good chunk of the world is on our little island kingdom you know we we struggle for attention we struggle to drive a tourism ministry even though we've got beautiful places for people to come to how do we drive attention on that how do we you know other other countries might spend vast amounts of money on marketing tourism but we find that by hosting a Grand Prix with all of the imagery that goes around that and the eyeballs that go onto that imagery is actually what helps drive our entire economy.

It also means that they can then have a motor a motor sports economy in Bahrain because they got a Formula 1 track there. So you can test cars, test products. It enables them to be a center for motorsport in the Middle East. So as an investment over a 20-year period, it's been extremely successful.

结语：Liberty 的信念与 F1 的新逻辑

So, this has actually been for most um of these deals, it's been a happy thing. For the BRDC, it was a little bit different. They were there. They were there first and they were a club, you know, not they're not trying to pay dividends and make big profits and all this type of stuff, but they've effectively been forced into the modern age into the 21st century by the economics of hosting a Gro and they've done it.

The F1 also have the flexibility to make the calls as and where they want to on which countries and which host cities will pay more for it. So that's a balance of heritage versus under Liberty they've been much more cognizant of that where under Bernie it was getting very merkantile it was whoever pays the most

it's the luxury of success when you're successful you can work out how to make those numbers work for you when you're struggling for cash you're making decisions but Liberty did this right from the start didn't they so they did it before they made a big success out of F1 they made decisions like we need to keep the British Grand Prix so it's it's a bit of chicken and egg involved here um but I think you have to give Liberty a lot of credit for saying we're not losing the British Grand Prix

brilliant

Brilliant. Fantastic.

item.description:
## 一场被"三重锁"逼出来的商业觉醒
最反直觉的一点是：**Silverstone 之所以变成一门年入 1.1 亿英镑的生意，恰恰是因为它差点破产。** 2015 年那份"5% 逐年递增"的托管费合约，其实是英国养老金里那套 "triple lock" 机制的镜像——一种在低通胀时代反而具有毁灭性的复利结构。BRDC 长期把自己定位为"俱乐部"而非"企业"，是这个被 Bernie Ecclestone 逼到墙角的时刻，才第一次真正激活了他们作为经营者的身份。**危机不是打断了它的传统，而是替它完成了本来永远不会自己启动的组织转型。**
## 从"寻租经济"到"IP 经济"的路径切换
这一段真正的隐藏叙事，是 F1 商业模型的一次范式跃迁：Bernie 时代运行的是**寻租型（rent-extraction）**逻辑——把 Grand Prix 当稀缺牌照卖给主权国家，谁付得多谁办；Liberty 时代运行的是**IP 复利型**逻辑——观众密度、情绪浓度、镜头美学本身就是资产，因此 20 万人山人海的 Silverstone 比一场空荡荡的沙漠夜赛更值钱。这几乎和迪士尼在 20

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The 1904 Map That Explains Every War Since – Sarah Paine
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一张1904年地图背后的"反重力"逻辑

大多数人看 Mackinder 的心脏地带理论，会本能地想："那是关于陆地 vs 海洋的争论。"但真正令人意外的是——Mackinder 的理论其实是在偷偷论证"地理惰性"胜过"地理机动性"。海权强调的是流动、贸易、连接；陆权的核心却是"被隔绝"这件事本身就是资产。他说心脏地带"不是被海洋隔离，而是从海洋中被隔离出来"——这句话的哲学意味被严重低估了：一个文明的战略价值可能不来自它能到达多远，而来自别人到不了它。这与今天硅谷"connectivity is everything"的信条几乎完全相反。

跨领域连接：心脏地带与"深科技公司的护城河"

有趣的是，Mackinder 的"pivot area"逻辑几乎可以一比一映射到现代深科技公司的护城河结构。台积电、ASML、SpaceX 这些公司之所以难以颠覆，不是因为它们"到处扩张"，而是因为它们建立了一个由技术复杂性、资本厚度、供应链纵深构成的"工业心脏地带"——外部力量（无论多有钱）都只能在"边缘"（Rimland）通过合作、代工、许可来施加影响。Spykman 说美国必须依赖"大陆盟友提供陆权支点"，翻译成今天的语言，就是：苹果需要台积电，OpenAI 需要微软的算力大陆。海权公司（分发、平台、品牌）永远需要一个陆权盟友来落地。这套 1904 年的地图，意外地是一份现代产业竞争的说明书。

隐藏真相：Sun Tzu 的"沉默"比他说的话更重要

Paine 提到 Sun Tzu "对海战几乎没有任何提及"——这被她一笔带过，但这里藏着一个关于战略思维本身的元洞见：一个文明能想象的战争形态，决定了它能想象的政治形态。中国两千年来的治理逻辑——"稳态、纵深、周边缓冲、内部秩序优先"——本质上就是"孙子式陆权思维"的社会化外溢。而海权文明（英国、荷兰、后来的美国）发展出"契约、保险、股份、议会"这些制度，是因为海上贸易需要在不可控的环境中和陌生人合作。

换句话说："中国为什么不发展现代金融和民主"的答案，可能早在孙子写下第一页时就已经写好了——不是因为文化，而是因为没有海洋，就没有陌生人合作的迫切压力。今天中美竞争的深层结构，其实是两种延续了两千五百年的"战争想象力"在正面碰撞。而 Mackinder 的地图之所以能"解释此后每一场战争"，是因为它无意中画出的不是地缘，而是两种世界观的物理边界。

📝 转录文本

一、Mackinder 的反论：心脏地带才是世界的枢纽

There was a counter argument to Mahan that comes a generation later by a Britain, Sir Halford Mackinder, who's looking at the world and looking at Britain's problems. And he thinks that actually Russia occupies the best geopolitical location on the world, not Great Britain.

Cuz in this is a map from his 1904 article, and he defines as the pivot area — Eurasia is the part of the world he's focusing on, and it's where Russia is. And he also calls it the Great Heartland.

And he said: "Look, that area, the pivot area, is the citadel of land power on the great mainland of the world. It is the greatest natural fortress on the world."

Why? Because it's insulated by mountains, deserts, frozen seas, and rivers that exit onto the oceans in very inaccessible places during wartime. And therefore, it is impervious to sea power. Britain can't really get at that area.

And he said: "But if you occupy the heartland, this pivot area, you have the possibility for defense in depth, strategic retreat, and self-sufficiency."

So, the heartland is not insulated by the sea — it's insulated from the sea. And a maritime power like Britain, if it wants to influence the heartland, it's got to do it on the periphery. In those days, it would be through having colonies in these very strategic locations. In our own day, it would be having allies with bases in these locations.

二、Spykman：控制欧亚，就可能控制世界

This man, Nicholas Spykman, was also very concerned about who controls Eurasia. He felt the country that controls Eurasia could well control the world.

And he had much to worry about when he completed his very important work in 1943, the year he dies. He's a naturalized American citizen from the Netherlands, then under Nazi occupation. And he felt that the Nazis had come really close to dominating Eurasia in 1941.

And here's what he said:

"Despite occupying the safest position of any nation in the world, we Americans have been involved in two devastating world wars in the space of a quarter of a century. And in the second one — i.e. the ongoing one — we were at one point in serious danger of defeat."

"The expectations of US statesmen regarding the outcome of their actions were consistently wrong. And their thinking about national security generally failed to provide successful answers."

Sound familiar?

三、海权、陆权与"边缘地带"（Rimland）

In addition, he's looking at the world and he said:

"Look, the influence of the United States can be brought to bear on Europe and the Far East only by means of seaborne traffic. And the power of the states of Eurasia can reach us effectively only by crossing the seas. And this is true despite the development of air power — because big things go by sea, and they still do."

Therefore, US security was a function of sea power. And that sanctuary at home — and by that I mean invulnerability to attack — is a function of a maritime shield.

And Spykman's really concerned who controls the Rimland of this great heartland, friend or foe. And here's why:

"The United States will have to depend on her sea power communication across the Atlantic and the Pacific to give her access to the old world. And the effect of this access — it's going to delimit what sort of foreign policy the United States can have. And what the United States requires is a continental ally who can provide a base from which land power can be exercised."

What he's saying in World War II — victory in that thing is going to depend on alliances and allies. Critical.

四、路线图：从大陆帝国到海洋帝国

All right. So that was the hors d'oeuvres. I've done the whirlwind tour of American history in a few minutes or less — you should be happy about that one. And then I've given you some of the most famous geopoliticians there are, a synopsis of what their ideas are.

But here's my main course:

  • First I'm going to talk about continental empires, because they come first in human history.
  • And then I'll turn to maritime empires, which come later.
  • But then there's the Industrial Revolution and the post-World War institutional framework that opens the possibility of a maritime global order.

So that's my game plan.

五、术语小插曲：大象与鲸鱼

Oh, I forgot one thing. I don't know whether you all like animals or not, but we at the Naval War College love animals. And so we often refer to continental powers as elephants and maritime powers as whales. Bonus terminology.

六、孙子与大陆帝国的世界

All right. I'm going to start out with China — not Russia, which Mackinder was preoccupied with — as the original land power.

And here is Sun Tzu, the preeminent Chinese theorist on matters of strategy, in his Art of War. And Sun Tzu's providing advice to kings on how to stay on the throne, to conquer neighbors, and do it efficiently.

And in this world, you've got lots of neighbors — any of which can invade at any time. Third-party intervention is the norm in this world.

And if you think about this book, it makes no references to maritime warfare at all, and it makes only tangential references to riverine warfare. And you go: well, why is that?

Well, excellent reasons. He is writing about the world of continental empires in existence long before the maritime things get going.

And if you think about the great civilizations of Eurasia, often at their basis is their great continental empires. And a civilization answers the questions: how should I live and how should I interact with others — and is basically a world order unto itself. And civilizations have often fought with each other to make their rules universal.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

大多数人看 Mackinder 的心脏地带理论，会本能地想："那是关于陆地 vs 海洋的争论。"但真正令人意外的是——**Mackinder 的理论其实是在偷偷论证"地理惰性"胜过"地理机动性"**。海权强调的是流动、贸易、连接；陆权的核心却是**"被隔绝"这件事本身就是资产**。他说心脏地带"不是被海洋隔离，而是从海洋中被隔离出来"——这句话的哲学意味被严重低估了：一个文明的战略价值可能不来自它能到达多远，而来自**别人到不了它**。这与今天硅谷"connectivity is everything"的信条几乎完全相反。

item.title: [Business of Sport] Norway's Talent Factory: Developing the Players That Are Gunning For England
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的核心发现：把精英俱乐部"隔离"在年轻人之外

真正颠覆认知的一点，视频里几乎一带而过——挪威系统最革命性的动作，不是"加了什么"，而是"减了什么"：他们把职业俱乐部从 12 岁以下的青训里彻底剔除了。

全世界（包括英格兰、西班牙、荷兰、阿根廷、苏格兰）的逻辑都是"越早识别、越早签下、越早集中训练=越强"。挪威用 Haaland、Ødegaard、Berg、Bodø/Glimt 集体证明了反命题。这本质上是在挑战一个自 20 世纪 70 年代以来被视作圣经的假设——"1 万小时定律"式的早期专业化。

这里藏着一个几乎没有人指出的幸存者偏差：那些用早期识别系统成功的球员（梅西 13 岁进拉玛西亚、鲁尼 9 岁进埃弗顿）之所以被反复讲述，是因为他们本来就是身体早熟型的极端个例。挪威系统真正拯救的是被现有体系直接筛掉的另外 80% ——那些和 Haaland 一样"12 岁前身材瘦小、看起来不像球员"的孩子。换句话说，其他国家的青训体系不是在挑选球员，而是在挑选身体早熟的儿童。

二、跨领域连接：这其实是"期权定价理论"在体育上的实现

如果你把每个孩子看作一份"未来球员期权"，欧洲主流青训做的事就是在最大不确定性的时点提前行权（early exercise）——在 9 岁时决定谁进职业俱乐部、谁被淘汰。金融学告诉我们，在不确定性最高时行权，是所有期权持有者最愚蠢的选择。

挪威做的恰好相反：延迟行权、保留可选性（optionality）到最后一刻。当孩子 15 岁时——身体特征、心理成熟度、内在动机三条曲线基本收敛——才做出选择。这在数学上必然是更优策略。

同一模式在其他领域反复出现：

  • 芬兰教育：16 岁前不做标准化分流考试，PISA 常年霸榜。同一逻辑。
  • 风险投资：一线基金 seed 轮宁可撒网 100 家、B 轮再重仓 10 家，也不敢在 pre-seed 押注 3 家 all-in。同一逻辑。
  • David Epstein 在《Range》中总结：网球界 Federer 20 岁才专攻网球，同期"小时候只打网球"的儿童选手 90% 在 15 岁前烧尽退役。同一逻辑。
  • NBA 案例：Steph Curry 高中被所有大学拒绝，Jordan 高中队被砍——他们都是"挪威模式"意外幸存者。

也就是说，挪威不是发明了什么，他们把一个跨行业的最优策略应用到了别人还没意识到的领域。

三、被严重低估的观察：Bodø/Glimt 悖论证明"顶级球员"其实是烟雾弹

大多数人（包括视频里的两位主持人）在讨论挪威时都聚焦 Haaland、Ødegaard——这恰恰是最误导人的切入角度。

真正证明系统的不是这两个星巴, 而是Bodø/Glimt：一个北极圈内、人口 5.5 万的小镇球队，在欧冠正赛中连续击败马竞、曼城、国米。这里面没有 Haaland、没有 Ødegaard，全是挪威本土球员。

这意味着什么？意味着挪威模式生产的不是"个别天才"，而是"合格球员的密度"。星巴只是分布的右尾——真正的护城河是分布的整体右移。这就像评价日本制造业时，不看丰田，而看"随便一家名不见经传的岐阜县町工厂做出来的螺丝也世界一流"。密度才是国家竞争力的真相。

这里还有一个让人后背发凉的连接：93% vs 70% 的数据（挪威 25 岁前保留 93% 的青少年在有组织运动里，美国 13 岁前 70% 退出）本质上不是体育数据，而是社会健康数据。 挪威在不经意间用体育构建了一套全民终身社群基础设施——它同时解决了肥胖率、青少年心理健康、社群凝聚力、军事储备体能……在人口老龄化的欧洲，这可能是比夺冠更值钱的隐藏红利。

四、附加的意外观察：苏格兰的困境是"资源诅咒"

苏格兰有 Celtic、Rangers、悠久足球史、与英超零障碍的迁移路径——这些本应是优势，恰恰变成锁死改革的枷锁。这与"石油诅咒"、"底特律汽车工业困境"、"柯达数码转型失败"是同一种病：既得利益的存在使得系统性改革在政治上不可能。

挪威能改革，恰恰因为它没什么可失去的旧体系。这引出一个近乎残酷的结论——在体育治理上，"落后"有时是最大的隐藏优势，因为它给了你重构的自由。

📝 转录文本

引子：为什么谈挪威 vs 苏格兰

Charlie, we are going to go back to, um, my second homeland — because while Norway have been tearing it up, should we say, at the World Cup, I think there's a more interesting story to really pull out that we've actually touched on a tiny bit in the past when we were going through Winter Olympic sports.

But we didn't really develop it. And now you've seen the development of Norway in a global sport, there's a real story here around how Norway are managing to produce talent that is competing on a global stage. And we've seen it across various sports over the last 10 to 15 years, and now we're starting to really see it in football.

So what we're seeing here — I tell you what, I want to take this story and use it as a comparison point against Scotland.

Right? Scotland is, you know, the land across the water from Viking land, and there have been all sorts of treaties in the past to do with the Shetlands and all this type of stuff. So you've got two countries which are both geographically quite contiguous, and also they're about the same size of country as well in terms of population, in terms of category of country. So we're not talking about one country that's 50 million and another one that's 5 million. We're talking about — what's the population in Norway, Charlie?

5 and a half. No, both pretty much 5 and a half million. It's 5.4, 5.5.

They're both same size of country, similar kind of climate for the most part. Probably you'd say Scotland has got the advantage in some ways of having some very large established football clubs who therefore would have the finances to finance proper youth development systems and all that kind of stuff. And they've got more footballing history as well, and they've also got extreme proximity to England, which means that players growing up in the Scottish system can translate into the English system very, very easily — both literally, physically, but also from a visa/passport point of view.

So there's a whole lot of reasons why Scotland should be advantaged compared to Norway. And yet they're not. So what have Norway been doing that Scotland have not been doing?

核心哲学：让运动首先是快乐的

Let me lay out the Norway case and give you the explanation for how they've actually done this, and you can then tie that into how we could look at Scotland. So I just want to read this out at the beginning, because this is going to build the fundamental structure of really how Norway have done it:

By the age of 25, 93% of Norway's population has been involved in some kind of organized sports. Conversely, the American Academy of Pediatrics report in 2024 that 70% of youth athletes drop out of organized sports by the age of 13. Injury and burnout are the principal reasons why. Discontinuation of sports during childhood plays a role in more than 75% of adolescents in the US who fail to meet physical activity recommendations.

So the reason I read that out is because it plays to the heart of Norway's thesis on sport, which is: sport should be fun and it should be built around enjoyment.

So Norway basically have this philosophy of — when up to 12 years old, you will only play in a kind of local club environment. You will try lots of different sports. It will not be boring in its tactical sessions. If you're playing football, it will be far more about how you enjoy and engage in the sport. So by the time you get to 12, when you're ready and starting to physically develop, you will hopefully be coming to that age going, "I love this. I love it. I haven't been overexposed. I've got the exposure that I needed, but I haven't been overexposed. By the time I'm 12, I'm ready to push on" — not "I'm feeling forced into doing this and I want to push back."

That system has basically allowed them to go, "Right, you enjoy yourself, have a great time. Then, at the right time, we'll start looking at how to develop you."

基础设施：平等主义信条的物理化

They've done that in a number of ways. Firstly, they provided facilities. So tie this into Scotland as well — this will be a key point.

They've got a real challenge with the weather, of course. Norway is incredibly cold and all the pitches freeze over for a huge part of the year. So what do they do? They invested heavily in the infrastructure. Over 60% of pitches were artificial by 2015. Most of them are free to play on. So there is no barrier for entry.

They basically said — you know, sport, and it's a very egalitarian country, Norway. There's very much this equal belief of access and rights. So why would we change that with our sporting philosophy? So we're not going to keep sports access for those that can afford it. It's going to be for everyone.

Landsskolen：12 岁之后的精英通道

What they then have done on top of that is created something called the Landsskolen — the elite academy post-12 that players will then get picked out of their football clubs to then go and play. So the top 10% will be nominated and they will then go and start playing in training camps together, which is then centralized really to develop the future Norwegian football team. But it comes out of the clubs that have been rearing these players in a very specific way from whatever young age they've started.

This is the track that Erling Haaland has taken. This is the track that Martin Ødegaard has taken.

So how does that translate? I'm going to ask you how important it is to ask here: up until the age of 12, they play in local clubs.

Local clubs.

Not in professional clubs. They play in local clubs. And then at 12, the national system picks them up. Okay? So at this point they're still not in professional football clubs.

No.

Right? So they are not entering a professional football club environment until they're much closer to the point of their professional career.

Haaland 案例：晚熟球员的幸存

So if you look at — again, if we go back to that statement at the beginning about age — Erling Haaland is of course a great example. Erling Haaland physically is an absolute specimen, and that is a huge part of his game. He was actually quite small when he was younger.

Is that how he escaped being a ski jumper?

I think it could be.

[laughter] And I'm delighted that he didn't become a ski jumper.

He was a late developer. Or or maybe not. I don't —

I was wondering that, because I was looking at him the other day going: surely a ski jumper. Surely a ski jumper. You can't make any assumptions.

You can't make any assumptions. But you know, there's a very high chance that in a different ecosystem, Erling Haaland would have fallen through the cracks. He would have suffered from the fate that a lot of footballers do, particularly at a young age, where physically a coach will assess them and say, "You're not quite up to it. You're not a striker. You don't have the physique." However, in Norway's system, it's perfect — because by the time he actually started to show his promise, he'd had the physical chance to start developing into the player that he's now become. At 16, he then goes to Molde.

与欧洲主流体系的关键分野

See, now this is the revolutionary bit, right? This is the bit where they are different from everywhere else in Europe — and indeed in Latin America — which is that the elite clubs are not being entrusted with developing these players.

Okay? The very local system and then the national system is doing it up until the age of 15, at which point the boy is starting to become properly physically developed and more mentally developed, and has decided "I really enjoy this. I love this. This is something I want to do." So effectively, they are then put into professional clubs at the age at which a professional career is right in their eye line. Right? "I'm two years away from making my first team debut. I'm going to probably play for the under-18 team." This is probably what they have in the Norwegian professional clubs. And then "I can see the pathway to the first team straight away."

Now, if you contrast that to the system that we have — before we even go Scotland has the same system as the rest of Europe effectively, where youth development is entrusted to the professional elite clubs. So in other words, what happens is: kid starts playing at 5, 6, 7, shows some promise, is the best kid in the local schoolyard. Somebody somewhere says to a scout who works on behalf of a local professional football club, "This kid's got some ability." At 9 years old, they're brought into that professional football club, and the professional football club will have a look at 80 of them at the same time.

So — sorry, just on that — a key point of difference there is you've already identified the sport that they're going to be playing.

Absolutely. Now, this this is a key weakness of this system, right? Which is effectively then from the age of nine they then only play football.

Yeah.

Pretty much. Now, there's a lot of research that's been done that suggests that that is a leading contributor towards injuries in the medium to long term, because effectively you are repeating the same movement patterns as your body is developing. And there's quite a lot of interest now at the better academies in: right, we actually need to get our kids in the academies doing other types of sport and moving in other types of ways. First of all, to keep them mentally fresh so that they are not just football, football, football, but also frankly so that their bodies are moving in different ways. Otherwise, we could end up with a lot of injury problems down the track.

小国的战略：把尽可能多的人留在系统里

What gives you a better chance of success, right? If you're a small country of 5.5 million people — again versus let's just say the US, because it's 350 million — you want to keep as many of those players in the system for as long as possible.

You have a much higher likelihood of doing that.

That's true.

If they enjoy it.

Yeah. So again, like a 95% rate of Norwegians playing a team sport by the time they're 25.

Yeah.

That gives Norway — yes, there's a small population, but it gives them a really broad pool of athletes to then be able to go and build elite professionals across various different areas.

交叉训练的红利：多领域冠军的家族

You have Johannes Thingnes Bø, the record Winter Olympian who won 11 gold medals — smashing the record of eight previously — in this Winter Olympics. Just to be clear, he won six this Winter Olympics, but 11 in total. He was always going to be a footballer. That was always what he thought. But then as he develops and gets to a certain age where, because he hasn't necessarily specialized or been pressured into one or another — "hey, cross-country skiing is actually not too bad and he's bloody good at it." And so there was then a choice that was made purely off his own free will.

Karsten Warholm, the 400-meter hurdle gold medal in Tokyo. Jakob Ingebrigtsen — we were talking about Josh Kerr the other week, the great rival of Josh Kerr in the 1500, and he's 5000-meter champion as well. Markus Rooth, decathlon — they won the decathlon gold in Paris.

So you're getting Olympic medal winners. You're getting Casper Ruud, the tennis player, been top of tennis for a very long time. Viktor Hovland, won eight PGA titles — really should be winning a major, Ryder Cups. Ada Hegerberg won the Ballon d'Or féminin just a few years ago. They've had players at the top of that game for a long time.

We're just now starting to see this be the fruits of the work that they've been putting in place behind the scenes really start to show the value in the biggest sport of all. And whether it's Haaland, whether it's Ødegaard, whether it's the team — I mean, Berg in midfield, again, without getting too tactical, but he plays for Bodø/Glimt. I thought — I was just watching the game and he just stood out a mile in the middle of the pitch. He was unbelievable.

And you suddenly look at it — part of the Bodø group. They've got this culture around the team, which also underpins — I don't want to get too wishy, but it's a really important point because it comes back to this: if you succeed in encouraging that enjoyment, you'll build better people, you'll build better team environments. And a huge part of what they have been able to achieve or are achieving as a team clearly comes from that collective approach and mentality.

总结挪威模式的两大支柱

There's just a few things I want to pick up here on the football side. Let's just summarize it thus.

First of all, keep as many promising footballers playing football and enjoying playing football for as long as possible — because you don't quite know at what stage a lot of them are going to develop. And if you leave it to the elite clubs, the elite clubs will start eliminating them at a much too early stage, because basically it's costing them money and they have to start making choices, they have to focus on certain players and all this type of stuff. So that's the first observation I'd make.

The second observation I'd make is cross-training — they're getting them to do other sports as well at a crucial physical developmental stage.

Now, those two things are what I think is underpinning this, and they are two things that the other small nations can copy. I think it's harder in a very big nation, because that national piece, when you bring them all in — at whenever it is, 15, right? What is it, 12 you said actually? Between effectively that's —

It's 12, but Haaland for example signed for Molde when he was 16.

  1. Yeah, well —

So he didn't make it into the top-end professional environment until he was at the stage where Wayne Rooney was making his debut.

Exactly right.

Or Max Aarons at 15 running around in Austria.

苏格兰问题：为什么改革不会发生

Right. So my point here, tying it back to Scotland — and the reason we're discussing Scotland and Norway is because Scotland had a terrible performance at the World Cup, and Norway have had an outstanding performance at the World Cup.

Both hadn't participated for 28 years. You literally had exactly the same —

Same size populations, all that type of stuff. So if you look first of all on the club side, you've got Celtic and Rangers massively underperforming in Europe, and you've got relatively modest Norwegian clubs like Bodø/Glimt massively outperforming in Europe. And then you go on the international stage, and you've got the same thing. Right?

So this is something which is quite consistent, and you can't just write off saying "they've got Haaland." Well, Bodø/Glimt don't have Haaland. Bodø/Glimt are doing it — they don't have Ødegaard. They've got Norwegian players.

Games in a row against Atlético, Man City, and Inter Milan home and away. I mean —

Yeah. So I think it's just something which might be interesting for Scotland to have a look at. Now, why won't that happen, Charlie?

The reason that won't happen is because — if we think that the FA in this country are stuffy blazers who aren't particularly progressive (which is an unfair characterization actually, I think) — the Scottish FA and Scottish football as a whole is the most hidebound, conservative, backwards-looking sport in terms of it being the national sport, which it is. It's quite extraordinary.

When I speak to people in Scottish football, the amount of time that they reference the 1960s, the 1970s as being "that's what we need to get back to" — as opposed to looking forward, they always look backwards. And then you've got the politics that surrounds Celtic and Rangers being the dominant forces within Scottish football, and then basically being obviously constantly at loggerheads with each other, and therefore the Scottish FA not being in a strong position when you've got these two mega clubs who basically are easily the wealthiest parts of the whole ecosystem.

So this would need to be something that was being decided — as happened in Norway — at a political level. This would have to be being decided up here, and you look at the Scottish government, and bless them, you can't see them having this kind of strategic overview or interest in excellence in any way.

反驳"因为挪威有钱"：这其实是社会架构问题

If they were listening to this, I could — I know the response that would be given. "Oh, but Norway are one of the richest countries in the world."

Yeah.

So yes, but actually really what we're talking about here — a lot of this is societal, and it's an approach, it's not the spend. If we're looking at spend, the spend here for Norway was creating an infrastructure system that facilitated all year-round football, if they could, on pitches that didn't freeze over. That's an expensive product. The other side of it is coaching. So they have 17,000 coaches that come through a grassroots system. It's basically a coach every 320 players. That's —

A coach every 320 people. That's a lot of population.

That's totally societal. So basically coaching is a national sport itself.

They also had 2,000 people that completed their UEFA B licenses in the last decade. It's a really incredible model. Like, it is — and you're bang on. I think it's absolutely possible in the smaller countries that have much more control around this type of thing, and it'd be very hard to do this in England.

Yeah. Even at 60 million, let alone — as I said, it's a bit unfair, but — the US. The reason I brought the US into it is because a lot of the US sporting analysts have been looking at Norway, and they did during the Winter Olympics. That was their moment to just try and understand how Norway would top the medals table for a third year in a row, for the third Olympics in a row.

结语：差异化战略才是小国的唯一出路

But anyway, I think, look, to conclude: it's a really interesting sporting success story. As you say, it stretches across many different sports. It's being brought to the fore particularly in football, which is the world's global game — and therefore, you know, having advantages such as loads of snow (helping being good at ski jumping) is not so relevant when it comes to football. And then just casting a light on how countries can think differently to be able to enable themselves to compete in what otherwise simply becomes a numbers game.

So in other words, if Scotland do the same as England, then England has 10 times as many people and therefore will do 10 times as well, right? So therefore, if Scotland really wants to compete against England, it has to do it differently.

Yeah.

And this is what Norway have done.

Completely. Absolutely.

So — well, look, I hope you get an absolute pasting. By "you", I mean Norway.

I'm not —

I hope Norway gets an absolute pasting. But if we were to have to lose, then we couldn't lose to better Vikings.

Exactly. And just to be clear, I'm firmly English shirt on the weekend. But it's fun. It's fun to give them the credit they deserve as and when.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的核心发现：把精英俱乐部"隔离"在年轻人之外
真正颠覆认知的一点，视频里几乎一带而过——**挪威系统最革命性的动作，不是"加了什么"，而是"减了什么"：他们把职业俱乐部从 12 岁以下的青训里彻底剔除了。**
全世界（包括英格兰、西班牙、荷兰、阿根廷、苏格兰）的逻辑都是"越早识别、越早签下、越早集中训练=越强"。挪威用 Haaland、Ødegaard、Berg、Bodø/Glimt 集体证明了反命题。这本质上是在挑战一个自 20 世纪 70 年代以来被视作圣经的假设——"1 万小时定律"式的早期专业化。
这里藏着一个几乎没有人指出的**幸存者偏差**：那些用早期识别系统成功的球员（梅西 13 岁进拉玛西亚、鲁尼 9 岁进埃弗顿）之所以被反复讲述，是因为**他们本来就是身体早熟型的极端个例**。挪威系统真正拯救的是被现有体系直接筛掉的另外 80% ——那些和 Haaland 一样"12 岁前身材瘦小、看起来不像球员"的孩子。换句话说，**其他国家的青训体系不是在挑选球员，而是在挑选身体早熟的儿童**。
## 二、跨领域连接：这其实是"期权定价理论"在体育上的实

item.title: [All-In Podcast] More Trillion Dollar IPOs, Anthropic $3T, Zuck's Price War, China Ends Open Source?, Trump Accounts
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、隐藏的系统性洞察：这是一场"托管战争"，不是"模型战争"

大多数人把 AI 竞赛看作"谁的模型最聪明"，但这期节目里藏着一个更深的信号——真正的战场已经悄悄从 model 转移到 harness（宿主/骨架）。Databricks 的 Ali 说同一个模型换个 harness，成本能砍一半；Uber 用 "agentic pods" 把工程师嵌入业务部门；DoorDash 在做智能路由。这意味着什么？

模型正在被"电力化"——它会像电一样被交易、被路由、被套利，而真正的价值层将是"AI 的电网调度系统"。Chamath 一句话被大家忽略了：他把 token 成本降了 95% 后，agent 从"每天跑一次"变成"每小时跑一次"，然后又拆成三个 agent。这不是效率提升，这是一次相变（phase transition）——当边际成本趋近于零，人类会自动重构工作流的时间颗粒度。你以为你在省钱，实际上你在改变时间本身的定义。

二、跨领域连接：Trump Accounts 和大语言模型是同一个"复利战争"

看似完全不相关的两个话题——AI token 经济学 和 Trump Accounts——其实是同一个数学结构的两面：

  • Chamath 的 CTO 说 token 成本每 45 天翻一倍，产出提升 5%——这是发散的复利（负 ROI 复利）。
  • Brad 的 Trump Accounts 说：0-25 岁是"人生复利曲线上最陡的三分之一段"，但历史上被完全浪费了——这是被截断的复利。

节目里没人点破的悖论是：美国正在同时进行两个复利游戏——一个把资本注入婴儿的账户里让它滚 60 年，另一个把资本注入 AI 模型里让它每 45 天翻一倍。前者是线性缓慢的社会契约，后者是指数狂奔的技术契约。这两条曲线一定会在某个时点相撞——当一个孩子 18 岁时，他账户里的 20 万美元买到的 token 数量，可能相当于今天一个中型国家一年的算力预算。Brad 无意中造出了"AI 时代的普世算力券"。

再拉一个更冷的连接：这与澳大利亚的 Superannuation、新加坡的 CPF、甚至 1935 年 Social Security 的诞生逻辑一模一样——每一次技术革命都会催生一次"强制储蓄制度的重写"。农业 → 封地；工业 → 养老金；数字 → 401k；AI → Trump Accounts。这不是政策创新，这是文明进入新能量密度时的必然社会补偿机制。

三、意外观察：中国"闭源化"其实是对美国最大的战略礼物，而不是威胁

节目里 Sacks 说"中国可能限制开源反而是他们在犯错"，但他没讲透背后的博弈论——

开源模型的真正杀伤力从来不在于"免费"，而在于它是认知殖民工具：当一个国家的中小企业习惯了 Qwen 或 GLM 的 API 语法、prompt 风格、微调工具链，它就永远绑定在了中文技术栈的思维模式里。中国过去两年做的最聪明的事，不是追赶 GPT，而是用开源在全球开发者大脑里安装"中国式 AI 直觉"——就像 Android 免费送出去，然后规定必须内嵌 Google 服务一样。

如果中国现在真的闭源了，等于自己拆了这颗已经埋下的思想炸弹。

而更反直觉的是：Chamath 观察到"每个国家都想要 sovereign AI"——这意味着未来 5 年最贵的不是模型，是"训练一个国家自己的 AI"的能力。Nvidia 已经意识到这一点，它才是最大赢家——不是因为卖 GPU，而是因为它是唯一可以"授权你建立自己 AI 主权"的中立方。这就是为什么老黄能被邀请进 UN 委员会而 Sam Altman 不能。

隐藏的元洞察

这期节目本身就是一个奇观：四个亿万富翁在讨论如何让每一个婴儿从出生起就成为资本家，同时争论着一场每 45 天成本翻倍的技术军备竞赛。他们没意识到自己正在描述同一个东西——人类第一次进入"资本 vs 算力"的双复利文明。谁能同时驾驭这两条曲线，谁就定义下一个 250 年。

📝 转录文本

开场：夏日档 All-In 第 280 期

All right, everybody, welcome back. Number one podcast in the world. It's July. All in episode 280. Friedberg is on a little vacay. We'll leave it at that. And yeah, Bestie Brad is here. How you doing, Brad?

I'm doing great.

Vacay in maybe Idaho or somewhere? J. Cal? Who knows? Who knows? It could be anywhere. It could be anywhere. I mean, there's lots of things. He could be in plenty of places. And looks like you are, yeah, somewhere in the Northeast. I'll leave it at that. You having a little vacay for yourself this week?

I'm in my flag room, very patriotic room here. Very nice.

You know, where I work on the East Coast in the summertime. Yes. And spent some time in DC this week. And it's been a great week. Been a great week celebrating America 250. Great.

And you're going to be out of there for the by the by the second week of August. Yeah, so I have it August 10 through the 30th. I'm good. Exactly. Exactly. Jason, Jason BNB. Oh, absolutely. You don't know the half of it, man. I am on a summer bender. I'm like, where's your where's your vacation. Where are you? Where are you?

I am in Paris. I did about eight interviews for at the Rays conference will be coming out in the all in feed. And of course, tackling from the factory. Look at him. You're working in the factory. Chamath Palihapitiya. It's going to be a hot software summer for Chamath. How's your hot software summer going?

It's good. Selling enterprise software is hard, but it's good.

Chamath's like, "Man, I was such a dick to all my CEOs in the SaaS period, and now you know."

Chamath 在日内瓦 & 联合国 AI 委员会

I went to Geneva. Shout out to Mark Benioff. I went to Geneva. He works out of Europe, sees all his European customers, and he had a dinner in Geneva, which I joined. And then Me, Jensen, Brad Smith, Anthony Tan from Grab, and a bunch of other folks were put on this UN commission for AI that Mark is the co-chairman of.

When you see Mark Benioff in Action Man, this guy is a master. He is the impresario of impresarios. Yeah. You see how he's built such a ginormous business. It's impressive. It's impressive.

What is a commission by the United Nations for AI? What is their calling? Open source. Uh no but no I mean it's like the united nations actually do anything actually actually it's so funny yeah I mean anthropic was there too some one of the co-founders tom brown i think this is yeah yeah that's good was the anthropic guy running around saying it's the end of the world it's the end of the world no no no he was very tom's awesome tom's awesome in fairness to him he wears it on its sleeve, which is like, hey, we really believe we're doing the right thing. Right. And just trust us.

And I think the future is open source for all these countries. Well, we're going to get into that. That's on the docket for sure.

万亿美元 IPO 潮：SpaceX 之后是 Anthropic 和 OpenAI

But let's start with the IPO update. You know, there's a trillion dollar IPO rush to the exits. And, you know, this was a big topic of discussion, Brad, at the liquidity summit last month. And we'd never seen trillion dollar IPO. We had one this year already. SpaceX trading right about where it went public. So it was priced, I guess, to perfection. And theoretically, going to see two more. Brad has the inside information, so I'll try to get it out of open AI and anthropic are slated to go out.

Let's just go quickly over what happened with SpaceX. It ran up to $200 a share. It's been down a bit. It's at $150 a share, as I said, that's right at the IPO price. So it's trading at that two trillion market cap, currently seventh largest company in the world. And anthropic confidentially filed on June 1, I don't know why they call this confidential filing when it immediately comes out. But I guess the information is confidential. Polymarket says 65% chance anthropics IPO will happen this year, on light volume 360k. And two weeks ago, Gavin Baker, another bestie said he thinks they're going to end 2026 with over $100 billion in revenue and very profitable. He said, a couple of us guests on the program, that he thinks it would trade at 3 trillion right now if it went public.

Chamath, you made a great call on the pod. You said, hey, good idea for Elon to get out first. What are the chances here Chamath that these other two get out this year, or maybe in like, you know, say nine months in the first quarter of next year? We'll start there.

Well, I think that these are all great businesses. I think the question is, what is the market clearing price? And I think that's more of a function of how much appetite the markets have to absorb new issues and at what scale. That's number one. And I think that's mostly determined by price. So I think Anthropic and OpenAI are probably in two different places. The last time we heard from OpenAI, their cash burn was still quite high just because of the diffuse nature of their business and more reliance on consumer than enterprise. I think Brad mentioned it in one of the pods that Anthropic may actually be accidentally profitable. I think he said something like that. Yeah.

Chamath 的 token 支出实况：每 45 天翻倍，产出只多 5%

Let me tell you something really interesting. I sat down with my CTO today and I said, "How are we doing on token spend?" And he said the most incredible thing. He said, "Right now, our token costs are doubling every 45 days." Okay. And I was like, "Ugh." And he said, "Yeah." And I said, "Well, what is the downstream productivity?" And he said, "Maybe 5% max." Okay.

And I said, "Okay, so my costs are doubling every 45 days. My upside is essentially flat." And he said, basically, and I said, "Well, explain why that is." And he said, "Honestly, what we're finding out is that you need to use a lot more tokens to get to this next iteration of improvement, because we've effectively already asymptotic." And I said, "So what should we do?" And he said, "Honestly, we have to figure this out. And so we're going to take a step back and try to figure out what to do."

I don't know how many other companies will actually go through this reckoning now. But the point is, everybody in the next three or four years will for sure go through it. You have to have the data set and you have to know exactly what you're going to use them for. But if you don't know exactly what you're going to use them for, you want the most powerful general intelligence that you can get, right?

So what he said is that for mature use cases, yeah, you want to go open.
