item.title: [Bits + Bips] Why Oracles Are DeFi's Achilles Heel and How to Ensure They're Not Manipulated
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、Oracle 的"沉默恐惧"：不是报错，而是失语

Nicholas 顺口提到的一个观察值得反复咀嚼——"Oracle 最糟的不是报错价，而是彻底停止报价"。这颠覆了大多数人对系统风险的直觉。我们习惯把"错误"当成最大威胁，但在 DeFi 里，"沉默"比"谎言"更致命：谎言至少给了协议一个可以反应的信号（哪怕是错的），而沉默让整个偿付能力判断陷入形而上学的悬置——你不知道自己是不是破产了。

这其实和临床医学里的一个悖论惊人相似：ICU 中真正致命的往往不是"仪器报警"，而是"仪器脱线"。心电监护仪响了，护士会冲过来；监护仪没信号，可能只是"设备故障"，被暂时忽略。在高风险系统中，"无信号"是最昂贵的信号，因为它伪装成常态。

二、跨领域连接：Oracle 其实是一种"分权制衡"，而非"数据管道"

Nicholas 用了一个非常关键但容易被忽略的比喻——Oracle 应该是 "neutral reconciliation agent"（中立对账代理）。他把它类比为传统金融里 fund admin / custodian / transfer agent 的角色分离。

这里藏着一个跨领域洞见：Oracle 的本质根本不是"技术基础设施"，而是"宪政意义上的分权"。它更像美国联邦制里的"独立审计署"，或古罗马的"保民官"——一个存在的意义就是打断利益共谋的第三方。

这个视角一旦建立，你会突然意识到： - LayerZero、CCIP 这些跨链桥，本质上也是 Oracle（Nicholas 亲口证实了这一点，这个观察被大多数人忽略） - KelpDAO 事件的教训不是"桥不安全"，而是"当发行者、桥、Oracle 出现角色重叠时，制衡失效" - 而当前 DeFi 生态里最危险的趋势，正是"一体化协议"试图把发行、定价、清算、风控揉在一起——这在宪政意义上是"三权归一"，是走向暴政的第一步

三、令人意外的观察：DeFi 的"进入容易退出难"，暴露了一个金融物理学的不对称性

最出人意料的洞见藏在 Nicholas 那段关于 leverage loop 的描述里。他指出：在代币化资产的 leverage loop 里，"建仓"可以用 flash loan 完成，但"平仓"却卡在赎回窗口上。

这不只是 DeFi 的技术问题，而是一个深层的金融物理学不对称：

杠杆的"熵"是单向的——建立需要 O(1) 的时间，拆解需要 O(n) 的时间。

这个不对称在传统金融里被"做市商 + 央行最后贷款人"这个双层缓冲吸收了。但 DeFi 想跳过这层缓冲，直接把不可即时清算的资产（CLO、私募信贷、REIT）塞进即时清算的引擎里——这相当于给一辆 F1 赛车装上了拖拉机的刹车。

Grove Basin 那种"redemption facility"其实是在用 DeFi 原生方式重新发明"做市商"。这暗示了一个反直觉的预测：

代币化 RWA 的下一个大爆点，不会是"更多资产上链"，而是"上链后的退出流动性即服务"（Exit Liquidity as a Service）。

谁能解决"下车问题"，谁就控制了下一个百亿美元的赛道。这个位置目前几乎是空的——而这，可能才是 Chronicle 这类基础设施提供者真正的护城河：他们不只是知道资产"是什么"，他们还是唯一有能力实时判断资产"能不能被卖掉"的人。

一个额外的系统性直觉：Nicholas 说他们"10 年零 Oracle 被黑"，这不是运气，而是"delusional, psychotic focus on security"。这句话隐藏了一个 Lindy 效应的深层机制——在加密世界里，"没发生过的事"本身就是资产，而且这种资产无法通过营销制造，只能通过时间沉淀。这也是为什么 DeFi 未来会出现一种奇特的分化：不是按 TVL 排序，而是按"未爆炸秒数"排序。

📝 转录文本

开场与免责声明

Sure, I mean at that, it's — people didn't know what they were buying, right? They were buying an SPV on an SPV on an SPV, right? They had like second degree, third degree exposure.

Hi everyone, welcome to another episode of Bits and Bips: The Interview. I'm your host Steve Ehrlich, head of research at SharpLink, and we've got a terrific episode for you today. But as always, before we dive in, just a little bit of housekeeping.

One, nothing that you hear or see on this episode should be considered investment or financial advice. Please see unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips for full disclosures.

And second, before we dive in, let's hear from one of our sponsors. This episode is brought to you by CAPE, America's privacy-first mobile carrier. Same premium service you'd expect from any other carrier, but designed so your number, your location, and your data actually stay yours. Get 33% off six months at cape.co/unchained.

嘉宾介绍：Chronicle Labs 与 Nicholas Kunkel

Alright, welcome back. So I'm here today with Nicholas Kunkel. He is the founder of Chronicle Labs, an Oracle provider supporting the entire DeFi space with a heavy focus on tokenized assets. And we're going to talk about the landscape of DeFi, the growth of tokenized assets, and how they're being integrated into the plumbing of the entire financial ecosystem.

Really, one thing I really want to get into is how we can understand the differences between various Oracle providers, given how critical they are to this space, and explore how the industry has dealt with certain challenges over the years as well. So a lot to talk about, Nicholas. Welcome. Thanks for joining.

Nicholas: Thank you for having me. Yeah, I'm a huge fan of the show, and I watch almost every episode. So great to finally be on here.

Steve: Okay, that's nice to hear, nice to say. I keep trying to get my kids to watch, and they are not interested at all.

MakerDAO 起源：DeFi 从零开始的那些日子

So let's really kind of get started, because Chronicle and yourself has a pretty interesting background. I mean, you've been in crypto for at least a decade at this point. And it kind of started with MakerDAO, the DAI stablecoin, SAI, I think now it's called USDS, etc.

It's a really fascinating project because in many ways it created the entire DeFi space. And what I find most impressive is that it hasn't gone down. I mean, just like Ethereum itself, it's operated through bear and bull markets alike. It's one of the real success stories. And that's the origin story for yourself and what you're building at Chronicle Labs.

So maybe just for anyone in my audience who's not familiar with your background, could you please walk us through what those early days were like and how it spawned the idea for Chronicle?

Nicholas: Absolutely. Yep, so my background — I was part of the founding team of MakerDAO. And I think looking back, it seems kind of like manifest destiny. But at the time, there wasn't really any DeFi. The only stablecoin was Tether. And there wasn't an ecosystem of, say, DEXes and lending protocols.

I think it was quite forward-looking at the time to say, "Oh, there's going to be this DeFi, and at the center of DeFi there will be these stablecoins, and we don't want centralized stablecoins — we want a decentralized stablecoin." In hindsight, it all looks very obvious, but at the time it was very out there and experimental.

Even Ethereum as a chain had only been live for maybe less than a year. There weren't really any apps on it — at least nothing serious. So we really had to build everything from scratch.

Oracle 的诞生：从没有 Solidity 调试器的时代

And this is really kind of where my journey started with respect to Oracles. One day we were building a debugger because there was no Solidity debugger. One day we're building a DEX because it was actually the first DEX on Ethereum. And it was because Maker didn't have any funding, and we're like, "How do we get funding? Well, if we build this thing called a DEX, we can sell some tokens and we can get some funding and maybe we can pay salaries this month."

It was very much DIY, build everything on your own. And that included Oracles. This notion of an Oracle didn't really exist, but we knew we needed a way to externally price something in a kind of trustless manner. And that really kind of set everything off for me.

Steve: Did you call it an Oracle then, or what did you actually call it? And what you needed, correct me if I'm wrong, is you needed to be able to understand the price of ETH because that affects the LTV for the stablecoins that were borrowed against that collateral.

Nicholas: Exactly. The way Maker works in a simple way is, you could lock up your ETH as collateral and you would borrow DAI stablecoins against that. And so the protocol needed to know how much is an ETH worth — both for determining the initial amount that you could borrow, but also just maintaining the collateralization of the system over time. If the price of ETH dropped, it would know which loans it would need to liquidate.

早期价格提供者的匿名传统

Steve: Just going back through a little of the history of this myself as I was preparing for the show, I didn't even realize this, but it seems like a lot of the individuals that were those early-day price providers were actually pseudonymous, because there were concerns that if they were identifiable, there could be pressure for them to manipulate the price and thus manipulate the value of DAI.

It seems like the antithesis of where Oracles are today, where it's named participants who provide that information — I'm generalizing across the whole span of Oracles — but in exchange for compensation. So it's really fascinating that you saw a security threat with that. Perhaps appropriately cautious to maintain the fidelity of the price feeds.

Nicholas: Sure. I mean, when you look at a DeFi protocol, the Achilles heel of any DeFi protocol is its Oracle. It lives and dies by its Oracle.

If an Oracle prints that ETH is a trillion dollars, anyone can just go and drain all of the liquidity out of any lending market, out of any credit protocol. Similarly, if the Oracle reports ETH is zero, you can liquidate all of the ETH positions in a lending protocol. You could potentially trigger — if you're looking at the likes of Aave and Sky — you could potentially trigger a liquidation cascade on ETH that has far more second-order effects than just the on-chain liquidations.

Oracle 的本质：数据完整性与可用性

Steve: Yeah, absolutely. And I guess just a level set for my audience. Most people probably know what an Oracle is, at least at a high level. But could you just briefly talk about how deeply Oracles have penetrated crypto? Across different types of either assets or different DeFi protocols. Maybe put a few dollar amounts behind it in terms of either TVL or the amount of daily DeFi activity.

Nicholas: I think it would be easier to say what are Oracles not touching in crypto than to go the other way around.

In simple terms, an Oracle is like a data reference. So you have some data somewhere and that somewhere can be off-chain, it could be another blockchain, and it needs to be transported to wherever that data is needed.

Let's use ETH as an example. You're fetching prices from all kinds of centralized exchanges — from Coinbase, Binance, OKX, Bybit. You're combining that with on-chain sources like Uniswap. You may even be combining that with the ETH futures on CME. You are aggregating all of this information, and then you're shipping it off to the blockchain where it's needed, so an application can consume it.

The primary two things here that an Oracle needs to concern itself with are:

  1. What are the data integrity guarantees? How do you ensure that [the data is accurate]?
  2. What are the data availability guarantees? How do you ensure that this flow of data continues and doesn't just stop?

Because the second worst thing to happen to an Oracle is not that it prints a wrong price, but that it just stops printing any updated price. And now a DeFi protocol has no idea whether it is solvent or not, or whether it is issuing safe credit safely, or whether it is issuing credit at a loss.

历史事件：Mango、USDe 与价格协调

Steve: Yeah, I mean, all those points make a lot of sense. And I think anyone that's followed the DeFi space over time has seen some of those concerns or risks really come to reality. We can talk about a few of the various issues with price — from like the Mango Markets exploit, which was a "quote-unquote very probable trading strategy" to exploit — even what happened on 10/10 when I think it was, what was it? Like USDe price was different on Binance compared to other exchanges. I think those two events are very different though.

Funny ways to have accurate pricing, also harmonize it across various platforms and stuff, so there's not those sort of hiccups that can lead to liquidations, etc. It just underscores the importance of the work that you guys do.

Chronicle Labs 与 RWA 焦点

So let's talk a little more about Chronicle Labs in particular, because one of the things I found really interesting about your company is how you really focus on RWAs now, and in particular, making sure that when someone buys a tokenized asset, they can be sure that the underlying collateral is actually there — maybe at the DTCC — or just it's not a token that's minted out of thin air. So can you talk a little bit about what you guys do and the problem that you're trying to solve?

Nicholas: Sure. So I don't think it's actually — iterating and expanding into tokenized assets is really all that surprising or different from what we previously did. The role of an Oracle is to preserve the integrity of DeFi.

And when DeFi was just using kind of crypto-native assets — like a Bitcoin or an ETH — that was actually relatively simple, because the protocol was holding on to the actual asset. And so it just needed to know how to price that asset.

代币化资产带来的机会与风险

With the expansion of DeFi into tokenized assets, there is an incredible opportunity here to: 1. Not just expand DeFi out of its very self-contained scope, but 2. Open it up to the entire ecosystem — a catalog of financial assets that are out there.

This is incredibly attractive for everyone involved. From an asset manager's perspective: "I want to give my clients more utility with their assets. So if my client can lend this asset to earn a yield, now I can provide more utility to my clients." I think if you're looking at a BlackRock or Fidelity, that's very, very attractive to them.

And from a DeFi perspective, we're about to take this from a $200 billion market to potentially a $100 trillion market.

However, there is a risk. And it's that tokenized assets are not crypto-native. Regardless of whether they are what you would call a wrapper, or whether they are issued actually on-chain but the underlying assets are sitting somewhere off-chain — that presents a whole new problem for DeFi to deal with.

And so it's actually imperative that Oracles are keeping up, and are ensuring that DeFi, through its integration of these tokenized assets — which I absolutely believe is necessary and critical — that DeFi protocols can maintain their integrity and maintain this anti-fragile property.

Like you mentioned earlier, it's amazing that Maker has survived — not just survived, but thrived — for 10 years. And that doesn't just happen through serendipity. That comes through very rigorous analysis of every part of the system and every edge case that you can find yourself with, and really mapping out how the protocol should respond in every single scenario.

As soon as you open Pandora's box with tokenized assets, that problem becomes a lot more complex. And so as an Oracle, we're here to simplify that. We're here to say, "Okay, here is this tokenized asset, and we can provide all of the metadata related to all of the context that comes with that tokenized asset," so a lending protocol — like a Morpho, like an Aave, like a Sky — can handle it in a responsible, risk-managed manner.

案例：BlackRock 与 BUIDL

Steve: So let's ground this in just one or two tangible examples. One of your clients is BlackRock, and I believe it's supporting their tokenized treasury fund BUIDL, which is the largest in the world. Talk to me about specifically what you are doing for them. What are the services? Just kind of please walk us through that relationship.

Nicholas: Absolutely. Yeah, so very important relationship for us. I mean, BlackRock is the largest asset manager in the world. And they recognize the need for Oracle infrastructure when it comes to tokenized assets.

So when you have something like BUIDL, from a DeFi protocol's perspective, it doesn't know what a BUIDL token is. Even if you feed it a price, it can't account for all of the other independent variables.

从"第一性原理"重建基金

And so what we're essentially doing is saying, "Okay, we can connect to the back end where all of the assets are actually being held." And we can say, "Okay, we know there is this treasury bill that was bought for this amount, it's going to mature on this date, and it's going to be worth $100 at maturity. And there is this overnight repo to JP Morgan, and there's this other overnight repo to Deutsche Bank or to Royal Bank of Canada."

So we can really rebuild the fund from first principles, from the ground up. Saying, "Okay, here are the actual assets underlying the fund," and now we can recompile in a way the entire fund.

This way we can independently come up with our own NAV and share price, identify any kind of maturity blips. Like — you want to have relatively smooth maturations of a portfolio of bonds and a portfolio of treasury bills. So you don't want to see that 30% of the portfolio is going to mature in June or next August, because it means if the fund needs to do a bunch of redemptions, they could potentially need to sell some of those at a loss.

CLO：更受关注但流动性更差

I think that problem is less pronounced with money market funds like BUIDL, like other money market funds that we support — like Centrifuge's Janus Henderson JTRSY, like Superstate's USTB.

I think it's more pronounced in products that are really popular right now: CLOs. Because as monetary policy rates are dropping, CLOs have higher yield. So those are a little bit more interesting, but they're also a little bit less liquid. So that's kind of where the risk part of the equation comes in.

Chronicle is really able to give you that magnifying glass — full view into the guts of the fund. So a DeFi protocol can basically plug that into their risk management system to handle these assets appropriately.

Oracle 作为"中立对账代理"

Steve: Okay. One more quick question and then we have to take another break. But I just want to drill down a little bit more specifically on what you're providing to BlackRock, because I just want to make sure my listeners understand.

Are you actually pricing the token, or pricing the treasuries that are in the other elements of the money market fund that underpin the tokens? Or is your job really to just verify that what CUSIPs are in there, what the maturity dates are, if there's any liens against them — that kind of stuff? What precisely is your role, just using that one engagement as an example?

And then — sorry, not to make the question more complicated — but it is interesting because things like BUIDL, or I guess maybe Benji as well from Franklin Templeton, etc., they're starting to be used as collateral. And I would imagine that you probably get some questions from primes and other people that are accepting them to make sure that those assets are fully backed. So I'd really just love to get a sense of that.

Nicholas: Yeah, absolutely. So as I kind of alluded to previously, we are starting from first principles. So we don't take anything at face value. We know which assets are in custody and we rebuild the fund from there.

What I mean by rebuilding the fund is, we say: "Okay, we are pricing these T-bills using this methodology. And given all of the treasury bills in the fund, given all of the overnight repos and their expected yield, we are coming up with this NAV. How does that compare to what the fund admin is reporting? How does the yield that's being reported compare and contrast to the yield that we are calculating?"

TradFi 分权制衡的类比

I think the way to think of an Oracle's role in tokenized assets is more of a neutral reconciliation agent. Because in traditional finance, you have all kinds of conflicts of interest. And so there is this separation of roles.

When you have a fund, certain parties — someone will be a fund admin, someone will be a custodian, someone will be a transfer agent — but you usually don't see a lot of overlap in these roles.

Take BNY as an example. BNY is one of the largest custodian banks in the world, is the custodian for BUIDL. But then on a different product that we support — for Securitize, a Securitize stack — BNY is the sub-advisor on that. So they're acting in this fund admin capacity.

DeFi 中的角色分离

And so it's kind of the same in DeFi. If you have a lending protocol and there's a risk curator and there's an Oracle — you don't want the issuer of a tokenized asset to also be the one that is reporting the pricing data into a market. The same way you don't want a risk curator of the market to also act as the Oracle. There needs to be this separation of duties.

The Oracle really nestles in nicely into being able to be this neutral arbiter of: "These are the facts relative to this tokenized asset."

I think it's important that you're not just doing "Simon Says" — like, not all Oracles go about this the right way. It's important that you're not just doing "Simon Says," like the issuer told me this or the asset manager told me this. That you're really going as far back into the chain, down this let's-call-it "factory line" that comprises the tokenized asset, and are really just checking every step in the way. It's okay to report what a fund admin reports, as long as you have reconciled and determined that those numbers are accurate.

类比：GPU 计算指数的利益冲突

Steve: Yeah, it's funny. This reminds me of my interview last week with someone who was a provider of basically an index for GPU computing. I think the first one was really kind of out there. And she told me that she's been getting flooded with inquiries from perp DEX providers that want to make markets. And they asked her if she would be willing to make a market on her index. And she flat out said no, because of the perceived conflict of interest in that.

I mean, not that she would, I guess, try to manipulate the price for the index to go up, but certainly the ability could be there for someone who was perhaps a little less honest. So I understand the importance of what you're saying there.

Okay, let's take a very quick break. But when we get back, we're going to talk more about where the RWA market is going, how Oracles such as this provider at Chronicle are going to fit into it, and a lot more. So stay tuned.

中场广告

If you hold crypto on your phone, your biggest vulnerability isn't your wallet. It's your carrier. AT&T, Verizon, and T-Mobile have been breached again and again, and SIM swaps are still one of the easiest ways for attackers to drain accounts.

That's where CAPE comes in — America's privacy-first mobile carrier. Same premium service, but CAPE rotates the identifier on your SIM every 24 hours, deletes your call and text metadata after a day, and protects against SIM swaps with a 24-word recovery phrase that only you control. You also get two middle-to-end encrypted secondary numbers for banking and signups, so you stop handing your real number to every app that asks.

Go to cape.co/unchained and use code "unchained" for 33% off your first six months.

RWA 的爆发：CLO 与代币化的下一波

Alright, welcome back. So I want to talk a little more about where RWAs are going and some of the unique challenges that these new directions could create for companies such as yours.

I've been in crypto for almost 13 years at this point — probably as long as you have, if not maybe a little shorter — but I've been in since 2013. And I've heard on and on again about tokenization, RWAs, etc., and some of the promises that are offered. From most of my experience, they've been unfulfilled. Although it does seem like today things are different.

I think there's probably about $30 billion in tokenized assets outside of stablecoins. The number may not be entirely accurate, but it's a ballpark. And it seems like two big categories are: money market funds/treasuries that we spoke about, but then also alternative assets — the ones that are not quite as liquid, private credit, etc. — that tokenization could offer a way to reduce some of these illiquidity discounts that they trade with.

I'd love to get your thoughts on that part of the market in particular. Are you doing anything to support them right now, and where do you think that could grow to?

Nicholas: Absolutely. I think we kind of get a frontline seat to seeing where the market is heading, because we see what clients are working on and how we need to adapt our technology to be able to support those use cases.

As you pointed out, I think the shift over the last 12 months as monetary policy rates have kind of steadied is: How can we increase yield? It's no surprise that in DeFi, people love their yield. They love their kind of yield-looping strategies.

CLO 与 Galaxy 的入场

So the race has been to create products that are attractive to that part of the market. And that's really where we've seen CLOs take off.

We saw Centrifuge with Janus Henderson launch JAAA. We saw — what else did we see? — I mean, Hamilton Lane and Acred.

Steve: Exactly.

Nicholas: There was the Apollo Accredix — all of these, which we actually support at Chronicle. And I think even just yesterday, or I think it was like two days ago now, we announced that we were supporting Galaxy's first tokenized CLO.

Which I think is quite interesting because — Galaxy, if they're getting into tokenization, they're going to be a really huge, huge player. They have the network and they sit kind of perfectly nestled in between DeFi and on the TradFi side. So I think they're going to do really well.

The product that we support there — Galaxy is either directly or indirectly letting people borrow against their crypto: against Bitcoin, against ETH, SOL, XRP, whatever it is. And so all that we are doing is: we are getting the loan book from Galaxy. And then we're going to their custodian, we're going to Anchorage, and we're checking: "Okay, is there actually all this BTC, all this SOL, all this XRP, all this ETH in custody? Can we trace every satoshi that is claimed to be as collateral on these books?" We identify the liabilities.

And now we can say: "Okay, this CLO is, I don't know, 200% collateralized. And it's yielding 7%." So it's giving pretty good yield.

Chronicle 的双重价值：透明度 + DeFi 集成

Now, one — anyone who wants to potentially buy this Galaxy CLO can look into the guts and know that someone independent like Chronicle has validated all of this. But two, now someone like Morpho — you can have a vault and you can create a looping strategy on this. So you can juice your 7% yield into 14% or 21%, what have you.

That's really what Chronicle enables: 1. Transparency that gives the confidence in these assets 2. The ability for DeFi to integrate them

In terms of what's coming up: we're seeing, for example, REITs — real estate investment trusts. There's a couple of those coming out, so we're excited about those. I think you may have seen Centrifuge just announced with New York Life that they're going to be releasing a new fund as well.

代币化股票的机会

I think equities right now are really hot. And I think it'll take a while to get there. Like you were alluding to before, there's like all these promises and some of them have panned out and some of them haven't. I don't think it's that it doesn't pan out — I think it's more just it needs more time than anyone thinks it does. But the promise is huge.

If you have some stock, if you have Tesla stock and you want to go borrow against your Tesla stock, your broker is going to charge you very high single digits, low double digits. If you're going to lend these things with an LTV of like 30% or something, DeFi will offer you mid to high single digits on this stuff — like all day.

If you look at, what do you get for lending USDC? You're getting 2.7%. You're literally getting less than T-bills — 2.7%.

So there's really an opportunity for DeFi to be the mechanism. One of the flywheels for tokenized equities taking off is just going to be this ability to get leverage against your equities at much cheaper rates than you can in TradFi with your broker.

SpaceX IPO 争议与代币化透明度

Steve: Yeah, one thing I was actually kind of really dying to ask you: I want to get your thoughts on some of the, I guess, controversy that surrounded, for instance, the SpaceX IPO and some of the markets that were spun up where people were able to kind of bet on the price of some of these pre-IPO shares or private market shares.

And I know with the SpaceX, I think there were a couple of tokenized providers that had to give back the money because they weren't able to procure the shares out there. And that seems to be like the types of issues that a company like yours would, I guess, make sure do not happen.

Nicholas: Yeah. I mean, at that point, people didn't know what they were buying. They were buying an SPV on an SPV on an SPV. They had second-degree, third-degree exposure.

And yeah, when it came time to "Hey, I want to get my shares," a lot of these things blew up — whether from fraud or just improper legal structuring, or just they never got the shares.

But yeah, this is really what Chronicle is here to do — is just give you absolute end-to-end transparency into everything. And that's what's going to keep DeFi anti-fragile. That's how we're going to scale DeFi with tokenized assets and keep DeFi from having the same systemic risks that we see in the traditional financial industry.

KelpDAO 事件与跨链桥即 Oracle

Steve: Yeah, and I also wanted to get your thoughts on — you talked a little bit about looping and just additional ways to get yield. Obviously, CLOs, private credit, etc., there is the promise, I guess, to make more money, even though the industry is a little under assault in recent months.

But yeah, like I said, I'm really interested in your thoughts on what happened with the KelpDAO account, and in particular, how it affected Aave. Because I don't have to rehash the entire story of what happened — I would imagine just about everybody listening and watching knows or could quickly look it up.

But because of the unbacked wrapped ETH — or whichever one it was — the markets basically froze up. I mean, I know Aave had one big liquidity pool, Morpho's more segmented, etc. So I get that there's some design differences, but it led to some very hard decisions. I mean, I'm not even getting into what happened with Arbitrum and what they did to freeze certain funds. But it exposed a lot of cracks in DeFi infrastructure.

And one saving grace, I think, is that it was primarily contained within crypto. As bad as it was, it was within crypto. But if people are going to start using tools such as yours to deploy looping strategies across DeFi with actual real-world assets — not just derivatives of ETH — that could become very, very problematic. Especially with assets where, for instance, the spark price may not be the liquidation price.

I actually wrote a story when I was at Unchained on Acred in particular. I noticed I think a mistake in some of the marketing material where it's an interval fund — which means you're not supposed to be able to get out your money. You can only get out your money on a quarterly basis, and they can cap the redemptions. And I think there were some discrepancies inadvertently on the marketing surrounding that.

But it just shows the danger of these types of products when people are just thinking about dollar signs. And I'd love to just get your thoughts on: what are some of the lessons learned for your business from that one exploit in particular?

桥就是 Oracle

Nicholas: I mean, I think it's very simple. I think what people fail to recognize is that all of these interop protocols — like a LayerZero, like a Chainlink CCIP — that's an Oracle.

Anytime you use cross-chain communication, anytime you're doing bridging — bridging a token from one chain to another chain — these are all Oracles, just white-labeled with a different name like "cross-chain" or "bridge."

So it really comes down to: the LayerZero Oracle was compromised, and the DeFi protocol was insufficiently risk-managed to deal with that.

It doesn't really call into question, "Oh, Oracles are bad." No, you need Oracles. But you need to be very careful about which Oracle you choose. And when you're deciding, "Oh, well, this one costs a little bit more, I'm going to go with the cheaper one" — you need to understand the choice that you're making, that you are directly trading security. Security of your users, of their funds, of the existential risk of your protocol or your company — to save a few bucks.

Lindy 效应与十年零事故

I think the bad players get weeded out over time. And you see players like Chainlink, like Chronicle, that have the Lindy. We've gone — I think not quite 10 years. I think we launched the first Oracles on Ethereum in May of 2017. So come May next year, we'll be celebrating 10 years on-chain without a single Oracle hack.

And that's what you don't see. That's in the marketing material. It's what you don't see. Because you don't luck your way into 10 years without an incident. That comes from a culture of just extreme, delusional, psychotic focus on security and durability.

跨链风险管理与断路器

Steve: How do you — I'm curious, and forgive me, I may not just be familiar enough with your business model — but how do you account for something like that? Like a token that, for instance, a BUIDL token — I know I think it's based on Ethereum, but I don't know if it's available on other chains or not. But how do you make sure that if certain tokens need to be able to move cross-chain, or even across L2s, for instance — what would you do if somehow somebody was able to trick LayerZero or one of those connectors to mint unbacked tokens? How would you handle something like that for one of your clients?

Nicholas: So on the lending protocol side — I'll just use Aave as an example here, because we're talking about the KelpDAO incident, but this goes for any lending protocol — they need to have circuit breaker safeguards in place. And whether they are getting that data on their own or whether they are getting it through an Oracle, they need to have those risk systems in place.

KelpDAO 事件的技术复盘

So in the KelpDAO incident, an attacker was able to convince the bridge — with this false message — to unlock the locked KelpDAO on Ethereum, that shouldn't have been unlocked. Because the corresponding Kelp on the source chain was not actually burned to do this.

From a risk management perspective, you should be checking the supply on every single chain that a token can be on, and summating those and ensuring that there is no mismatch between the amount that is floating around versus what the token — even just asking the token contract, "How many RSEs exist?" That's not enough.

And if you notice any wonkiness, what do you do? You immediately freeze the market. You do not allow anyone to have the protocol take on any more risk by taking on more debt against these assets. Nor do you allow anyone to repay and take stuff out until this whole mess is sorted. You just pause.

代币化资产将放大复杂度

And this has nothing to do with tokenized assets already. This is just a slightly more complex, let's call it, DeFi asset. And we're already seeing lenders struggle to keep up with the complexity of that, when the risk management solutions are quite simple.

So when it comes to tokenized assets, the complexity of this just becomes exponential. So yes, it's on the Oracles to develop the ability to get all of the required data, all of this metadata, all of this context, compile it so it can plug into the risk management systems of these lending protocols.

But the onus is on the lending protocols to take that risk management seriously, and to create — not like a phone call, or there's an alert that goes off or something. That's already too late. It needs to be real-time, automated, and triggered by data that is provably correct.

未来展望：代币化资产将成为主战场

Steve: What do you see as the biggest areas of growth for your business in the next, say, five to ten years?

Nicholas: I mean, it's hands down tokenized assets. Like — let me paint you a picture.

So today, Chronicle secures just under $15 billion across DeFi and tokenized assets. So we're not quite at Chainlink level, but we're five times larger than say a Pyth or a Redstone. It's not even close.

And within that business, about $10 billion of it is pure DeFi and $5 billion of that is tokenized assets. Those $5 billion in tokenized assets, we have done that over the last 18 months. Like, we didn't release our tokenized asset Oracle till January of '25.

So that is easily, by far, the fastest growing part of our business. And I expect that by the end of the year, frankly, the tokenized asset business will be bigger than our pure DeFi business.

代币化股票的 S 曲线

On that front, to me, it has to be equities. There are problems that you have with funds that you don't have with equities, and vice versa. And I think there's like a kind of step function here. You're not going to see very gradual, incremental growth. I think you're just going to see huge S-curves, localized S-curves, play out.

赎回难题：进得容易出得难

Let me give you an example. On the money market fund side, these have existed for a while now. People — we know people in DeFi love to do their little leverage loops and stuff.

One of the problems, not just with money market funds but with these CLOs and other types of funds, is the redemption ability. You touched on this earlier with the Acred product — like, okay, you can only redeem on a quarterly basis.

So there's kind of a problem here where you can get into the position: if you want to build a leveraged position, you can enter it very easily. Lock up your Acred, lock up your JAAA, lock up your BUIDL, your USTBs. You borrow against them. Great. And then you just go and you buy more of that initial asset. So you can enter a position extremely efficiently.

The problem is when you want to unwind this whole thing. In DeFi, we have a flash loan. You take out a flash loan, you repay your whole debt, you can now withdraw your entire collateral, and now you can sell your collateral on a DEX, and you use the proceeds from selling your collateral to repay the flash loan. That's an amazing superpower that DeFi has.

However, now when it comes to — if the underlying asset is not something that you can just sell on a DEX — there's not millions of dollars of liquidity for, I don't know, some private credit product or for some CLO on a DEX. Well, what do you do?

Someone needs to essentially offer a redemption facility, for a fee, and essentially take on directionally that underwriting risk, or find ways to hedge that on the other side. That's still been like a really big piece that's missing.

Grove Basin：赎回原语的解锁

But just recently, for example, we saw Grove — shout out, a great group of guys. They just did their TGE a couple of days ago. So cool. They launched a new product called Grove Basin.

And essentially, for right now, they're just supporting money market funds like BUIDL, like Centrifuge Janus Henderson JTRSY. But they'll now basically — they have a billion-dollar credit line from Sky to just, in-kind, allow you to redeem your JTRSY or your BUIDL for USDC.

So now you can exit these positions extremely efficiently, and not having to go to the issuer and do stuff, you know, like paperwork stuff or do stuff off-chain. It's all just in a transaction, right? Completely composable, one transaction. DeFi at work.

So that's — it takes new utilities like that that just unlock the ecosystem. It's like a bathtub filling with water. There's only a certain — if the bathtub is this size, there's only a certain amount of water that can fit into it. As soon as we open up these new primitives, now all of a sudden the bathtub is much bigger, and it'll fill up with water like immediately. I mean, greed is ever-present. If there's money to be made, money will find a way.

原生链上发行 vs. 代币化封装

Steve: Okay, we're just about at time, but I have one more quick question I want to ask you. Tokenized assets are clearly a growth area, but we are starting to see some issuances directly on-chain without an underlying, like, more traditional asset backing it. I'm just curious your thoughts on that, and how you might account for it moving forward.

Nicholas: So — actually, it's kind of funny because I get this question a lot. Like, it's supposed to be some kind of gotcha.

Steve: I mean, it's not meant to be a gotcha. It's just it's more just sometimes, like, for instance —

Nicholas: Yeah, no, no, I understand.

Steve: Yeah, so why have something that's backed by something where something could just be issued directly?

链上发行不等于底层上链

Nicholas: So one thing I want to say, for example, is: you can have on-chain issuance where the assets are still custodied off-chain. An example of this is the new BNY / Baillie Gifford product that was just announced recently, where it's issued natively on-chain. But the underlying loans in the fund are still off-chain. And so those will still require what I kind of consider the very standardized Chronicle Proof of Asset Oracle.

Now, you mentioned specifically funds that are issued on-chain where the components of the fund are also on-chain. For instance, JP Morgan just announced they're going to issue a money market fund on-chain. Yeah, but where are the underlying T-bills for that money market fund? Are they off-chain or are they on-chain? I would tell you they're going to be off-chain.

Can't wait for the day when Treasury starts issuing bonds on-chain. That would be awesome.

递归问题：即使底层上链，仍需 Proof of Asset

But let's take this example further. Let's assume that we start tokenizing individual T-bills. That's really cool. Now we can actually create a natively issued, on-chain money market fund where even the underlying T-bills are on-chain.

But you still now have the problem of: "Okay, well, I know that the money market fund is composed of these on-chain T-bills. But how do I know that these on-chain T-bills are actually legit?"

And so, again, it just becomes the recursive problem of: I need to go back and now I need to have Proof of Asset for the underlying T-bills all over again.

Steve: Let me find that source of truth, I guess, one way or another.

Nicholas: Exactly.

Steve: Okay, alright, well we need to end it there. So yeah, Nicholas, thanks so much for joining. We'll have to have you back again.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、Oracle 的"沉默恐惧"：不是报错，而是失语
Nicholas 顺口提到的一个观察值得反复咀嚼——**"Oracle 最糟的不是报错价，而是彻底停止报价"**。这颠覆了大多数人对系统风险的直觉。我们习惯把"错误"当成最大威胁，但在 DeFi 里，**"沉默"比"谎言"更致命**：谎言至少给了协议一个可以反应的信号（哪怕是错的），而沉默让整个偿付能力判断陷入形而上学的悬置——你不知道自己是不是破产了。
这其实和临床医学里的一个悖论惊人相似：ICU 中真正致命的往往不是"仪器报警"，而是"仪器脱线"。心电监护仪响了，护士会冲过来；监护仪没信号，可能只是"设备故障"，被暂时忽略。**在高风险系统中，"无信号"是最昂贵的信号，因为它伪装成常态。**
## 二、跨领域连接：Oracle 其实是一种"分权制衡"，而非"数据管道"
Nicholas 用了一个非常关键但容易被忽略的比喻——Oracle 应该是 **"neutral reconciliation agent"（中立对账代理）**。他把它类比为传统金融里 fund admin / custodian / transf

item.title: [Business of Sport] Norway’s Talent Machine Unpacked, Silverstone’s Record Weekend, & NBA Europe’s Billion Dollar Bids
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

三个隐藏的系统性真相

1. 挪威模式的反直觉："延迟专业化"是一种高级形式的风险管理

表面上看，挪威把足球职业化推迟到 15-16 岁是"人文关怀"，但这其实是一种期权定价思维在体育人才培养中的应用。

传统精英俱乐部体系（英格兰、苏格兰、南美）本质上是在 9 岁做投资决策——他们必须在信息极度不完整时"押注"哪个孩子会成才。这就像风险投资在 Pre-Seed 阶段就要 All-in，注定错误率极高。而挪威系统实际上是把决策点推迟到"信号-噪声比"最高的时刻（青春期发育之后），这时候每一份投资的边际回报率呈指数级上升。

哈兰德是最好的证据——他早期身材瘦小，在任何 9 岁筛选制的国家都会被淘汰。挪威系统"保留了他的期权"，直到他自己行使了它。这跟金融里的"美式期权 vs 欧式期权"是同一逻辑：保留决策权到最后一刻，永远比提前锁定更有价值。

2. 一个跨领域的意外连接：特朗普事件与 Silverstone 的 BRDC 揭示了同一个"治理悖论"

这两个故事看似毫不相关，但在系统层面上讲的是同一件事——"程序正当性"作为一种稀缺资产的定价。

  • FIFA 允许特朗普通话后启动"独立小组"程序，即便小组做出的是"正确"决定，程序正当性也已经归零。因为感知即现实（perception is reality），这是体育治理最脆弱的资产。
  • 反过来看 BRDC——一个由 850 位"血统纯正"的赛车贵族组成的、看似封建的私人俱乐部，2015 年濒临破产。它没有 DEI，没有政府背书，但它有跨越百年的程序正当性储备。正是这份"我们不是为了赚钱而办赛"的可信度，让它在 Liberty Media 接手后能够重新议价，并从 5 万英镑的破产边缘走到 500 亿英镑估值。

洞见：现代体育治理中，"程序信誉"是一种可以被瞬间摧毁却需要几十年重建的资产类别。Infantino 一通电话烧掉的信誉，可能比整届世界杯的商业收入还要贵。

3. 最令人意外的观察：NBA 欧洲和挪威人才工厂在做完全相反的赌注——而两者可能同时错

NBA 欧洲的核心逻辑是：IP 空投——用 10 亿美元的特许经营费在伦敦、巴黎凭空造出一个篮球品牌，假设"美国式的球迷经济学"会跟着 IP 一起漂移过来。

挪威模式的核心逻辑恰好相反：IP 培育——从 5 岁开始用 20 年时间把整个国家的"运动身体"变成人才的原生土壤。

有意思的是，这两个模型都在挑战同一个残酷现实：在体育产业里，"人才"和"关注度"其实是同一种商品的两个面。你不能只买其中一面。

  • NBA 想只买"关注度"（用特许权费吸引资本、用美式娱乐化包装），却发现欧洲的顶级球员和俱乐部（Barcelona、Real Madrid、Fenerbahçe）根本不搭理。
  • 反过来，Euro League 只有"人才"（这些俱乐部有百年历史），却做不出商业化包装，永远无法从纯赛事升级为文化产品。

深层洞察：这与网飞早期做原创内容 vs 迪士尼买 IP 的对抗是同构的。真正赢家往往是既有原生人才又有跨界娱乐化能力的混合模型——这也许解释了为什么足球（既有本地俱乐部的百年根系，又有 FIFA 的全球化叙事）能吃下体育产业的最大份额，而其他体育联盟无论怎么砸钱都只能做局部市场之王。

啊哈时刻：网球其实也困在同一个陷阱里——它有全球顶级人才（Sinner、Alcaraz），但没有本地俱乐部根系（大满贯是候鸟型 IP，不是常驻文化）。所以你会看到本期播客里出现的"看似矛盾"的现象：网球是全球观看量前三的运动，却挤不进商业价值前十。根系决定天花板。

📝 转录文本

开场：热点抢先看

He started off by going, you know, "I know sports." About a minute and a half later said, "I didn't know what a red card is." Whatever happens after that, independent panels or not, whether this is the right call or not, it doesn't really matter. When Donald Trump admits he's made that call, the integrity of any decision made disappears.

This is the first time that one of the major American sports has made a serious effort to establish a European league. And it makes sense, right? Because basketball's got much stronger roots in Europe than American football and baseball have.

Do you know how many people turned up at Silverstone over the weekend? 564,000 people. By 2015, they're on the verge of bankruptcy. I mean, it's the most astonishing sports business turnaround.

At 95% rate of Norwegians playing a team sport by the time they're 25. That gives Norway a really broad pool of athletes. There's a very high chance that in a different ecosystem, Erling Haaland would have fallen through the cracks where physically a coach will assess them and say, "You're not quite up to it."

Substack 上线预告

But first, big announcement alert. We are launching on Substack. The first few months have been awesome. Now, we want to do more with you, get to know you better, and bring you another layer of content and conversations. We're talking bonus segments, Q&As, written pieces, events, votes, all launching officially next Friday. We'll be posting exclusives from tomorrow to give you a sneak peek. So, click on the link in the description and come join us for real.

温布尔登近况与英式着装闲聊

Good morning, Charlie. Very early morning, Charlie, this morning. So, I'm sorry to drag you out of bed into the London studio.

I wouldn't say very early morning, Charlie. Both of us have young daughters. Um, 9:00 is absolutely absolutely fine. And also on a day which is going to be as hot as today means that we can get in here before the tube becomes too sweaty. You had a few jibes the other day about me not wearing linen, which I didn't really sort of get, but today I'm wearing more linen than a Brideshead Revisited rehash.

I am muk linen today. So I am — off. I'm very lucky to be off to Wimbledon after this.

Yeah.

Breakdown morning linen for Wimbledon at lunch. However, I obviously have to be wearing a shirt, right, for the lovely part of Wimbledon. I'm going to have literally that shirt. So we could have been sitting here identical matchy matchy.

Yeah, I've been invited by a business of sport alumni and an absolute legend who I used to watch at Stamford Bridge when I was about six or seven years old. It's not John Terry. It's not John Terry.

It's Mr. Graeme Le Saux.

Graeme Le Saux. He's a great guy.

He's a lovely guy. The sophisticated footballer. Huge tennis fan. Famously reads the Guardian, which we won't hold against him. Well, look, he reads. He reads.

Anyhow, off to Wimbledon. What have you made of Wimbledon so far?

女子网球缺的是"人格魅力"，不是天赋

So, we said last week that we thought that it needed a bit of a spicing up, a bit of a kick. People came back going, "Oh, but no, look at the women's game. It's been stocked with talent." I think maybe we were misunderstood. I don't think it was really a question of talent. I think some of the games and matches and players are exceptional. What we were really talking about is the feeling and the atmosphere that comes with the biggest profile players and the personalities that we've had in the past that add to that excitement.

And if you look at those top women's players, Sabalenka, I think has done a very good job on building a kind of brand and a personality beyond her game. But without being too harsh, your number two seed was Elena Rybakina. Don't know a huge amount about — very very quiet. Iga Świątek, exactly the same. Jessica Pegula, who's the four seed, again quite similar. So your top players, while being excellent, exceptional tennis players, don't have that kind of personality factor.

And we've already discussed the Jannik Sinner challenge, which is the most incredible player, but also suffers from a little bit of the —

Yeah, he wouldn't have charisma as his top trump. Yes. Um, you know, sort of 10 rating, but is nonetheless an outstanding tennis player.

英国男单：Arthur Fery 的意外崛起

So, long and short of it, we've — I think firstly from Britain, England — we said that we were short a player or two to follow in this tournament. And Arthur Fery seems to have picked up.

I don't think that anyone — I mean, I'm not a tennis cognoscente. I just like watching it. So, perhaps there is somebody somewhere out there in the ether who was going, "I tell you what, you need to keep an eye on this Arthur Fery bloke." But I certainly didn't hear it. He was a long long way down. Even the British pecking order — and the British tennis pecking order is a long long way down the international pecking order. So Arthur Fery, who I assume was given a wild card — he was given a wild card — and he's now into the quarterfinals.

Into the quarterfinals, which is an astonishing achievement. We do have to enter a little asterisk, Charlie, which is: he's French. Arthur Fery — it's a wonderful name. He sounds like the kind of bloke who would have been playing cricket for Yorkshire in the 1930s. "Arthur Fery came out the mine and bowl quick. He bowled quick, I tell you." But actually, as it turns out, he's not only French, but he's the son of a hedge fund boss, which is brilliant.

I'm grateful to have him here.

Yeah, we're grateful to have him here.

Well, I am. Yes, absolutely. Go Arthur. Absolutely. Brilliant.

Go Arthur. Let's be quite clear. To be fair, he's grown up in England, so he is English, naturalized English, but he is basically French.

Wimbledon 的钱到底流向哪里？

But yes, this goes back to the conversations we've been having about Wimbledon and prize money etc. Because of course whatever the top tennis players want in terms of how much money they want Wimbledon to give them — at the moment that money, the vast majority of it, does go to enabling people throughout Britain to play tennis. That's where that 40–50 million quid a year goes — to the LTA, who then spreads it throughout the country.

And leaving aside young Arthur, most of the other British players actually come from reasonably humble backgrounds and have been enabled through LTA schemes. So, if we give all that money to the top players in prize money as they would all like, then there's not as much money to open up access to everybody else.

Now the criticism that would legitimately be made is: okay, well that's really nice for Britain because you happen to have a Grand Slam in your country, but is that really opening up access to the rest of the world, and why should I, a Belarusian tennis player, care about access in Britain? So there are arguments to be had around it.

Incidentally, I did check in — I sent you a couple of notes over the weekend over this tennis player strike, i.e. "we're only going to spend 15 minutes of time with the media. That'll show you." But even that is not being broadly observed. I mean, it's about 50% of the players who have agreed to only spend 15 minutes of their time with the media. And I wouldn't particularly say that —

I mean, do we want more than 15 minutes?

Oh, really?

15 minutes is fine. In fact, I would almost pay a bit more money. I'd pay a bit more money to get it cut back to 10. Most of these interviews I do with the media are so astonishingly banal. You only see one-minute clips on the BBC really anyway. So, I mean, that's what it's all boiled down to. Yeah, again, if you're going to strike, strike. If you've got the points to make, strike on that.

That final piece you just made there was really interesting. The show that we did with Craig Tiley, which will come out today — that was a key point we were talking about for the broadening of participation. And it was a key point you made of tennis being a top three viewership sport in the world, and not in the top 10 commercial sports in the world. So, they are not making enough money to be able to facilitate the broader participation.

Ecosystem very focused on Grand Slam places, very focused on the areas that already have established tennis backgrounds, but they need to spread far more money — and that just comes from the sport really not managing itself well enough and therefore not making enough money.

That was a point actually that Sampras made a comment on the weekend. He said this whole thing over squeezing the Grand Slams for more money is absolute rubbish. He said what the focus has to be on is the ATP Tour and the WTA Tour. That's where the problem is, not the Grand Slams. The Grand Slams are paying vast amounts of prize money. The issue is in all the other events. That's where the real challenge comes.

Yeah. So, it's good. But yeah, I always love Wimbledon regardless. Even if it seems flat at the beginning, everyone, you know, if you enjoy your tennis, everyone generally gets into it.

世界杯：特朗普致电 FIFA 事件

There's been some big news in the World Cup, Charlie. Not on the football pitch —

Well, there has been big news on the football pitch, but we may come to that a little bit subsequently. But I think what you're going to refer to, Charlie, is big news off the pitch.

Big news off the pitch. I don't want us to go all talk sporty as we occasionally get accused of if we show our real views on England. What a game though. Wow.

Okay. So, yes, we'll get that out. That was fantastic to watch. I'm very excited because obviously, Charlie, this is my derby. This is the Stebbings derby. English England versus the ski jumpers. Sorry, not even the ski jumpers — the people who were not deemed suitable for ski jumping probably for physiological reasons and who therefore converted into being footballers. Exactly.

So, the Stebbings derby. Any conflicted loyalties?

No. You're England all in.

I'm England all in.

I said, as we'll cover shortly, you know, I'm Norway when it suits me, and increasingly so in sport, it suits me to be Norwegian quite a lot because they're increasingly good across various elements of the sporting world. But they were exceptional against Brazil. Brazil have been pretty average throughout the whole tournament. Again, I don't think that takes a genius to figure that out, but the way they closed out that last quarter — and it is interesting, isn't it? Because I do feel strategically with these hydration breaks it does feel like quarters. That last quarter they were awesome. So it'll be an interesting one to see how England approach it.

Balogun 红牌事件与特朗普致电

But what's been happening off the pitch, because there's just been a small incident that seems to have captured the imagination of many people. I mean really, it surprises — I would never have associated with these two individuals.

So in America's last 32 game — yeah, Balogun, their star young attacking player, got sent off. And if we're being honest, it was a bit of a soft sending off. It wouldn't have been a total shock if he hadn't been sent off.

It was accidental. Yeah.

But he was sent off.

Exactly. Right. The bottom line is he was sent off. Now, there is a procedure to appeal a sending off and to say that wasn't a correct sending off — incorrect.

There is not.

There is not.

Well, there is not.

There is not. That is where this is all — so there is no appeal system in this World Cup to appeal a red card.

Interesting. I didn't know that. So now, just being in football, automatically you assume there is an appeals process in place. And I think Gary Neville said it actually — look, there should be, because in a major tournament like this if a player gets unfairly sent off, you want them to participate. You've already suffered for the first one that you've played with 10 men. But there is no official appeals process.

And so when an independent panel — this was the strange thing. I think FIFA's justification for this incident, we'll explain if you have been under a rock, is that the independent panel is always independent and will come to its conclusions based on the facts. But the independent panel in the first place shouldn't really be hearing this. And so why was it hearing it? Well, it was hearing it because after this sending off it came out that Donald Trump put a call into Gianni saying, "I don't think that was a red card."

Now whatever happens after that, right, independent panels or not, whether this is the right call or not, it doesn't really matter. This is a game of perception. Sport is really a game of perceptions. And so when Donald Trump admits he's made that call, the integrity of any decision made — the legitimacy of the sport — disappears if the decision sides with Trump.

Yes. All he had to do in that moment, Infantino, is say, "Look, unfortunately now that you have made that call, I can now not allow this to happen whatsoever, because if it's perceived, Mr. President, that you yourself have changed something to do with the competition, that would actually be worse for all of us. It just won't work."

It was quite extraordinary. I mean again, I don't — I'm not surprised at all by this because this is just the Trump playbook. This is exactly what you would expect from Donald Trump. So I don't look at it and go, "Oh, this is out of character and unusual." On that front, it's on FIFA for entertaining this in the first place considering there is no process in place to appeal. So that is the biggest question.

But I mean obviously with his statements — I did see he said, "He started off by going, you know, I know sports," and then about a minute and a half later said, "I didn't know what a red card is."

悬置禁赛：技术细节与政治意涵

So let's — sorry, let's just... I think we got ahead of ourselves here. So what has happened is that the technical decision is that the one match ban has been suspended for a year. So they officially haven't got rid of the ban. They've sort of pushed it off into the future at some point pending good behavior or something like that. I don't really know now.

As long as he doesn't reoffend.

As long as he doesn't reoffend. I mean, as if.

Right. So, I think you're right. Let's not get too involved in the technicality of this rule and this committee and this rule and that committee. The bottom line is straightforward, which is every single red card in this World Cup has resulted in a one-match ban.

Correct.

Right. End of. Right. Jarrell Quansah got sent off for England against Mexico. He will be banned for the quarterfinal.

Yep. And there have been 12 or 13 red cards. Everyone has been treated the same up until this point. And then who is that that gets the exemption? Oh, it's the host. It's the person that has called up the president of FIFA.

And Gianni, obviously acting like a kind of head of a nation state himself, which always seems to be his strategy.

He, you know, I think he looks — he feels like he's a politician, right? And he loves it. And even in his justifications, "I feel calls from world leaders every day. This is — there was nothing unusual about this."

So, well, again, kind of irrelevant. Do you think, Charlie — okay, we can all agree, and I think the entire football world agrees, that this was crass yet again. Okay, so let's not labor the point.

I see in a lot of the media things like, "Infantino under pressure, Infantino, blah, blah." Is he really? No, he isn't. That's the bottom line. So however much everybody else is foaming at the mouth and jumping up and down saying, "This is absolutely outrageous," Infantino is still traveling around in his private jet going to matches, and at the end of the World Cup he'll say, "That was an amazing World Cup. I did a great job. Nothing to see here."

Infantino 为什么无法被推翻

So, there are three ways that he could be deposed if you want. He can resign. It's not going to happen.

People in power enjoy the power. He certainly does.

Not going to happen. Two, the FIFA council can get together and basically remove him if there is enough support in there. Or there can be an election. Now, we've talked about this at length previously as well.

What has Gianni done amazingly well for a lot of the voting members of FIFA? Cape Verde — are they better off from Gianni's expanded World Cup? Absolutely. You've talked about the blocks of voting that have happened in the Caribbean. Every single Asian country, I think — correct me if I'm wrong on this — but I think every single Asian country came out a few days ago literally saying they are supporting Infantino as a block.

So, there's 207 members of FIFA. He needs about 104, let's say, to win the vote. And I think basically before he'd even go to a vote, he's got about 110 of those votes in the bag already. Most of them not from major footballing nations, but from the "one vote all equal" system.

Therefore, all of this conversation — he should resign. Well, he's not going to resign, and FIFA aren't going to remove him. So really, what do we ask for? What are we crying about? We of course cry about the problems that this creates and where does it stop? Thomas Tuchel, you know, very fair point, was: where does it end now? Literally that was just all he said. And I think a lot of other countries have said it. Belgium's response was actually, "We're not fighting for us. We're fighting for the integrity of the whole sport."

Yeah, of course they are. But unfortunately, as I said, I've joked, but — are we surprised? This is the behavior that both of these people have for a long time.

意外后果：美国队反被拖累

Let's not labor the point. I think to complete the discussion, Belgium thrashed the United States last night. And the US played appallingly badly. And I think it's actually quite an interesting reflection on the law of unintended consequences, which is: Trump's assumption was, as somebody who doesn't necessarily really understand the way in which sport works, "Well, if I make a huge halaloo and that particular player gets reinstated, then that makes it more likely that we will win."

But it's funny when these types of sort of massive amounts of hoopla surround a particular sporting team or an individual player, it's quite rare you get a very high level of performance out the back end of that. Because effectively you're losing focus and focus is the important thing. So instead of Pochettino and his boys being, "Okay, we're without Balogun for one game, but that's okay. It's fine. We got a strong squad. It's fine. This is how we're going to play. This is what we're going to do." First of all, not knowing whether Balogun is going to play or not going to play for a bit, and therefore not quite understanding what your game plan might be. But secondly, just physically during those training sessions, there would have been people coming in and out of meeting rooms going, "Uh, the latest I'm hearing is this, the latest I'm hearing is that," and everyone's mind space being taken up with this issue which otherwise would have been a total non-issue.

So anyway, I think everyone can be thankful that the Belgians won. Probably Infantino also should be thankful that the Belgians won, because if this had run — if the Americans had kept on going in this competition — then that might have started to look a bit more ugly. Maybe there had been some sort of appeal by the Belgians. Maybe even some sort of like CAS appeal or something like that. The whole thing could have got quite ugly.

So, it's probably for the best that Belgium won, and probably for the best going forward from here that the less we see of Donald Trump, the better for the rest of the tournament. Yeah.

Anyhow — until the trophy presentation, obviously.

That'll be quite something, won't it?

NBA Europe：十亿美元的欧洲豪赌

So, moving on to another thing that we need to catch up on, which is NBA Europe, Charlie. Just to remind listeners and viewers — this is quite early in our podding journey — we discussed this issue, which is the NBA (that's the American franchise basketball league) having an aspiration to create something called NBA Europe: an equivalent basketball competition in Europe under their auspices, using their brand. This causing a few ructions with the pre-existing and quite significant Euroleague Basketball, which is where professional European basketball has been at for forever and a day, including with some really big franchises.

So, what's the latest?

招标进展：伦敦、巴黎估值破 10 亿美元

The latest is that the NBA has been running a bidding process for the franchises. There are going to be 12 franchises to kick off the league. And that bidding process closed on June the 30th. And they fielded, I think they said, over 20 serious bids, some of which topped a billion dollars for the biggest cities. And those were assumed to be Paris and London, the most valuable. Some others would go down to about 500 million. There was suggestions from those trying to spin negatively that a few of the smaller ones hadn't met the minimum 500 that they were hoping for. But either way, I think you could safely say that the NBA is pretty delighted with the numbers that have been put on the table for the franchises so far.

NBA 与 EuroLeague 的博弈

The interesting story of course with this has always been the relationship of the NBA and the Euroleague, as you suggested, and how this would actually ever come into fruition. Because you've got the Euroleague on one side basically threatening all kinds of challenges to this. You've got the NBA basically going, "We don't care and we'll find our way through this regardless."

The NBA have been suggesting that for teams to get out of the Euroleague and into the NBA system, there's break clauses in contracts of about 10 million euros. I think reportedly the Euroleague have been saying it's more like a hundred million euros. You've got football clubs in Euroleague who have been a huge part of this. So for example, Barcelona, Real Madrid, you got Turkish clubs — Fenerbahçe I think — and Beşiktaş, Olympiacos in Greece — huge. There's a real kind of thread between —

So just to make that absolutely clear, there are some major European football clubs who also have basketball teams, who have been playing in Euroleague, who are very significant size. And effectively the NBA has been trying to tempt across various of those clubs — not all, but various of those clubs — into NBA Europe, so they sort of have some pre-existing fan bases effectively coming across to start the whole league.

But of course if you've got a billion valuation for, let's just say, a Madrid franchise, and you're Real Madrid, how are you running the economics of that as the NBA? Because Real Madrid are not in a position, nor are its socios, of sanctioning a 1 billion entry into a league. And Real Madrid, Barcelona, a few of the others — there's been a pretty strong suggestion that they believe that the NBA needs them far more than they need it.

NBA 的让步：3 年盈亏平衡承诺

And by the way, Charlie, in the interim period between when we first discussed this back in January and now, the NBA and Adam Silver, the commissioner of the NBA, have had to make some significant concessions to make these franchises attractive. Chief of which has been the amount of money which the NBA subsidizes NBA Europe with.

The original concept was — if they could have pulled it off, this would have been great — was first of all, we're going to charge entities huge amounts of money to have a franchise, and then effectively they are then going to have to also bear the cost of the league as a whole. And it became clear that wasn't really going to fly. So what happened was, the NBA said, "Right, of the franchise fees that get paid, we are going to reinvest a significant chunk of that in effectively frontloading the costs of the league for a substantial period of time until it gets itself fully up and running."

Which part of this pitch was that the franchises will break even in three years.

Yeah. So coming back to why people always want to invest in sport, the importance of that investable environment is: you're not going to lose bucket loads of money if you invest in it. Now, if you're already spending a billion and you're having to pump in a billion over the next 3 years — let's say, or 5 years — you're very happy to hear that there's a period in the near future that won't require you to do that.

Euroleague 的反击与结构性挑战

However, the Euroleague is kicking hard. It's fighting hard. It's trying to sign its current teams to 10-year deals. Real Madrid actually committed the day before the close of the bids to the Euroleague. So, they've actually gone and put their colors on the mast of the Euroleague. You're back in the situation of very high franchise fees being paid for assets that don't exist yet, right off the back of the success of something that works incredibly well in — as we know — a very difficult environment to replicate in Europe, i.e. the American sports ecosystem.

Now, are we saying that it wouldn't work? Absolutely not. Basketball is a huge sport in Europe. But what we have discussed previously at length is the difference between establishing brand new IP and assuming that audience and fandom and passion that is associated with European sport follows, versus could we look at the Euroleague and build something out of that — in a similar way that we suggested LIV Golf and the PIF should have done with the European Tour. Complexities in that. And by the state of the Euroleague's comms, I'm sure they would have told the NBA exactly where to stick it if that had been the communications anyway.

But this may be great. There's a long way to go, I think, in making it a reality in the ways the NBA would perfectly like. There'll probably have to be a few more movements and concessions over the next few months because it's meant to start next year.

Yeah. I mean, at the moment, it looks like there's going to end up being two leagues.

Yeah. And that's really not beneficial because in terms of narrative that then becomes quite a difficult thing to explain to the broader sporting public, which is really the point of this. Okay.

So if you think about basketball in Europe — professional basketball in Europe — the Euroleague is already having an established audience in certain places that has been built up over decades and decades and decades. And that's great. These are what might be called the basketball fanatics. These are the people who really really care about basketball.

Now the question is, for the new franchises to work, it's not going to be enough simply to transfer across some of the professional basketball fanatics. They're going to need to penetrate the broader audience. So, if you think about that London franchise or the Paris franchise, whatever it might be, they're going to need to fill a big arena. And they're going to need to be able to draw in a much broader cross-section of the sports watching community than those people who are already established, passionate basketball watchers — otherwise why bother, frankly, overall.

So I think it's going to be really interesting to watch. This is the first time that one of the major American sports has made a serious effort to establish a European league. A serious one. A really serious effort. Massively heavily backed, very ambitious, right? "We're going to really, really go for it." And it makes sense, right? Because basketball's got much stronger roots in Europe than American football and baseball have. So, of the three, this is the right one to be giving it a real go.

And I don't want this to be us being critical. I think this is having a look at it and trying to pull out where it is right now and where some of those challenges will be. But as you say, at the heart of it, basketball is a big sport in Europe.

It is a big sport in Europe. Some of the biggest attendances in indoor arenas, great viewership as we — I mean, it's like 500 million, wasn't it, across Europe on broadcast. So that is absolutely there.

But just as a lot of other audiences have been there for sports that have also spun off new leagues — again, it just comes back to the point of a break even in 3 years will also have assumptions of X amount of attendances and participations from a fan base that may not have actually been converted to a London franchise or a Paris franchise. So, if you don't break even in 3 years, the NBA is going to have to subsidize some of that to ensure that those franchise owners don't come back and go, "Well, that was a key part of our investment thesis."

Yeah. Let's keep watching. It'll develop.
