item.title: [GoldRepublic Global] Alasdair Macleod: How China Is Preparing for a Dollar Collapse
item.dc:creator: GoldRepublic Global
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、真正在崩塌的不是"美元"，而是"用纸面定价物质"的整个文明范式

大多数评论把这场变局讲成"东方囤金 vs 西方抛售"的地缘博弈，但 Macleod 无意间揭示了一个更深的裂缝：LBMA 与 COMEX 本质上不是"黄金市场"，而是"信用市场穿着黄金的外衣"——银行家不关心金属本身，他们经营的是"以美元计价的合约"。所以当美元的定价能力枯竭时，垮掉的不是黄金供需，而是"用一种可以凭空创造的东西去为一种从地里挖出来的东西定价"这套心智结构。

这里有一个让人意外的观察：COMEX 未平仓合约跌到十几年新低，并不是投机者不玩了，而是做市银行主动撤出——他们在悄悄闭店。也就是说，西方黄金定价权的死亡，不是被东方"夺走"的，而是被西方交易员自己关灯走人的。这和 2008 年前投行悄悄关闭 CDO 台子的模式惊人相似：市场往往不是被击败的，是被内部人先一步放弃的。

二、中国真正在打的牌不是"金本位"，而是奥地利学派意义上的 "crackup boom"（崩溃性繁荣）

Macleod 用了一个几乎没人在中文财经语境里讨论的概念：crackup boom——米塞斯说的"当持有货币的人集体意识到问题不是物价在涨、而是货币在死时"发生的最后疯狂。有意思的是：PBOC 现在的行为不像一个央行，反而像一个米塞斯派的持币者——她在用一切能出口挣来的美元，疯狂换成银、铜、金、镍、甚至硫酸原料。她不是"投资于战略资源"，她是在跑路。

跨领域的意外联系：这与生物学中的"加速末期反应（terminal burst）"极其相似——濒死的细胞会突然消耗掉所有 ATP 做出最后一波剧烈代谢。PBOC 正在把美元当作即将过期的 ATP 燃烧。你可以从铜库存的堆积节奏、稀土/硫酸/化肥出口的骤停中，读到同一种"生理反应"。中国不是在囤货，她是在做代谢层面的资产负债表重构。

三、被忽视的真正主角：不是中国，而是俄罗斯

所有人都盯着上海金交所和港深金库，但 Macleod 抛出的最颠覆性的观点是——真正能第一个"扣下扳机"的可能是俄罗斯，不是中国。原因反直觉：俄罗斯政府债务占 GDP 不到 20%，个税约 13%，卢布几乎没人在境外持有——这意味着西方对冲基金无法"做空卢布对黄金"来打破挂钩，因为他们手里就没有卢布可卖。

这是一个极其罕见的博弈论构型：流动性缺乏在此不是弱点，而是护城河。跟历史类比一下——1717 年牛顿在英国建立金本位时，恰恰是因为英镑当时并非国际主流货币，反而没人有能力狙击它。同理，被西方长期"金融孤立"的卢布，反而变成了唯一可以无痛切换到金本位的主权货币。制裁把它锻造成了一把只有俄罗斯能挥的剑。

四、隐藏的系统性真相：储蓄率才是货币命运的锚，而非汇率

Macleod 在最后一击很轻描淡写地说了一句可能是整场访谈最深的话：贸易顺差/逆差不取决于汇率，而取决于储蓄率的差。中国居民储蓄率 30–35%，美国 7% 以下——这才是"中国出口机器"能与"强货币"共存的根本原因，就像 1990 年代马克强势时德国照样保持顺差。

这里有一个让人意外的政策含义：如果这是真的，那么川普加多少关税都无法逆转贸易失衡，因为关税不改变储蓄率。你唯一能做的，是要么让美国人存钱、要么让中国人消费——而美联储 40 年的低利率政策恰恰是在摧毁美国人的储蓄能力。也就是说，美元帝国的敌人不在北京，而在自己的利率决议室里。凯恩斯主义的核心工具，正是它自身的掘墓人。

五、一个跨领域的比喻收束

如果把这场变局翻译成生态学语言：美元是一个顶级捕食者物种，它的灭绝不会创造出一个"替代顶级捕食者"，而会释放出一整套新的生态位。中国和俄罗斯不是要变成"新美元"，她们是在利用生态位的真空回到更古老的物种形态——金本位是货币界的"活化石"，它之所以还能被复活，因为它从未真正死过，只是被压制。

这就是为什么 Macleod 反复说："每次政府试图逃离黄金，最终都失败了。"——这不是意识形态，这是热力学。信用是低熵状态，需要不断输入能量维持；黄金是高熵稳态，什么都不做也能存在。哪个更长寿，是物理决定的，不是政治决定的。

📝 转录文本

Alasdair Macleod: How China Is Preparing for a Dollar Collapse

GoldRepublic Global — 2026 年 7 月 7 日

开场：中国正在为一个没有美元的世界做准备

Alasdair Macleod: What we are seeing is no less than China planning for a future without the dollar. Not just her being without the dollar, but the world being without the dollar. She sees the collapse in the dollar coming through fairly fairly soon.

And this all sort of comes in towards a gold exchange standard between the yuan and gold being established to replace a failing western currency, fiat currency system. This is rather like PBOC deciding that this is the time for a crackup boom.

It's not just a question of, um, all the gold going east, but now the market is as well. That's where the future of the future market is.

So I think that the Chinese government has probably accumulated a further, what, 20–25,000 tons, something like that. So they've got 50,000 tons as a state quite easily. You know, we're looking at as much as 30 to 35% of global above-ground gold stocks actually being locked up in China.

What's being signaled is the end of the American empire — to an end very, very rapidly.

主持人开场

Alex A. Jordov: Welcome to the microscopic podcast. My name is Alex A. Jordov and today is the 7th of July 2026, and I'm joined by my recurring and guest of honor, Alasdair Macleod. Welcome back.

Macleod: Nice to be back, Alex. Thank you for having me.

Jordov: So Alasdair, last time we spoke was the end of March, and obviously we had quite some developments since then. One of them being the Hong Kong clearing system that went live this morning. I wanted to know from you what it entails when we talk about what you also mentioned about China and Russia — that could go on a gold standard when necessary.

The question is obviously, is this moment approaching, if it hasn't already happened in an indirect way already? How do you then describe the system as it is now? And what does this opening of the clearing system in Hong Kong actually mean within this picture?

一、G7 货币的十字路口：PBOC 正在制造一次 "crackup boom"

Macleod: Yeah. I mean, it is fascinating. We are at a crossroads, I think, for the future of G7 currencies is what it boils down to. China appears to have taken the view that these currencies are going to die — and they're going to die sooner rather than later.

Bear in mind that China's exports are at record levels. She is earning dollars in particular, and I suppose yen and euros to a lesser extent, but particularly dollars — and she's wanting to get rid of them.

We can see this: instead of exporting silver to try and suppress the price, she's now just turned 180° and is now importing silver to get rid of dollars. She's importing gold to get rid of dollars. She's importing even copper to get rid of dollars.

So this is rather like, if you like, um, the PBOC deciding that this is the time for a crackup boom — which, you know, Austrian economists sort of talk about. It's the final death of a currency, when everybody realizes that it's not prices going up, but the purchasing power of the currency going down.

While the rest of us are not taking this view at all, the Chinese PBOC is doing exactly that. She's just getting out of every dollar she possibly can.

一系列信号

There's been a sequence of events. Back in, I think it was in February, she told her banks: don't hold too many US treasuries. Now I think that's, if you like, a signal — you know, it's not just a question of "don't hold US treasuries," it's get rid of them. That's really what she's saying.

And since then as well, we have seen huge, huge imports from her of silver, which has happened, I think, principally because the western markets have provided the opportunity. You know, we've been selling gold and silver because they rushed up too far, too fast, whatever, whatever — and so she's been getting out of them.

二、金库、SGE 与新型 Bretton Woods

Now the other side of the crackup boom obviously is: if the dollar is toast and the G7 currencies are toast, then she wants to prevent her own currency sliding down with them into valuelessness, because it is a fake currency just the same as the dollar.

And it's really to this end that her forward planning — she's opened, well, so far as we know, two vaults so far: one in Hong Kong and also one in Saudi Arabia, where gold and yuan can be exchanged for each other.

There are further SGE vaults planned in Southeast Asia. There was mention of one in Dubai, which I think has probably been put on hold by the Straits of Hormuz crisis. And there was even some mention that one might be opened in Switzerland.

I think if you put it all together, it's clear that China sees the importance of gold for her own currency. She will link the currency to gold, and that's what all this is about. Bretton Woods Mark II, if you like.

纸面市场的死亡

And I think from there, she's looking at the future of the paper markets in the West. Now quite obviously, they're going to die along with the dollar, because they're paper markets in currencies which basically refer to bullion.

The banks dealing on the LBMA and also in the COMEX futures — as far as they're concerned, they're dealers in credit. They're not dealers in physical. So the whole basis of the paper markets is that physical is an inconvenience.

Now this basically means that once the means of pricing them collapses, then those markets will collapse as well. So China is building, if you like, the market of the future. So not only have we seen gold march from west to east, but now the market is marching from west to east as well. And it's not going to be a dollar-priced market — it is going to be a yuan-based market.

You can see that she's importing, as I said earlier, gold, silver, copper. She's also stopping exports of various other things which would earn her dollars — like sulfuric acid, like fertilizers and so on. So yeah, she is, I think, signaling that as far as she is concerned, this is a crackup boom.

三、"中国金本位"到底是什么？内外双轨的可能性

Jordov: That's a fascinating, fascinating thought. I want to maybe dissect what it then means, per se, when we say "the China gold standard." You've just mentioned more of like the gold settlement network with the SGE vaults.

So when we look at, let's say, the fact that it would be backed by yuan — there's, there might be some subtleties to that, right? An international yuan linked to gold, but also then having a domestic yuan that may remain managed as well, right? Because we shouldn't forget about the nature that the yuan has, being controlled.

What is then the key difference here that you can explain?

Macleod: Well, it is interesting. Of course, you do have exchange controls which are policed by the PBOC. There was a little-noticed announcement by, I think it was the PBOC, that it would no longer be required for the PBOC to give permission for gold to move out of China into Hong Kong — it would purely be a customs matter.

Now this, to me, is opening up — certainly as far as gold is concerned — so that you're not having quite the same sort of strictures, if you like, of exchange control with respect to gold. Now this is very, very important.

If people — I mean, we're talking principally about people with wealth and all the rest of it — if they can move their gold into Hong Kong, then this, if you like, will provide liquidity for the new Hong Kong development. And not only that, but China has also announced that refining facilities to feed into Hong Kong will be based in Shenzhen.

So the whole thing is actually changing, and I think by freeing up the gold market, the next step is to free up the yuan. Now this, I think, is an extremely important development. Because so far, those of us who have been observing the inevitability of a link between gold and yuan have been thinking in terms of foreign exchange in terms of trade settlement, and so on, through the CIPS system — the new CIPS system.

But it appears that it's actually going to be even freer than that. So I can see now the possibility — early days, but the possibility — that when China fixes the rate between yuan and gold, it will be available to everybody.

CIPS 是什么？

Jordov: What do you mean by the CIPS system?

Macleod: The CIPS system is the Chinese international payments system, which was set up really as an alternative to SWIFT. SWIFT since then has been, if you like, effectively shut down to Russia etc. They've used it as a means of weaponizing the dollar. So China obviously realized that it's got to have its own systems.

And so far, there are an enormous number of countries — I can't remember the exact figures — but the majority of countries around the world have CIPS accounts now. So far, CIPS is not anything like a rival to the dollar in terms of trade settlements, but you can see that as the dollar demises, you're going to need to have CIPS, because the basis of SWIFT basically is paper currencies.

So this is going to be an interesting one. And I think that what we are seeing is no less than China planning for a future without the dollar. Not just her being without the dollar, but the world being without the dollar. She sees the collapse in the dollar coming through fairly, fairly soon.

四、结算的落地：海湾产油国如何用人民币换黄金

Jordov: Maybe more in practical terms, talking about the settlement system. So let's say there's a Gulf oil exporter holding yuan and wants gold in the Saudi SGE. Who would bear then the counterparty risk, and at what prices, and the yuan-gold rate then fixed in this case?

Macleod: Well, so far what we're looking at is gold being exchanged for yuan in the SGE vaults outside China. And I don't see that changing, because the whole basis of it is that there will be a fixed exchange rate between gold and the yuan. All that we need to know is what that rate is. And China is in a position to basically say, "Right, this is the rate. Bang. That's it."

And you can, if you're worried about the value of the yuan, then you can sell your yuan and have gold. And you can take it out of those — I mean, eventually you'll be able to take it out of the SGE vaults anyway. There's no reason why it shouldn't be withdrawn.

So in other words, it would work as a gold standard in the old-fashioned sense. I mean, that is the way in which the relationship between money and credit has been since Roman times. And every time a government has tried to get away from it, eventually that has failed. We always return to the system whereby we guarantee the value of a currency by making it exchangeable for gold.

You know, it hasn't been like that for us for a very, very long time. I mean, even before 1971, when the last vestige of a gold exchange standard was finally suspended, we're looking at the end of the fiat currency system, Alex. It is no more, you know, it's as simple as that.

This is what China is telling us — not me. China by her actions. It's absolutely clear.

And of course, the majority of people in the investment industry who have no experience, if you like, of this — they haven't grown up with a gold standard. A lot of them are sort of effectively Keynesian anyway, so they don't understand the importance of guaranteeing the value of a fiat currency and turning it into a gold substitute, which is the best way to describe a currency or credit which circulates, whose value is exchangeable for gold, and therefore tied to the value of gold.

五、中国到底囤了多少黄金？1983 条例、租赁骗局与 20,000 吨假说

Jordov: And obviously in order to make this or expand the system, there needs to be this base of gold reserves. You've covered this extensively, I think, as well — based on the 1983 regulations of the PRC on control of gold and silver. You've written about this also end of June in your newsletter, and you describe how this 43 years of policy has been continuous.

In the last months — I think in the last 19 months — China has been accumulating gold and barely anything came out of China as well. Could you maybe walk us through this arithmetic of the calculation, based on your thesis and this policy that I just described — how much China really holds, or let's say we could estimate holds, to back up this type of system in order to work?

Macleod: Well, it's quite clear from those original regulations, which appointed the PBOC with total responsibility to the state — to the Chinese Communist Party — for the management of the nation's gold and silver reserves (both, by the way, but they've been treated differently, so we concentrate on gold).

1983–2002：国家垄断积累

It is clear that between 1983 and 2002, the PBOC was accumulating gold for the state. Individuals were banned from owning gold. This cleared the decks as far as the PBOC were concerned, so they could accumulate sufficient gold in order to satisfy, if you like, the government itself — the state itself.

2002 之后：上海金交所与私人持有

Now from 2002, she then turned round and set up the Shanghai Gold Exchange and permitted individuals to buy gold. And indeed at one stage, I think in about 2006–7, roundabout then, she was even advertising: "Run out and buy gold. It's good for you," whatever the strapline was.

And since then, individuals have taken delivery out of the Shanghai Gold Exchange to the tune of about 28,000 tons. So they've got about 28,000 tons.

Now in addition to that, there are investments in the form of ETFs, in the form of gold accumulation accounts and so on and so forth, held by private individuals, which I assume are probably in the region of a further 5,000 tons. We don't know the figure, but that is an assumption.

国家层面：黄金租赁与 Frank Veneroso

As to what the state did — it's very interesting that when China was quietly accumulating gold, remember the People's Bank of China also had sole responsibility for all the nation's foreign exchange dealings. And as you referred earlier, Chinese citizens were not permitted to hold foreign exchange.

So in the early days, in the mid to late '80s, there was massive inward investment as American and European companies were setting up factories and all manufacturing facilities and all these relationships. So this was an enormous amount of foreign exchange inflows. And then from there, the inflows continued into the '90s, but at the same time you had large amounts of exports beginning to be generated. So again, the People's Bank was handling massive flows both ways of foreign exchanges.

Now if we go back to the early 1980s, the gold carry trade in the west began. And this was the means whereby bullion banks, if you like, would lease gold from central banks, sell that into the market, and invest the proceeds — and not only them but hedge funds as well — invest the proceeds in US treasuries.

Now the lease rate would be something like 1%, and at the time US treasuries were yielding as much as 10% (I mean, looking at T-bills, say six-month T-bills). Now that gold was sold into the market and — the analysis from an analyst who really looked at this in detail, Frank Veneroso, was that this gold didn't come back.

So where did it go? Um, I think he made a flippant comment in 2002 in a speech in Lima that perhaps it was adorning the necks of Asian women. I have a different story. I think it disappeared into the market and was bought by the People's Bank of China.

Not only did something like a third to a half of all central bank gold out on lease not return — and is still sitting there, if you like, in IOU form — but as well as that, the West, in this huge great bear market, was liquidating its portfolio positions. I mean, when we went into the 1980s, people reckoned they should have at least 10 or 20% of their portfolios in gold or in gold-related investments.

So particularly the Swiss private banks were selling gold right towards the end of the century. In addition to that, there was a large increase in the amount of above-ground gold as a result of mining activities.

估算：国家 50,000 吨，全球 30–35%

So all the gold was there for the PBOC to accumulate a figure like 20,000 tons. Now that's my estimate — I don't know the answer at all. I do know that it's spread around various party organs like the People's Liberation Army, like the youth wing of the Communist Party, and so on and so forth. So we know that gold is there. We don't know exactly how much.

I reckon 20,000 tons basically — that being an assessment of about 10% of the foreign exchange flows which were crossing the PBOC's foreign exchange desk. So that was the basis of my estimate.

Since then we have seen gold continue to migrate from west to east, and China has been the big buyer. I think at the same time, estimates of what India actually has — the Indian population — have been wildly exaggerated. They're talking about 20,000, 30,000 tons or something in the hands of the Indians. That's completely false. I won't go into the reasons for that, but there are very good reasons for making that statement.

So I think that the Chinese government has probably accumulated a further, what, 20–25,000 tons, something like that. So they've got — I mean, they could have 50,000 tons as a state quite easily. And then of course you've got all the holdings by individuals and so on. So we're looking at as much as 30 to 35% of global above-ground gold stocks actually being locked up in China.

香港自由化的含义

Now, the importance of this move from the PBOC saying "you no longer need to seek our permission to export gold into Hong Kong" — or I suppose anywhere else for that matter, it's just a customs matter — all these things are customs matters. But essentially it has freed it up.

That, I think, basically means that you've got this huge great mountain of gold which is going to back the new market in gold in Hong Kong. The new settlement system is unashamedly based on the settlement system in the LBMA, but it's going to be not effectively a paper market. I mean yes, there is obviously — if you've got a deferred settlement, then there is a paper market — but the point is it's going to be considerably more physical.

And also, being in Chinese yuan, it's something which, I guess, the authorities will have greater control over. And this all sort of comes in towards, if you like, a gold exchange standard between the yuan and gold being established to replace a failing western fiat currency system.

I think it's absolutely clear if you look at all the steps that have been taken. It's absolutely clear that this is where this is going.

中国对海湾局势的先知先觉

But the thing that's interesting is that China obviously has a very, very good intelligence on what was going on in the Gulf ahead of that war. She reversed her policy towards silver in particular at the same time as rare earths had already been restricted. But the first we became aware of the change in the policy on silver was that suddenly there was a crisis on the LBMA with a spike in lease rates up to about 40%, I think that was on the 9th of October.

So this is it — she's behaving as if she regards the western currencies and the dollar in particular as being in a crackup boom, and she's now got to protect her own position.

六、俄罗斯：真正可能率先扣下扳机的国家

The other thing we haven't mentioned is Russia. Russia is fascinating.

Her finances are actually in very good order thanks to her ability to export energy in particular. Government debt to GDP is less than 20%. Personal tax rates are around about 13%, something like that. To libertarians, this is heaven.

She can actually go on a gold standard tomorrow. And I think that — I mean, if I was in Putin's shoes, I would be very tempted not to aggressively attack the Europeans who are arming themselves for a future conflict against Russia (Britain, France, Germany in particular). But I think I could quite easily just destroy them by putting the ruble on a gold standard.

想象一下会发生什么

If you think what would happen then — at the moment, interest rates in Russia are — I think it's about 13% or something. Inflation is running last I saw something like 8% at consumer price level. If they put the ruble onto a gold standard, what would happen is that the Russian banks would be in our markets buying gold, leasing it even for 1%, and — because the ruble would be a gold substitute effectively — shipping it into Russia with a return of something like 13%.

You know, what's not to like? This is the way in fact in which gold standards are maintained. If you've got to run on your gold, just raise the interest rates. You'll find the run will stop, and it will reverse if you raise them enough.

So Russia is already in this position. And not only that, but of course nobody outside Russia really holds rubles. So it's not as if you're going to get — they're going to be immediately challenged by malevolent forces in the West flogging rubles to undermine, you know, to destroy the peg — because they haven't got the rubles to sell. It's a wonderful position.

And if they did that, they would actually destroy the finances of Britain, France, and Germany completely. They wouldn't be able to prosecute war. They'd had to come begging: "Um, perhaps we need a new diplomatic approach." I'd be very tempted to do that. I really would if I was in Putin's shoes.

Glazyev 的信号

And not only that, but Sergey Glazyev wrote an article in a Moscow business magazine about, what, three years ago, four maybe four years ago — saying, "You know, it'd be very easy to put the ruble onto a gold standard." And he was right.

It was interesting that that was published, because I think it was a signal that this is something which was being thought of, let's put it that way, in the highest circles in Russia. They know they can put the ruble on the gold standard.

And you know, we're now in a situation where the Chinese are saying "these dollars are toast." You know, they must be thinking in similar lines to China in Russia now.

俄罗斯的真实黄金储备

And the other thing is that Russia can easily disclose far larger levels of gold in her reserves than just the 2,300 tons or whatever that the central bank holds — because they've got sovereign wealth funds. And intelligence suggests that they've got something like 10,000 tons between the two principal sovereign wealth funds.

And of course, the level of gold mining has accelerated quite substantially in Russia. So much so that she probably now rivals China as a gold miner. So yeah, she's got the gold to do it — and more gold effectively than America says she has.

七、美国怎么办？"她把别人的黄金偷完了"

What the Americans' response to this is going to be — help. I mean, they've got no answer to it.

First, they need to prove that they haven't got rid of all their gold. Go and speak to the Bundesbank and say what their opinion is. They were the ones who tried to get 300 tons out of the New York Fed back in whenever — I think it was 2012, 13 or something. And they were told it would take seven years. Come on.

Americans haven't got anything. They've stolen everybody else's gold, I guess. I mean, it's all part of the gold suppression scheme. Because I'm pretty sure that of the 10 to 14,000 tons which were leased by 2002 and identified as such — well, identified, probably "estimated" is a better word — by Frank Veneroso, I reckon the bulk of that is missing from the US, and basically from the earmarked reserves held by the US as custodian for the property of other central banks in Europe principally. And also, I guess that she's also drawn down on her own reserves.

一段历史

So what's she got? She's got a problem. She went nap on trying to replace gold with a dollar, and is now being called for wanting. I mean, if she actually had any sense of history, she wouldn't have gone down this route. But it was politics that decided it.

When you bear in mind that in 1948, she had over 20,000 tons — which was something like 60% of the above-ground stocks in the world at that stage — and she frittered them away all the way down to 9,000-something tons in 1971 when she was forced to suspend the Bretton Woods agreement. I mean, one wonders whether they had 9,100 whatever it was tons then.

So basically they're being fraudulent on the rest of us, and they're being found out. China knows this. I'm sure that their intelligence, and Russia's intelligence, knows a lot more about the true position of US gold reserves — and what's held by the New York Fed on an earmark basis — than we can ever know.

八、储蓄率才是命运的锚：出口机器与硬通货并不矛盾

Jordov: We'll talk about this in a bit. But as a last question around China, I wanted to know how would you think about the following: the fact that China also runs large deficits, and we also mentioned the capital controls, but also that its export model is heavily relying on the weak yuan — and that a gold standard would also unleash deflationary forces. But being an exporter, having a gold-back currency will strengthen the real exchange rates.

So how would then China square the being hard money with keeping the export machine going?

Macleod: Well, there's a huge, huge great misconception at the center of your question, and that is that the balance of trade depends on the relative values of currencies. They don't. What matters is the differential in savings rates. That's the thing you've got to look at.

What happens in China is that households save something between 30 and 35% of their income every year. Compare that with the United States, for example. What, 7% if you're lucky, probably even less. So that is the thing that gives rise to, if you like, the trade deficit on one side and the trade surplus on the other.

美国将变成 1920–23 的德国

So in a sense, if you're going onto a gold standard and the dollar collapsing and all the rest of it, I mean basically America will be in the same position as Germany was in 1920 to 1923. Yes. I mean, some manufacturing businesses will do extremely well. That, of course, assumes that tariffs and all the rest of it are not introduced by China, which could well be the case. I don't know. She needn't do it.

Because as I say, the important thing is the savings rate, the differential in savings rates. And we see this — we've seen this before the days of the euro, when the Deutsche Mark was strong. It was consistently strong. And of course everybody said that they're suppressing it, which is why Germany has got such a large export surplus. Well, how was it that with a strong Deutsche Mark, you had an export surplus? The answer was that Germans saved.

They saved. Instead of going out and spending everything they earned, they put money aside. And this meant that domestic demand for goods was contained. And it's exactly the same in China.

So there is actually no problem at all in this regard, and I think you've got to stop looking at the idea that exports and imports are the be-all and end-all of economic activity. No, they're not. It's a side issue. But it's an issue which you can actually make backfire on yourself by taxing savings, discouraging savings, suppressing interest rates to encourage consumption. That way you destroy your economy.

So you've got to dismiss all this Keynesian rubbish which everybody believes in.

九、西方纸面市场的静默死亡：银行家自己关灯走人

Jordov: And now moving on to what you just described as well before — how the markets themselves are shifting, and by that I mean the physical gold market is shifting from west to east, and so does the pricing at some point as well. And you've also written lately about the fact that today's low open interest are akin to the 2015–2016 gold bottom. What is, let's say, different this time?

Macleod: Well, the thing about western paper markets is that basically it's run by banks. Now banks are not interested in holding physical metal. Their deal is in credit. You know, they will lend at one rate and effectively sell funds at one rate and buy them in at a far lower rate. And a lot of that rate they can just create themselves at zero. That's their business.

They're not in the business of tying up balance sheets with physical metal, owning physical metal. That system effectively is declining. And if you look at the open interest on COMEX, in both gold and silver, they are hitting historic — well, certainly lows for over the last 10 to 15 years. And I don't see that really recovering.

银行想退，但退不干净

So apart from anything else, the reason that it's so low isn't so much that speculators aren't interested in, you know, say buying gold / sell dollar or sell gold / buy dollars — you know, that sort of pairs-trade thing. It's just the banks don't want to deal. And it's quite simple: if you want to go and buy a future, you get made a price which is way out there. So that's the way you basically exit from the market. And that's what they're trying to do.

Despite the fact that in trying to exit from the market, they still have enormous short liabilities. You look at the swaps — it's come down recently. At the peak, I reckon that the average swap short was around about $3 billion worth; now I think it's down to probably less than a billion. So they have managed to get it down a long way, but they can't get it really much lower.

This is a real problem for them. And I think, in a sense, they probably welcome the markets moving away from them. And then, if they can sort of quietly get out of their positions, they'd be absolutely delighted. But it's difficult to see how they'll do that.

I mean, theoretically they go short on COMEX and they're long in London — but it's paper-long in London. So it's all paper, paper, paper. And guess what: the two sides of the paper — you might be a gold contract, but actually it's a dollar contract. That's the key to understanding where the risk in this lies. It's a dollar contract. And you can manufacture dollar contracts in anything you like, but the question is: can you actually close them off at the end of the day? And that is the bit they're finding difficult, I think.

如果伦敦不再够深

Jordov: And also COMEX inventory are in lower and lower levels steadily. But if bullion banks are then short on COMEX, as you mentioned, then they also normally hedge through London. What would happen if then the London physical market then is no longer deep enough to offset that risk? What will happen then?

Macleod: Well, the way in which these markets will die is basically they will die because the currencies die. That's the thing. I wouldn't say that it's the total end of the LBMA. But just imagine, if one ounce, if you like, is the equivalent of a million dollars — which will be the situation in a currency collapse — then how important does London become?

I mean, the volumes might sort of appear to be fantastic, but if a million dollars doesn't buy you a cup of tea, so what? This is the danger that China obviously sees. And this is why she's setting up an alternative, which is a physically based settlement system between — you know — and would be pricing it in yuan.

套利与新银行业

So the one thing she is yet to do is actually to fix the rate and say, "Right, this is the rate." In a sense, the speculation would then be taken out of it. And I think that the speculation would then, in gold anyway, work on arbitrage.

You know, if someone has a gold standard which promises you a higher rate than what you're getting, let's say, in the yuan as a gold substitute, then what do you do? It's quite simple. You sell your yuan, buy your gold, ship it to Russia where you get 14% or whatever — currently — out of rubles as a gold substitute, and say thank you very much. This is banking. That is banking. That is arbitrage. Makes sense. Makes a lot of sense.

But I think that's about the only sort of business that will happen. And it's for this reason that once Russia does put the ruble on a gold standard, interest rates will fall quite rapidly. And it will, I think, lead to an enormous revival of economic activity on a non-inflationary basis — so long as they continue to tie that ruble to gold with a proper exchange standard.

This is something which must be done — for the good of not just for the world (looking at this chasm of currency collapses), but as far as China and Russia are concerned, this is going to add so much strength to their economies. It's a must whichever way you look at it.

I know that macroeconomists don't understand it, but then that's their problem. Read a bit of history and learn what happened in the UK, and also Europe incidentally, in the 19th century under gold standards. We didn't need to have this Keynesian stimulation to develop our Industrial Revolution. No, it worked because there was stability.

十、白银：中国从压制到反转，供应链的连锁反应

Jordov: I'd also like to talk about, obviously, silver. So you've mentioned a few things about it already, but I want to get your take on what you wrote about the fact that China suppressed the silver prices by exporting stockpiles — and that this policy has now actually ended. So what evidence should investors then watch to confirm that China is no longer capping the price?

Macleod: Well, not only has it ended, but it's been reversed. All you need to do is to go into your favorite AI machine and just ask the question: "What are China's silver imports and exports in 2026?" And you can have a follow-up question: "How does this — what were China's imports and exports between 2020 and 2025?"

And you know, it all comes up. So this is the great thing about AI. You can ask these questions and they'll give you the sources too. So you can say, "Oh, I just — I don't think that's a very good source, but this is a good source. I would respect that. I would trust that more than that one." So you can assess the validity of the AI responses to your question. It's good. And I would encourage everybody to do it. Then you learn the truth, not what your government's telling you.

霍尔木兹危机的二阶效应

Jordov: Because we have also mentioned the Hormuz crisis a few months ago, that's still ongoing. We saw strikes just this morning on some Qatari ships if I'm not mistaken, while they are mourning Khamenei's death. And this has second-order effects — and you've also written about it: the fact that sulfur and sulfuric acid are also quite essential in what it is meaning in the metal refining. So mainly Chile and Peru are actually struggling with that. How do you see that affect the supply of, let's say, copper, zinc, and the byproduct of silver supply?

Macleod: Well, it will only really affect, I think, the really marginal producers who can't pay the higher prices for these chemicals. But I think the other effect is that it'll just accelerate, if you like, the trend for China to process everything.

You know, I can see she already imports an awful lot of ores, and most of her silver doesn't come from mines. I think something like 76% or 75% comes from refining copper, zinc, lead and gold. The rest of it is mined. But I mean, we're talking about roughly a quarter of it coming from mines.

So I think that because she's going to hang on to her sulfuric acid — which is vital in the processing of those non-ferrous metals — I think she will, I think that the imports of these non-ferrous metal ores will increase. And so I think she's stockpiling copper. She needs these things in terms of long-term planning.

美国也想要白银

America also wants silver — of course, because it's now declared as critical mineral. Did you really think that the Chinese were going to supply the Americans with a stockpile of silver? I think that was part of the decision to reverse their suppression-of-silver policy, which is really why they were exporting such huge amounts.

So the silver squeeze — we've just seen phase one of that. That's going to turn around, and we're looking at essentially a squeeze due to industrial demand and nothing else.

Just imagine, as the price of gold appears to rise (which of course is the value of the dollar falling), once that gets going again and people try and hedge out of dollars, pounds, euros, whatever, yen — or yuan, there's another story — then you're going to have a combination of industrial demand just cleaning out silver liquidity, and then you got individuals thinking, "This is a cheap way in to the gold story," which is the way they'll look at it initially, until they realize actually it's their currency going down.

So I can see a huge great, if you like, conflict between industrial demand and investor demand — what we call investor demand — mounting in the coming months.

十一、为什么中国关掉了国内杠杆交易，却鼓励黄金账户？

Jordov: And one — actually, because we're talking about China again, one thing I forgot to ask is about the fact that China also shut down leveraged gold and silver trading, but then still encouraged the gold accumulation accounts. How do you explain that?

Macleod: Well, it's an interesting one, because what she's done is she's stopped speculating in the domestic market. That tells me — because for every buyer, you know, in futures for every buyer, you got a seller — yeah. So they got to wind it down.

If they're going to put the yuan onto a gold standard, that does imply that the gold price is going to go through the roof — because of the consequence for the dollar, if you like. It's not gold going up so much, but it's the dollar going down.

So this would absolutely create a financial crisis in the Shanghai Futures Exchange. And that's why, obviously, the banks have been told: "Look, once this current contract ends, don't open new positions, and don't let your customers, your clients, open new positions." Instead — I mean, it's interesting because at the same time, I think it was ICBC reduced its charges on the gold accumulation account. I think it reduced them down to 0.2% or something, I think, from probably about 25 basis points.

银行在为爆发做准备

Anyway, this is a thing which is interesting because we have seen that gold imports into China have increased quite substantially this year so far. Now this, to me, is the major Chinese banks accumulating gold positions in the anticipation of greater demand for their gold accumulation accounts.

So this sort of investment — which is investment, not speculation — is being encouraged by the authorities. You have to ask yourself: why are they doing this? I think they're doing this for one simple reason. They can see that the gold price is going to go up very substantially as a result of fixing — or, if you like, even speculation that the yuan will be fixed to gold.

Because what's happening is the paper side of these transactions is collapsing in its value. And you've only got to see what's going on, as you've mentioned Hormuz — you've only got to see what's going on there and understand that the Americans have lost the war. Understand that they're incapable of admitting it. Understand that therefore this is going to continue through to the end of the year and probably beyond.

Then you can see that the US economy is facing a slump because of the energy crisis. And not only that, but because inevitably the Fed will print, print to stop markets collapsing, to bail out the zombies and all the rest of it — whatever — you can see that they're going to destroy their currency.

That's what the Chinese — you don't need to be the brain of Beijing to understand that that is the outlook. So no wonder they're getting the hell out of the dollar by buying everything they can possibly think they might need for the next 10 years. You know, silver, gold, copper, nickel, whatever. Sulfuric acid — well, they're not; they just stopped exporting those things: sulfuric acid, helium, and nitrates and all the rest of it.

No, this is — they're just protecting themselves. We might think that they're being antagonistic by withholding supplies from us, but no, they're just protecting themselves because they don't want our money. Well, what we think is money, but actually is credit — and therefore suffers from our rating. How we are rated. But China is obviously not rating us very highly.

十二、两个平行的金融系统：一个死去，一个崛起

Jordov: It's very interesting to see then — if I resume it to the grand scheme of things: on the one side in the West, the paper market is collapsing, so this whole system of suppressing what ought to be price discovery is rather price control, with all the conversation that we had before. And meantime the East is building a whole infrastructure of nodes with SGE and so on so forth to be able to attract the physical.

So we've already seen the divide also between the price of the East and the West have been some large premiums, sometimes exceeding 20 dollars an ounce for the silver price and more. So the natural flow of things would be then that the gold, let's say, from the West would actually also start moving East, because that's where the buyers are. Meanwhile the West is implementing taxes, financial controls, and these kinds of things to actually keep the gold — well, through confiscation maybe, even, or other means. So then we would have like a two-tier system then.

Macleod: Yeah. Well, it's been developing this way for some considerable time. And as I said earlier, it's not just a question of all the gold going east, but now the market is as well. That's where the future of the future market is.

And I mean, we see effectively that what's being signaled is the end of the American empire. And I think that the crisis in the dollar could actually bring it to an end very, very rapidly. And it will also undermine us in Europe too.

I mean, we haven't got a hope really once the dollar goes. I'm not sure how many hard-money men there are left in the Bundesbank. But again, you see, okay, they've got a lot of their gold back — but where's the gold in the New York Fed? The indications are that it's not really there.

I think the Netherlands did jolly well to get their gold back. I think they got about a thousand tons back. But I mean, that was on the back of such a bad stink over how the Bundesbank was treated that it was realized, I think, that "we've got to hush this one up." So give the Netherlands back their gold. I think it was about a thousand tons, wasn't it? Something like that.

Jordov: I can't recall exactly, but I trust —

Macleod: I tell you, your nation was very lucky — very lucky to get its gold back. Others less so.

Jordov: Well, I'm more German than Dutch, but yeah, I live in the Netherlands, so practically I am Dutch by now. I even have a Dutch accent, I get told sometimes. And being born and raised in France, I take this as an insult to have a Dutch accent over French accent. But yeah —

Macleod: Your English is very good, very good, Alex.

Jordov: Thank you. Yeah, I try to hide the French influence in that.

Macleod: No reason why you should. I mean, French culture, you know — I mean, all the European nations have got wonderful cultures and all the rest of it. But culture isn't enough, I'm afraid. Sound money is required as well.

Jordov: It is, it is.

十三、如何保护自己的资产？远离银行、跨司法管辖权

Jordov: I mentioned more about — I mean, I can talk like this with a very sick French accent and be very understandable, that's what I mean by that. But to conclude: so what would be, let's say, your recommendations for people who want to store and be safe from any potential risk of confiscation? I've just mentioned this scenario where the West could actually start — and we've seen the first proof of this in many different levels, that I don't want to go too much into details with, but the trend is there.

And thereby the question is then: do you then store it in Switzerland in the Swiss jurisdiction, or even maybe in Asia, probably in Shanghai, or perhaps even Hong Kong? Or how do you see this?

Macleod: Yeah, I mean I think the important thing is not to store it in a bank. Not to have a bank involved. I think that's the key thing.

And, you know, I'm also a strategic adviser to Von Greyerz, who have private vaults in Switzerland, and I think that it does make so much sense. I don't know that you can really 100% guarantee that your gold and silver will be beyond the reach of every government, but you can get as close as you can to it.

Also, in the past I was associated with GoldMoney, and I still write for them once a week. And that is more retail — a retail market. And there you've got a number of different jurisdictions. So what you can do is you can have it in a proper insured vault in a jurisdiction different from where you live, which makes it harder for your government, if you like, to go through the hoops to get you — to steal your gold off you. Which, I mean, we've got to think like this, which I think is crazy.

I'm not saying you break the law. You just make it slightly harder for governments to implement that law against you.

Von Greyerz is really for the high-net-worth individuals and institutions, and we're talking about really very, very big money, privately owned and all the rest of it. So it's not even part of, let's say, an LBMA system. Because one of your problems is that the LBMA could well be leant on by the Americans through the British, if you like, to give all the details of who owns what in all the vaults. I don't rule that out. I wouldn't rule that out. And once they do that, of course, they can then start leaning on Americans in this case, or whatever. And then you're getting into all sorts of murky waters.

So this is difficult, this is difficult. And I think you've got to pay attention to keeping it out of the banking system, keeping it out of your jurisdiction. And if you are very, very wealthy, you can go into a private porting system such as Von Greyerz. And I think that is the ultimate, if you like, in terms of protection. So anyway, that's my view, and history suggests that that is the way you should look at it.

Jordov: And if you don't have like a couple of 100,000 to invest on Von Greyerz, you can also go at goldrepublic.com.

Macleod: Absolutely, yeah.

Jordov: We also have a vault in Switzerland. But yes, indeed it's — it depends. You always have to do your due diligence, if it's GoldRepublic or anyone else. Always see also in terms of being the importance of being the legal owner of your investment as well. And always make your due diligence about who runs the organization, how transparent they are, how frequent they do the reports, if it's possible to also visit the vaults and all that. I think it's very important, no matter where you buy your gold or silver.

Macleod: I think desperately important. This is a business which has expanded quite recently, quite sharply, as the gold price has gone up from what, just over a thousand in 2016 to 5,000 recently. Undoubtedly, you've got a lot of crooks entering into this business. You've got to be very, very careful who you deal with. And if it comes to storage — very careful how that is stored. Yeah, absolutely.

结束语

Jordov: On this note, Alasdair, always a delight to be the recipient of your precious knowledge and time. Thanks a lot for all your takes. And I remind to everyone who doesn't know — or just as a refresher — you write a Substack. I'm subscribed since many, many years. I always love reading them every week. MacleodFinance on Substack. And you're also active on X. That would be for the place that people can find Alasdair.

Alasdair, thanks a lot, and I hope to speak to you very soon again.

Macleod: Thank you very much for having me, Alex. Here.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、真正在崩塌的不是"美元"，而是"用纸面定价物质"的整个文明范式
大多数评论把这场变局讲成"东方囤金 vs 西方抛售"的地缘博弈，但 Macleod 无意间揭示了一个更深的裂缝：**LBMA 与 COMEX 本质上不是"黄金市场"，而是"信用市场穿着黄金的外衣"**——银行家不关心金属本身，他们经营的是"以美元计价的合约"。所以当美元的定价能力枯竭时，垮掉的不是黄金供需，而是"用一种可以凭空创造的东西去为一种从地里挖出来的东西定价"这套心智结构。
这里有一个让人意外的观察：**COMEX 未平仓合约跌到十几年新低，并不是投机者不玩了，而是做市银行主动撤出——他们在悄悄闭店**。也就是说，西方黄金定价权的死亡，不是被东方"夺走"的，而是被西方交易员**自己关灯走人**的。这和 2008 年前投行悄悄关闭 CDO 台子的模式惊人相似：市场往往不是被击败的，是被内部人先一步放弃的。
## 二、中国真正在打的牌不是"金本位"，而是奥地利学派意义上的 "crackup boom"（崩溃性繁荣）
Macleod 用了一个几乎没人在中文财经语境里讨论的概念：**crackup boo

item.title: [Bits + Bips] How to Prove a Token Is Really Backed
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个耐人寻味的错位

最反直觉的观察：这段转录内容与视频标题构成了一个绝妙的"隐喻性错配"。标题问的是"如何证明一个代币是真的有资产背书"，但转录内容却在讲——"看太阳，看它周围的黑暗，那是因为没有大气层"。这看似是剪辑错误或无关插播，但它意外地成为整个加密资产证明问题最精准的隐喻。

跨领域连接：真空中的证明学

在真空里看太阳，你能看到它真实的样子——没有大气散射，没有蓝光弥漫，黑白对比极端锐利。而地球上的太阳观察者永远看到的是"被介质修饰过的太阳"。

这与稳定币储备证明的核心困境惊人地对应： - 地球视角（当前审计模式）：所有的储备证明都要透过"信任介质"——审计事务所、托管银行、法律实体。你看到的太阳是被大气折射后的太阳，你看到的储备是被中介翻译后的储备。 - 月球视角（链上证明的理想态）：Proof of Reserves 的原教旨主义者想要的，正是"去掉大气层"——用密码学 Merkle Tree、zk-proof 直接观察资产本体。黑就是黑，亮就是亮，没有中间层。

Tether、USDC 之争的本质，其实是关于观察者所处的位置——你在地球上还是在月球上决定了你能相信什么。

隐藏的系统真相：证明的悖论

真正让我震撼的洞察是——一个代币越"需要"证明它有背书，恰恰说明这个证明本身就是可疑的。真正的信任系统（比如比特币的 UTXO）从不需要"证明"——它的存在本身就是它的证明。而所有需要外部审计来证明储备的稳定币，都在结构上等同于"需要论证太阳存在的太阳"——这个论证行为本身，就暴露了它不是自明的。

这与哥德尔不完备定理形成奇妙呼应：一个系统无法在自身内部证明自身的完备性。稳定币的储备证明永远是"系统外"的——它引入了审计师、银行、法律，也就永远无法成为纯粹的加密原生真相。

留白的追问：如果这段转录真的是视频开头的一句"引子"（很多财经播客喜欢用宇宙类比开场），那么主持人可能正准备说：储备证明就像看太阳——大多数人满足于地球视角的"看起来是亮的"，但真正的加密精神要求你走到月球，用眼睛直视那颗恒星。

📝 转录文本

开场引子：真空中的观察

If you can see the sun in the frame, look at it.

Look at how black the sky is in the middle of a clear day.

Look at how bright the sun is and how it blooms.

That's because there's no atmosphere.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Kalshi's Tarek Mansour: Chaos by Design
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的洞察：这家公司的组织结构，就是它所在市场的镜像

Kalshi 是一个"预测市场"——本质上是把创新的野心和监管的克制放在同一张订单簿里做市。而 Tarek 和 Luana 的"共同 CEO + 永久性分歧"结构，几乎是这个市场机制的人形化：一个天生做风险经理（Tarek 的黎巴嫩童年 + 母亲的偏执），一个是"非理性乐观主义者"（Luana 的信念驱动）。

他们说这是"设计好的分歧"（disagreement by design），但真正的洞察在于：Kalshi 的组织架构与其产品的定价机制是同构的。预测市场的价格是多空双方持续博弈得到的边际共识，Kalshi 的公司决策也是通过两个持续对冲的创始人得到的边际共识。他们不是在"运营"一家做预测市场的公司——他们把自己变成了一个内部的预测市场。这解释了为什么这种"混乱"能扩展：因为价格发现机制本来就是混乱的，但它收敛。

二、跨领域连接：Tarek 的"共振频率"概念，其实是把物理学偷渡进了营销学

大多数创始人谈营销是在谈漏斗、CAC、A/B。Tarek 反复用的词是 resonant frequency（共振频率）——梅西退役前两天投广告、Chalamet 事件 12 小时后上线、Giannis 宣布不离队后一天官宣代言。这不是"营销时机好"，这是受迫振动理论：外部世界持续在震荡（新闻周期、meme 生命周期），只有当你的信号频率与世界的固有频率对齐时，才能产生振幅放大。错过 12 小时，共振腔就塌了。

更令人意外的是——这个物理模型解释了为什么"聘请一个专业营销主管"反而会失败。因为专业营销总监的流程（3 周计划、法务审阅、创意评审）本质上是一个低通滤波器，会滤掉所有高频信号。而共振捕获恰恰发生在高频段。Tarek 的品牌负责人以前是"反 Cramer"推特账号运营者——一个天生活在高频段的人。这不是招人品味，这是信号处理选型。

跨领域的类比还可以推得更远：这个"共振营销"模型和 Renaissance Technologies 的高频交易策略是同一件事——都是在利用市场（无论是注意力市场还是股票市场）的短周期低效。Kalshi 表面上是消费品牌，骨子里是量化交易团队在做营销。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：他们的商业模式让他们必须"反社交媒体"

这是整段访谈里最容易被忽略的一句话："在 Kalshi 上，用户的损失不流向我。我抽 1% 手续费，无论谁赢。"

赌场的商业模型是 revenue = customer losses，所以它必须在长期系统性地培养损失行为——这与推特/TikTok 的算法逻辑同构：它们的收入 = 用户被剥夺的时间与注意力。赌场和社交媒体本质上是同一门生意的两种形态——都靠制造上瘾牟利，都要屏蔽"聪明用户"（赌场屏蔽算牌者，社交媒体降权深度内容）。

而 Kalshi 的商业模型是 revenue ∝ liquidity ∝ smart traders。它越赚钱，就必须越多地奖励做研究、看新闻、校准概率的人。这是一个反脑腐（anti-brainrot）的经济激励。所以当 Tarek 说"孩子们在 Kalshi 上是在变聪明"，这不是营销话术——这是他的目标函数决定的。他能否兑现取决于监管和执行，但激励方向确实指向那里。

这个洞察的深层含义是：判断一个平台是否会长期毒害用户，不要看它的 UI 或成瘾机制，看它的收入方程里是否包含"用户损失"这一项。 Kalshi 是罕见的、结构上与用户长期利益对齐的高频交易场所——这可能是 Sequoia 押它的真正原因，而不是 TAM。

四、彩蛋洞察：他和母亲、和 Luana 的关系是同一个函数的两个采样

Tarek 说母亲的口头禅是"总能找到答案，撞南墙也要撞出个洞"，Luana 的口头禅是"不管多不合理，只要给足够时间就会成真"。这是同一种世界观的两种表达——一个通过穷尽所有失败路径来抵达真相，一个通过忽略所有失败可能来抵达真相。而 Tarek 自己站在这两者之间，用扑克玩家的语言（expected value vs. outcome）把它翻译成了金融风控。

创业者常问"我该学谁"。Tarek 的答案更深：他不是学人，他是在他成长环境里两个最强意志的向量之间做角度扫描，最终找到了自己的方位角。这提示了一个反常识的规律：你最好的"CEO 模板"不是硅谷偶像，是你童年里那两三个最强意志的人——因为你已经在他们身上跑了 20 年的 A/B 测试。

📝 转录文本

Cold Open: Why Kalshi Exists

The thing that was unique about us is we never pivoted. It was always Kalshi. I mean, Kalshi is everything in Arabic. Like, we always wanted to build the everything exchange — exchange for all these different assets. And we didn't build Kalshi to build a company. We built a company to build Kalshi. It's a bit different.

I see.

We were dragged by the idea. I wasn't the type that wanted to be an entrepreneur. Like, I think if you reroll the dice multiple times, I'd probably just be a trader or risk manager. Like, I would not be an entrepreneur.

Got it.

But the idea was so glaring in front of us that we just had to do it. Like, we felt that we had to do it. And so the whole point of doing this whole thing is the idea.

Host Intro

Hey everybody. Today we have Taran from Kalshi. He is a gem. This is a really good episode. They walked through the desert for many, many years in this company and went sideways. Then they made a very risky bet to sue the federal government and won. And this thing has ripped ever since. It's growing, you know, similar rates to OpenAI and Anthropic, but a few years behind. I think you're going to like it. I'll be back at the end for my takeaways.

The Co-CEO Model: Disagreement by Design

We just met for the first time a couple weeks ago at David Solomon's house, and it was a CEO dinner — and you had some very unusual ideas about CEOing. This is why I wanted to have you on the pod.

Yeah.

And so I want to get into those. Let's just start with your co-founder.

Yeah.

It sounded like it's a semi co-CEO relationship. How do you two run the company? How do you split it up?

Yeah, it's interesting. Over the years we've constantly been asked this question, and we never really had a very clean answer to this, and we always thought it was sort of like a weakness — and our perspective on that has changed. I mean, dramatically. I actually think whatever model works for us works for us. Great. You know, and then if you figure that out, then —

The best way to describe it is: I operate at the very high-level strategy — make sure that we're going in the right direction. Where's the world heading? Where are our rights to win? And just thinking, a lot of thinking, continuously. And then I'm very, very low in the details.

Okay. You're high and low.

Yeah. Extremely high.

Head in the sky, feet in the ground.

Yeah. But very, very low. I'm talking like coding.

Yeah. And more specifically, like marketing and the copy and the details, and everything around how we're marketing something.

Yeah. How we're talking about the product. All these little things that the end consumer — whether it's someone looking at a billboard or someone who's in the product — is going to experience. What are they going to feel? And everything else that is actually running the company is Luana.

Okay.

That's basically — I think one of the best ways to describe it. And then I do the external stuff: the regulatory, the policy, the fundraise, all the other external aspects of the job that tied to marketing and sales. But the day-to-day running of the company — making sure that the company is actually run — is Luana.

How Founder Disagreement Becomes a Feature

How do you disagree about stuff — you must disagree about stuff?

Disagree a lot, like all the time.

So how does that play out?

I actually think we kind of disagree by design. Like, we have this dynamic — over time it's become a thing where we essentially will always take the opposite side of the argument.

Are you a contrarian?

I'm the guy in the room that will be sort of the negative — everybody's pumped about a plan, I'm like, "Oh, here's how it's going to fail and all the issues I have." But we tend to just disagree with each other often. And I think — people say, "How do you disagree?" and continuous disagreement is an anti-pattern. But for us it ended up being a pattern, because we're this very complicated company that has this constant, continuous, delicate balancing between how much risk to take on the regulatory front and innovation. And you have to always balance those two things out.

You see now in AI — safety and innovation, all of that. That debate is a continuous force to drive us to the middle. We end up getting — you know, not always the right answer, but we're not too far from the right answer, and we never oscillate between the two extremes. And I think that's one of the powers of having two equally powerful co-founders in an organization. Because if you're the only one, anyone that reports to you — anyone that you hire that is not a founder — it's inevitable that they won't always tell you the truth. It's inevitable. No matter what culture you build, etc., there's a different incentive structure than someone who's a founder that owns as much equity as you. And so you can stray. Right? And here it's very hard for either of us to stray.

So I talked to Luana to prepare for the interview. It sounds like you're kind of the more conservative of the two.

Yeah.

And maybe you're on the regulatory side and she's on the innovation side. Is that the way it sort of plays out?

More often than not, yeah.

[Luana] is very faith-based, like optimist. Sometimes I would say, like, super, incredibly, irrationally optimistic. She sometimes doesn't really do the math on how something is going to work out — on why it's going to work out. And that's continuously happened at Kalshi. And that used to frustrate — I mean, for years, it frustrated me. I was like, this doesn't make any sense. And I'm the other side: I'm a risk manager. I'm paranoid. I have a paranoid demeanor — which is also usually an anti-pattern for a founder. I think about risk and expected values. I think probabilistically about the world, and here's all the things that could go wrong.

I think that tension has led to all the decisions that we've made over time. But it's interesting, because Luana's approach to things — I now believe that, given enough time horizon, it's going to end up being true no matter how irrational it sounds. It's kind of amazing. But I think my side is the thing that — for a company that has to get regulatory right, you have to make sure that you're doing things the right way. I'm kind of that forcing function in the company. And we land somewhere in the middle, where we've managed to achieve the results we've achieved.

I'm actually very bad at a lot of things. It's actually shocking. Like, if you see my day-to-day —

Because you're obsessive. You're very good at a couple things.

Exactly.

A lot of founders are obsessive.

Yeah. But I'm kind of a procrastinator. Like, I'm so disorganized. If people that work around me — you ask them — it's just such a shit show. But Luana orchestrates everything.

She's organized.

Very. And I'm like the opposite of what you imagine for an operator. But I can get so obsessed with certain things that I want to get good at that I can get good at pretty fast. So I don't think I'm a good marketer — I just obsessed about it for long enough that —

That's how I work with my co-founder. He's very obsessive. He goes very deep down a rabbit hole and you can't get him out of that rabbit hole.

Growing Up in Beirut: The Origin of the Risk-Manager Brain

You've had a long, strange trip growing up in Beirut. Certainly an unusual childhood. Is the risk aversion sort of rooted in growing up in such an unusual place?

For sure. Most things that end up sticking with you are really how you grew up. This definitely applies here. So I grew up with a single mom, and I grew up in Lebanon. And in Lebanon — I feel like oftentimes the headlines make it sound like —

Sounds like a constant war zone if you read the New York Times.

Yeah — it's not. It's actually more of a volatile country. Like, the word is volatility. It's a lot of ups, a lot of downs. You can have a lot of fun, great life, and then all of a sudden you're running from —

So the mix of those two. My mom was paranoid. She was always afraid. We would take a lot of issues that would been thrown at us to the extreme and we would carry them. And I was really young, and I'm the eldest child. We're two brothers. And that doesn't leave you. So my brain is wired to think about all the ways that something is going to go wrong. If I get on a hot air balloon, I can list you immediately —

Like, any — yeah. On a flight too. Like, I can list you immediately all the 20 ways this thing is going to go south.

But it's important for our company. We're a company that has financial risk involved, regulatory — all these different things. That has helped us over time really become very hyper-attuned to what exactly is our risk in a variety of different ways, and where we can take risk and where we can't.

His Mother: The Tiger Mom and the "Everything Is in Your Control" Worldview

Okay. I've heard you on other pods bring your mom up. Can you just give us some more paragraphs on her?

Oh, it's interesting. She's very — she's definitely a tiger mom.

Rip on your mom.

So, tiger mom, very intense.

You are too.

Yeah, I am very intense too. Yeah. But — my way or the highway, uncompromising, completely. And then it was a sort of thing where — I think she had to basically sacrifice a lot of her life. Because my dad left us at age six, and she had to sacrifice a lot of her career — everything she wanted to do — to basically make sure that we're okay. And she wanted us to have the best life we could and come back to America. So I was born in California, went to Lebanon, and she wanted to come back and get to a top school, etc.

And so what happened is, I think a lot of her personal pride ended up being attributed to her kids. Like, "My kids' success are going to be what I deliver in life." And so her bar was so high on us. Everything — we would do a sport, go play tennis or play soccer, and you have to win. And if you come silver, it's bad. So that creates a hyper-competitive nature. But also there's this feeling of, "There's always an answer." Whenever we had a financial issue, you don't give up — you bang your head against the wall, you go up, you go left, you go right, you go down, it doesn't really matter — you're going to figure something out.

All of that, decades later, is all in me. And it matters, because there's this higher gear. Most people have this thing of, "Well, there's certain things that are within my control and certain things are not." I don't really believe in that dichotomy.

Okay.

I think it depends on the person. Some people can just make a significantly larger subset of the universe of things that are —

I feel like it can make things happen, maybe irrational.

Yeah. You can bring more things into the control.

Some people just think, "Okay, I can do it."

Yeah.

And the scale of the problem could even become big. Elon has a little bit of it, right? Like a lot.

I feel like Elon sometimes — as long as it doesn't break the laws of physics, we probably can make it happen. It's our control.

No Playbook: The Rejection of the CEO Manual

Yes.

We're going to get into more CEO stuff. We just heard Ken Griffin from Citadel speak at this conference we're at, and he talked about picking best practices from different companies and sort of assembling his CEO playbook from other people. He learned from Jack Welch, etc. Do you look up to one CEO? Are you following a playbook, or are you just — I'm going to make it up as I go?

I have a lot of "I'm going to make it up as I go." And I think this applies to Luana too. Both of us were probably very entrepreneurially illiterate. Like, we haven't read all the books. We haven't watched all the podcasts.

You don't live on Twitter.

I do Twitter a bunch just because a lot of our users are on Twitter. But not like in the sort of "here are the four steps to build a company" sense.

Okay. You're not in that ecosystem.

But I have — there's a lot of aspects of CEOs I look up to. The extreme boldness of Elon, his ability to attract talent, is amazing. I think Tony Xu — I always go back to, because his operational discipline and execution is honestly one of the best. His business is so hard.

So hard. So is yours, by the way.

Yeah, ours is hard — marketplace and all these issues. But those are two names that consistently come to mind. And then there's — the Collisons have done such a great job at creating this sort of brand aura around their company to attract incredibly talented people to build something that just goes beyond what the company is exactly building. So I observe a lot of these different things and figure out what I can take.

But then after that, it's continuous iteration. What we do well is we just try, and we try fast, and we're continuously like: "Did this work? Didn't work? Okay, change it fast." Whether it's a hire, whether it's an approach, whether it's my own voice — whatever. And that's the best way to find the right answer. Because oftentimes these things are very — I'm going to say company-dependent, but as I was saying it, it's very CEO- or founder-dependent. It's to your image. It's very hard to market even your product if it's not coming truly from who you are. Because the company is going to be to your image, no matter what you do.

Why Not Silicon Valley?

And your headquarters is in New York. Do you think if you were based here you would be more in the mold of every other company?

Probably. I also think it would have been harder because —

You would talk yourself out of some of your crazy ideas.

Yeah, maybe to some extent. The harder piece I was thinking is just recruiting. One of the reasons we moved — what I like about New York is, for our company's mission, which is figuring out how to build this idea of infinite market, the next generation exchange, fundamentally rebuilding Wall Street — this new Wall Street that is more global, more for the people — it has a stronger market fit with the talent in New York. Much better. And there I felt confident we could hire the top 1% of talent. Whereas here — maybe, but I don't see it. It doesn't feel like it burns as cleanly. I think here it's really AI right now.

Even if — I mean, we didn't necessarily know that in 2020, but even if it wasn't AI, it was going to be something that is not finance.

130 Direct Reports and Chaos by Design

Okay. You and Luana have, I think, 130 sort of direct reports, if I got that about right.

Yeah, yeah, pretty much. There's some functions that we sort of let them do what they do, but pretty much most of the company reports to between the two of us.

Okay, that's unusual.

Yeah, it's pretty unusual.

It's unusual because it's small, and it's unusual because it's zero hierarchy, and it's unusual because they report to both of you. How does it actually work? How is it not chaos?

It's kind of chaotic.

Okay.

But I think you could build an organization that's somewhat okay with that. Because what you get out of chaos is continuous, constant adaptability. It's very easy for a company to adapt. Very easy. Sam talked about pivoting — pivoting feels like a bit of a strong word, but the point is you want to constantly reorient and reassemble around the biggest challenges or biggest opportunities for the company, and you want to be able to do that with no friction. That's inherently chaotic.

How do you —

It's a complex system. It's like organisms and cells are sort of moving around and floating in the ether, and then they — you know what I mean? The world is an inherently chaotic system. We're trying to impose structure that is unnatural given how the world is moving, and in a world that's accelerating over time.

You kind of have to be more sensitive to the fact that it's increasingly more chaotic. And so your structure needs to be as adaptable as possible.

Okay, this is sort of the crux of the podcast — because the CEO playbook that I grew up with, handed down through the generations, I think is getting a big rethink. And Brian Armstrong and Jack Dorsey are talking a lot about this new way to organize where it's not a pyramid, it's a circle. AI's in the middle. You're training the AI. All your systems are legible. Your people are feeding the AI, and the AI makes more and more decisions. Org charts gone. Compensation's different. All the things are getting thrown out. Are you following along with any of that, or are you just kind of making it up as you go?

Making it up as we go. I didn't even know about that. I never heard about that.

Adapting Faster Than the World Changes

One of the things I've learned, by the way, about all this — I've interviewed all these CEOs — there's no one way to do it. The way Jensen does it is totally different than Elon. It's totally different from —

Yeah. Imagine Jensen running Citadel.

Yeah, totally.

That would be nuts. Or, even funnier — imagine Ken getting dropped into OpenAI to run it. It would be so crazy.

But I'm actually curious to hear your thoughts. The world is more interconnected than ever. And things are accelerating — the rate of learning, the rate of change. If it used to be, "You have to readjust fundamentally every three to four years," then every year — I honestly think you have to readjust fundamentally every month or two.

I kind of agree. I'm learning so much faster than I used to learn. I'm picking up skills so much faster than I used to.

Yeah. The world has changed like January to today. It's so crazy. It's hard for me to imagine making a certain set of decisions in January that truly applies still today.

What Do You Even Present to the Board?

Okay. So how do you plan dynamically? Right. Okay. But it's the end of December, you got a board meeting, Alfred Lin's on your board. What do you actually even present to the board?

Yeah. It's kind of hazy a bit. The board used to be like, "Oh, it's not structured enough." But now I think they're kind of used to it.

What is it?

It's usually — look, it's a good question.

I tend to not have too much structure. And another thing at Kalshi: we're not super metrics-y.

Okay. In general — I thought you'd be the opposite.

No, we're metrics-y very hardcore on things like risk — how much margin we're giving to customers — things that go directly into the product. But in terms of decision-making, we should be a little bit more, and we will probably get there a little bit more over time. But it's really more like: What are the customers saying? What are the pockets of demand where the energy is? And where can we basically be first, or be most aggressive, or have a right to win? That's all the conversation.

And then there's usually one or two key challenges that tend to be the single most important challenges in the organization. Let me give you the last maybe 18 months. Off of the election, all the conversations: What are we going to do about elections? How are you going to cross-sell people? Are they going to stick?

Yep.

The Compounding Growth Chain: Elections → Network Effect → Brokers → Direct

What we said at the time is: Let's prove that this network effect engine now that we have — we had a massive growth catalyst — that it is going to create the chain reaction. Let's figure out how to make sure that people are adopting other products, and get going. And that worked. We really created that network effect, where we used that new demand to attract more liquidity, which boosted all the other markets, and then that helped us get demand at a lower cost. And then we basically compounded there.

Then after that it was essentially: Okay, we need to prove our broker strategy is working. So we had the direct-to-consumer that was growing pretty well, but we wanted to make this bet that — becoming an infrastructure layer like all traditional financial markets, that we enable a bunch of brokers to basically go and — could we prove that we can do both at once? And that one was going a little bit slow. So the next six months were like: scaling that. We got a bunch of brokers that really started working out.

Then the next challenge was actually proving that we're not too dependent on the brokers.

Okay, 'cause those became — worked too well.

They became 80% of our revenue. So all of a sudden it's like, oh, amazing — the revenue ripped, but now you're super dependent on a bunch of your big broker partners. So what are you going to do now? It's like, okay, now we have to go back to focus on the direct, and really scale that, now that we have an extremely liquid ecosystem because the brokers brought more demand, more liquidity. The whole thing is flowing now. Now the brokers are like 10% of our volume.

Okay. All the way back to 10.

Going Mainstream Invites Society's Reckoning

Yeah. So, that — and I want to talk a little bit about this adaptability thing — because all of these were — we're talking about going from elections, then sports, then we got into financials, then you went to consumer business, then a B2B enterprise motion, and we went back and we did all these in the span of like eight months. And then it was a lot of the policy stuff, and we should talk about that a little bit. But — you're like exciting, exciting, exciting, all the way till you get mainstream. And when you get mainstream, society all of a sudden is like, "Whoa, whoa, stop, stop right there. Let's figure out what this is. We don't understand it." Now you have the grandmas and the —

I feel like that's going on right now.

Yes, exactly. It's been going on for six months. But in AI, AI is battling with it. Like Airbnb, Uber, all of them at some point you get a reckoning. And now — number one priority since January — and I think we've done a great job at positioning ourselves as a safe, responsible actor. But — particularly right now after all the news about Polymarket recently — I think we've done a really good job at always being kind of regulatory-first, do the right thing, but we had to kind of tell that story. And that's also hard and comes with all these challenges.

But think about that. It's completely different functions. Even if I build the best consumer marketing company, how is that going to help me with the brokers? And if I build the best sales function, how is it going to help me — and so it goes back to: I think, okay, how do we solve these problems? Generally, there needs to be at least one founder in any of these big, meaty problems that we need to get right. I really strongly, affirmatively strongly believe in that.

The DRI Model and the "Hole in the Ship" Analogy

And so is there one of you that's the DRI on these big hairy issues? Okay.

The responsibility — the person that is going to get grilled — is one of Luana and I on these top issues, these things that we need to get right. And I cannot — and I may be wrong, and over time as the company gets more mature, I may change. So maybe in a few years we should have another chat and see what I think.

But in this growth phase, my analogy is a bit — and honestly this applies to any company at any scale. I really really think so. It's like you're on this big ship and you're driving the ship as fast as you can. You're trying to win this — but there's always a hole in the ship, and that hole is leaking. There's water coming out of it.

And I think there's two types of organizations. One where the CEO or the founders are straight-up staring at the hole all day, every day — which is brutal. It's painful. It's like —

That was me.

Yeah. It's you, right? I think you talked about that. And the other type of organization sort of either gives it to someone else. Generally, what that someone is going to do — it's very painful. Why would they take through all that pain? You know how painful it is, right? It's constant pain. It's essentially continuous torture. And so they add a rug on top of the hole and — "We're good, things are growing." But inevitably that hole at some point is going to start sinking the ship. Inevitably. Whether it's your Google and AI is coming after you, or you're an AI and 90% of America hates you, or — you know what I mean.

What Hole Are You Staring at Right Now?

What's your hole now? What hole have you got?

I think in January what I would say our big hole was: We need to differentiate ourselves with the unregulated stuff, and the insider trading issue. Because you've always taken it seriously, but — you poll people and they have no idea what the difference is between the regulated onshore prediction markets (like Kalshi and Robinhood and others) and some like Poly and others that were doing it offshore. And that was really bad for the industry. So we had to kind of clean up the industry's reputation. And I would say we've made a significant amount of progress, but that hole is not fully covered yet. We still have work to do in terms of explaining that: Look, there is the — the right solution to all these things is: get it regulated, do it the right way, and there are people doing it the right way. And here's how. Like with all things, right? The right solution for AI is: you don't decelerate AI, you add the right guardrails. And I think figuring out what those guardrails are, and figuring out how to communicate it effectively, figure out why they're the right ones — is the hole. And that's where I spend pretty much 80% of my time in right now.

On Polymarket, Competition, and Abundance

Okay. The hole is an interesting spot. Your competitor has been called out by the Wall Street Journal for their marketing practices, and Congress is taking up money on it. You're used to all this stuff. Is this thing that's happening to Polymarket at this moment good for you, or bad for you?

I think it's bad for us.

I think it is too.

Yeah, for sure. When you start competing, you realize competition is actually really never a real problem. I really think so. Unless you're in a small market — I guess maybe that's a problem. But if you're in a small market, then you figure out the next big market, and figure out how to go take it and split it with the competition. It's okay.

We used to always say at HubSpot — we competed with Salesforce.com — and every time Salesforce would move the ship a little bit, everybody in the company was like, "We got to move the ship in the exact same direction." And I would say, "Every time they zig, we got to zag."

Yeah. There's always — let me say it maybe a slightly more nuanced way. I don't think competition will ever be a true company killer. If you're in a big market, you're going to have a big piece of the pie. And if you're not, you should probably be constantly iterating and shipping product and finding new untapped markets to basically go and win in. I believe in abundance. It's hard not to believe in abundance, right? Look at the last five years. It's crazy.

Why Being Slow and Regulated Won

And so you were well behind them, and you've — from what I can tell — past them, a lot of it in sports. Really crushing that.

We're bigger than — we're like in the US, 95% market share now.

Okay. Across the board.

How'd you do it? I mean — you were way behind.
