It's interesting. We were way behind in the headlines, maybe. So we started the company in 2018 — it's been a long time. But we didn't re-launch the general public until end of '22. And I think our proper launch was the end of '24, when we were allowed to do the markets that people really want, which is the election and other markets. And they launched in 2019 — a year later, or started the company — and then they launched immediately without the license. So they were the brand that sort of did it. But we were very dogmatic in our approach, which was: we're going to do it the right way.

Why?

I think there's two reasons. One that is practical: I think in financial services and healthcare, I don't think you can cut too many corners. I don't really believe in that. In Silicon Valley it's like "move fast and break things and figure out how to regulate later." Not in financial services. In financial services, inevitably things go wrong, and when they go wrong, they go wrong bad. If you want to build true mainstream adoption and true institutional adoption — these people will care. How are you doing things? Are you regulated? So that was one.

Number two is honestly a bit more philosophical. I was excited about changing the system fundamentally — building the next generation New York Stock Exchange from within, changing the system no matter how hard it is — than going offshore and building something on the side. That was just not exciting to me. I didn't feel pumped about doing that. So we stayed dogmatic about that approach. But what we were doing is building an incredibly great product, and great infrastructure, and great regulatory infrastructure — the word "trust." I think people trust us more. When they put their money, they feel good about putting money with us. They feel good. There's not a —

I put some money on the Red Sox last night, and they beat the Yankees.

Nice. Great.

When we did get regulated and we got approved, our trajectory just went really parabolic. And I think it's a mix of that foundation-laying that we've done for a while — which I really believed in. If you believe that you're going towards a good market, building a solid foundation, even if you're not growing, is okay. And then I think we just have superior execution on product and growth. We compound at a much, much faster rate. And give it enough time, the results start showing.

Walking Through the Desert

While you're there — it's 2018, start the company. You wandered through the desert for a long, long time.

Very long time. Yeah.

CFTC was smacking you, saying no.

Yeah. The government was trying to kill us.

Yes. It must have been brutal. What was the lowest point?

Honestly, I don't even remember. It was truly just — it was bad. And it's like you're constantly in a state of grief, and then you're in this desert and you start seeing fake —

Mirages.

Mirages. Yeah. You see something — "Oh, here it is finally." And then it's not true, and it's disheartening, and it's just hard.

But the thing that was unique about us is we never pivoted. It was always Kalshi. Kalshi is everything in Arabic. We always wanted to build the everything exchange — exchange for all these different assets. And looking back, when I was thinking about it: we didn't build Kalshi to build a company. We built a company to build Kalshi. It's a bit different.

I see.

We were dragged by the idea. I wasn't the type that wanted to be an entrepreneur. If you reroll the dice multiple times, I'd probably just be a trader or risk manager. I would not be an entrepreneur.

Got it.

But the idea was so glaring in front of us that we just had to do it. We felt we had to do it. So the whole point of doing this whole thing is the idea.

Okay.

When that's the case, then if you lose the idea, then —

Advice for CEOs in the Desert

A lot of people listening to this are walking through that desert.

Yeah.

I read this book a long time ago — it was called The Dip. And you're cruising along, you get down the dip, and you don't know if you're about to hit the bottom and come up, or if it keeps going.

Everyone thinks you're delusional.

Yeah. Advice for the CEOs out there walking through the desert.

You know the book — is it Ben Horowitz that wrote about the "hard thing about hard things"? There's that paragraph that's so good, which is: the reason why it's hard — it's not like you're lifting something heavy. It's that no one can reassure you that it's going to end.

What I firmly believe — and I really do — is the number one risk —

Because you want to give up.

For sure. Every day you wake up, you're like, "What am I doing?"

And was Luana like, "No, we're sticking with it"? What's the dynamic?

I mean, we both had our points. Luana was definitely much more stubborn. Continuously. Luana was just like — Luana had a lot more, "Stop being a little bitch, basically. Life is fine. Whatever. Let's move on."

Yeah.

And what I believe is: if you give yourself enough of a time horizon, you inevitably win.

Or you run out of money.

Yeah. But I don't think that's a real killer of companies.

Okay.

You can always extend your runway. It's not that hard to raise money these days. And you can pivot with a new idea. It's really the number one risk is giving up.

When to Give Up: The Opportunity Cost Question

But — when I say that, to me the message should not be, "Oh, I should do it at all cost." I actually see a lot of founders and they're walking through the desert — I think they should give up, because it's opportunity cost.

Yes.

You're 35, you're going to spend the next four years walking through the desert. Start something new.

I think just ask yourself why you're doing it, right? To me, maybe there's a few buckets. There's the idea is something that you're really passionate about — you should keep going. Because you're going to regret that. Then there's a second bucket — sort of all the things that people believe to be true after you make it. And I think that's a bad reason. Because it's a career, right? It doesn't end. You have all sorts of different issues over time. That thing in your gut stays there. It never leaves you. You're always stressed. You have to make sacrifices in your life. Entrepreneurs don't have a good life in the traditional sense of the term. You don't get to that oasis that you thought you were going to get to. You actually just get into different deserts, and those deserts get maybe less — there's some water in the desert, but —

What Rocket-Ship CEO Life Is Actually Like

Okay, maybe the better analogy is: you go from a desert to a storm, and that storm sometimes is very clean water, sometimes it's a shitstorm. It's a different type of craziness. And maybe it's more motivating day-to-day. It's stress, and it's not just depression. But it's not the life that you like — "Oh, you finally made it, you're successful, you're on the pedestal."

Okay. So what do people think about what the life of a rocket-ship CEO is like versus the reality? What do people have wrong?

Is it glamorous?

No, no. Some people, I see it sometimes — when they get to that and it's finally made, they really go enjoy life. But inevitably then the company goes sideways.

What's your life like?

I work a lot all the time.

Are you married? Do you have kids?

I have a partner. It's been like four years and some. She's honestly great, but it's tough, right? She sacrificed a lot to for me to get here, because she supported me in the hard times, she supported me in the good times — which are also hard.

Keep her.

Right. She's the best. But there's sacrifice on that front. I travel a lot. I wake up basically every day at 7. I'm usually at the office by 8, and I work — I leave the office usually at 10. I work a little bit at home. I usually maybe waste time on Instagram Reels for like 30 minutes, and I go to bed.

What's the weekend like?

I usually try to go out for dinner one of Friday or Saturday. Generally Saturday, because Friday I'm so tired — I just need to rest. And if I'm basically in the office Saturday, Sunday — I go to office Saturday. I kind of like being in the office.

Founders Set the Pace, Not the Rule

Because everybody by the way is getting —

Most people, most people —

They're in there.

Yeah. But it's not forced. It's not like, "Oh, hardcore." We try to do it in a way where no one is ever kind of slapped, and there's no expectation you need to be in the office with these hours. I don't believe in that sort of "put everyone in the office 24/7 hardcore grind." That's going to — I do believe the founders can do it, because we have the most incentive to do it. And if the founders do it naturally, others are motivated. They want to be next. They like coming on Saturday because there may be a little bit less people, and they can sit next to you and ideate about a bunch of things. And hopefully you got the right people that would show up. But they don't have to.

What Scales Linearly (and What Doesn't)

Okay, let's just get back to the CEO-ing since we're there. 150 direct reports. It's an interesting number — because it's Dunbar's number. It's hard to keep track of anything north of 150. It's hard to interview those people. Most companies started hiring middle managers.

Is it 170? Three years from now, is it 500? Are you like William — I'm friends with William Hockey — I call him and he's like, "I'm at 130." He's like, "I'm staying at 130." Is that you?

William is very good. I think he's done a great job. I don't know. I'm just thinking about the things that scale linearly. Historically, support — but we have less than 10 support people. We have, obviously, millions of customers.

You can automate a lot of that.

Yeah, it works really well. If you train the right — it's amazing, honestly. So that's a linear thing that's gone. As we scale our institutional side, I think potentially sales could scale somewhat linearly. But I don't know. I'm kind of a believer — I'm actually curious what you think — but I'm kind of a believer in strong marketing and brand marketing as a fundamental enabler of sales, and enabler of top of funnel.

Yeah.

If you do that very well, people just sort of follow each other.

Perfectionism, the Last 10%, and Resonant Frequency

Where does that come from — from you? You mentioned that a couple times — you're really detailed in the copy and all that. You grew up as a kind of a math CS guy. Where does that come from? And is it —

Part of the paranoia, the perfectionism that came from my mom. I tend to have OCD and be obsessive with these things, and I want things to be as perfect as possible. And it actually really matters. This is the key thing: all the results are in the last 10%. Most people do 80/20 — that doesn't work. Everybody's 80/20.

Okay.

It has to be perfect.

Okay.

And especially with marketing, there's this sort of resonant frequency that you have to —

This is your mom's rick talking right now.

But this resonant frequency concept is very interesting. Because when you hit it, you get these dramatically improved results.

Give me an example.

The Billboard Bet and the Timothée Chalamet Ad

There's so many, but I'll give two separate ones specifically on marketing. So one — when we first went mainstream was during '24 election. One of the ideas I had is: we should do billboards across the country. And the billboards are basically the product. It has to be the exact same as the product, and it's live — piped with the API. Every time a trade happens on the app, it's going to be projected out in Times Square and in LA and all these billboards in real time. That had to be perfect, so that every time people look up, they build a habit of, "I want to see the odds," and "my couch is there for me to see the odds." And if it is, then you get the viral mechanism, and all of it works. And it worked. But it took maybe something like 20 iterations of putting it out there, retaking it back, to get it right.

Okay.

It's brutal. Your designer gets exhausted. The engineer gets — everyone is exhausted. But that's the sort of stuff that no one's going to do other than the founder.

The other thing I believe strongly in marketing is: the timing needs to be exactly right. It really matters. People don't care about us. People don't care about anything. They just care about themselves, and then what they're reading on any given — it used to be any given month, then week, then day, and maybe now every few hours — that changes. They forget. There's so much stuff going on all the time. You have to basically bring Kalshi to whatever is happening, or yourself to whatever is happening in the wild — not try to push whatever you're offering to people.

In the last few months, if you look at all the marketing integrations we do — a lot: we partner with this and that. So we did Messi two days before his first game — of his last workout. Why? Because it's two days before his last — I mean, he's peak relevance right now, right? Timothée Chalamet — we launched that commercial 12 hours after the whole thing that happened with the Knicks. Why? Because everyone was talking about Timothée Chalamet at the time. If we had done it now —

You also did the AI video before anyone did the AI video.

Because it was a time when there was a whole debate about the artist — like, the artist, are they going to go, now it's not cool to do —

Because you're obsessive about that kind of thing, and nailing that kind of thing.

And always knowing what — but now if you do the AI video, you should not do AI videos anymore. Now it's cool to do real things.

Yes.

Waiting for Peak Relevance — Even If You Miss the Window

The timing with these things — okay, that's what you mean about that. The Messi timing was incredible, and the Timothée Chalamet timing was incredible. I assume you had that in process with —

Yeah, but we were always waiting. Giannis — when we announced him — like, if the Knicks get knocked out in the earlier round, you're still going live with that.

Yes, but we were waiting for the right — we used to think we were going to do it at the Oscars, but then we waited.

I see.

We were waiting for the perfect moment, because then that's when you get the 100x outcome. When we announced Giannis — a basketball player — it was essentially a day after he announced that he's not going to leave his team. There was —

Now he's going to Miami.

Now he's going to Miami. He's peak relevance at the time, and now he's peak relevance again.

But that applies to everything. When we did the news integrations back in the fall, we announced CNN and CNBC and Fox. All of these were: we really wanted to time them at peak moments where people were talking about this debate between "Is the news gone? Is it a thing of the past?" And for us the angle was never, "Oh, couch partners." It was: "The news is actually modernizing."

How did — I know you want to time it well, but does Timothée care? Does CNN care? Does the New York Times care? Do the counterparties —

Battle all these different things. And you can have too much process, and you have to break their own processes. It's all of the same that we discussed. That's a shit pipe.

If you have a head of partnership that's doing a whole structure and they plan for three weeks, you can't do that.

Did you hire any professional marketing person inside your company?

Not like a head, in any way. We have a CMO, Alan — he's incredible. So Alan takes care of all the scientific pieces — very good at spending money with high ROI. And then the rest — the person that leads our brand — doesn't have any direct reports. He's just sort of flying around country and doing cool stuff. And he ran a Twitter account before — the inverse Cramer.

Wait — that's where he started?

He was the inverse Cramer account.

Remind me.

Yes. You know, the inverse — like Jim Cramer, when he picks stock. So he's like the inverse Cramer index. So you make more money betting against.

Yeah, exactly.

And I hired — I was like, "You want to come and do our social?" He started doing our social. He came up with the AI idea. He has a knack for these sort of weird, esoteric ways to just be part of the zeitgeist. And what's interesting — that has worked equally for consumers and for institutions.

Okay, I didn't expect you to say that.

Yeah. People would think, "Oh, institutions want sort of this." They do — and they want to talk to your chief risk guy, and we have all that. We have a very strong, robust foundation. But being top of mind just matters continuously. Top of mind is —

Suing the Federal Government: Whose Idea?

You've got a knack, I'll give you that. Okay, I want to wind the clock back, because we haven't completely finished the desert story. At some point, the conversation happened inside of the company: "Hey, we should sue the CFTC." Whose idea was that, and what was the reaction?

But what'd you say?

I mean, it's crazy, right? The — I mean, it is crazy. It ended up being crazy. Even though we won. Because it's so much more than just being right. It's so much more than just the law being right. It's unfortunate, but it's the truth. A lot of things are political, and depend on how the winds are shifting. And the government is truly all-powerful. They can just do things to private companies. And by the time you hold the government accountable to that thing, you probably will be dead basically, or the cost would be so ginormous. The government doesn't really incur any cost — they're protected from any cost.

Okay, but take me in the room when she brought it up.

The Long Setup Behind the "Overnight" Lawsuit

So there's a little bit of context there, because we started trying — and this goes back to how much foundation we built. People always think overnight success. "Oh, it's been two years." But it hasn't. All of this has been part of a very long-term plan. A bit like Jensen, right? He's built such a foundation for so long, and that's how these things go.

We started engaging about the election market before we even launched to the general public — in the end of '21. Started talking to the government, saying, "We want to do this. It's very important. Here's why it's going to work. It's going to be the most accurate gauge, and here's how it's legal" — which it is. And they're like, "Okay, fine, we'll work with you." Month after month, they wouldn't give it to us. They wouldn't give it to us. I had bet all the company on the election market for the '22 midterms. That was what we were aiming towards. The board is ready for it, etc. And they keep delaying, delaying, delaying — up until they pushed it after the midterms. So they pocket-vetoed. They didn't say no. They just managed to politely delay us enough that it didn't happen.

So that was heartbreaking. We started in 2018, finally got to 2021, finally excited about launching — they're not letting us launch. And all the issue about launch started working on the election market, and it gets banned at the end of '22. So now we launched with a very limited set of things that's going to be very hard to get the exchange running. But they banned us. We lost a lot of the team over it. And we had to do a layoff.

Did you push them out, or they left?

A bunch of people left. And then we had to do a layoff.

Expected Outcome vs. Outcome: The Poker Digression

Because what happens in these situations — I'm going to go on a tangent. I think it's an interesting thing.

Let it rip.

There's this concept of expected outcome and outcome. And they're often times not the same. There's a variance for the outcome, and the expected outcome is — based on your decision-making — where it should land. And then there's a bunch of variance in the world that pushes you in either direction.

The world doesn't reward you for expected outcomes. They reward you for outcomes. Expected outcome is what you have control over — the work you do. And at that point, the coin flip landed against us, and the entire war is like, "Wrong strategy, wrong execution," etc.

And it's interesting because that was the same time when — I'm a big Messi fan. So Argentina won the World Cup, super happy. But the day after Argentina won the World Cup — I don't know if you know this — the French population wanted to kick out the French coach Deschamps, who is an absolute legend. The guy won them the prior World Cup, incredible performance, got them to the final of the next World Cup and to the penalties, and obviously penalties are a coin flip. And they blamed him. The number of teams that made it past the group stage after having won a World Cup is basically trivial. It's very hard to go twice in a row. But he — the coin landed against him, and people wanted to axe him immediately. And so we lived through that.

Did anyone want to axe you?

No. But people lost confidence. It's like, "We should pivot. Wrong strategy, wrong execution. We're not doing a good job." Basically, it's on us, and it's fine — you should hold the founders accountable.

But the key learning — what's interesting a little bit with poker: the best poker players, they know when they're playing a good hand, and they're okay with the variance. They can lose a lot, but they'll stick to their game. And then they know that over time the variance is going to come back, and with enough plays, they're going to win over time.

And you see with Elon, you'll see with some of these founders — they can take these extreme risks as long as they know their expected outcome, and they can intake the variance over time. And I think there's a lot of alpha in there. For founders that have gone through that multiple times — I see it with Vlad and Robinhood — over time, they gain a certain level of confidence that is hard to describe, but it's like: "I've gone through it. I'm okay with the fluctuations."

The Decision to Sue

Is this making sense in the rearview mirror, or was that making sense at the time?

No, not at the time.

Okay.

But now in the rearview mirror. So '22 happens, and our answer at the time: "We got to reset. We're going to do something else, we're going to figure out what to do." But again, we want to build Kalshi. That's what we want to build. And we cannot do it without the election markets. We're going to try again.

Okay. So you go back to board. "We're going to try again."

Everyone's like, "I mean, okay, these guys are delusional." We tried till the end — another year, end of '23. Now they say no. They reject us.

Okay.

So again — it's like it goes from a low to a lower.

Yes.

And this was — we're in the room. It's like, "Oh, what do we do again?"

Rollercoaster.

And, you know what, this time we'll sue them.

So how did it actually work? You got the email that —

Like, no. And then right away she's like, "We should sue"?

That summer was like, "We're probably going to get a no again, so we should start preparing the lawsuit."

Okay.

We started talking to the board. We had a lot of conversation with Alfred. Michael was at the board at the time.

Alfred Lin's Response: "You're Going to Lose"

And what did Alfred say the first time you pitched him on it?

He's like, "It's a crazy idea. You're a tiny startup. Even if you win, you're probably going to lose." And he's right. We were probably going to lose. And he's like, "For a company of your size — even for bigger companies — you never win against the government. They have all power over you. They're your regulator. Suing them — they're going to kill you."

I was super dogmatic. I was battling it. But then there's a few things that came out of that. Like, one was complete no-brainer. But I would oscillate all the time. I would just be like, "This is crazy. They're going to come after us. They're going to come after us personally."

I think CEOs are going to love this, because I oscillate all the time on stuff like that.

I oscillate a lot, which I think is okay.

I hid my oscillation as much as I could, but it definitely showed up.

Yeah. And I think it's okay, honestly. Because it's hard to have the right — I mean, it's so complicated, right?

I would oscillate, etc. I remember we got to a meeting at some point where I was still oscillating. The night before we're going to file, etc. Luana was like, "Are you kidding me? Like — " she went so hard. She's like, "Fuck this. We should just do it. We're wasting time." And everyone else in the room — there was this sort of missionary feeling. You could feel it: "We came so far. Fuck it. Let's just go full-on war. Let's bet everything."

Asymmetric Bet, Bet the Farm

In your poker head, where you're like, "There's probably a 20% chance this will work"?

It was so hard to price. But I thought through all of it, which was basically — and Alpha was right in many ways — they could kill us. Because they could essentially yank the clearing house from under us and kill the company. We would die maybe even before we even get the result — which is a risk. They could — "death by a thousand paper cuts." They can start doing a bunch of things to you.

But then I was like: if it does work, the outcome is so big. And the expected value seems pretty attractive, actually. Even if low percentage odds of success, the outcome is so big.

It's an asymmetric bet. It's a big one. And we bet the farm, and we're like, "Let's see what happens." No plan B.

The Winning Call

In the moment — you know, the timing was extremely good on winning. What was — was it an email? Did someone call you? What was going on? Where were you?

Yeah. So the litigator, Yakov Roth, who led this — you know, we interviewed everybody. I picked him because he's extremely smart. But the thing that I felt he was really good at: he was missionary. Like, this man wakes up in the morning and thinks about how to put the federal government in check. You want that person. He's an amazing litigator. But in the summer of '24 — usually we text — and he called me. And I got really anxious. I thought that the court hearing came. I was like, "What?" He's like, "What's up?" And he talked to me about something else. But like, dude — my heart dropped. "Don't ever call me. You should only call me once you have the court decision. Never call me ever again. Text me. I don't want you to call me — because in the 10 seconds where I have to pick up the phone, my heart dropped." "Okay, fine, I won't call you until the decision."

And I was sitting on my desk. I remember Rainer, one of our engineers, sitting next to me. And his name pops up on my phone. I could feel — you know these moments where you feel your entire body just paralyzed. Like, "Oh, fuck." I pick up the phone and I was like, "Why are you calling me?" And then he's like, "We won." And after, I don't remember — we were throwing chairs. Yeah, we were throwing chairs in the office. We basically destroyed the office.

Why the Win Felt Vindicating

It was unbelievable. Because you want — and in that year we suffered so much, because you had the competitor — probably getting the brand, and we're staying true to our strategy. We're going to stick to it. The government was yanking the clearing house from under us, enforcement action after the other. They were finding random things to just create actions against us. Audits — you know, the audits that usually take 10 days — they were mil to nine months. It was just sort of like truly painful. The whole "warfare" thing — it truly happened, and they make your life miserable. And not just the company. You, personally. It's kind of this intimidation tactic. Up until you cave, up until you're so exhausted.

And it was just so vindicating. It was so nice. Finally, like, we finally had the opportunity to just win. For the first time.

After the Win: The Machine Started Going

Okay. You win. And of course, you knew this was going to work. Did it work like way better than you thought?

Not in the first few days.

Oh, really?

It's like all things. You have to build emotion, and you have to build a brand. So we — but this is the thing: you win and then you're like, "Oh, let's launch it." And then nothing happens.

By the way, the time you won this case was —

Four weeks — like three weeks and a half before the election.

Was that random timing?

Yeah.

You got to get it sometime. Fascinating.

You know, Tony says it sometimes: you need luck sometimes. Is it because we're just lucky? I think, yeah — you need to get lucky. But you took risk. And we took five, six years of expanding that luck surface area. So at some point we could —

But did it work even better than you imagined after we started compounding?

So it takes time. Everything takes time. But in those three weeks and a half — look, we had three weeks and a half to make this go mainstream. Everybody's in the office. I don't — I like, basically, honestly would shower maybe like once every four days. I was just like, "I don't care about anything. This is my shot." And so we worked so hard in those four weeks. It was slow, slow. But then it started upticking, and then the machine started going. And then when it started going, it was beautiful. It was amazing.

The Uncomfortable Question: Are Kids Getting Addicted?

I got a 21-year-old son, and he and all his friends use Kalshi.

Yeah.

And I know you're going to say it's trading, not gambling. But I'm a little afraid some of them are going to get addicted.

There are similar impulses. Look, I think there's a few ways to think about it. Let's talk about the policy and actually what we're doing — because I care. I think about that a lot, and I care about that.

Yeah.

That's my paranoid brain. I don't want people to get hurt. And with all technologies, people inevitably get hurt. They will use it in a bad way. This is true for Airbnb and — and are you hearing from mothers?

Yeah. And actually it's mixed. Let me walk through a few things.

The Playbook of the Incumbent: Call It Gambling

First, the policy aspects. This is important for everyone. At some point, you're going to build something very big — especially in consumer — and when it truly hits mainstream, you're going to get haters. There's going to be a bucket of society that's going to be worried because they don't understand it or they don't get it, and they're worried about the risks. A lot of people don't see the point of AI: "Why lose our jobs over something that can do our homework better?" I think they're missing the point, obviously. But you need to educate that part of society.

And then you have incumbents. Because inevitably, if you go mainstream, you're going to be taking it from someone. And those incumbents will go very hard after you. When the taxis went after Uber, they didn't go after them saying, "Hey, our margins are threatened, that's why you should shut it down." What did they say? They said it's unsafe, and kids are getting hurt and kidnapped. And Airbnb was the same thing with the hotels. They would find one case of one person that had a very terrible bad experience, or something really horrible happened, and they would blow it up in the New York Times. The press likes scandals and drama.

And so for us, the easy low-hanging fruit is: call it gambling. And it's interesting, because pretty much all trading products that have ever come to society have come through a fight — have basically emerged through a fight of them being called gambling. So grain futures were actually legalized with the Supreme Court decision in the 1900s. Like, futures — a boring commodity futures market at the time. The states were suing —

Futures Started as "Gambling" Too

My son is not trading commodities futures.

But they used to be — fine, the farmers were —

But consumers — but kids weren't.

No, that's not true. The farmers were 20 and 18. By the way, the age to trade futures is 18.

Okay.

Right. It's actually — I mean, now there's other, more interest —

Maybe my son runs in a different crowd, but none of —

But I want to address this. That's because society evolved. There's now crypto and meme coins and all these other things. At the time there was nothing else — this was the primary mechanism of speculation. That's what everybody was doing. But the claim at the time was: this is gambling and we should basically regulate it as gambling and not as a financial market. And Supreme Court said: speculation does look like gambling, because it does.

Yeah. So does the stock market.

Yes, exactly. But there's a price discovery aspect to this, where you're on an open, transparent exchange, where people are trading against each other. That makes it a financial market, and that's why it needs to be regulated as financial market. And so on and so forth.

The Business Model Difference: Casinos vs. Kalshi

Fast forward to us. There's a few things that are very important. One: the incentive structure in the model. Gambling — I don't gamble. I don't like gambling. I like trading. Gambling is a business model where the revenue of the company is equal to the customer's losses. So over time, you block the winners — which is what they do. They don't want the smart, mathematically-oriented people that are doing research. They want the people that continuously lose. And those people, they give them promos to come back. And that's how you create these addictions. But they cannot really solve the problem of addiction, because the revenues are equal to the losses. So over time, what do you do? Well, you have to increase the losses. You have to create those types of behaviors — or at least not throttle them too much.

The losses don't go to me on Kalshi. That's the beauty of a derivatives market. I take a 1% fee, whether somebody loses or not. So when someone is exhibiting bad patterns — those losses are going to someone else. I have all the incentive in the world to basically throttle them and figure out how to get it right.

Card Sharks vs. Casinos

But let me just push back a little bit on that. In a casino, they try to block the card sharks.

Yeah.

But in your world, there's a lot of card sharks betting against my son's friend who's going on a gut feel.

Yeah, that's absolutely right. Absolutely. Now, it depends on the incentive structure. I want smart trading. I want to incentivize people to do research. Because the more liquid, the better the forecast; the more truthful my forecast, the more people look at it, the more my top of funnel increases. I want the smart traders. Which is a very different structure from the casino, which is: you don't want the smart behavior. You want just the excessive money-losing, continuously money-losing behavior.

Throttling, KYC, and the Parent Portal

Now, it's important to understand: not everybody wins on Kalshi. Not everybody wins in the stock market. Not everybody wins in the options market. Not everybody wins in athletic competitions. It's a highly competitive activity in nature. And inevitably there's going to be some people that win and win more. Like, how many people win the NBA finals? How many people win the Champions League? It's a small percentage of participants.

Now, here's what's interesting: everybody can win. It's a fair, neutral platform. And my incentive is to give as many tools as possible for people to do the right thing and do research and do all of that. And what we do — and what we do really well — is we do take this issue of excessive behaviors pretty seriously.

So, for example, in the 18-to-20 bucket — I think the data doesn't show dramatically different behaviors, but society tends to be worried about that. We add a lot more throttling to the accounts.

What does that mean?

Like, you cannot basically lose too much in excess. We add throttling, and we start asking for additional verification that adds a lot of friction for you to keep going.

And the reason we do that is because the positive side of this — so when I ask the parents and the mom question, and their 25-year-old is trading on Kalshi — what they get excited about is: "They're actually getting smarter about the future. They're smarter about the world." Because the alternative is they're spending time on Instagram and social media, where it's extremists and all the hate and constant brain rot.

And they get addicted to that, of course.

But they can't lose all their money on it.

I think they're losing a lot more —

They can lose their — they turn their brain —

I think sometimes I am losing my brain on that. But my point here: they're spending time getting smarter about the world, which I think is the whole premise of what we're building. This is going to be the ultimate antidote to a lot of the polarization, extremism we're seeing. Because these calibrated, well-reasoned takes are not getting rewarded on social media, but they get rewarded in prediction markets. And that trains people over time to get a little bit smarter.

And I wanted to be a tool for that — especially for younger people — rather than a tool that "I'm losing all my money and I'm doing excessive behaviors." And there's easy ways to do that: you can cap how much people are doing, you can throttle these accounts. One of the things we do very well — I think better than even some of the financial brokers or traditional brokers — is we self-regulate. We don't just do what regulation requires us to do. We go above and beyond, because I think about all the risk. Minors, for example — obviously they're banned, and we do KYC and all these different things. But the way that minors access all these platforms is they use their parents' IDs. So one of the things we created is a parent portal, where moms and parents can give us their ID and tell us: "Don't let anyone else use it." That solves 95% of the issue.

And I think we're doing a great job. The numbers show — the percentage of people that show these signs of these patterns of excessive behaviors, or are losing excessively, or losing in general on Kalshi, is actually lower than options trading, and it's lower than any active trading of stocks. So if you buy a stock and hold it for five years, it's investing. But if you're trading it on a daily basis, 75, 80% of people lose. And I think people have not come to terms that there are irresponsible behaviors in all these markets. And the way to do it is: you have to figure out how to flag these behaviors and get better at throttling them.

Final Advice: Why Are You Doing This?

Okay. I guess my last question — this pod is a lot of CEOs and founders listen to it. You've had a really unusual, really long, strange trip. One last piece of advice or tips for folks going through the desert.

I would say a few things. One: really question, really hard, why you're doing it. Because the thing that you're — like, if it's not for the company itself — the thing that you're hoping to get, I don't know if it's really there. Which is a glamorous —

Yeah.

I never found it. Obviously financially rewarding at some point — after — there's many ways to do that these days. The opportunity cost is very high. If you can join a fast-growing — there's so many ways to do that. So the monetary reward just feels risk-adjusted is probably a stupid move.

Yes. And if you adjust also for lifestyle and amount of pain —

Yeah.

It gets pretty unattractive.

Okay. Your little brother comes to you and says, "I want to start a company." What do you say?

I just say, "Why?"

You push back.

Yeah. Why? Explain. What is it like?

Oh, I want to be my own boss.

You're not really your own. You got a board. You work for all your employees. You work for everyone. Your users. If you're a good CEO, you're basically — you work for everybody. I don't think that sense of freedom is real in any way. Actually, you're the least free. And the more it grows, the less free you are.

Be Yourself — Everyone Else Is Taken

The second thing is: I really strongly believe in the theory that it has to be true to you. It's going to be very hard to build a company, market a product, build a product that is not you. It's very hard. I honestly don't see how that works. Every time you see a big company, and then you meet the founder or the CEO of that company — and you'd probably agree with me on this — it's shocking how similar them.

Yes.

The office is them. Like, you meet them — "Oh, it's — I totally understand why that company is the way it is."

Right.

And I feel like people — I feel like that's not put into practice enough. Does that make sense? I think people just try to figure out all the right ways to do things. I mean, I wish there was — but if there was, then everybody —

This is a really good quote: "Be yourself. Everyone else is taken." You're definitely yourself. Thanks for coming on the podcast. Congrats on all your success.

Thanks so much for having me. This is great.

Host Takeaways

Okay. Hope everybody liked that. I really enjoyed talking to him. It's the second time I met him. He's a gem. Couple of my takeaways:

Holy crap, is he mission-driven. He had a mission of how he wanted to see the world change, and the company was just like, "Okay, we're going to build a company to pull that mission off." People talk about mission a lot. Very much in his case, it rhymes with kind of how HubSpot started. Our vision, mission was: we wanted to change marketing from outbound to inbound, and we were just like, "Oh, we need to create a company to pull that off." It wasn't originally a company, it was an idea. So I like that.

And I like founders who are — he's very obsessive and very mission-driven. I feel like today with startups, everyone's trying to win Twitter for the day, and sometimes making short-term decisions. The thing I like about Kalshi and the thing I really like about SpaceX is: they made some big foundational bets, and were super patient, and were really rewarded once those things paid off. And I think more founders could learn from that.

Last thing I would say is: he's got a unique relationship with Luana, his co-founder. Luana is the kind of organized one and very risk-seeking, and he's a little bit all over the place and he's risk-averse. It was the exact opposite of what I thought those two relationship was. But it's very much 1 plus 1 equals 3. It's reminded me of my relationship with Dharmesh and how complimentary the two are. Hope you liked it.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的洞察：这家公司的组织结构，就是它所在市场的镜像
Kalshi 是一个"预测市场"——本质上是把**创新的野心**和**监管的克制**放在同一张订单簿里做市。而 Tarek 和 Luana 的"共同 CEO + 永久性分歧"结构，几乎是这个市场机制的人形化：一个天生做风险经理（Tarek 的黎巴嫩童年 + 母亲的偏执），一个是"非理性乐观主义者"（Luana 的信念驱动）。
他们说这是"设计好的分歧"（disagreement by design），但真正的洞察在于：**Kalshi 的组织架构与其产品的定价机制是同构的**。预测市场的价格是多空双方持续博弈得到的边际共识，Kalshi 的公司决策也是通过两个持续对冲的创始人得到的边际共识。他们不是在"运营"一家做预测市场的公司——**他们把自己变成了一个内部的预测市场**。这解释了为什么这种"混乱"能扩展：因为价格发现机制本来就是混乱的，但它收敛。
## 二、跨领域连接：Tarek 的"共振频率"概念，其实是把物理学偷渡进了营销学
大多数创始人谈营销是在谈漏斗、CAC、A/B。Tarek 反复用的词是 **r

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] Robinhood最新報告：散戶投資績效比機構差很多！但買的股票都很像，所以為什麼？Johann Kerbrat【邦妮區塊鏈】
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：Robinhood 的真正对手不是券商，而是"时间的稀缺性"

大多数人把 Robinhood 理解成"零佣金券商 + 加密货币"。但仔细听 Johann 的话，他反复强调一件事："be early in the investor journey"——要在用户金融旅程的最早期就出现。这暴露了一个惊人的战略真相：Robinhood 卖的不是交易，而是"时间复利的入口权"。

更值得玩味的是那个看似离题的"现金上门配送服务"。为什么一家券商要送现金？因为他们意识到：用户走去 ATM 的那 15 分钟，就是他们流失注意力的窗口。这和 TikTok、外卖平台的逻辑完全一致——消灭一切"离开 App 的物理理由"。Robinhood 表面在做金融，骨子里在做的是"用户时间的独占"，这与字节跳动、Meta 属于同一个物种。

跨领域连接：Robinhood 链的诞生，其实是"苹果 vs 安卓"故事在金融基础设施层的重演

Johann 解释为什么不用现成的 L2 时，用了一个非常关键的词："合规能做在链层，而不是智能合约层"。这句话如果翻译成消费电子的语言，就是"我们要做垂直整合的苹果，而不是拼装安卓机"。

有趣的对照是：Coinbase 的 Base 链走的是"我们只做基础设施，应用层放开"（更像 Google 的安卓策略）；而 Robinhood 选择把合规、清算、资产发行、AI 代理全部收在自己链上。这意味着未来 5 年加密行业会分裂成两种商业模型——"链是操作系统"和"链是内容平台"，而不是现在大家以为的"所有链都在竞争 TPS"。TPS 之争其实早就结束了，真正的战争是"谁定义链上默认行为"。

隐藏的系统性真相：散户跑输 NASDAQ 不是能力问题，而是"产品设计的必然结果"

你问他"散户跑输 NASDAQ 说明什么"，他没有正面回答。但如果把他的所有回答串起来——推出预测市场、期权、加密、代币化股票、稳定币收益、AI 助手、循环定投——你会发现一个残酷的系统性事实：

Robinhood 的商业模式，天然与"让用户跑赢指数"存在结构性冲突。因为： - 用户被动持有 SPY 十年 → Robinhood 几乎赚不到钱 - 用户高频交易期权和 meme 币 → Robinhood 赚 payment for order flow

所以他反复说的"我们只是提供工具，让用户自己决定"，本质上是把"结构性诱导"包装成"用户自由"。这不是道德问题，这是所有零佣金券商的宿命——当交易免费时，用户本身就是被交易的商品。

最意外的跨领域连接：这和赌场的免费酒水、健身房的会员定价、外卖平台的满减券是同一套心理机制——用"零摩擦"制造"高频率"，用"高频率"消耗"判断力"。散户跑输 NASDAQ 的真正原因，可能不在散户本身，而在于他们使用的界面被设计成了"注意力吸尘器"——而 AI 助手的加入，只会让这个吸力更强、更个性化、更难察觉。

一个被忽略的时代信号

Johann 提到"越来越多年轻投资者开始为退休做打算"——这句话听起来鸡汤，但背后是一个代际级别的信心崩塌：年轻人不再相信养老金、社保、房产升值这些"旧承诺"，所以他们必须自己上场。这意味着未来 10 年最大的金融浪潮，不是加密、不是 AI，而是"Z 世代的自我救济式投资"——Robinhood 只是这条河的第一块石头。

📝 转录文本

开场：散户跑输 NASDAQ，说明了什么？

主持人： Right now, the retail investors are underperforming NASDAQ.

Johann： Retail investors, the people who use Robinhood have moved on from Bitcoin because it's become too boring and old and now they're, you know, they're doing everything except Bitcoin.

Johann： No, we see a lot of younger investor now thinking more about their retirement than in the past.

主持人： Does data show you that investors actually become better over time?

We're back at Consensus Miami with my co-host Bonnie Ch, and uh joining us today is Johan Kerbert. He is the head of crypto at Robinhood. Welcome to the show, Johan. We're going to be talking about the entire crypto industry as well as Robinhood chain and much more.

Robinhood 投资者指数：散户为何跑输 NASDAQ？

主持人： Uh Robinhood has this Robinhood investor index that tracks the performance of the top 100 most owned investments on Robinhood and right now the retail investors are underperforming NASDAQ. What does that tell you?

Johann： Uh you know I think for us uh the main goal has been to give all the tool for retail investor to access uh the market and to be able to really decide on their investment. Um the index itself has different ways to look at it and if you look at specific period you can always find different ways to uh interpret it, but I think what we've seen in a lot of situation is that our customer are actually able to get in front of the market.

主持人： Is it fair to say that retail investors are acting more and more like institutions? For example, the top 10 investments right now — Nvidia, Apple, Tesla, Amazon, Microsoft, Palantir — these are all owned by institutions and now retail investors also love them. So is it fair to say that most people should just own these big mega cap stocks?

Johann： Uh we think that customers should be able to decide what they want to do. And so for us that's really the point, right? Like we want to give them the access. We want to give them the tools to decide by themselves.

Um, I do think it's true that a lot of retail made some of these companies that you mentioned really big before even the institution really cared about them. Uh so I think that's really something that we've been focused on.

And the other part that we really care about is giving access to companies sooner for retail investor. Right now if you think about private equity, um it's something that is really excluding most of retail investors because you have to be accredited in the US and a lot of the population is not. Um and so I think for us with Robinhood the idea was really to find ways to give access. Uh we launched for example fund called Robinhood Venture — uh it's a closed-end fund that include many of private companies — and that way the retail investor can get in at the same time as institution instead of waiting for the company to go public.

预测市场爆火，Robinhood 如何应对？

主持人： I want to get your take on Robinhood's greater strategy here for attracting retail capital and attention. Over the last year, we've seen the rise of prediction markets with prediction markets volume soaring by 400% across several companies in 2025 alone, with more retail investors switching now to that particular market. Um, how are lowcost exchanges like Robinhood competing?

Johann： Uh, you know, for prediction market, the main reason that we see a lot of interest from our customer has been that it's a brand new way to invest into the market. And I think, you know, until now it was very difficult for you to hedge against AI, against new economic situation. And with economical contracts — like, you know, something around the rates dropping or the rates increasing — you have different ways to engage before an announcement and you don't have to just invest in one stock or one fund. So I think it really changed the game on how we can actually invest.

Um, and I think for us the superpower has been to have everything within one app. Um, you know, you can go on Robinhood to buy crypto, equities, options and at the same time also prediction market — and I think that's a big difference for us.

为什么要自建 Robinhood Chain？

主持人： Next. Next. You built Robinhood chain. Why?

Johann： Um, so we launched a testnet a few months ago. Uh, so far it's been doing very well. We've seen more than 100 million transactions on our testnet.

And the main reason why we wanted to build it was to create a chain that is really focused on financial instruments. Um, a chain that is optimized for real world assets uh at the time of AI and agentic trading as well. Um, not just something that is made to, you know, optimize for gas fees or sequencer fees or something like that.

So for us it was important to understand how we can bring the technology aspect of crypto into the traditional finance system that has a lot of complexities and still a lot of limitations. For example, 24/7 is not something that is supported, instance settlements is not supported. And so when we launch our tokenized products we want to make sure that we can offer all these benefits of the crypto technology to our customers.

主持人： Why don't you just pick one of the layer twos or whatever faster blockchain instead of building your own?

Johann： Yeah, we've thought about it. Uh, you know, it was a long discussion for us internally.

Um, the main reason we decided to build our own chain was: we wanted to get access to the liquidity of Ethereum and the security of Ethereum. You know, they spend a lot of time thinking about all these aspects. And at the same time, we wanted to have enough ways to customize a chain.

So for us, compliance is very important. Uh, you know, we are a global company working in many countries and we want to make sure that we can implement all the systems that our regulators are asking for — and we can do it at the chain layer instead of doing it at the smart contract layer or not doing it at all like some of our competitors. So for us it was important to have this customization and also all the advantages of a layer one like this layer.

Robinhood 的定位：民主化访问

主持人： How does Robinhood position itself right now? There's no-cost ways to transact peer-to-peer through DEXes. There's also higher cost if it gets through centralized exchanges. Where does Robinhood sit in the grander picture?

Johann： Um, you know, for us the question has always been about democratizing access. So when we launched stock for the first time without commission, a lot of our competitors or incumbents were saying that, you know, it was not something possible — and then they all kind of replicated the model.

Um, and same thing for crypto, you know, we offer the lowest cost on average on crypto. Um, and if you enable some features like volume tier for example, you can get as low as three basis points.

AI 如何重塑用户体验：Cortex Digest 与 Cortex Assistant

主持人： How would you use AI to make your fees even lower?

Johann： I think for us the AI advantage is going to be what are the new features that we can unlock for our customers.

So for example we launched Cortex Digest — um, that is an AI agent giving you all the information about the news for your portfolio, but not just at a summary level but also at detail level. So if you see the Bitcoin price moving for example, you tap on the notification — all our customers were asking like, "okay, Bitcoin is moving 5%, but I don't really understand why." Now we have this digest explaining to you what happened. And the goal is not to necessarily give a recommendation to the customer but give them all the information that they need to make the decision that they want to do on purchasing or not the asset.

Uh, but we didn't stop there. We also launched our Cortex Assistant, and you can actually interact with the agent and understand more like your strategy around your own portfolio. And so we will understand a bit more what is going on for you before we can actually give you information about your portfolio.

5–10 年愿景：成为全球超级 App

主持人： What is Robinhood trying to be in 5 to 10 years?

Johann： Um, you know, at this point we are just trying to become this super app not just in the US but also internationally.

Uh, we recently received approval in different regions. So we launched in the EU for example, we launched in the UK a couple of years ago. Um, we also have our exchange named Bitstamp that is live in Singapore for example. Um, and so for us the goal is to really start expanding what has been a success in the US but also outside of the US.

主持人： For someone who has never used Robinhood, how do you explain to people why are you better than other competitors?

Johann： Yeah, I think at this point what we've been saying is that you're basically at a disadvantage if you're not using Robinhood to invest.

Uh, you know, we have a full suite of products in the US — from a credit card, banking, to investing not just in one specific category but in an array of investments. Um, and really all the information that we are gathering when you interact with all our different products — we are able to help you across your journey to really make the right decision.

And we think it's really important to double click on this, right? Like with the advance of AI, being able to have information about your portfolio — not just about generic like what you will see on the press — is very important.

主持人： Do you think your discussion internally and the ambition is different? Like what is the discussion like within the company, what is the ambition?

Johann： Oh, the ambition is the same: being the super app everywhere in the world.

And I think, you know, for us we are a very big company in the US uh with 27 million net accounts, um but we are still very small outside of the US. And so for us the question is really how do we get to the next level outside. We also have our Robinhood wallet which is a self-custody wallet available in 100+ countries. Um, so, you know, we want to make sure that we can grow across the globe.

加密投资趋势：比特币还香吗？

主持人： Within the crypto arm of Robinhood, which is what you're focused on, what have investor trends taught us about sentiment in the crypto space? What do investors like right now? What do they dislike?

Johann： Well, you know, we've definitely seen the market pulling back a bit in term of volume. Um, and I think it's understandable, right? Like the action has been very cyclical.

Um, but we still see a lot of our customer segment that are investing, taking the chance of buying the dip, uh, these kind of things. And we've been building a lot of tools around these type of moments. Uh, so we have recurring investment for example. So a lot of our customer are using to dollar cost average on Bitcoin. Uh, we also have new order types like stop-loss for example to make sure that if the price of an asset goes too low you can actually exit right away.

Um, and I think it's been very important — a lot of our customers are on the more advanced side of things and so they want to have these tools that were not existing before in Robinhood.

主持人： So for example, I've heard the narrative that retail investors, the people who use Robinhood, have moved on from Bitcoin because it's become too boring and old, and now they're doing everything except Bitcoin. Um, has the data proven this theory?

Johann： No, I don't think that's correct. You know, Bitcoin is still one of our top assets on the platform — uh, and by far.

So um, I think Bitcoin has also the value of being this like store of value and safe type of asset. So I don't think people are bored necessarily, but I think where it might be true is that people want to do more with their assets. They want to go on DeFi, they want to go on web 3, they want to engage with DeFi protocols. And so for that they are often using Robinhood to buy some specific assets and then transferring them on their software wallet.

房地产代币化：机会还是新泡沫？

主持人： You also work on tokenization, right? Um, I want to ask you about tokenizing real estate. So, a quick interesting fun fact: in Taiwan right now in Taipei, it costs 185 months of household salary for an average apartment. So that is very expensive. Now, if we tokenize real estate or housing like stock split, that would drive up demand because now I can trade 1% of that real estate. Does that just drive up the price even more?

Johann： Um, I think it all depends, right? Like you always have this question of demand and offer.

Um, I think making assets more liquid doesn't necessarily mean that the asset will be going up. Uh, but I think it will give access to more people because right now — to your point — buying the entire apartment or household might be expensive, but buying a fraction of it might be actually more accessible to a lot of the population. So um, I think it will depend.

The other advantage of tokenization I think is that it removes a lot of companies and people in the middle. Um, so I don't really know how it works in Taipei, but for example, in the US, you have a lot of costs that are kind of in the middle for like just getting your mortgage, understanding the underlying assets and everything. And if we're able to streamline all of this using smart contracts, using mass of the smart contract, uh, we should actually be able to reduce the cost to get a mortgage for example, and so make it more accessible for more people to get into real estate.

主持人： What would that look like in 5 years or sooner? Let's say I want to buy a house and you tokenize my mortgage. What would that look like? Like how is it different from the process now?

Johann： I think it won't be very different at the end of the day, but you will just remove a lot of these banks and underlayers that are in between. And so you will be able to reduce the cost a little bit — because the less companies are in between, the everyone taking a cut, you are able to cut that and you are able to reduce the cost.

Um, but you know, for us we do see that most assets in the future are going to be tokenized. Um, it just makes it a lot more easy to custody, to see the trace case of ownership. Um, and so I think it should streamline things quite a bit.

代币化股票 & 与监管共舞

主持人： When can we start trading a tokenized share of Robinhood on Robinhood?

Johann： Uh, well, we already have our stock tokens, uh, you know, in the EU for example, that have about 2,000 US stocks and ETFs that are represented on chain. Um, Hood is one of them.

Um, but you know, for us the main idea about this stock token is to be able to give access to more assets. So right now we only did US stocks and ETFs, but we think in the future we can open to more exchanges, private equity, maybe real estate, and more.

主持人： So when can I send a Hood stock to you? Like can I transfer ownership? That is the idea.

Johann： Currently we are not offering DeFi, but it is something that we're excited about. Um, when we announced our project last year, we had a three-phase plan. Uh, we are still at the beginning of it, but we will give an update on the phases pretty soon.

主持人： Well, technically it could work if governments do not stop you.

Johann： Uh yeah, I mean any product that we launch, we work with regulators. So that's something that, you know, we've never taken a shortcut on regulation. Um, you know, we have multiple licenses in Europe, in the US, in Singapore. Um, and for us, it's extremely important to work with them, to show them the products that we want to launch. Um, so that, you know, what you just described doesn't happen.

主持人： Yeah, I totally understand and I love what you guys are doing. But my question is, we want this grand vision of, you know, a farmer in Indonesia — he or she can buy a piece of Manhattan real estate or US equity on their phones. But governments do not want that because they want some sort of capital control or market control, right? How do you see that play out?

Johann： Um, again, for us the idea will be to work with the government. So for example, with Indonesia, we announced acquisition of two companies that are Indonesia based. One is a crypto company and one is a brokerage.

Um, and you know, we really think that if we're able to work with them and show them the value of bringing the population into the economy, it will actually be beneficial for the economy and the population as well.

主持人： So you guys are actively talking to governments and, you know —

Johann： Absolutely, all of them.

主持人： I love that.

M2 货币供应量与交易量的关联

主持人： I'm going to throw you a bit of a curveball in terms of numbers here. Um, this is the St. Louis Fed M2 money supply in annual percentage terms.

Johann： Okay.

主持人： So if I were to overlay this with trading volume on Robinhood, what would you expect the correlation to be?

Johann： Well, that will really depend I think on —

主持人： Money supply increased. This was during the height of the COVID era and also the meme stock craze era that partly drove Robinhood popularity globally. And then shortly after, the Fed engaged quantitative tightening and now we're seeing what they call QE light. I wonder how much liquidity is directly tied to trading volume on exchanges like Robinhood.

Johann： Yeah, I will say for us, you know, the focus has been more on diversifying the business. Because to your point in 2021, a lot of our revenue was coming from pure trading activity.

Um, since then, you know, we've been diversifying a lot. Right now, we have 11 different businesses making $100 million in revenue or more.

Um, and I think that's really the beauty of Robinhood as a company — meaning that if there is a shortage of liquidity on trading for example, we will have other businesses that will probably benefit from what is going on. So for example, when the credit rates are changing, it's either trading that we see a bit more happening, or we see a bit more people just saving money. Um, and so in both situations Robinhood is able to generate revenue.

And I think that's what's really important. You know, we didn't just stop at pure trading — we also launched recently a credit card, the banking application, we have the prediction markets that you just mentioned. So, you know, for us, no matter what happens in the market, we want to make sure that Robinhood can actually benefit from it.

有趣的插曲：现金上门配送服务

主持人： That reminded me of I think a couple years ago, you guys had a cash delivery service.

Johann： Yes, we still have it.

主持人： Okay. What is that about? I've never had a chance to use it, but —

Johann： Um, basically if you're using our banking system, you can, you know, ask for — instead of going to the ATM — you can ask for the cash to be delivered to you. Uh, which is a good way to save time because sometimes you want to go on a trip or you need to pay somebody but you didn't have time to go to the ATM, and you can have someone meeting you with the envelope.

主持人： I love that during Chinese New Year, man. Uh, we don't have that in Canada. Unfortunately, not to the same extent as Bonnie. That would be a lot of fun.

代币化时代的价值归属

主持人： Now, if assets become more tokenized, who captures the value?

Johann： Well, uh, you know, for a lot of cases, we actually want to give it back to customers.

So if you think about stablecoin — which is probably the most tokenized asset that people are talking about, like the US dollar being tokenized — um, we've been advocating publicly that we want to pass the yield to the customer and we want to make sure that they are able to benefit from it. Right now we feel like a customer is almost at a disadvantage if they use stablecoin versus just holding cash on Robinhood, because on cash we're able to pass a yield — not on stable — until the clouds define this a bit more clearly.

So I think it's going to be similar to some of these other assets that you're talking about. If there are ways to generate money on it, uh, for us the goal will be to find a way to give it back to the customer.

不同地区的加密用户差异

主持人： You're operating across several different continents. I wonder if the data shows a clear delineation or difference in terms of consumer and retail investor interest in certain cryptos between Asia, Europe and North America or any other region that you operate in.

Johann： Um, yeah, we have different customer segments everywhere, and the geo location is also very different. So our centralized exchange is kind of live only in some specific jurisdictions. So mostly Europe, like I said Singapore before, and the US. Uh, but then we have our self-custody wallet that is available across the globe.

So um, you see different usage based on customer types. We have, you know, people that are more like buying and holding for a long time, and you have the very active traders and everything in between.

山寨币季何时到来？

主持人： You know, one of the great things — sorry, one more question I'll pass to Bonnie — one of the great things about being in your position is that you get to see user data in real time. And I think a lot of crypto users have been asking the question for months and months on end: when is altcoin season happening? I think they've probably given up asking that question. But what data indicators would you look at specifically from your platform Robinhood that could help answer that question?

Johann： Uh, usually I will say DeFi activity, you know, like the on-chain transfer, the number of transactions on chain — is when we see people getting more into that space.

Um, but you know, that's also something that always depends a bit of what is going on on the macroeconomics. And as always, you know, we also need utility, right? So when you see platforms that are being built on chain that drive utility, you see a lot more enthusiasm from customers than when it's just like a, you know, "we are hoping to see something in the future" of being developed.

全球化战略：财商教育与投资者养成

主持人： You guys are going global. And I recently watched your CEO's interview, and he said you guys are not trying to compete with other exchanges — rather, you guys want to be the first platform that people put their money in. So these monies could have been spent somewhere but they choose to invest those money. So that is what you're trying to do?

Johann： Yeah, I think it's really important to be early in the investor journey.

Um, I think for a long time, financial education was not necessarily part of what you learn in high school or in middle school. Um, but the sooner you can start thinking about your investment, about your retirement, about the long term, the sooner it is that you start having the right question and starting to build the habit to save, to invest on a regular basis.

Um, and so I think for us it's important that we are here no matter your stage of the journey. We invest a lot on education and learning tools. Um, so that the first time that you open the app you understand what's going on — you understand like what is a limit order, what is a market order, why should I invest in XYZ. And that's why also all these AI tools are really important, because we can give you a lot more information than if you were just like opening the app for the first time without any of that.

Um, and then when you start building on top of Robinhood, you're basically building a different profile. And when you get to 30 years old or when you get to retirement, you have an actual portfolio that you've been able to manage with time.

年轻投资者：更冒险还是更谨慎？

主持人： I've heard this theory, so when you're younger, you should take more risk. You should learn to invest because you don't have that much to lose. Does data show you that investors actually become better over time, or —

Johann： Um, I don't think we've looked at that specific question.

Um, and I don't think it's necessarily true that younger investors are taking more risk. I think it depends on many profiles — how did you grow up, like what is your net worth, what is your risk profile.

Um, we see a lot of younger investors now thinking more about their retirement than in the past for example, because in the past there was like more confidence on pensions and all these things. So, you know, things have changed quite a bit. Uh, what I've been seeing is also that more and more younger investors are thinking about their future, which I think is a great step forward.

主持人： What is telling you that? Is it how they behave on the app, or —

Johann： Well, if you look at some of our competitors and the average age, and you look at Robinhood's for example average age, you will see that a lot of our customers are a bit younger because they are starting earlier in their process.

Um, and I think it's part also of UX and UI — the fact that we're mobile first. Um, all of that really made it in a way that people don't necessarily dread the idea of like calling an investor or something on the phone or their bank. Uh, they can do all of this on the phone whenever they want. We have 24/5 for stock trading for example, allowing you to even trade during the week. So I think it's important.

最后的问题：5 年后 AI 交易占比？

主持人： I have one final question for you. How much of total trading volume do you think will be AI bots in 5 years time? If you have to take a guess.

Johann： It's hard to know because, you know, the hedge funds are already doing a lot of —

主持人： Even retail investors all over Twitter — I'm seeing people writing tutorials on how to use Claude to build a bot for trading.

Johann： Absolutely. But my point was more like, you know, the hedge funds for example and big shops are already using AI a lot, and so they have a massive amount of portfolio that they are using to trade. And so I think it's hard to say like how much retail is going to change, but I do think AI is going to be part of your journey in the future.

You can get a lot of information very easily and I think that's something that we want to make sure that people can use Robinhood for. So for example, we have a crypto API that you can connect to whatever AI system that you want to use. Um, it's very simple and you can start trading on your account with it.

So we think, you know, it's something that is here to stay. Uh, people are going to start implementing more and more of these tools. Um, and you know, I wouldn't even wait 5 years to see a decent amount of volume being AI powered in some way.

亚洲扩张计划

主持人： A lot of centralized exchanges started in Asia and we want Robinhood here too. Do you have any plans expanding to any Chinese speaking regions, aside from Singapore?

Johann： Uh, definitely plans at some point to be more and more global. Uh, right now we've been focusing on Singapore and Indonesia like I was mentioning earlier, and we will basically go from there. But I think for us, making sure that we can be a great partner in this region is very important, and it's one of our top priorities.

主持人： Okay. Is there a timeline that we can expect?

Johann： Uh, not at this point.

主持人： Johan, thank you very much for giving us an update on Robinhood. Best of luck with your launch of Robinhood chain, and we'll talk soon.

Johann： All right. See you soon. Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反直觉的观察：Robinhood 的真正对手不是券商，而是"时间的稀缺性"
大多数人把 Robinhood 理解成"零佣金券商 + 加密货币"。但仔细听 Johann 的话，他反复强调一件事：**"be early in the investor journey"**——要在用户金融旅程的最早期就出现。这暴露了一个惊人的战略真相：Robinhood 卖的不是交易，而是**"时间复利的入口权"**。
更值得玩味的是那个看似离题的"现金上门配送服务"。为什么一家券商要送现金？因为他们意识到：**用户走去 ATM 的那 15 分钟，就是他们流失注意力的窗口**。这和 TikTok、外卖平台的逻辑完全一致——**消灭一切"离开 App 的物理理由"**。Robinhood 表面在做金融，骨子里在做的是"用户时间的独占"，这与字节跳动、Meta 属于同一个物种。
## 跨领域连接：Robinhood 链的诞生，其实是"苹果 vs 安卓"故事在金融基础设施层的重演
Johann 解释为什么不用现成的 L2 时，用了一个非常关键的词：**"合规能做在链层，而不是智能合约层"**。这

item.title: [No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups] Travel Through the Lens of AI with with Booking.com CEO Glenn Fogel
item.dc:creator: No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：Fogel 的"护城河否定论"其实是护城河本身

大多数CEO会向投资人反复强调"我们的护城河很深"，但Fogel 反复说 "There is no such thing as a moat"。这句话表面像是承认脆弱，实则是一种认知层面的护城河——它内嵌了一种组织级的"永久恐慌"。

有意思的是，这与 Andy Grove 的 "Only the paranoid survive"、贝索斯的 "Day 1" 心态、以及生物学上的"红皇后假说"（Red Queen Hypothesis：为了留在原地必须不断奔跑）形成了一条隐秘的思想谱系。Booking 真正的护城河不是 860 万套房源，而是 25,000 名员工共同持有"没有护城河"这个信念——这才是最难被模仿的资产。竞争对手可以复制库存，可以复制 UI，甚至可以复制 AI 模型，但很难复制一种集体性的、持续 27 年的不安全感。

跨领域连接：旅行搜索的困境，其实是"婚姻决策学"的翻版

Fogel 讲了一个很坦诚的细节——他和妻子会为"谁来做旅行规划"而争吵。这个私人瞬间意外地揭示了整个 OTA 行业的底层逻辑：

旅行预订不是信息问题，而是"决策疲劳的转移"问题。

这与行为经济学家 Barry Schwartz 的 "The Paradox of Choice"、以及离婚律师圈子里一个冷知识——"家务分工中，决策劳动（decision labor）比体力劳动更消耗关系"——是同一件事。

这解释了一个我以前没想通的现象：为什么 Google 拥有全世界最好的搜索，却始终打不过 Booking？因为 Google 提供的是"更多选项"，而 Booking 售卖的其实是"让某个人/某个系统替我承担选择的痛苦"。Penny（AI agent）之所以转化率快速提升，不是因为它更"聪明"，而是它进入了一个更深的心理生态位——充当那个"愿意做决定的伴侣"。

未来的 AI travel agent 之战，本质上是一场"代理决策权"的争夺战，而不是搜索精度之战。谁能让用户最舒服地把决策权交出去，谁就赢。

隐藏的系统性真相：Fogel 的"三次死亡"决定了 Booking 的组织结构

注意他讲述人生的方式：父亲去世 → 被裁员 → 祖母去世 → 狗也死了 → 开始写小说 → 相亲遇到妻子 → 加入 Priceline。

这不是一段励志故事，这是一个"完全清零后重启"的样本。而他随后加入 Priceline 后立刻遭遇股价从 300 亿跌到几亿、$1 保命价、反向拆股——他的职业生涯是在一具尸体上开始的。

这里有一个几乎没人指出的系统性联系：Booking 之所以是全世界少数能在 OTA 里做出高利润率的公司，是因为它的高管群体经历过"完全归零"。你会发现，Fogel 在讨论资本配置时（回购 40% 股份、$3.6B buyback、"用不好就还给股东"）表现出一种近乎宗教般的克制——这不是 MBA 教出来的，这是从 $1 股价的深渊里爬出来的人才有的资本纪律。

跨领域连接：这与创伤心理学中的 "post-traumatic growth"（创伤后成长）研究一致——经历过组织性濒死的公司（Netflix 邮寄 DVD 到流媒体、Adobe 从盒装到订阅、微软从鲍尔默到纳德拉）往往表现出更高的资本回报纪律。反过来说，从未死过的公司（比如很多今天 30-50B ARR 的 AI 公司），一旦泡沫破裂将没有"细胞记忆"来处理——这才是 Fogel 在类比 1999 时真正想说但没明说的话。

一个让人不安的推论：AI 时代最危险的不是失业，而是"选择的假象"

Fogel 谈到 50 岁卡车司机的困境，谈到民主政治中人们说话不诚实。把这两点合起来，会浮现一个隐藏结论：

AI 不会创造大规模失业，但它会大规模消灭"中年人的重新选择权"。

在过去，一个 45 岁的人还能"再选一次职业"。但 AI 的迭代速度让"重新学习一门手艺"变成一场追不上的赛跑。这与 Fogel 反复说的 "choose wisely, you only get one life" 形成了一个可怕的镜像——他这句话是对年轻人说的，但真正的受害者是那些"年轻时以为自己选对了"的中年人。

这也解释了为什么 Booking 内部把 "upskilling" 提到了 CHRO 每周汇报级别——他们不是在做 CSR，他们是在防止组织内部出现"选择僵尸"：技能过时但不敢承认、心态封闭但仍在关键岗位上的人。这才是所有大公司未来 5 年最真实的隐形危机。

📝 转录文本

开场：没有护城河的信条

There is no such thing as a moat. There is no such thing as somewhere you're going to be protected against innovation. Today we have a competitive advantage on areas absolutely, but those can go away tomorrow. The only way to really long term is to continue to develop new services, new ways to do things. How can we do your business better? What do you need? Where you need more demand? Where are you hurting? It's so much more complex. If you think you're just going to come in and do this business and knock away these very big players, I say you should really understand what the business is before you decide to commit your capital.

主持人引入

Today I know Prior, we're talking with Glenn Fogle, the CEO of Booking Holdings, a 20-some year veteran of the company who joined when it was just Priceline and worth a few hundred million in the year 2000, and has since helped grow it to a hundred billion plus company with multiple massive assets across travel that are now being used in the AI era.

We will talk about a variety of topics with Glenn that span everything from the growth and changes that have happened in Bookings over time, as well as his own career and perspective on AI.

So Glenn, thank you so much for joining us today.

Well, thank you very much for having me.

Glenn 的早年经历：从 Wharton 到大型机操作员

Yeah, so you've had a really interesting career. You went to Wharton, you went to Harvard Law, along the way you worked in I think the MIS program at Morgantown. I'd love to just hear a little about your early background and how that led you eventually to Booking.com and eventually Booking Holdings.

Yeah, I can do that. So, real short, I came out of Wharton with a green finance and I end up in the back office in MIS. My first job is putting tapes on a drive in a data center of IBM 384s and the discs that went and the tape drives, and putting the tapes on the drive. That's where I started as an operator, actually being an operator of a mainframe. Very different — nothing that you prepared for that in college for that.

And I then become a developer, but I basically learned that this is not a career for me, I should do something else.

转型：法学院与华尔街

And all the people I knew undergrad were gone off to investment banking and made all this money and I thought I should try to do that. But you can't go — I've now gotten down on one shoot and you can't just jump out of that one to become an investment banker. So, I got a degree in finance, and Harvard led me into law school, so I'll do that because that's another route. And I did that and I end up getting a job on Wall Street as a banker.

I did that till 1995, and then the bank was bought by another bank and they fired all the bankers — almost. Not every bank, they fired most of them, including me. But not everybody, because they fired everybody — say, well, they fired everybody but they didn't fire everybody. So there was actually some people were picked to stay. I was not one of them. That's pretty bad.

So a really good lesson, though, having been fired and knowing what it's like. That is something I've kept with me throughout my career about how to do it right, not do it wrong, and understand what goes through the other person when you tell them that "I'm sorry, but there's no longer a room for you here and this is not the place for you to be." And I really learned that firsthand being on that side of the table.

人生低谷与一本没出版的小说

And so now I'm unemployed and my father had just died not that long before this happened. And my father's died, lost the job. My grandmother died. And even the dog died, which is kind of sad. And I'm like, what do I want to do with my life now? My young 30s, I'm alone. I'm saying whatever.

And I said, you know, I always wanted to write a book. So I start writing a book. I write a novel. I get it done. And now I'm going to try and get it published. And it's not really self-publishing by anyone on your own — you're trying to get some agent to pick it up.

And I was introduced to a woman as a blind date from a friend. And I think she was a lawyer, but I don't know. They said, "Well, she used to work at Random House as an editor." I said, "Oh, I'm very interested in having." And so we have a date, and the book never gets published, but I do end up marrying her and have two great kids and it's a wonderful life.

But while I was trying to get an agent to be interested in it, eventually she said, "You know, if this relationship's going to go forward, you should get a job." I said, "Damn, the boy." I didn't want to go back to banking.

回归华尔街，与偶遇 Priceline

And a friend of mine from law school was a senior-type person at Morgan Stanley, and I told him, "Look, Amy says I have to get a job." He said, "Well, we have this trading position here that you could do." I've never traded anything in my life. "Ah, don't worry, you'll be fine." Okay.

So I end up being head trader for a guy named Barton Bay, who's kind of a Wall Street legend. I do that for a number of years but I just don't like it. It's just not for me.

And that's when the internet was really taking off — that first real explosion, the internet boom. Late 90s. So 1999, I started trying to interview and I think, "Well, I got some skills. When I was an IT person, so I got that, and I know a little bit about corporate development being a banker." And I started interviewing the only real company on the East Coast at the time of internet abilities — Priceline. And they had a job for corporate development. Perfect.

加入 Priceline：从 $30B 到 $1 一股

So I get an offer from them, but I said I want to wait till I get my bonus for 1999, which gets paid at the end of February 2000.
