item.title: [Lex Clips] The greatest military secret weapon in history - Greek Fire | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"国家机密"作为一种制度技术——希腊火的真正秘密不是配方

大多数人听到希腊火，第一反应是"那是种化学武器"。但真正令人震惊的是：一千多年过去，历史学家至今仍在争论它到底怎么运作。这不是化学的胜利，而是保密制度的胜利。

Kaldellis 提到"我们还在争论它——这本身就说明他们守密守得极好"。这句话反过来推，我们能得出一个反直觉的结论：拜占庭在情报隔离、专业分工、跨代际知识管控方面，比同时代任何政权都更接近现代军工复合体。它建立了世界上最早的"曼哈顿计划式"组织形态——专职工程师核心、原料链条分割、部署权集中、知识不外传。希腊火之所以是"国家秘密"，不是因为它稀有，而是因为帝国建成了一套能让秘密不泄漏的官僚神经系统。

一个跨领域的意外连接：现代半导体产业的 EUV 光刻机、可口可乐配方、KFC 香料，这些"活着的秘密"能延续，靠的不是加密算法，而是组织的分工结构。拜占庭在 7 世纪就跑通了这套逻辑——它保护的不是分子式，是组织。

二、"手榴弹版希腊火"揭示了一个被忽视的战争经济学原理

对话里有一个极容易被忽略的细节：手榴弹版本的希腊火"对人不划算，对攻城器械才划算"。

这句话看似技术评论，其实是极其现代的成本-效益推理。在一个据说"以宗教为核心动力"的中世纪帝国里，出现"一枚燃烧弹烧一个人是浪费"这种冷冰冰的边际收益判断，是很惊人的。它说明拜占庭的军事思维根本不是我们想象的那种"英雄式冲锋"，而是资源约束下的运筹学——很像现代美军对导弹单价与目标价值的计算模型。

跨领域延伸：这与今天无人机战争在乌克兰的演化路径几乎一模一样——廉价燃烧武器 vs. 高价值静态目标（攻城器械 = 当年的雷达站和补给车）。1300 年前的希腊火手榴弹，本质上就是FPV 无人机的思想原型：低成本、单次消耗、专打价值不对称的目标。

三、真正救了帝国的，不是希腊火，而是"承认自己变小"

大多数叙事把帝国存续归功于武器、城墙、狮心统帅利奥三世。但 Kaldellis 反复强调一句被观众容易忽略的话："这是一次痛苦的行政重组"。

这里藏着最反直觉的洞见：帝国的存活不来自"反攻"，而来自"缩小并加固内核"。他们把军队从叙利亚、埃及撤回小亚细亚，重构后勤、财政、征兵体系。这是一次典型的"战略性收缩"——但收缩之后的东西不是残骸，而是一个更硬、更军事化、更集权的新形态。

这里可以做一个出人意料的连接：这与生物学中的 r/K 选择理论 惊人相似。前期的罗马帝国是 r 策略——版图大、扩张快、边界薄；7 世纪危机之后的拜占庭切换成 K 策略——版图小、密度高、投资深。它不是"衰落中的罗马"，而是"进化后的另一物种"。

更进一步的跨领域连接：这也几乎完全对应 Clayton Christensen 的"颠覆式创新中的在位者困境"逆命题——通常大公司死于不愿放弃旧市场；而拜占庭做了几乎所有大帝国做不到的事——主动割肉、主动缩表、主动重构组织架构。它的存活是历史上极少见的"成功的战略收缩"案例，比亚马逊放弃 Fire Phone、Netflix 放弃 DVD 都要更极端、更彻底、更成功。

一个隐藏的系统性真相

把三条线索合起来看，你会发现拜占庭在 7–8 世纪其实同时完成了三件被历史书低估的事：

  1. 知识的秘密化（希腊火作为组织秘密）
  2. 暴力的成本化（手榴弹式的资源计算）
  3. 规模的自我压缩（K 策略化的行政重组）

这三件事在现代管理学里对应：知识产权 + 单位经济 + 战略聚焦。

也就是说，拜占庭在公元 700 年前后，几乎跑通了现代企业存续的核心公式。它之所以能再撑 800 年，不是因为它是"古代"帝国，而是因为它在某种意义上，比同时代所有政权都更早地"现代化"了自己。

📝 转录文本

帝国如何自我重建

Lex: So how does the empire rebuild itself? You know, re-stabilize — because it doesn't collapse there, right?

Anthony Kaldellis: Yes, it survives. There's interesting low points for the empire where it still survives. This is one of them.

Lex: And that is just interesting — as the survival at the top and the flourishing survival at the bottom is also super interesting. So what happens? How does it come to stabilize?

Anthony Kaldellis: Very slowly. This is a painful process. And it takes them centuries, you could say.

Lex: Yeah.

先止血：守住防线

Anthony Kaldellis: First you got to stop the bleeding. Just try to hold the line. It takes them decades to just to hold the line. They get pummeled by the Arabs for decades in the 7th century, until finally by 660s, 670, they finally begin to beat them — beat them on land and hold the line on the sea.

And so we now have an investment in fleets. Eventually, you get the Greek fire, which is the flamethrowers. And when the Arabs try to take Constantinople, these attempts are defeated like twice. And that ensures the survival of the Roman state in some form.

希腊火：一个至今未解的国家机密

Lex: How were the flamethrowers used?

Anthony Kaldellis: Yes, it's an interesting question. We're still kind of debating that.

Lex: Oh, it's not fully understood?

Anthony Kaldellis: Well, it's a state secret. They didn't want this getting out. So they kept it pretty well. The fact that we're debating it will tell you that they were pretty good at keeping secrets.

So — possibly through a pressure valve.

Lex: Mhm.

Anthony Kaldellis: Right? So you have this flammable compound whose preparation and components is also a state secret. So there's a specialized core of engineers that prepares this and deploys it.

Lex: Mhm.

Anthony Kaldellis: And it's possibly through a heated pressurized container with a spigot, let's say, that unleashes this thing through a nozzle — and it's lit — and basically spews flaming napalm…

Lex: [gasps]

Anthony Kaldellis: …on other ships, and it will continue to burn even when it's on the water.

Lex: Mhm.

Anthony Kaldellis: Right? On the top. So you can't just swim away or anything. So this is a very, very destructive weapon.

从阿拉伯人到维京人：一件武器的长寿命

Lex: Terrifying.

Anthony Kaldellis: They used it against Vikings, too. This is much later. So once the Vikings show up —

Lex: Mhm.

Anthony Kaldellis: — like when they would raid, attacked Constantinople in the 940s, then again in the 1040s —

Lex: Mhm.

Anthony Kaldellis: — yeah, the Romans used Greek fire fleets to just incinerate them. And that'll teach Vikings a lesson. If you're in that situation, you'd rather serve under the emperors than attack them.

手榴弹版本的希腊火

Lex: And so this is a kind of nuclear-weapon-level state secret.

Anthony Kaldellis: They even had it in hand grenade form.

Lex: This is a real thing?

Anthony Kaldellis: Oh, yes.

Lex: Hand grenade form?

Anthony Kaldellis: Yes. So it's a clay container with the compound, and it has some sort of fuse that you light on the outside and you throw it.

Now — you can throw it against people, but it's not going to be that effective. Maybe you burn a person, whatever. You just wasted a hand grenade for one person. It's most effective against things like siege engines.

Lex: Mhm.

Anthony Kaldellis: You throw that on a wooden frame, and, you know — yeah.

Lex: This is incredible. So this is one of the things that finally stabilized — stopped the bleeding — from the military perspective.

军事化的行政重组

Anthony Kaldellis: Yeah, eventually. Now, they pulled the armies back into Asia Minor, so they're beginning to defend and fortify Asia Minor. Now, you have to find ways to pay and support and feed all of these soldiers, and this is a whole administrative — a wrenching administrative readjustment — but they go through it. It takes them a long time. Eventually, they emerge with a different kind of strategic logistical system.

Lex: So it's a smaller, harder core of the empire.

Anthony Kaldellis: That's right. Very militarized.

8 世纪：君士坦丁堡第二次围城

Lex: Very militarized. And then in the 8th century, the second Arab siege of Constantinople — that somehow fails.

Anthony Kaldellis: In the early 8th century, yes. That was the second siege.

Lex: Yeah, the early 8th century. So why did it fail? Can you tell the story of that siege? That was really the empire hanging by a thread.

Anthony Kaldellis: Yes, but it happened to have a very competent man in charge. This is Leo III, and he's an emperor who — he's a usurper. He's just seized power. They knew that this massive Arab attack was coming, and so it's quite possible that the Roman elites preferred to put someone capable in power at that moment.

The city was very well prepared. I encourage people in the audience to go and read the sources that we have for that. Or you can find many accounts of it online. It was just very, very well done.

保加尔盟友、希腊火与战略消耗

He also had Bulgar allies. So these are the Bulgars who had just crossed the Danube just a few years earlier, actually, and settled south of the Danube — where eventually Bulgaria emerged.

So he had Bulgar allies, and he used the Greek fire very, very strategically. He used his own fleet. He contained the Arabs to certain places where they kind of starved, and wore them down. Yeah, it's just very well done.

Lex: From a military perspective.

Anthony Kaldellis: From a military perspective.

是运气吗？

Lex: Was it the walls, the navy, the weather, the diplomacy — just good luck — that helped Constantinople hold?

Anthony Kaldellis: I don't see much role for good luck there. I mean, I think he had it all pretty planned out. It was all of the elements that you mentioned. And yeah, he just deployed them very, very astutely.

And went on to rule after that for another 20-some years. And his son continued. They formed a dynasty — we call it the Isaurian dynasty, though they were not from Isauria.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、"国家机密"作为一种制度技术——希腊火的真正秘密不是配方
大多数人听到希腊火，第一反应是"那是种化学武器"。但真正令人震惊的是：一千多年过去，历史学家至今仍在争论它到底怎么运作。这不是化学的胜利，而是**保密制度**的胜利。
Kaldellis 提到"我们还在争论它——这本身就说明他们守密守得极好"。这句话反过来推，我们能得出一个反直觉的结论：**拜占庭在情报隔离、专业分工、跨代际知识管控方面，比同时代任何政权都更接近现代军工复合体**。它建立了世界上最早的"曼哈顿计划式"组织形态——专职工程师核心、原料链条分割、部署权集中、知识不外传。希腊火之所以是"国家秘密"，不是因为它稀有，而是因为**帝国建成了一套能让秘密不泄漏的官僚神经系统**。
一个跨领域的意外连接：现代半导体产业的 EUV 光刻机、可口可乐配方、KFC 香料，这些"活着的秘密"能延续，靠的不是加密算法，而是**组织的分工结构**。拜占庭在 7 世纪就跑通了这套逻辑——它保护的不是分子式，是**组织**。
## 二、"手榴弹版希腊火"揭示了一个被忽视的战争经济学原理
对话里有一个极容易被忽略的细节：手榴

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1449期】付费都没有的内容：如何判断逆境反转，实例展示NKE✨20260705#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、隐藏的悖论：小涛在"教估值"的同时，其实在教"反估值直觉"

表面上这期是"如何判断逆境反转"，但如果你把三个案例（NKE、SBUX、MU）叠在一张图上看，你会发现一个反直觉的模式：估值先行的方向永远是错的方向。

  • 星巴克：估值从 20 倍飙到 35 倍时，EPS 还在 -46%、-35%、-19% 地跌 —— 估值涨了 92%，价格只涨 41%。
  • Nike 24 年中到 25 年中：估值从 20 多倍反弹到 45 倍（+102%），股价只涨 11%，然后市场"醒过来"再把估值砸回 23.7 倍。
  • 美光：市场喊着"要 rerate 到 15–20 倍"的时候，其实 25 年底早就买过 14 倍了。

意外的观察：小涛没有明说，但他实际上在描述一个"估值前置指标"—— 估值扩张先于基本面 1 到 2 年，但只有一半的时候会兑现。星巴克兑现了，Nike 第一次没兑现。也就是说，市场的"逆境反转预演"是一个精度不到 50% 的信号。这意味着，如果你把估值当作反转指标去做多，你的胜率其实和抛硬币相当 —— 真正让你赢的不是估值本身，而是估值 + 管理层是否承认错误这个复合条件。他反复强调"nike 管理层否认丢市占率"—— 那才是真正的滤网，估值只是入场券。

二、跨领域连接：Nike 的困境是"NBA 冠军曲线"，不是零售曲线

小涛在结尾提到管理层用 Knicks 拿 NBA 冠军做类比："十年磨一剑，给我们时间"。这个类比其实比他自己意识到的更精准。

跨领域的意外联结：Nike 面临的不是消费周期问题（cyclical），不是竞争问题（competitive），而是一个类似于职业体育队的"文化周期"问题——

  • On、Hoka 这类新品牌之于 Nike，就像雷霆、掘金之于湖人：它们不是在同一套体系里赢，而是换了一种打法（DTC、跑者社群、材料创新）。
  • Nike 想靠"更多创新 + 中国本地化"翻身，本质上是老球队大换血。历史上体育队的重建平均需要 3–5 年，这与市场预期 27 财年 Q3 才见拐点、28–29 年 EPS 增速 20% / 15% 高度吻合。
  • 更有意思的是：小涛提到微软"自研模型 + 用中国开源模型 + 保留 Anthropic"的多模型策略，本质上和 Nike 现在要做的"高端 + 本地设计 + 全球品牌"是同一种组织学范式—— 大公司在成熟期都必须从"押注单一路径"转向"投资组合式路径"。这条平行线小涛没点破，但它解释了为什么星巴克能反转（承认要重做产品组合），而 Nike 之前不能（否认丢份额 = 拒绝组合思维）。

三、被忽略的第四类公司，以及"估值修复"背后隐藏的时间套利

小涛把公司分成三类（讲情绪、贵得离谱如 Palantir、基本面追赶价格如英伟达）。但认真看他的星巴克分析，会发现他其实无意中定义了第四类：估值修复型公司。

这一类的特征是： - 估值和基本面同向但不同步，中间夹着 6–18 个月的时间差 - 这个时间差本身就是 alpha 的来源 - 它的胜率取决于一个非市场变量 —— 管理层承认错误的速度

惊人的发现：这个时间差可以量化。星巴克从 EPS 首次转负（24Q2）到财报确认反转（26 财年最新季度），花了大约 2 年。而估值从最低 20 倍开始反弹的时点，比 EPS 拐点早了大约 12 个月。也就是说，估值市场比基本面市场提前了 12 个月，但比事后诸葛提前了 24 个月——这中间的 12 个月，就是"相信管理层"的信仰溢价窗口。

更耐人寻味的是，小涛结尾强调"越低估值买 = 承担越大不确定性"—— 这句话听起来是废话，但它其实揭示了一个反常识的真相：逆境反转的最佳买点不是估值最低点，而是"估值刚开始扩张 + 管理层第一次说人话"的那一刻。用这个框架回看，Nike 现在正好处于这个窗口的入口 —— 估值已经杀到 5 年中位数以下，管理层刚刚在这次财报里第一次系统性地承认了中国市场的问题并给出路线图。这与其说是"逆境反转的开始"，不如说是信仰溢价窗口的开启日。

📝 转录文本

开场：从一条留言说起

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分析员小涛。

今天在我 X 下面有一条留言，让我非常的欣慰。和我分享的是，他说今年我教会他最多的就是怎么去看估值，并且怎么去运用这个估值去进行买入和买出。在过去这段时间，我和大家分享过很多标的的一些估值——贵的、便宜的，什么样的区间是一个合理的一个区间，那么希望可以给大家一些参考。

那讲到估值这一块，今天我再教大家——这时候教大家——就和大家分享一个方法：怎么把估值和基本面财报会结合在一起，去判断一家公司有没有可能会出现逆境反转。今天我们就来举一个例子，拿 Nike 的数据和财报会去判断一下接下去有没有可能会出现这个逆境反转。

好吧，那在和大家分享这一个方法之前，我们照例回顾一下市场的一个走势，然后去聊一下估值。我知道大家肯定非常关注芯片板块出现了下跌，OK，那现在芯片板块的估值在什么的位置？现在美光的估值在什么那个位置？然后有两条和 AI 相关的新闻和大家一起聊一下。

好吧，那我就废话不多说了，直接开始。在开之前，大家记得老规矩，记得留言、点赞、加转发，让更多的人可以看到我们这期内容。你们的点赞还是非常的有帮助啊，可以帮助这期视频的点击量可以稍微高一些。那么包括大家的一个留言也非常的有帮助。

一、市场回顾：债市、大盘与芯片板块

债市与利率

好吗，先回顾一下资产价格的一个走势。周二市场这边两年、十年期美债收益率都出现了上涨，TLT 再次出现了下跌，上涨的幅度还不小。然后 TLT 出现了下跌，YTD 到现在为止 TLT 下跌了 3%。那我今天去看了一下，9 月份加息的这个概率还是保持在 80% 左右。

十年期美债收率的这个上涨并不是凯文（鲍威尔）是想看到的。因为截止到今天，非但两年期的美债收率上涨、非但美联储加息的这个概率在上涨，然后 10 年期的利率从他开会到现在也出现了上涨，那就代表房贷利率出现了上涨。这并不是他想看到的一个结果，也并不是川普想看到这个结果。对于川普想要去刺激整个楼市的这个政策来说，并没有任何的帮助。

三大指数与板块

吧，三大指数这边都出现了下跌，标普 0.4，那只下跌的幅度比较大一些 1.2%，指下跌了 0.2%。今天非常有意思，当然原油反弹，然后 VIX 小幅的反弹，这边都还好；IGV 啊、罗素 2000 啊、比特币的跌幅都还好。

那今天非常有意思的一个点是：前期大科技股今天是在横盘的，标普的等权指数今天也只下跌了 0.1%，也就是等权指数没有跌，前期大科技股也没有跌，但是芯片板块今天的跌幅 5.1%。

芯片板块的回调

如果大家没有看过我上一期视频，对于下半年的这个讲完，我鼓励大家可以去看一下。包括我在上周三所在 APP 里面所做的这个直播，我回头挑一些片段出来和大家分享，我认为里面有些观点是值得大家可以参考。

而最近这段时间市场的回调，个别和芯片板块相关的一些标的，从最高点的回调幅度超过 50% 以上了，少一点的话 30% 多，一点的话 50% 以上。那标的本身就芯片板块硬件这块，我先放一边，主要是情绪面的这个堆积，然后最近这段时间下跌个 30% 到 50%，我认为是非常的正常。那很多芯片板块是有基本面的，有基本面的一些标的，我并不认为我们需要担心。

二、三类公司的分类框架

第一类：不赚钱、卖故事的公司

这是第一点，然后我觉得所有的标的其实可以分为三类，大致可以分为三类。

第一类标的就是不赚钱的公司，就是到现在都在售卖一个情绪，或在售卖未来的这个梦想。这一类的标的我认为是要非常小心的。因为这一类的标的事实一再证明，短期之内可以有情绪面、可以有故事先去把股价拖在那边，甚至于不断的破形高，但是这种情绪面是根本没有办法持续，也经不起市场的这个考验。一旦整个市场出现情绪面有些下跌，或者大盘出现下跌，这一类标的下跌的幅度绝对是 50% 以上。

为什么在前段时间我和大家举了一个例子，我说价值就我们看市值的话，100 个 billion 的这一类的公司，它类似于这一家公司，你就给他 25 倍的这个 PE，那就是代表这家公司一年的一个盈利，盈利啊是 net income 要到 4 个 billion 点，40 亿美金。那即使今天这家公司不赚钱，它的估值可以达 100 个 billion 点，没有问题。但我们自己要问自己，那在未来什么样的一个时间点——是在一年以后，3 年以后，5 年以后，还是在 10 年以后——这家公司的盈利有可能达到 4 个 billion 点。

我们知道盈利如果达到 4 个 billion，那起码假设你的 operating margin 是 40%，那也就说你的营收要达到 10 个 billion，price to sales ratio 100 个 billion market cap，100 个 billion 营收 110 个 billion，也就是 price to sales ratio 是 10 倍。10 倍已经是一个相对比较高的一个 price to sales ratio。就类似于这样的一个概念，我们要有就这家公司一个营收要达到 10 个倍的，100 亿美金啊。很少有公司其实营收可以达到 100 亿美金的，而且这个 margin 可以保持在 operating margin 可以保持在 40%，那么并且这么持续下去。

所以我们假设如果我们预计啊 5 年之后，我现在投这家公司有可能到那边的话，那就很简单，那就现在这家公司的估值起码是透支了未来 3 年。那如果现在这类的公司连盈利都没有做到的话，然后就达到 100 个 billion 的话，我们绝对对于这类公司要非常的小心。

你可以说服自己说我投这家公司，我就投 10 年也没有关系。既然如果你有这个 conviction 去投 10 年的话，那就必须要有这样一个定义：准备好在这未来的 10 年的区间里边，至少会出现 3 到 4 次这一类的公司直接腰斩，甚至 60% 70% 以上的一个回调幅度。我认为这是一类的公司，然后这一类的公司我认为是非常需要小心的。

第二类：Palantir 式的极高估值公司

然后第二类的公司我觉得是类似于是 Palantir 啊这类的公司。这一类的公司呢，估值可以非常的高，300 倍、400 倍的 PE 可以保持在高位非常长的一段时间，就保持不理性的时间可以非常的长，这时间跨度是一年到两年去计算的。

但是最终我们会发现市场还是会回归理性。那过去这一年左右的一个时情，一年不到的一个时间里边，Palantir 的一个价格出现下跌，但是它的每次的财报都非常的好，甚至于财报是在越来越好。那么但是它的一个价格下跌，为什么？这就是一个非常标准的市场在对 Palantir 进行估值修复，这个估值修复是从高修复到一个合理的位置。当然了，你单去看它的一个 PEG ratio、单去看它的一个 PE 或者 forward PE 的话，还是在一个相对比较贵的一个位置，但是它 EPS 的增速很快，所以它的一个 PEG ratio 现在就合理很多。这是第二类标的。

第三类：英伟达式基本面追赶股价

然后第三类标的我认为是以英伟达为代表。这个标的就是从 23 年、24 年、25 年至 3 年英伟达的一个走势，23、24、25 这三年，英伟达的走势非常非常的快速，但是英伟达的一个财报、它的 EPS、它的营收在 24 年和 25 年追赶的这个速度，也是历史上从来都没有看到过——那么大体量的公司，每一年还可以以这样的一个速度去增长，从未见过。

那所以英伟达是一家基本面就价格一定是价格先行，价格先行之后马上还没有等你这个情绪面退却之后，财报跟上，或者基本面跟上。23 年到 25 年，英伟达的这个走势其实是非常非常少见的。就你股价可以涨那么的多，然后基本面跟随股价的这个速度也可以跟得那么的快。

就简单来说，长期来说，彼得林奇不是说过吗，长期来说，股价和 EPS 这个增速是同比的，是一个正相关。EPS 每年增 15%，那你假设你 PE 不变的话，那你每年股价就涨 15%。那反过来，如果一家公司的股价翻了一倍，那是不是代表它的一个基本面——起码和以前就它的盈利，起码和以前去做对比的话——也要翻一倍。正常来说，一家公司盈利哪有今年和上年去做对比的话翻一倍，那么很少见的。

好吧，所以我们在买公司的时候，这就是我一直劝大家去注意这个 PE、一直去注意这个估值。买公司的时候，我们一定要分清到底是买哪一类的公司，那每一类的公司背后所代表的这个风险都是不一样的。Palantir 仍然是一家非常好的一个公司，只是估值过贵了。就前段时间只是估值过贵啊，这段时间进行估值的修复完之后，我认为它的一个 PEG ratio 已经完全进入一个合理区间了。

三、芯片板块与美光的估值判断

好吧，稍微绕开了一下，然后我们回到芯片板块的一个估值这边。那随着这段时间的一个下调，芯片板块的估值从前段时间最高的将近要 30 倍——29、29.6 倍吧，我觉得最高的是 29.6 倍——将近在接近 30 倍的时候出现了回调，现在回到了 23.1 倍。23.1 倍差不多是过去 4 年的一个中位数，相对是在一个比较合理的一个位置。那很多人认为啊比较合理的位置，接下去又一定要出现反弹？不一定的。那么它可以继续出现回调，但是再往下回调的话，整个芯片板块就会变得便宜了。

美光的估值故事

然后我们来看一下美光。那这条线是美光的一个 forward PE。一个多月之前对吧，市场在讨论的是什么？美光有没有可能 rerate？什么叫 rerate？就是把它的 PE 改成买成一个 15 倍的 PE、20 倍的一个 PE，那就是在 PE 在 11 倍、12 倍的时候。

那当时我拿这张图出来和大家分享，大家看完之后恍然大悟：哦，原来其实在 25 年年底的时候，市场就已经把美光的 PE 买到 14 倍了。所以在 10 倍 11 倍的时候，市场当时在讨论有没有可能把美光的 PE 买到 15 倍、20 倍，其实我认为就是一个纯故事线驱动，纯故事线索驱动，使得很多散户 FOMO 进去的一个故事线，就单纯的一个故事性而已。因为在 25 年的时候，市场早就把美光的估值买到比现在还要高，对吧。

所以我一直和大家分享的是，我觉得我的一个期待——对，在美光这一块——我觉得一个正常的一个期待是：你市场不需要对于它 rerate，不需要把它的 PE 定价到 15 倍或者是 20 倍，你可以持续保持在一个低 PE，因为你每一年 EPS 的这个增速已经非常非常的快了，你的股价每年能够涨个百分之六七十，已经是非常非常好的这个结果了。

那么当然在美光上一次财报之后，我说我看到了市场接下去有可能会对于美光的 rerate。然后底下就有人问我：那你为什么不买美光？我说我不愿意去给人抬轿子。我觉得很多时候我们要分清楚"一家好公司"和"好公司加上好价格"。那这两者，很多人认为好公司就是一个好价格，但是好公司不代表好价格。

特别是很多时候短期之内市场的这个情绪面，我们应该能够感受得到，而不是被这个情绪面所带着走。相反很多时候情绪面非常高涨的时候，要稍微冷静一下，问自己：那么现在非常高涨的那个情绪面是不是可持续的？现在的这个股价里面到底反映了多少这个情绪面？虽然我们没有办法，很多时候没有办法把它去量化，但是我们身在当下、身在市场里边，我们应该有一个非常深刻的一个理解。

啊，所以关于美光、关于芯片板块，如果跌下来，就现在已经跌下来，然后再继续往下跌的话，我认为就会是一个机会了。我并不认为这一波的 AI 走势已经结束了。当然，市场情绪面的一个消退所持续的这个时间，这个我并不知道，但是通常来说它所持续的这个时间不会太短。所以如果需要去开仓或者要去加仓的话，其实不需要太过着急。

四、AI 相关新闻：微软自研模型的深意

然后另外一条和 AI 相关的消息呢，最近这些大科技都有些消息放出来。今天微软有一条消息：微软内部试图去替换 OpenAI 和 Anthropic，然后使用自己的一个自研模型。

你想一下，从微软这个角度去出发，原本是 100% 压注于 OpenAI，后来看到了 Anthropic 在 enterprise 这一块的落地，明显比 OpenAI 要好，然后和 Anthropic 进行合作。现在再到了两周之前，选择把 DeepSeek 加入到他们模型的一个队列里边来。再到了今天的这条信息，我们试图去替代 OpenAI 和 Anthropic，用自己的一个自研模型。

当时微软有宣布自己自研模型的时候，市场里面很多人不理解。那么在很多散户的一个眼里，包括机构的一个眼里，他们认为：你自己弄一个自研模型没有意义啊，你现在明显两家大模型已经跑出来了，你再自己弄一个自研模型，难道去和这两家大模型竞争吗？没有任何的一个可能性你竞争得过他们。但其实微软去建自研模型本来就不是和这两家大模型去竞争的，这也就是很多人误解的这一个点。

用中国开源模型的经济学

就包括最近这段时间，我们看到中国的开源模型已经被美国的科技公司所使用了。那么包括 Quinn 贝的 CEO 说到，他们现在的 OpenAI 总共的一个花费保持不变，但是他们烧掉更多的 token，为什么？因为他们现在叫做 model optimization。

那吧，选择如果有必要，他们会选择用 OpenAI、用 Codex 或者用 Anthropic 的一个模型，但是如果没有这个必要的话，他们就会去选择中国的开源模型。为什么？因为中国的开源模型更加的便宜。所以微软选择用自研模型也是一样的一个道理，不是 100% 的应用场景都需要去选择这两家大模型，因为这两家大模型贵啊。而相反，非常有可能背后底层的客户 80% 这个需求，只需要自研模型就可以解决了。

Token 价格与 AI Agent 的落地闭环

另外一点，未来 AI Agent 在 B 端或者在 C 端这一块要落地的话，那 token 的一个价格必须要下降，或者说用 edge AI 就是离线的这一个模型。那离线的模型对于硬件的这个需求又非常的高，然后硬件现在的一个 infra flash 现在通畅又非常的高。所以感觉是成了一个没有办法完成的一个闭环。所以就看到底是 API 的这个价格先——所以 API 的价格下来，其实对于硬件也好、对于所有东西落地，真的是一件非常好的一个事情。

但现在因为 coding 这一块落地非常的好，然后市场又非常的大，科技公司又愿意花钱，所以基本上 coding 占据了很多这些大厂大厂背后的这个算力，而同时 AI coding 这一块的一个落地，也把其他方面的一些落地往后去挤了。所以大科技也好、或者其他的科技公司也好，有自研模型是一定是正确的一条道路。因为自研模型有可能可以解决百分之 50 这个场景，或者 50% 以上的这个场景啊，这个场景通过自研模型去解决的话，这个成本会比使用大模型去调用大模型的这个 API 便宜非常多。

五、如何判断逆境反转：以 Nike 为例

然后我们就来聊一下，怎么去判断一家公司有没有可能会出现这个逆境反转。那我一步一步带大家推理下去。

首先：管理层是否承认错误

首先我们先来看一下 Nike 这家公司。大家应该已经知道，我差不多一两年前我就已经说过，我记得非常清楚，当时我在聊 Nike 财报的时候，我说一家公司的管理层……首先，市场对于 Nike 有一个误解：当时市场认为 Nike 的一个下跌是消费者情绪也非常的低，所以 Nike 下跌。但是我说消并不是消费者弱，而是 Nike 自己公司本身出现了问题。

很多人把 Nike 股价的下跌，约等同于 consumer 的消费非常的虚弱，这完全没办法画上等号。 一个是 company specific 公司内部本身出现的问题，一个是整体像美国的一个消费情况，两者之间，我看到有人去画上等号，完全是非常错误的一个结论。

所以 Nike，我记得是一两年前，我忘了具体什么事，我记得非常清楚是那一个季度财报，我听完之后，我说当时管理层说"我们的市场份额完全没有被抢走"——我说你错了。对吗？因为我自己原本是投资过 Nike 的一家竞品，那么 On Holding 这家公司 ONON 我是在 25 年买的，然后 25 年的后半年是卖掉，那这一波是翻了一倍，然后之后卖掉。然后现在其实 On Holding 走的也不好。因为当时我说个 On，我对于 On Holding 的一个期待就是可能和 Lululemon 类似——On Holding 市值的只能 40% 50% 左右，就是潜在的一个市场涨到了那就卖。所以当时在 On Holding 上的一个买点和卖点都是非常的不错。

但是也正是因为我投资了 On Holding，我才知道 On Holding 和 Hoka 这两家公司抢了非常多 Nike 的市场份额。但是管理层在财报会里面讲到"我们的市场份额完全没有被任何的一家公司给抢走"。因为我从一个竞争者，我投资了他们的这个竞品，然后 Nike 却告诉我——就明明股价下跌、明明市场在惩罚你，但公司管理层却不承认错误、不去正面的面对错误——那这家公司是没得救的。

所以接下去我们看到 Nike 的股价几乎是一直低迷，这一个逆境反转的一个困境，直到现在都没有，就直到现在还是在逆境里面，还没有出现一个反转的信号——起码从股价上还看不出来。所以当时我就认为这起码在财报会里面给我是一个非常大的一个 red flag。但这类的公司完全是不值得去投，因为管理层根本就没有意识到这个问题。

传统品牌的共通点：缺乏创新

而这类的公司像 Nike 啊，就传统公司里边——Nike、Lululemon 和星巴克这三家公司我认为最大的一个共通点就是缺乏创新，就是吃老本。而吃老本吃到后面，随着新的竞争者进来之后，自然自己原本的这个业务就受到了破坏。

然后现在其实星巴克已经慢慢的走出来，稍后我有星巴克的一些数据和大家分享。星巴克的估值现在其实已经又去到了一个非常高的一个位置，那也是随着他们基本面的这个回归，估值出现了这个回归。

Nike 的估值现状

好吧，那先来看一下 Nike。那 Nike 截止到今天的收盘 23.7 倍，就 24 倍的这个估值。虽然 24 倍的这个估值对于一家价值股来说仍然是在一个相对比较高的位置，但是你如果横向去做对比的话，看过去 5 年 Nike 的这个估值，现在已经是在一个什么区间？已经是在一个相对比较低的位置。

或者我们可以这么理解，那么市场一直给 Nike 有一个估值上的一个 premium，一直有一个溢价在那边。那平均的时候平均的这个估值差不多是在 30 倍，高的时候一度去到了 40 多倍。而且我这边看的是一个 forward，是一个动态的一个市盈率，并不是一个静态的市盈率。

估值先行的信号与失败

我们来看这张图。图里白色这条线是前面 Nike 的这个估值完全不变，然后我加了一条线蓝色这条线，蓝色这条线是 Nike 的一个股价。你可以看到从 21 年股价最高 180 跌到了现在股价多少 40 多块钱。

在 24 年年中一直到 25 年年中这段时间，我们看到白色这条线估值出现了反弹，幅度非常的大，差不多啊也是当时在 20 多倍，然后一直反弹到 40 多倍，要将近要 45 倍。估值反弹了多少？反弹了 102%。但是我们看蓝色这条线价格呢，当时股价只上涨了 11%。说明什么？说明 100% 的这个估值的一个翻倍里面，市场在预期什么？市场在预期它的一个基本面的回归，市场在预期它的一个逆境反转。 为什么？因为当时换了 CEO。

但是在 25 年的下半年，市场意识到——在过去一年对于 Nike 基本面的这个乐观、对于他们逆境反转的这个期待——时间点错了、过早。那么一旦等市场意识到这一点时候，那怎么会意识到？就是因为财报出来之后越来越差。那么完全没有任何逆境反转的这个信号，那市场慢慢的意识到之后，再次估值从最高的 43、44 倍，跌到了周二的收盘 23.7 倍。

所以现在估值又来到了一个相对比较低的这个区间，又出现了一个有可能会出现逆境翻转的一个 setup 或者条件之一——估值在一个低位，已经满足了。

Nike 的 EPS 数据

我们来看一下基本面的一些数据。EPS 体量： - 2024 年：3.95（每股收益 3.95） - 25 年：2.16（同比增速 -45%） - 26 年：从 2.16 跌到了 1.58（同比增速继续下跌 27%）

当然他们的 26 年这边是他们 26 财年，并不是我们说的一个 calendar，也就是他们的财年和 calendar 也稍微有一些差别。

然后我们看一下刚刚发布财报，就上周二发布财报，第四季度这个财报同比增速 43%，看起来好像是出现了一个逆境反弹。但其实并没有：同比增速 43% 是因为一次性的一个退税，就是原本他们因为受到关税的这个影响，然后 IEEPA 这一次川普关税被判违法嘛，所以这个钱就退到他们，退还的这个钱是一个 billion 点。所以这一个 billion 夹到了这一个季度的 EPS 回来之后变成了 43% 的一个同比增速。所以这是一次性的这个调整。如果没有这一次性的一个调整的话，那 26 年这个 EPS 的增速负增长比现在 27% 的话会更高。

那我们来看一下市场对于 27 年的这个预期：似乎 27 年的第三季度，他们财年里面 27 年的第三季度 24% 的一个同比增速，似乎市场预计的这个转折点就在 27 财年的那个第三季度。而且下去你看 28 年同比增速 20%、29 年同比增速 15%。如果说接下去 Nike 的财报能够做到像现在市场预期一样的话，Nike 的这个估值是可以回去的。

六、平行案例：星巴克的逆境反转

那前面管理层所指的这个 margin……那前面我讲到另外一个逆境反转的一个例子是星巴克。

两条线：蓝色白色这条线是星巴克的一个估值，现在在 35.8 倍。35.8 倍啊，星巴克的这个估值。然后价格是在 103.6，这是收盘价格。

你看到白色这条线，最近这段时间，星巴克的一个估值已经明显比 22 年、23 年、包括 24 年的时候要高了。24 年的时候最低的时候只有 20 倍，那所以估值从 24 年最低等 20 倍到现在 35.8 倍，而且我看的是 forward P，已经上涨了 92%——92.6%。然后同期价格上涨了 41%。价格上涨的幅度比估值上涨的幅度要小，就代表什么？市场提前去走——提前去走逆境反转。

星巴克的 EPS 数据

同样我们来看一下具体的数据。星巴克过去 3 年 23、24、25 财年： - 23 年 EPS：3.54 - 24 年：3.31（同比负增长 6%） - 25 年：2.13（同比负增长 36%，最糟糕的一年）

就明显当时星巴克受到了非常大的这个挑战。在中国那边受到了茶的这个挑战，中国这边各式各样的茶，星巴克在中国那边受到了非常大的挑战。在北美这一块也受到了挑战。那么然后公司内部一系列的一些改进，包括一些 points 的一些积分、一些活动等等，开始慢慢的把星巴克给带回。

整个这就是非常明显：一家公司时间久了之后，特别老牌公司，时间久了之后，管理层就放任，就不做事情，也不做任何的创新。 星巴克也是一样，你需要创新啊。我记得我以前和大家聊过星巴克的基本盘：说你，你去星巴克，每一次进去就是 pumpkin facility，就是你没有其他的一些新的东西可以去点。那么他们在新品上的一些创新基本上都是一个失败，很少有新品可以留在 menu 上面。

星巴克的反转已在兑现

那所以我们看股价已经反弹了一年了，但是你看如果看过去四个季度——过去四个季度 EPS 增速是多少？同比负的 46%、负的 35%、负的 19%，和刚刚过去这个季度终于来到了正的 22%。然后接下去两个季度：30%、31%。

这样的 EPS 增速——加价值股这样的一个同比增速——那市场一定回归对吧，你的估值也一定回归。这就是为什么估值走到了基本面之前，那么估值明显是先行了。 因为当时估值在出现反弹的时候，它的 EPS 增速还是负增长的对吧。所以估值出现了反弹，然后价格也出现了反弹。

然后你看一下市场对于 27、28 年两年的预期，非常的乐观：28% 和 21% EPS 增速——28%、21%。很多科技公司你都不见得能够走出 28% 和 21% 的 EPS 增速。所以我们把这三年加在一起，12、28 和 21，你平均一下的话呃，EPS 这个三年的一个平均增速差不多是在 20% 左右。那配合现在静态市盈率 50 倍，也就是它的一个 PEG 数现在已经到达了 2.5。

所以现在的星巴克，你说它有没有出现这个逆境反转？那过去的这个季度财报已经证明了逆境反转的第一个季度的一个财报非常明确的证明了。但是价格已经走了一年了。然后而且从现在这个角度去看，它的估值就没有这个空间在那边，就估值这个扩张已经没有空间在那边。接下去再上涨的话，它需要什么？需要 EPS 这个增速。

就回到了我前面美光的这个例子，也就是很多时候我们再买一只个股的时候，我们如果知道我们买入这只个股的时候，它的 PE 在什么那个阶段，未来的 PE 有没有扩张的这个空间，我们就知道它大概接下去的一个涨幅的一个期待是多少。

那而我们站在当下，如果回过头去看的话，星巴克的这个逆境反转花了多长时间？花了差不多要两年的时间吧。第一次出现一个同比 EPS 一个负增长是 2024 年的第二季度。

七、Nike 财报会：细节拆解

所以从前面的数据，我们可以看到起码估值的这个 setup、出现逆境反转的这个 setup 已经在了，就现在的估值在一个相对的低位。

第四季度业务全面下滑

那我们再次重复一下第四季度。你可以看一下中国。就首先整个 Nike 这边的一个营收：direct sales 下跌了 9%，digital.com 下跌了 12%，Nike store 下跌了 7%，wholesale 这边增长了 1%，gross margin 49.2%。几乎所有的业务全在下跌。

中国这边是更惨。中国那边竞争非常的激烈。第四季度中国那边营收同比下跌 17% 啊，同比下跌 10%，就说明你的市占率全被其他人抢走。我有查过中国那边鞋子这边的一个销售同比增速——同比增速非常的（快），我记得是 15% 还有 20% 吧，因为国产品牌非常的受欢迎。那也就是说整个行业在中国它是在扩张，但是你自己反而出现了一个负增长，就说明你的一个市占率被其他的同行给抢走了。

啊，所以这是非常糟糕的一个情况。然后稍后管理层在财报会里面有给出了非常明确怎么去针对接下去中国市场的一个方法和解决方案。那么这个解决方案本身非常的重要，因为这将决定了他们 Nike 在中国到底有没有可能可以出现一个逆境反转。

指引与宏观假设

然后我们来看指引，指引也非常的重要。他说首先给了接下去两个季度的这个指引，从宏观这个角度，宏观对于他们有一些影响。这些宏观包括油价啊、战争啊等等，包括消费者的一些情绪面等等。首先他们并不认为宏观……就假设非常的重要，你要知道他们做这些假设的一个前提是什么，就他们给出这个指引的一个前提是什么。指引的这个假设是宏观并不会出现非常明显的这个改善。

记不记得我在和大家分析吉利财报的时候，我说过，一的财报里面第一季度财报发布完之后，后来市场出现了下跌。当时我说，吉利给的一个全年的一个指引，是期待着整个战争的一个影响可持续的，并没有任何的一个反转。但是我说只要你相信战争会结束的话，那基本面就基本就能够回去。为什么？因为管理层说过，如果不是因为战争的话——不是因为我们预期战争对于我们基本面的这个影响、对于航空业的这个影响——我们第一个季度财报发文之后，马上就要上调全年的这个指引。所以他们其实背后的一个订单、背后的一个需求非常的好，只是因为他们对于战争这个不确定性保持了一定的一个相对悲观，保持了这个假设在背后。那如果这个假设最后被验证没有他们的预计那么糟糕的话，那最后的财报出来就会比他们的预期要好。

那么所以 Nike 现在也是一样，就类似于的一个情况。他们那个假设是整个宏观环境在未来的 6 个月并不会出现非常明显的这个改善。然后他们在上一个季度财报的时候给出了这个指引，上一个季度财报是给了三个季度的这个指引，然后这个季度财报给了两个季度这个指引。所以他们在过去三个月之后，他们仍然坚持在一个季度之前所给出这个指引，所以总体来说还是 OK 的。

就是一个在过去的这一个季度里边，对于未来两个季度这个指引并没有任何的改变，只是从组成上去看的话稍微有些改变：就是营收可能比他们原本的预计还要稍微差一些，但是 margin 利润率比原来的预计要稍微好一些。所以最后从 EPS 的这个结果去看，还会符合预期，甚至于比预期要稍微好一些。

就是说你对于下一个季度的一个财报，如果营收 miss 不要感到意外，但是它的一个 margin 比市场预期要好也不要感到意外，或者 EPS 增速比上预期要好也不要感到意外，因为管理层在这次财报会议里面已经在指引里面全部和市场去说明了。

Margin 的关键性

我们顺着 margin 的这个话题继续聊下去。因为稍后有另外一位分析员问了管理层：那你怎么哪来的这个信心，你认为未来这个 margin 会出现 expansion，未来的 margin 会比现在越来越好？

那管理层有说到，从这一个季度的一个结果告诉我们，使我们有更多的这个信心。我们认为我们公司本身的这个 margin、这个利润率已经稳定下来了，然后我们已经开始看到了一条非常健康的一个道路——margin 可以出现一个扩张。

比如说刚刚过去的第四季度，原本我们的一个预期给的一个指引是 margin 将会同比出现 -25 个基点至 -75 个基点的一个缩小——缩小就 margin contraction。但是我们看到这个 contraction 这个缩小只有 10 个基点，就是比我们的这个预期要好。margin 这个结果比我们的预期要好，而且如果我们就看过去的四个季度，每一个季度环比都比上一个季度要好。并且我们看到了一个 meaningful opportunity，看到了一个非常明显的一个机会、非常重要的一个机会——structurally 从结构上来说，gross margin 毛利润会越来越好。

我们在 26 财年就是已经刚刚过去的这个财年所做的这些投入将会在 27 财年体现出来，特别是在 margin expansion 上体现出来。并且我们认为我们在一条道路上能够把 margin 带回到 10% 以上。

那前面管理层所指的这个 margin 是 operating margin，是他们的一个运营利润率。一家公司如果有一个指标最重要的话，那就是运营利润率，没有其他任何的指标。我认为运营利润率的这个扩张是华尔街最关心的，任何一家公司都是一样。

Operating Margin 数据

Nike 21 财年和 22 财年它的一个运营利润率 15.6% 和 14%，就还是在一个 double digit，而且是在 mid double digit。然后 23、24、25、26：11.55、12.29、7.99（25 年啊），然后 26 年——6 财年就刚刚过去这个财年 8.18。其实 26 年比起 25 财年，这个 margin 已经稍微好了一些，就是 margin 已经开始出现了稳定下来了。

那么但是这一个 margin 里面有一个我前面说到 IEEPA 的一个退税问题。如果把你把这个退税给去掉的话，应该会比 25 年还要糟糕一些。所以在这个基础上，27 财年你看一下现在市场的预计的毛率是多少？7%。也就是我们假设现在市场的预计是正确的话，你看一下 28 财年 8.48、29 9.53。那如果管理层说他们有信心能够把 margin 再次带回到 10% 以上的话，即使每年只有 100 个基点到 150 个基点的这个扩张——就 1% 到 1.5% 的这个扩张——对于他们 EPS 这个增速也是一个非常好的一个帮助。

投资的核心：往未来看

我们要记住这一点：我们买公司、我们投资公司的时候，历史有没有用？历史有用，就历史可以告诉我们很多这家公司历史上哪里做对了、哪里做错了，但是我们在投资时候一定是投资未来，不是投资过去。就 Nike 的历史已经成为历史了，我们要往未来去看：这家公司有没有可能会出现反转？往未来去看这家公司的一个 margin 有没有可能从现在 8.18% 的基础上会越来越高？

中国市场的三大策略

最后是他们中国这边的策略：

  1. More premium——做高端产品。我觉得完全正确。你在中国你做低端产品，完全做不过国产。
  2. Culturally relevant——第二点，就是你得从文化上有一个贴切。怎么在文化上做到贴切？怎么在产品的文化上做到贴切？你必须要有 local experience，你必须要和地方去合作。所以他们也选择了和中国这边的一个就本地的这个团队去合作。那本地的团队接下去将会包揽什么？设计本地团队、开发本地团队、产品的制造也在中国完成。而他们现在所针对的时间点是什么？中国 2027 年的新年 holiday——也就是 2027 年中国的春节。所以我们的一个期待是，即使现在我们都不知道，我的一个期待就是在 2027 年，Nike 在中国可能会设计出一些非常中国化的产品。
  3. Have to lead with sports——最后咱们赢夺中国市场，得从运动出发。

八、结论：Nike 的逆境反转是否可期？

所以看完之后，我个人认为大家应该知道啊，就如果我关注我频道，我在过去几年对于 Nike 一直都比较悲观的，但是听完这一次财报之后，我个人认为 Nike 是有可能会出现逆境反转，这家公司是绝对可以值得关注的。

几点都满足了： - 第一点，估值在一个低位 - 第二点，承认历史上的这个错误 - 第三点，怎么去纠正、怎么去纠偏，有一个对于每一个市场有一个非常明确的一个态度和路线。那接下去能不能做到？就管理层也表达了这个决心了。

在他们这一次财报会里面，甚至于管理层有聊到 Knicks 对吧——纽约尼克斯，他们这一次获得了 NBA 的总冠军。这 NBA 的总冠军不是这十年磨一剑，所以他说你们得给我们这个时间。但是也表达了非常强的这个决心。

所以你听财报，你可以听到很多管理层的一个态度，怎么去看待这个问题。那最终就是取决于你是不是相信这个管理层。那所以最终能不能做到，就取决于管理层的一个执行力。而作为一个投资者，我们可以选择再看一看，也可以选择在现在这个位置就去相信管理层。当然你在 PE 越低的位置，你越买入的话，你承担的这个风险和不确定性一定就更高对吧。你买的越高，一定是这个确定性就比现在来说就会更高。啊，所以风险和回报它永远是匹配的。

我希望通过这个例子比较完整的从估值到财报会，然后最后得出一个结论——这家公司有没有可能逆境反转，那怎么去判断有没有可能逆境反转——和大家分享一些。那么我希望通过这个例子，当然我并不是鼓励大家去布局 NIKE，但是我觉得现在是可以去布局、可以去思考的。但是我只是通过这个例子教大家一个方法：从估值到财报会到基本面，把这三者结合在一起，去判断那么这家公司有没有可能会出现逆境反转的一些信号。

好吗，那希望这一类的分享对大家有帮助。

结尾：活动与告别

最后是今天的活动。福图摸摸： - 美国市场这边现在通过开户且入境最高可以获得价值 1000 美金英伟达的这个正股，同时闲置资金最高可以获得年化 8.1% 的回报。 - 加拿大这边通过开户且入境最高获得 30 股免费的股票，前 30 天免佣金和最高闲置资金年化 6% 的这个回报。

大家如果感兴趣的话，可以在置顶的留言里面找到福图摸摸的开户连接。

那今天这期内容就到这边，我们下期见。嗯。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、隐藏的悖论：小涛在"教估值"的同时，其实在教"反估值直觉"
表面上这期是"如何判断逆境反转"，但如果你把三个案例（NKE、SBUX、MU）叠在一张图上看，你会发现一个反直觉的模式：**估值先行的方向永远是错的方向**。
- 星巴克：估值从 20 倍飙到 35 倍时，EPS 还在 -46%、-35%、-19% 地跌 —— 估值涨了 92%，价格只涨 41%。 - Nike 24 年中到 25 年中：估值从 20 多倍反弹到 45 倍（+102%），股价只涨 11%，然后市场"醒过来"再把估值砸回 23.7 倍。 - 美光：市场喊着"要 rerate 到 15–20 倍"的时候，其实 25 年底早就买过 14 倍了。
**意外的观察**：小涛没有明说，但他实际上在描述一个"估值前置指标"—— 估值扩张先于基本面 1 到 2 年，但**只有一半的时候会兑现**。星巴克兑现了，Nike 第一次没兑现。也就是说，市场的"逆境反转预演"是一个精度不到 50% 的信号。这意味着，如果你把估值当作反转指标去做多，你的胜率其实和抛硬币相当 —— 真正让你赢的不是估值本身，而是**估值 +

item.title: [Mercatus Center] Paul Dragos Aligica — 2024 Markets and Society Conference Keynote
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被骂"新自由主义"的悖论：批评者无意间为哈耶克加冕

演讲中最耐人寻味的转折是：生态学、可持续性研究、复杂系统理论的学者们，把"韧性"（resilience）研究骂成"新自由主义的化身"——因为它太像哈耶克。Aligica 引用了一整摞标题："Intuitively neoliberal"、"Resilience is a quintessential neoliberal concept"……这些批判本意是划清界限，但从思想史的角度看，它们干的事情恰好相反：它们承认了自组织、分布式知识、局部适应这些概念在解释复杂现实时的解释力已经如此不可回避，以至于必须先"打倒"它。

换句话说，每一篇愤怒的引用都是一次生产性引用。这是学术界的"史翠珊效应"——你越想遮蔽，你越强化。真正被边缘化的思想不会被专门写文章批评；被写文章批评的思想已经赢了议程设置。Aligica 用了半场演讲展示"主流经济学不谈韧性"，然后又用另外半场展示"复杂系统的人一边用哈耶克一边骂哈耶克"。这两个观察拼在一起构成了一个反讽结构：主流政治经济学错过了资源，主流复杂科学则在无意识地消化它。

二、从"共有规则"到"可互操作性"：他偷渡了一个计算机网络概念进政治哲学

真正让我意外的是 Aligica 用"interoperability（可互操作性）"来重新定义 polyentricity（多中心秩序）。表面上他讲的是罗马尼亚—俄罗斯—保加利亚的铁路轨距，但这个概念的真正家乡不是政治学，也不是经济学——它是计算机网络（TCP/IP、USB、HTTP）和系统工程的核心术语。

这一步偷梁换柱极其大胆。传统 Vincent Ostrom 的多中心理论一直被诟病"最终还是要靠一层 overarching rules 兜底"，因此与 Rawls 的"重叠共识（overlapping consensus）"陷入同构——都需要价值层面的收敛。Aligica 的动作是把"共识成本"替换为"边界处的翻译成本"。这意味着：

  • 多中心秩序不需要价值观趋同，只需要接口设计成本可承受。
  • 各系统内部可以彻底异质，只要边界"协议栈"可跨接。
  • Coase 的交易成本理论从此可以直接嫁接到治理结构——互操作成本就是治理层面的交易成本。

这里跨领域连接的强度非常罕见：他让 IETF 的"rough consensus and running code"哲学在政治经济学里获得公民权。硅谷的协议设计者其实早就在做多中心治理的实证工作，只是没人这么叫。

三、Ostrom 的"意外遭遇"与研究项目自身的反脆弱性

Aligica 有一句几乎滑过去的话："out of that without intending, Elinor Ostrom stumbles upon the issue of resilience（她无意间撞上了韧性问题）。"这句话的分量比表面大得多。Ostrom 一开始不研究韧性、不研究复杂系统、甚至不研究公共池塘——她研究自治。commons 只是自治议程的一个案例研究。研究着研究着，发现"为什么有些制度能延续几百年"这个问题冒了出来，于是研究项目自己完成了向韧性的适应性演化。

这是一个漂亮的元层递归：Bloomington 学派的研究议程本身，就是它最终要理论化的那种现象的一个案例。一个开放的、演化的、由边缘现象反哺核心的知识生产结构，无中心地在自组织中生成了对无中心自组织的理论。

这让我想到一个更大的跨领域观察：几乎所有革命性的学科分支都是"意外之子"——青霉素、微波炉、便利贴、GPT——它们的共同点是原始意图与最终产品不匹配。反过来说，一个"知道自己要发现什么"的研究项目，通常只能验证已知，无法生成新范式。Aligica 在这里不动声色地暗示了：主流经济学之所以错过韧性，可能不是理论问题，而是研究结构问题——它太"计划经济"了。

四、隐藏的第四条洞见：效率—韧性权衡是一个跨学科的"通用律"

Aligica 用 COVID 供应链危机举例：全球化把库存压缩到极致，追求"精益（just-in-time）"，然后一个尾部事件就击穿系统。他把这称为"efficiency-resilience tradeoff"，甚至画了一条倒 U 曲线。

但真正惊人的地方在于——这条倒 U 曲线在无数学科里已经独立出现过，只是没人把它们串起来：

  • 生态学的 r/K 选择（繁殖速度 vs 生存能力）
  • 控制论的 鲁棒—性能权衡（H∞ 控制）
  • 演化生物学的 单一栽培脆弱性（爱尔兰马铃薯饥荒、香蕉巴拿马病）
  • 机器学习的 过拟合 vs 泛化
  • 金融的 杠杆 vs 流动性
  • 心理学的 Yerkes–Dodson 定律（唤醒水平 vs 表现）
  • 毒理学的 hormesis（低剂量压力提升韧性）

这不是"类比"，这是跨系统的同构（isomorphism）。Aligica 的贡献可能不止于把它引入政治经济学，而是隐含地提示了一个更大的假设："过度优化 → 尾部脆弱"可能是任何复杂适应系统的普适性定律。 如果真是这样，那么"韧性"不该被视为经济学与生态学之间的一座桥，而应该被视为一整个新的元学科的组织原则——一个我们还没命名的领域。

而最讽刺的一层是：Aligica 的这次演讲，本身也是他所讲理论的一次演示——他没有用一个"总揽性规则"整合奥地利学派、公共选择、Bloomington 学派，而是在它们的边界上设计了几个可互操作的概念接口（symbiotics、polycentricity、interoperability）。演讲本身就是它论题的原型实现。

📝 转录文本

Podcast Introduction

You're listening to the Hayek Program Podcast. This podcast includes audio from lectures, interviews, and discussions from scholars and visitors of the F.A. Hayek Program for Advanced Study in Philosophy, Politics, and Economics at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University.

To learn more about the Hayek Program, visit hayek.mercatus.org. To learn about graduate student fellowship opportunities with the Mercatus Center at George Mason University for students at Mason as well as at universities across the globe, please visit students.mercatus.org.

Introduction of the Speaker

To start us off with the conference, we have our own Dr. Paul Dragos Aligica as our first keynote speaker. Paul is the KPMG Professor of Governance at the University of Bucharest and a senior research fellow with the Hayek Program at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University. Last year he was elected as a member of the Academy of Europe.

His thorough and extensive scholarship focuses on governance theory, institutional analysis, and political philosophy. His most recent book, The Institutionalization of Indoctrination: An Exploratory Investigation Based on the Romanian Case, is co-authored with Simona Prada and published in 2022 with Lexington. He also recently worked on Public Governance and the Classical Liberal Perspective: The Political Economy Foundations with Pete Boettke and Vlad Tarko — that's Oxford University Press, released in 2019.

Paul is a nuanced scholar who cares deeply about bringing theory to practice in order for all of us to live better together. He is a patient and precise mentor and truly a delightful colleague. I think Paul encapsulates the curiosity and humility of his own teacher, Nobel Prize-winning economist Elinor Ostrom. Tonight he will discuss the importance of resilience. Please join me in welcoming Paul.

Opening Remarks

Thank you, Stephanie. It's a real privilege — I know it's a commonplace, but it is a real privilege to be here with you tonight. And I'd like to thank the organizers of the event for giving me the chance to give the opening remarks for this conversation.

It's an ongoing conversation. We have started it in this context two years ago. This is the third event, and this is a conversation which is pivoting around an intellectual tradition. We are restructuring, reorganizing an intellectual community and a network of scholars around that intellectual tradition, which might have many labels, but substantially we are on the same page when it comes to the principles, values, and approaches which are defining it.

The Mainline Political Economy Tradition

One of our colleagues — somebody who is deeply associated with the Mercatus program, with the Hayek program — Pete Boettke, has labeled recently, in the new intellectual and ideological circumstances, the tradition "mainline political economy." So in my presentation tonight I'm going to focus on a research frontier within mainline political economy.

But keep in mind the following thing: this is an interdisciplinary tradition, an intellectual tradition which is associated with names like Adam Smith, 19th-century Tocqueville, Mill, and in the 20th century it is associated with three schools of thought which happen to be at the core of the research program at Mercatus Center, at the Hayek Program:

  • Austrian School of Economics
  • Public Choice
  • New Institutionalism in the tradition of Elinor Ostrom and Vincent Ostrom

Stephanie has already mentioned my association with that specific dimension of this research program. So we are an intellectual community, and we are coming into this space — let's call it for now the "mainline perspective" — from different disciplines, different angles. We see each of us, in function of the angle that we are approaching this space, as a series of concentric circles.

Tonight's Agenda: Resilience as a Vehicle

What I'm going to explore together with you tonight is a specific vision of this intellectual tradition and a reconstruction of that tradition at the end of the 20th century — and indeed the beginning of the 21st century — around the space at the interface between three schools of thought: Austrian, Public Choice, and Bloomington New Institutionalism.

But I'm not just going to give an overview of the entire research frontier which is inspired by the theoretical lenses defined and redefined at the interface of these three schools of thought. I'm going to do something even more focused and more precise. I'm going to take the lenses created by the analytical and methodological synthesis of these three schools of thought and apply them to one of the most interesting and important phenomena defining the current landscape of research — both in social sciences and in applied policy and administrative sciences.

This is the issue of resilience.

So on our agenda tonight, the first step, the underlying logic of our approach, is to take the theoretical lenses from this space and apply them to the problem of resilience. At the same time, we are going to use the issue of resilience in order to get a better sense of the nature of the research agenda and the kind of insights we could get when we put these three schools of thought together in the larger context of the intellectual tradition uniting all of us — again, mainline political economy, though some of you may have different ways of labeling it.

What Is Resilience? Four Conceptualizations

The first step is to get a better sense of what this issue called resilience is. There are a lot of labels floating around, and I think that there are four ways of thinking about it. It's a family of concepts, but the most important thing is the kind of phenomena that they designate:

  1. Absorbing shock without deterioration — the ability to respond to a shock without deteriorating a system, an organization, a firm, a community.
  2. Bouncing back — you get the shock, a little bit of deformation, you bounce back.
  3. Learning to withstand — you are not only able to bounce back, but from now on you are better able to respond and withstand that specific type of shock.
  4. Anti-fragility — not only the ability to bounce back and be much better in the future in responding to that specific type of shock, but in general to improve your abilities to respond to those shocks.

This is preliminary. I'm using it as a vehicle in order to get a better understanding of the magnitude and importance of the phenomenon of resilience. It's extraordinarily important — for organizations, firms, communities, economic systems, platforms, great systems of artificial intelligence which are combined with technology and institutions. It's not just natural systems. It's not just social systems, but in the space of the artifactual, resilience is extremely important.

The Puzzle: Why Doesn't Economics Talk About Resilience?

Very interesting — and I'm using a quote from David Colander here, an author who has done a lot in exploring the interface between complexity sciences, resilience, institutional theory, and economics. They look at the literature and say: "Look, it's amazing. This phenomenon is extraordinarily important. And yet economics and policy sciences look like they are not interested in it."

If this phenomenon in its various facets is so important, how come people are not interested in it? I'm not going to respond to this question. My first step was simply to look in the literature to double-check if that is the case.

Comparative Economic Systems: A 70-Year Review

If you're interested in resilience, the place you are going to check whether what Colander says is correct is comparative economic systems. This is a discipline where over the years we have accumulated a lot of insights regarding institutional economic performance criteria and structural features of economic systems.

I looked at 70 years of textbooks in comparative economic systems. First, indeed, in the very logic of comparative economic systems there is a place for resilience. The Montias framework is pretty simple: you have an economy, which is a set consisting of a system, the economic policies within that system, the environment of that system, and the outputs. The outputs are a function of the system, the policies, and the environment.

Within that structure, you could have a lot of norms to assess the system — static efficiency, dynamic efficiency, justice, equality, freedom. Structurally, resilience might be one of those. Simply input it in the basic Montias framework, and you have an entire research program which might be dedicated to resilience.

But to make a very long story short: if you look at 70 years of textbooks of comparative economic systems, there is not much about resilience. Despite the fact that if a system is not surviving, why should you talk about its efficiency, social justice, or freedom?

Adjacent Debates That Touch Resilience

There are some places in the literature which are relevant:

  • The economic calculation debate — you could make it a debate about the possibility of socialism, but you could also make it a discussion about feasibility and viability of socialism.
  • Business cycle theory and the viability of capitalism — another source of insights.
  • Economic history, especially on the institutionalist lines of Douglass North talking about structure and change — another source of insights.

But none of this amounts to an explicit discussion about resilience similar to our discussions about static efficiency, dynamic efficiency, social justice, or equality.

The Explosion of Resilience Research

In the last 10–12 years, we have an explosion of papers, studies, books, and discussions where the concept of resilience is crucial. Where is the conceptualization coming from? Not from political economy, not from economics, but mostly from environmental sciences, ecology, physics, mechanics, biological sciences, and complexity theory.

Entering Mainline Political Economy: The Bloomington School

I'm now setting up the stage for the moment where mainline political economy — and more specifically, the Mercatus research program at the interface between the three schools of thought — steps in. When you think about this analytical construct, obviously the first thing when you say "resilience" is Elinor Ostrom and the Bloomington School.

We are all familiar with the research program of Elinor Ostrom, and we know very well that the research program was not about the commons. The research program was about self-governance. The commons was just one branch of the research program. At one point this research program on self-governance evolves into a discussion about the commons which is illustrating some points about collective action and self-governance. And out of that, without intending, Elinor Ostrom stumbles upon the issue of resilience.

How? "How is that long-established institutions have been more resilient than other institutions?" — this is the question that appears while she is studying the "governing the commons" research program. This is the precise point where the commons agenda is transmuted into a resilience-focused research agenda. Unfortunately, that was towards the end of her career.

This is the point where she is looking at the institutional design principles and the various factors determining long-enduring institutions — the resilience of institutional structures dealing with solutions to the commons problems.

Ostrom's Restructured Agenda

The very moment Elinor Ostrom got into this space, she restructured her agenda:

  1. She adopts the socioecological systems framework and starts to develop and focus on the social side of it. Whether that was a fortunate move is a separate discussion, but the fact is that this is exactly what Elinor Ostrom has done.
  2. She outlines the terms of the approach. First, it's an empirical approach — we are going to look at the economic history of the West for economic performance, economic growth, economic development, and to the entire range of cases which might be associated with the idea of resilient institutions.
  3. Second, her approach is policy-oriented. She encourages scholars to move away from traditional command-and-control strategies. Her concern is of an applied level, driven by a policy concern.
  4. Third and very important, she focuses on the dominant mental models approach. She is not offering solutions or the solution. Her concern is that the dominant mental models assume that solutions to complex problems are best developed by scientifically trained analysts and implemented by centralized national-level authorities.
  5. She argues for a need to streamline the technical vocabulary — we need better conceptualization, better theoretical framework, and better operationalization.
  6. She combines the Institutional Analysis and Development (IAD) framework, the stuff developed together with her team in studying the commons and polycentricity, with socioecological systems.
  7. She starts to operationalize key entities and links between them.

Where the Agenda Stood in 2009

This is where the agenda was when Elinor passed away in 2009, with two directions to go:

  • First direction: further elaborate the socioecological framework, and moreover to elaborate it in an empirical direction by building inventories of links and factors.
  • Second direction: focus on specific cases and structural-functional analysis — trying to understand better the structures, functions, and mechanisms. Here already we have a contribution from the Mercatus perspective, with a series of specific cases focusing on various facets of resilience.

The elaboration of the socioecological system framework and the case studies are putting pressure on us: the conceptual and theoretical apparatus is not sufficient. We have to move the agenda to a second-order level of conceptualization and theory-building.

Two Sources for Second-Order Theorizing

There are only two directions, two sources, two streams inspiring any possible theoretical developments:

  1. Complexity theory — theories of emergence. The basic theories that have inspired the socioecological systems framework. Holling and Folke Gunderson are the key names here.
  2. Mainline political economy.

This is not a speculation. The literature on socioecological systems and resilience acknowledges that Holling and Hayek, coming from two different directions, are creating the foundation for resilience studies and complexity theory.

Graham Room, in what is probably the best book on complexity, institutions, and public policy, says the following: "Despite the contributions of Kaldor, Keynes, Myrdal, cumulative causation, economics without equilibrium, path dependency, Lock-ins, increasing returns and so on, it has been with a tradition of Schumpeter and Hayek — rather than Keynes and Kaldor — that these connections have predominantly been made."

On the other side, mainline political economy — authors like Koppl or Paul Lewis — recognize that the frontier of the research agenda of mainline is complexity theory.

The Bad News: Resistance from Social Sciences

There is another news. This was reflected in an article published by a group of researchers on sustainability from a European large research center on sustainability, which asks: why is resilience not appealing to social sciences?

We see resistance. They say that in the end it is difficult to conceptualize and operationalize, and in social dimensions vs. natural and environmental sciences, there is a clash of conceptualization.

Let's take a closer look. Look how they exemplify the problem:

  • Concepts like emergence and self-organization are seen as non-problematic in ecology or natural sciences. But in the minds of many social scientists, they are associated with Adam Smith's invisible hand and Hayek's spontaneous order, or with rational choice of Ostrom type — therefore, "neoliberalism."
  • In ecology and natural sciences, function and functionalism are central. However, functionalism in social sciences is still associated with Parsons and Merton — in the continuity and change structure, they emphasize continuity and consensus — therefore, "conservatism."
  • Finally, there is a misunderstanding that the approach is geared towards homogeneity and consensus — which we all know is not the case.

Examples: Resilience Framed as Neoliberal

Some titles illustrate the point — and the magnitude of the influence of mainline political economy on this field:

"Intuitively neoliberal: Towards a critical understanding of resilience governance."

"Owing to its emphasis on self-organization, it appears that this agenda is aligned with a contemporary neoliberal economics paradigm. This entails a proliferation of market-based instruments for ecosystem management. Therefore, resilience thinking shares Hayek's idea of fundamental un-knowability of the world, and then has a radical cast for freedom into a celebration of local knowledge as the expression of ontological un-knowability."

"Ostrom — on the one hand, institutional lenses are very good because they could provide a linchpin to connect the social with the ecological environmental. But then in the next paragraph: self-organization is aligned with rational choice theory as seen in the works of Elinor Ostrom — guilty of being methodological individualist, seeing macro patterns as resulting from aggregation of decentralized choices and of seeing economic change as determined by factor costs, land, labor, capital, and so on."

"The neoliberal discourse of Hayek and Friedman has been the main force driving resilience to its current omnipresence. Resilience is a quintessential neoliberal concept, intellectually situated in the works of Friedrich Hayek."

I'm not taking a position in this outline. I'm just illustrating how influential and important the magnitude of mainline political economy is currently on the current agenda on complex systems and resilience.

Two Observations, Two Elements for the Agenda

When we focus on Public Choice, institutional, and social processes theory as a possible input into second-order theorizing on this research line, we are focusing on something which has already happened — is happening.

  1. Based on the intrinsic logic of the socioecological systems agenda, something pretty much similar with what we have called mainline political economy is required in order to make that theoretical and analytical apparatus work.
  2. The intrinsic logic of the mainline — Ostrom, Buchanan, and Hayek — leads to complexity, adaptive systems, and knowledge and learning processes as theoretical lenses.

Once we have made those observations, the next step is clear:

  • First, further advance the intrinsic logic of the mainline and continue to develop conceptual structures and theories on that frontier.
  • Second, while doing that, provide the missing social element in socioecological systems.

The Austrian Perspective: Hayek as Precursor of Complexity

The Austrian perspective is clear — it was foundational, through Hayek, to this entire research domain. So our first step should be to revisit the Hayekian vision and Hayek's contribution as a precursor of the theory of complexity and complex adaptive systems.

The very idea of spontaneous order, so familiar with us, is at the core of this perspective. Just revisiting and advancing that line of research is by itself a contribution to the resilience and complex systems agenda.

This is well recognized by the major figures in this field:

  • Axel Rod draws attention to the fact that the problem of emerging social order and the role of rules has already been charted by Hayek.
  • Brian Arthur (Increasing Returns and Path Dependence in the Economy) draws attention to the fact that Hayek is the one who developed the idea of distributed knowledge and localized interactions as factors generating path-dependent processes and increasing returns.
  • Paul Ormerod (Butterfly Economics) is explicit in recognizing Hayek's contribution to nonlinear dynamics and emergent behavior.
  • Geoff Hodgson (Evolution), one of the main evolutionary institutionalist theorists, is recognizing and advancing a Hayekian perspective.
  • John Holland (Emergence: From Chaos to Order) — another recognition of a specific aspect of the complexity agenda inspired by Hayek.

In brief, Hayek has developed the critique — and an alternative — to rationalist scientific constructivism, and in doing that he has become the foundational philosophical stance of modern complexity theory. Most of us are familiar with his well-known credo: the curious task of economics is to demonstrate to men how little they really know about what they imagine they can design. This could be used as a motto for this approach.

A New Vision of Governance

We criticize rationalist, scientistic constructivism, and as a result an alternative vision of governance is emerging.

  • The standard approach to governance: look at issues of uncertainty, then try to convert those into risk, convert that into point predictions, and then develop a planning strategy to deal with those.
  • Hayek denies us that path because he questions the ability, the knowledge we have — emphasizes the limits of knowledge for such strategies — and points to pattern predictions.

If we are able to do pattern predictions, then the way we think about governance structures should be defined by the limits of point predictions and the abilities (also limited) to generate pattern predictions. Hence a new approach to governance:

Instead of trying to plan things, try to build up within the system institutional capabilities able to adapt, learn, evolve — in order to respond to those challenges. This is what we defined at the beginning as an anti-fragility form of resilience.

Instead of looking at optimality as a key concept — and we'll see Buchanan later criticizing this as means-rationality over-defining and restraining our understanding of systems — we should look at resilience as a concept reflecting a skeptical view about prediction and control necessary for optimization. Resilience becomes an institutionalized adaptive epistemic process which is at the core of complex adaptive systems built up for governance purposes.

We may not like the label — this is the phenomenon we are dealing with irrespective of the label. The properties of systems or subsystems responding to challenges: we may use Shackle's kaleidic or non-ergodic terminology, or Doug North's ways of framing the problem. But the problem and the solution are pretty clearly defined by Hayek.

The Public Choice Contribution: Four Dimensions

Now the question is: what is the Public Choice contribution to this perspective? It looks like Ostrom and Hayek converge on complexity issues, but we are a little bit reluctant to think in terms of Public Choice so easily converging on those lines.

I have identified four dimensions or four areas:

  1. Go back to the basics of Public Choice: apply economic reasoning to non-market settings. Resilience is a non-market setting.
  2. Populate the "social" in socioecological systems with models, theories, insights, and social mechanisms identified and explored in public choice theory over the last 50 years.
  3. Symbiotics — developed by Buchanan in the context of investigating and questioning what economists should do.
  4. Constitutional political economy — crucial for the relationship between the realm of the artifactual and evolutionary processes.

1. Basic Economic Reasoning Applied to Resilience

This is the low-hanging fruit and where the frontier currently is. Simply go back to the comparative economic systems textbooks — those people tried to develop the idea of static efficiency, dynamic efficiency, and so on as institutional or economic performance criteria. We could do the same for resilience.

Take Schumpeterian and Kirznerian entrepreneurship: tendency towards equilibrium (Kirznerian) vs. breaking an equilibrium (Schumpeterian) and redefining the production possibility frontier to a new level. This sounds familiar when thinking about resilience — it's bouncing back and it's anti-fragility.

2. Trade-offs and Comparative Institutional Analysis

Public choice is about trade-offs. One of the most striking things in the last 10 years is the trade-off between resilience and efficiency. This was striking during the COVID crisis, when we discovered that we tried to optimize supply chains for efficiency, and in doing so we made them very vulnerable, non-resilient.

But we could go further. What if the relationship between resilience and efficiency has different functional forms? For instance, an inverted U-curve, where efficiency and resilience go together up to a point, and then increases in efficiency lead to a collapse in resilience. Very interesting applied question.

We could do the same for other institutional performance criteria — equality, freedom, liberty. What are the trade-offs? What is comparative analysis in this context? This is pure Public Choice at its best.

Unpacking Resilience: There Is No Universal Resilience

Up to this point, we've looked at resilience on one hand vs. efficiency, social justice, etc. But if we unpack resilience analytically, we discover something fascinating. Under the definition of resilience we have two hidden aspects: the disturbance and the persistence criterion within the system.

This is huge. All of a sudden, if we introduce that nuance, we discover that there is nothing like universal resilience. The definition of resilience should always be calibrated to a specific disturbance and to a specific persistence criterion.

In other words, there are multiple resilience metrics — even within one system. Two major implications:

  • To compare resilience between systems is not to compare resilience in general. You look at resilience in system A under a specific persistence criterion and a specific disturbance, and resilience in system B under the same specific criterion and disturbance.
  • There are trade-offs between different types of resilience.

For example: it depends on how the disturbance is located in the social or natural space. Under a normal distribution, you have one specific type of resilience — close to the average. Under a power-law distribution — long tail, rare but major events — you have to think in terms of power-law resilience, which has implications for what is called highly optimized tolerance.

You have to think about how to calibrate your approach: either in the direction of a normal distribution (much more predictable), or in the direction of the long tail. Trade-offs — this is Public Choice.

3. Injecting Polycentricity into Socioecological Systems

Another layer: how to populate the social space in socioecological systems by taking the entire theoretical apparatus of Public Choice and injecting it into sociological. One of the best ways is to take polycentricity — which comes with an entire theoretical apparatus (public goods, trade-offs, Tiebout effects, transaction costs) — and use it as the main vehicle for socioecological systems.

I think that was the direction Elinor Ostrom was going. Why didn't she take polycentricity from the very beginning as a structure and inject it directly, using for instance the concept of panarchy from complexity people to create a hybrid structure? We could speculate about that. But I want to take the story forward from that counterfactual.

Introducing Interoperability

To do that, I'm going to introduce a concept: interoperability.

Think about two systems. Take the railroad system in Russia or the Soviet Union — specific infrastructure, governance, and a specific distance between gauges — and the railroad system in Romania, which has different gauges, different infrastructure, different regulation. Those systems become interoperable when, at the boundary between them, the two governance systems (Russia and Romania) organize an interoperability point that makes the transfer easy. This is a dyad.

Think of a triad — Bulgaria has the same problem, the same difference. Romania makes the interoperability point at the boundary between Bulgaria and Romania. By implication, that means Russia is now interoperable with Bulgaria. So interoperability captures the logic of transitivity in complex systems.

In a network of n systems, transitivity allows for the emergence of a complex comprehensive interoperability framework. And this is a bottom-up approach.

Implications for Polycentricity

The implications are formidable for the way we think about polycentricity.

Everything depends on how much it costs to create the interoperability interfaces between the systems. We have a structure of interoperability costs which is determining the structure, intensity, and complexity of a polycentric system. Interoperability costs go up → interoperability goes down → polycentricity goes down.

But we are all familiar with this logic — this is the logic of transaction costs. All we have to do now is to apply, from the structure of the market, the basic moves and approaches we've learned in transaction cost economics to governance systems in polycentric structures. We have a structure based on cost and choices for analyzing polycentric systems.

This is a new key, a new way of understanding polycentricity — one that avoids the dead end most of us went into by following the Vincent Ostrom definition that used the "overarching system of rules" as the safety net and backup for our explanations.

What is polycentricity? We learn by heart: it's an overlapping competing system operating under an overarching system of rules — and then we stop thinking. But if instead of looking at the rules we look at interoperability, we have a totally different understanding. The overarching system of rules is just the extreme formula of interoperability, where interoperability has been totally systematized and synchronized for all units in the system.

With this, we avoid the most important trap of polycentricity analysis — being considered just one version of overlapping consensus approaches. Overlapping consensus (Rawls-type) perspectives assume there is an overall convergence vision on rules and values in order to make a system operational. Interoperability demonstrates that you could do a bottom-up approach in complex systems which remain heterogeneous, and yet you could have polycentricity in such systems.

Convergence with Buchanan's Increasing Returns

This vision converges with James Buchanan's agenda on increasing returns — that was one of his last projects. Jim Buchanan said something like this: "Look, for all its major analytical achievements, neoclassical economics abandoned the central principle of Adam Smith's Wealth of Nations, which stressed the importance of division of labor and its relationship to market size as the primary determinant of economic well-being. The neoclassical approach put a static framework and left no room for direct linkage between policy action on the size of the economic nexus and the rate of growth."

His goal was to revive the Smithian approach — a theory of increasing returns, which he actually called a generalized increasing returns theory.

Once we have introduced the increasing returns perspective with interoperability and transitivity, we have a unified perspective on both market processes (division of labor, specialization, size of the market, intensity of exchanges) and polycentricity. We could see how they operate under the increasing returns principle and under the transitivity principle. Market settings and non-market settings — but the structure is unified.

Why? Because the phenomena have something in common. Polycentricity has this aspect of increasing returns and network effects. Markets have those structures. We have an explanation of bottom-up emergence of patterns of order, which is not just hand-waving but operational in terms of looking at the structure of costs, trade-offs, and incentives determining the two. Finally, we have a structure explaining adaptive scaling, division of labor, specialization, polycentricity scale.

Vulnerability in Interdependent Systems: Perrow's Normal Accidents

This structure allows us to look at aspects that create vulnerability in interdependent systems. We've mentioned the trade-off between resilience and efficiency and supply chains. Now we have an instrument to analyze that, and we can make the junction with organizational sociology — like Perrow's study of normal accidents.

Perrow creates a 2x2 matrix. One dimension: linear vs. complex relationships between system components. Other dimension: tightly coupled vs. loosely coupled. When you have complex systems strongly tied up in an interdependent structure, you have a problem — because there might be cascading failure. Also, you might have what he calls normal accidents: accidents happening not because of a failure or an external shock, but because in the normal functioning of a complex tied system, statistically speaking, a combination of variables might combine in a certain way that generates a failure in the system.

Now we have an analytical tool: looking at interoperability, the structures of polycentricity, and the Public Choice technical apparatus (public goods, asymmetric information, rent-seeking) in order to explore both the positive gains and the negative drawbacks of functional interdependence.

Symbiotics: What Should the Economist Do?

Once we've put the issue of interdependence on the table, the notion of symbiotics developed and advanced by Buchanan makes sense.

What should the economist do? He says: "Look — I don't like the mean-sense rationality, the optimization stuff. I don't even like the name of economics. If it were for me, I would give up economics — even political economy, even if this term is better. Instead, I should use catallactics or even better symbiotics."

Symbiotics — association defined as association between dissimilar organisms in the idea that they are mutually beneficial to all parties. Unique sorts of relationships between structures, organizations, individuals, entities that have different interests.

Catallactics from Buchanan and Hayek resonates with this. So it's convergent with mainline in terms of symbiotics, catallactics, polycentricity — concepts trying to capture the same reality. At the core, but also at the margin of this approach, when you have entangled political economy (Big Wagner) or robust political economy (Pennington), these ideas emerge in a natural way within this research framework.

The Shared Features of This Convergence

Within this tradition, we have an approach that:

  • Emphasizes complexity amidst interdependence.
  • Is very reluctant to embrace optimization and efficiency 100%, other than for instrumental reasons.
  • Thinks about the relationship between evolving social artifacts — constitutional structures, institutional structures — and evolutionary processes.
  • Highlights the significance of belief systems, values, mental models in shaping social order.
  • Incorporates normative analysis — the very idea of constitutional political economy, science of the artifactual, or Constitution of Liberty. A strong position on normative issues. Implied in the approach is not just the analysis of institutional architecture at the constitutional level, operational level, public choice level, but also the problem of values and norms. Obviously positivism is rejected.

Returning to Socioecological Systems

Getting close to wrapping up, we go back to the socioecological systems. Remember we started here. We need to elaborate the framework and the case studies; that puts pressure on us to develop a second-order conceptualization. That puts us in position to delve deeper into this tradition — complexity theory, panarchy — and mainline political economy, and try to build up a synthesis between those.

Seeing that resilience leads to a paradigm, we have focused on this tension: on one hand, the domain of the artifactual (Herbert Simon's Sciences of the Artificial), and on the other, the domain of evolutionary processes.

I picked up two quotes. You would expect that Hayek is obviously on the evolutionary side, and that Vincent Ostrom is more on the artifactual side. But look at what they are saying:

  • Hayek: "While the rules on which spontaneous orders rest, they might be of spontaneous origin, this need not always be the case. People gradually learn to improve those rules, and it is at least conceivable that the formation of a spontaneous order relies entirely on rules that were deliberately made."
  • Vincent Ostrom: on the other hand, you'd expect him to be more on the artifactual side, but he is actually articulating an argument for co-evolution between the space of the institutional-artifactual and evolutionary processes.

The Normative Vision

Let me finish by going back to the normative vision, because I think it is very important. People doing socioecological systems, people doing resilience, are often convinced — or maybe I'm overdoing it, not always convinced, but many seem to be convinced — that they were able to unlock a positive science of resilience and systems which is able to overpass the normative dimension.

But this is not the case if we take seriously the mainline perspective. For instance:

  • Hayek draws our attention that the argument for liberty (which is a normative position) is not an argument against organization, but against a specific type of organization based on coercion.
  • Buchanan — his position on the primacy of voluntary: "I don't care how people are going to organize, as long as they are going to organize on a voluntary basis. They might decide to do things collectively. They might decide to do the other way." The analysis as such is neutral in respect to the public-private structure of organization.
  • Elinor Ostrom expresses this normative position when overviewing the Workshop history and what the Bloomington Workshop has done: "Complex adaptive systems involve learning. The role of knowledge is fundamental. What we are doing at the Workshop is deeply rooted in the central tradition of human and social sciences: how can fallible human beings achieve and sustain self-governance entities and self-governing ways of life? Similar questions were asked by Aristotle, by Madison, Hamilton, and Tocqueville. Our greatest priority was precisely to produce this kind of knowledge and to impart it in the scholarly community, to entrepreneurs, decision-makers. If we fail in this, all other investigations and theoretical efforts are useless."

Closing: Vincent Ostrom and the Federalist Question

I'm going to end with a quote from the person who I think best summarizes this piece — Vincent Ostrom — who reminds us what is at stake.

What is at stake is something already captured by the Founding Fathers in the Federalist Papers, a quote very dear to Vincent: the question of "whether societies of men are really capable or not of establishing good government from reflection and choice."

Vincent says: "It remains to be seen if people are able to use such methods of discussion, reflection, and choice to fashion the future course of human civilization."

What is at stake: the future course of human civilization. And it remains to be seen if we are able to do that.

But Vincent says: it might be a moment when new patterns of communication and interaction will emerge and spread. With the emergence and spread of those new patterns, new opportunities for reflection and choice will emerge. As a result, the institutional possibility frontier will shift.

There is always the potential of failure. But at the same time, precisely because of this possibility, there is a chance to actually advance the frontier of a society or a civilization based on reflection and choice — one which will not be confined only to the exigencies of accident and force.

An Invitation to the Research Frontier

A lot is on the table when we are talking about mainline political economy and the research agenda based on resilience. We have seen how:

  • Resilience may be a vehicle.
  • Resilience may be a new theory of governance.
  • Resilience may be a systemic feature.
  • Resilience may be a new paradigm.

Whatever perspective we take, we discover the importance of this concept — and not so much the concept, but the phenomena and challenges captured by it. This, I think, is one of the most interesting and important frontiers of mainline political economy and of the research agenda we are developing at Mercatus.

In our opening conversation for this event — a meeting of an intellectual community, a network of scholars exploring these issues from different fields — the organizers thought maybe one of the best ways of opening the conversation is by simply sharing with you the research frontier that we are trying to advance here, and to ask you to join us to further advance it in the intellectual division of labor.

Let me stop here. Now we have question-and-answer section, and let me take it from there. Thank you.

Podcast Outro

Thank you for listening to the Hayek Program Podcast. To learn more about the research, scholars, and work of the Hayek Program, visit hayek.mercatus.org. For more information about graduate student fellowship opportunities for students at Mason as well as at universities across the globe, please visit students.mercatus.org. We hope you recommend students to our programs or consider applying yourself.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被骂"新自由主义"的悖论：批评者无意间为哈耶克加冕
演讲中最耐人寻味的转折是：生态学、可持续性研究、复杂系统理论的学者们，把"韧性"（resilience）研究骂成"新自由主义的化身"——因为它太像哈耶克。Aligica 引用了一整摞标题："Intuitively neoliberal"、"Resilience is a quintessential neoliberal concept"……这些批判本意是划清界限，但从思想史的角度看，它们干的事情恰好相反：**它们承认了自组织、分布式知识、局部适应这些概念在解释复杂现实时的解释力已经如此不可回避，以至于必须先"打倒"它。**
换句话说，每一篇愤怒的引用都是一次生产性引用。这是学术界的"史翠珊效应"——你越想遮蔽，你越强化。真正被边缘化的思想不会被专门写文章批评；被写文章批评的思想已经赢了议程设置。Aligica 用了半场演讲展示"主流经济学不谈韧性"，然后又用另外半场展示"复杂系统的人一边用哈耶克一边骂哈耶克"。这两个观察拼在一起构成了一个反讽结构：**主流政治经济学错过了资源，主流复杂科学则在无意识地消化它。**
#

item.title: [Lex Clips] Did the plague kill half the Roman Empire? | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"缺席的证据"才是最有力的证据——反向历史学的胜利

大多数人以为历史学家靠"更多史料"来推翻旧结论，但 Kaldellis 用的是一个反直觉的方法：用"没发生的事"来证伪"发生了大事"。查士丁尼在 541 年之后仍在四五条战线同时作战，税收照收、法庭照开、军队照征——叙事文本里连一个"停顿"都没有。

这是一个极其精妙的否定式推理：如果瘟疫真的抹掉了一半人口，社会机器不可能不发出刺耳的摩擦声。14 世纪黑死病之后欧洲"停摆数年"是有据可查的，而 6 世纪却是"一切照旧"。沉默本身，就是最响亮的反驳。

这让我想到刑侦学里的"狗没有叫"——福尔摩斯破案的关键正是那条本该吠叫却没吠叫的狗。历史学、犯罪学、乃至今天的数据科学（异常检测）都共享同一个底层直觉：当一个巨大原因存在时，它必然在无关的角落留下指纹；指纹缺失，则原因存疑。

二、实验室科学的"胜利陷阱"——一个跨领域的警示

这里有一个令人意外的观察：分子生物学越先进，历史误判的风险反而越大。Yersinia pestis 被基因测序确认，这是硬科学的胜利，但它只能告诉你"病原体存在"，无法告诉你"社会崩溃"。

这与当代医学里的一个经典陷阱惊人地对称：CT 影像越清晰，"过度诊断"就越严重——我们发现了很多本来不会杀死病人的肿瘤，然后误以为它们必然致命。分辨率的提升制造了因果的幻觉。

同样的模式还出现在： - 神经科学：fMRI 越精细，越容易把"相关脑区激活"读成"心理机制"； - 经济学：高频交易数据越丰富，越容易把噪音识别为规律； - AI 可解释性：能画出注意力热力图，不等于理解了模型的推理。

Kaldellis 的谨慎背后是一个深刻的元原则：能被测量的东西，会挤占那些无法被测量却更重要的东西的话语空间。 病原体可以测序，社会韧性无法测序——于是前者主导了叙事。

三、"极大化叙事"的社会学动力——为什么历史学家爱说"50%"

这段访谈里藏着一个 Kaldellis 没有明说、但极其锋利的东西：为什么"最大化解释"如此有市场？

一个"杀死一半人口的瘟疫终结了罗马帝国"是叙事上的完美产品——它有戏剧性、有单一因果、有道德寓意（人类在自然面前的渺小）、有当代共鸣（新冠之后尤其如此）。而"瘟疫有影响但社会照常运转"则没有故事可讲。

这与一个跨领域现象完全同构：媒体报道飞机失事而非飞机安全降落，投资书籍讲述暴富而非平淡的复利，医学研究偏爱阳性结果而非阴性结果。 这是一种"幸存者偏差的镜像"——不是幸存者被记住，而是戏剧性结论被记住。

更深的洞察是：当一门学科开始接入公众注意力经济时（新冠之后传染病史突然火爆），它的解释会系统性地向"极大化"漂移。这不是学者的道德问题，而是注意力生态的引力场。Kaldellis 在维护的其实不是一个具体的死亡率数字，而是历史学抵抗当代焦虑投射的能力——他在守护的是"事实的自主性"。

一个啊哈时刻：每一个时代都会用它自己最恐惧的东西去解释古罗马的衰亡——启蒙时代说是宗教（吉本），冷战时代说是道德堕落，环保时代说是气候变化，新冠时代说是瘟疫。罗马从未真正死过，它只是不停地被后世的恐惧重新杀死一次。

📝 转录文本

引入：从"十大皇帝"到瘟疫

There's a grand scale to the good and the bad.

— Yes. Yes.

For many people that's what makes for a—

— Exactly.

—top 10 emperor list.

Uh, what about the plague?

— Well, he wasn't responsible for that at least. [laughter]

Okay. The the plague of Justinian.

— Yes.

541 AD：起点与主流叙事

AKA 541 AD. So, some historians claim that it killed half the population and ruined the empire. Uh, you are skeptical on this view.

— Yes. So, this is a bubonic plague. Um, which lasts uh more or less for two centuries. It comes and it goes. And it's kind of endemic to large parts of the world. 541 is when it starts to break out in the empire in Constantinople and other cities, and it spreads.

新科学的进入：Yersinia pestis 被锁定

There's been a lot of debate about this recently. Um, cuz now we've actually isolated the pathogen. It's Yersinia pestis. Like, we know this. Just like a generation ago, this was just guesswork. But now there's all of this new, like, laboratory science work that's come in to fill in some of the gaps in in history.

— That's so cool.

— It is. It is. There are lots of discussions about that. Um, and so um the study of diseases and virology have just become much more important lately.

科学的边界：实验室告诉不了你的东西

It still doesn't tell you, however, like mortality rates and historical impact. Like, for that you still need standard old-fashioned non-laboratory historical work.

And there's a debate about the impact of this uh plague, and it's ongoing. I should say that. Um, and some scholars argue for a kind of maximalist position of 50%. I think that would have brought this society to a halt. Just logistically speaking. Um, and so I I believe in a much more moderate impact.

真实的影响会长什么样？

— And the real impact is what? The tax base shrinks?

— Oh. Oh, yeah. The tax base would shrink instantly. You wouldn't be able to recruit armies. You know, there would be massive social dislocation.

Like what happens in the in the Black Death in Europe in the 14th century. Like, we have very good records for what happened to those societies, and basically everything that they were doing stopped for a few years until they sort of tried to put things back together again.

反证：6 世纪的"照常运转"

This does not happen in the 6th century. In fact, Justinian is at that time waging war on something like four or five different fronts. If you read the narratives of those wars, there's no pause. There's no there's no impact from the plague on the conduct of those wars. They just continue on. Taxation continues, the courts continue. Like, you just don't see it in the narrative.

And so I think that there was a much less impact than certainly than 50%, yeah.

观看原视频 →

大多数人以为历史学家靠"更多史料"来推翻旧结论，但 Kaldellis 用的是一个反直觉的方法：**用"没发生的事"来证伪"发生了大事"**。查士丁尼在 541 年之后仍在四五条战线同时作战，税收照收、法庭照开、军队照征——叙事文本里连一个"停顿"都没有。
