item.title: [Lex Clips] Future of human civilization - historian explains | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的时代情绪定律

Kaldellis 无意间提出了一个惊人的观察——集体情绪与客观威胁常常呈反相关。80 年代活在核战阴影下，流行文化却明亮、天真、乐观；90 年代冷战结束、威胁消退，音乐却变得阴郁、抱怨、"whiny"。这不是矛盾，而是心理学里的"幸存者反相"——当外部压力真实存在时，人类会分泌一种集体性的乐观来对抗它；当压力撤除，被抑制的焦虑反而浮出水面。

这解释了一个当下的悖论：为什么在人类繁荣度最高的历史时期，末日叙事最盛？ 因为压力从外部（核弹、饥荒、瘟疫）转成了内部（存在意义、身份、注意力）。当你不再需要为生存打仗，你就开始为"我是谁"打仗，而后者没有停战协议。这也意味着 Lex 那句"如果我们仅仅醒来看看周围"——其实是不可能的，因为繁荣本身就会关掉那种视觉。

三阶段模型里藏着一个"外化"的隐秘节奏

Kaldellis 把人类史分成狩猎采集、农业、工业/技术三阶段，但没说破一个更深的模式——每一次转型，都是把某种原本"内在于身体"的东西外化出去： - 农业革命：把食物获取外化给土地/系统（不再逐兽而居） - 工业革命：把肌肉外化给机器（不再用体力驱动世界） - 当前正在发生的：把认知/推理外化给 AI

按这个节奏，"第四阶段"未必是继续外化——而可能是方向反转：把外部工具重新内化回身体。基因编辑、脑机接口、可植入 AI……这不是延伸人体，而是把技术圈折叠进生物学圈。如果三阶段是"人向外扩张"，第四阶段可能是"世界向内塌陷进人"。届时"human"和"tool"的边界将不再有意义——这才是 Kaldellis 说的"未来人回望我们时可能不再认得我们"的真正断裂点。

跨领域连接：为什么请一个研究"消失文明"的人预测未来最靠谱

Lex 请一位拜占庭史（medieval Roman Empire）专家谈未来，本身就是最耐人寻味的选择。这里有个跨领域的规律：最沉稳的未来观，往往来自研究已死亡系统的人。就像研究黑洞的物理学家最不怕重力、研究病毒的流行病学家最少恐慌——当你把"崩溃"当作日常研究对象，你就获得了对崩溃的免疫式冷静。

Kaldellis 顺口一句"if we can rein in some elites, a lot of these problems can be fixed"——从一个研究拜占庭的人嘴里说出，重量完全不同。因为在他的专业视野里，帝国崩溃的第一因几乎总是精英过度攫取，而非外敌、瘟疫或气候。他说这句话时的轻松，来自千年样本量赋予的把握。

一个更尖锐的观察：意识作为"最后阵地"的心理学

Lex 说"也许人类的独特性不是智能而是意识"——这句话背后藏着人类自我定义的溃退史： - 我们有灵魂 → 动物没有？未必 - 我们会推理 → 动物会 - 我们有语言 → 动物有 - 我们会用工具 → 动物会 - 我们有智能 → AI 有了 - 我们有意识 → 这个无法证伪

意识之所以成为"人类特殊性"的最后堡垒，恰恰因为它是唯一无法被测量、无法被反驳的属性。这不是发现，而是撤退。真正的"啊哈时刻"是：也许第四阶段人类需要放弃"寻找我们独特之处"这个游戏本身——就像哥白尼让地球放弃宇宙中心的位置反而解放了天文学。放弃独特性，才可能开启新阶段。

📝 转录文本

一、我们处于人类历史的哪个阶段？

Lex: Just because you've seen this grand history, I apologize for the ridiculous question, but if we look at the entirety of Earth and we fast forward a million years from now — ooh — and the history of life on Earth is written, do you think we're at the very beginning of human history, or are we in the middle, or are we close to the end?

Kaldellis: A million years from now? I mean, I can't project or predict anything like a year from now. And I've become actually increasingly skeptical that I can do so, at least.

However, so I don't know about beginning, middle, and end. It's easier to think in terms of numbered phases, like significantly different phases of human history. And I would say we're probably in the third. I think there've only been three phases.

The first one being sort of hunter-gatherer period, which lasted for the majority of human history so far. Then there's the agricultural revolution, basically — and this is when you start to produce surpluses that create more complex societies. And we're now living through a technological — with in first industrial now —

Lex: Yeah, you can throw it all in the same bucket, industrial-technological.

Kaldellis: Industrial, technological, which is modifying the parameters of what it is that human beings can do and possibly be.

Lex: Mhm.

Kaldellis: And who knows where this will go, but I see these as very distinct in many ways. It's all human beings —

Lex: Yeah, it's all human beings, but they're almost modifying themselves.

二、会不会有"第四阶段"？

Kaldellis: So the question is: is there a fourth?

Lex: Is there going to be —

Kaldellis: There will be.

Lex: And what does that look like? And when they look back at the third, would they still see us as the same as them, or would it be something completely different?

Kaldellis: Oh, no. I mean, we look back at the Romans or the Greeks or any period in history, and we're like, "Yeah, no, I see what you're doing there. You're doing it with the technological capabilities that you had at that time. You're doing it with the materials that you had, but I see what you're doing." You've got families, and you've got armies, and you bury your dead, and you know —

Lex: But now we're increasingly actually having the tools and technology to modify — to deeply modify — what it means to be human. Genetic engineering, brain-computer interfaces; as we understand the brain more, we can modify things, expand completely. Plus artificial intelligence — it can expand the knowledge base and the intelligence of the human mind.

Unless we figure out the human mind is actually incredibly special to the degree where, at least with the tools we have this century, we could not achieve the level of general intelligence that humans have.

Or — one of the things we might figure out in stage four of this long history — is that the thing that makes humans special is not intelligence, it's not any of the things we kind of value each other by, it's actually consciousness. And consciousness is the thing that is very difficult to engineer.

So that's the common thing from the hunter-gatherers to before the Industrial Revolution and after: we're still the same. We still feel like something to be alive, and that's maybe an important component of what makes us fall in love with each other, or fear each other, or suffer — the full range of the human condition, the human experience.

Maybe even technology cannot replicate that, cannot modify that. Maybe that's what we learn. Or very possibly we destroy this whole thing — because now, for the first time in human history, we have the tools, unfortunately, to destroy everything.

Kaldellis: This is all very eloquent. I mean, after so many hours of my rambling about the medieval Roman Empire — yeah, I'm impressed.

三、对未来的希望：一个历史学家的视角

Lex: Okay. What gives you hope about this whole thing — about the future of human civilization?

Kaldellis: Look, there are a lot of challenges that we face, and we talk about them a lot. As a historian, I've seen other periods that were far, far more difficult, when people could have far more easily have given up hope — and sometimes did. But I don't think we're in one of those.

There are some major issues, like the uncertain future of technologies, what they might do — exactly what you were talking about. I think they're solvable problems to a certain degree. Like if we can rein in some elites, a lot of these problems can be fixed. And that's not — I mean, it's difficult, but it's not impossible.

四、80 年代与 90 年代：情绪与现实的错位

Kaldellis: Look, so I grew up during the Cold War. Let's just take the '80s for example. In the '80s, there was a pervasive sense that nuclear war could happen at any time and just annihilate everything, and we lived under that.

If you look at the popular culture of the '80s, it's all upbeat and cheerful and naive — like there's just no correlation. And then you move into the '90s, and the Cold War is over, and everyone was all depressed. The music gets all whiny and — like, what happened? There's just no correlation.

Anyway, I just think that it's a pervasive sense of hopelessness or despair doesn't always match with — if you look at the situation objectively. I mean, yeah, it can be pretty bad. But there's so much that's happening that is good all the time.

五、我们其实做得不错

Lex: So this period of human history — because we've talked about the ups and downs of the Roman Empire — I think unquestionably this period in history, the late 20th century and the 21st century, will be seen as a flourishing of humans. So if we actually just wake up —

Kaldellis: Yeah.

Lex: — it's like, everything around us, by most measures of human flourishing, we're doing pretty good.

Kaldellis: The amount of sheer misery is probably objectively decreasing every year. Now, you know, we're also creating problems — yes, that will cause misery down the road. Yes. But overall, I don't see a reason to despair and not have hope.

Lex: Yeah, we're doing okay.

Kaldellis: We can do better. Let's do better.

Lex: Let's do better.

六、结束语

Lex: Well, Anthony, this was an incredible conversation. Thank you for all the amazing work you do and for the whirlwind journey through such a fascinating part of human history. This was fun. Thank you for talking today.

Kaldellis: It was a pleasure to be here — and thank you, not just for inviting me. I mean, this is probably the largest audience I've ever spoken to. But also just for giving my field and this civilization that I study the opportunity to be seen and heard from more people. I think that's very important. You've done a real service here. So I thank you for that.

Lex: Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反直觉的时代情绪定律
Kaldellis 无意间提出了一个惊人的观察——**集体情绪与客观威胁常常呈反相关**。80 年代活在核战阴影下，流行文化却明亮、天真、乐观；90 年代冷战结束、威胁消退，音乐却变得阴郁、抱怨、"whiny"。这不是矛盾，而是心理学里的"幸存者反相"——当外部压力真实存在时，人类会分泌一种集体性的乐观来对抗它；当压力撤除，被抑制的焦虑反而浮出水面。
这解释了一个当下的悖论：**为什么在人类繁荣度最高的历史时期，末日叙事最盛？** 因为压力从外部（核弹、饥荒、瘟疫）转成了内部（存在意义、身份、注意力）。当你不再需要为生存打仗，你就开始为"我是谁"打仗，而后者没有停战协议。这也意味着 Lex 那句"如果我们仅仅醒来看看周围"——其实是不可能的，因为繁荣本身就会关掉那种视觉。
## 三阶段模型里藏着一个"外化"的隐秘节奏
Kaldellis 把人类史分成狩猎采集、农业、工业/技术三阶段，但没说破一个更深的模式——**每一次转型，都是把某种原本"内在于身体"的东西外化出去**： - 农业革命：把食物获取外化给土地/系统（不再逐兽而居） - 工业革命：把

item.title: [Lex Clips] Persian Empire vs Roman Empire: The war that destroyed both empires | Anthony Kaldellis
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"英雄叙事"是历史学的糖精：Heraclius 悖论

大多数人记住 Heraclius，是因为他"拯救"了罗马帝国、夺回真十字架、成为"第一位十字军"。但 Kaldellis 揭开了一层反直觉的真相——Heraclius 本人正是灾难的合谋者。他在 608-610 年发动的三年内战，把罗马军队从波斯前线抽离，等于给 Khosrow II 送了一份战略大礼。换句话说，他先制造了危机，再"英勇地"解决它，最终又把一切输给阿拉伯人。

这里有一个让人不安的模式：历史上被神化的救世主，往往是自己所解决问题的最初制造者。这与现代政治、企业管理甚至个人成长中的"英雄回归"叙事惊人一致——一个 CEO 先做出灾难决策，再"力挽狂澜"，被写成商学院案例。中世纪修士与今天的公关文案，用的其实是同一套叙事引擎。

二、两大帝国的"互相掏空"：一个被忽视的系统性洞察

Kaldellis 提到的最有力观点是：罗马-波斯战争没有"赢家"，它只是为第三方——阿拉伯人——腾出了权力真空。这不是简单的"渔翁得利"，而是一种系统性的自毁耦合（mutually destructive coupling）：两个体量相当、防御方向相互锁死的强权，会把彼此的国力压榨到临界点，直到某个"侧翼玩家"从未被防御的方向进入。

跨领域看，这几乎是冷战末期美苏军备竞赛的镜像——只不过"阿拉伯人"变成了后来崛起的东亚制造业。更抽象地说，这是博弈论中"消耗战均衡"的历史实证：当两个对手都不能承受失败，双方就会走向共同的结构性衰竭。生物学里叫"红皇后陷阱"，商业里叫"零和内卷"。历史学家 Kaldellis 用波斯-罗马为我们提供了一个近乎实验室级别的样本。

三、"希腊火"是国家机密，"圣像破坏"却是历史噪音——一个惊人的信号/噪音反转

这段对话中最令人拍案的洞察，其实藏在两个看似不相关的话题里： - 希腊火：一个真正影响帝国存亡的技术秘密，配方失传至今，连学者都无法完全复原。 - 圣像破坏运动（Iconoclasm）：一个被中世纪史书浓墨重彩渲染的"重大宗教冲突"，Kaldellis 的研究却发现，真正在乎它的可能只有一两百个精英——修士、主教、皇帝——而他们恰好是唯一会写字的人。

这里有一个反直觉的历史学定律：史料的浓度与事件的真实社会重要性成反比。谁掌握书写权，谁的偏执就会成为"历史"。这与今天的社交媒体算法惊人相似——最响亮的声音未必代表最广泛的现实，而是代表最愿意发帖的少数人。拜占庭的修士就是 8 世纪的 Twitter 用户。

更进一步：一个帝国最重要的东西（希腊火配方）被小心藏起，最不重要的东西（图像神学）被大声记录——这几乎可以作为一种"文明诊断法"：当你想理解一个时代真正在乎什么，去看它拼命保密的东西，而不是它拼命书写的东西。

📝 转录文本

一、Heraclius 的登场：一个"救世主"的原罪

So, speaking of which, let's talk about Heraclius. Uh, he didn't make — he didn't make your list either, which is a shocker for a lot of people. Perhaps you can describe the nuance of that, but he took over the Roman Empire on the verge of extinction. Uh, Persians at the gates, treasury empty. So, uh, maybe first tell the full saga of the Roman–Persian war that threatened the empire's existence.

Sure. Well, he — he wasn't entirely, you know, um, free of blame for the situation that he inherited. He he was in part responsible for it. So, so he doesn't get a pass on that.

So, Heraclius is basically a rebel and a usurper. Uh, so for various reasons, um, Rome and Persia are at war starting around 602. Um, and the the Persian Shah at this time, Khosrow II, has basically decided on a policy of conquest, not just raiding. Like, "I'm going to go in and take your people, some cattle, and some statues and take them to, you know, Mesopotamia." He starts — what I mean, I think there's some evolution in his thinking and his planning about this, but pretty soon during the 600s, he realizes that there's potential for actual permanent territorial gain here, and his armies start moving in.

二、内战的代价：Heraclius 的叛乱与波斯的机会

And the Roman emperor is this guy called Phocas, who is himself a usurper, military background, and he now faces a rebellion by Heraclius, whose base is in North Africa at this time — like around Carthage. And they have a civil war for 3 years, 608 to 610.

That civil war that Heraclius initiates causes a diversion of Roman armies to the civil war, away from the Persian front. So, it's a massive gift to the Persians. Plus, a lot of the fighting takes place in Egypt. And Egypt was a province that had, up until that point, not really been harmed by war. Now, it is.

Right. So, Heraclius's forces — he's not in Egypt, but his backers are fighting a civil war in Egypt. So, that province is now, you know, um, ravaged in part. The Persians make tremendous gains in the east. Heraclius heads straight for Constantinople during all of this. And to make a long story short, he manages to take Constantinople, execute Phocas, and become emperor. Right? But now he's facing a problem that's much worse than it was before he started the rebellion.

三、"第一位十字军"：一个中世纪神话的诞生

The reason why Heraclius is sort of lionized — and so, well, actually there's a very long tradition of doing this which goes back to the Middle Ages, in Western Middle Ages. Heraclius was sometimes considered like the first crusader.

Interesting.

Yeah. Yeah. So there's all this medieval literature about Heraclius as being a proto-crusader type and and all that, um, for reconquering, like, Jerusalem from the Persians and restoring the True Cross and and and all that. So he had this kind of aura, and I think his modern um admirers are basically just continuing — I mean, they might not know it, but they're kind of continuing this very, very long tradition of treating Heraclius as this heroic holy warrior.

Anyway, uh, from my standpoint, it's a complete wash. In other words, yes, he does beat the Persians. Um, and he gets, like, in my view, full credit for that, though he did it with, I think, the help of some Turkish allies from Central Asia. These were, I think, the major component of his success at the very end. But no — so he held the Persians off and ultimately defeated them. Yes. And then he loses everything to the Arabs.

四、战争的代价与阿拉伯征服的伏笔

So the Arab conquests are 630s and 640s, but the Persian war is 602 to 628.

Yes.

So what can be said about this — what seems to be a pretty costly war, I guess, uh, Persians.

So the war was costly initially only for the Romans. Um, the the eastern provinces were conquered by Persia. That means Syria, Palestine, Egypt. So that's a loss. You lose all that revenue. And then they start raiding into Asia Minor.

By the way, this is exactly what the Arabs would do, you know, just a couple decades later. They would conquer Syria, Palestine, Egypt, and they would start raiding into Asia Minor. So almost like this phase of East Roman history begins already during the Persian invasion. It's just a kind of a prelude. It was in fact the Arabs that would do that for the next few centuries.

So, Heraclius — it takes him a very long time to get organized. Um, in fact, something like 15 years. And it's not entirely clear like what he's doing all this time. Um, but he ultimately manages to defeat the Persians by waging these really, um, spectacular, weird campaigns in the east.

五、直击敌人心脏：Heraclius 的反常规战略

He decides to strike into the Persian heartland, as it were. Well, first in the Caucasus region, and then he goes into Mesopotamia. So, not to try to defeat their armies that are rampaging around Asia Minor, or even besieging his capital in 626.

The the Persians and the Avars — people from this nomadic warrior group that settled in roughly modern Hungary — they're besieging Constantinople. Heraclius doesn't even go. Um, and he's just kind of preparing his armies in the east. And he strikes the Persians right where it hurts.

And this now begins to degrade the Persian heartland and their infrastructure, because remember, both of these empires are going to be in shambles by the time the Arabs come along. And this is what enables the Arabs to conquer them so quickly. They destroyed each other um in these wars. And eventually Heraclius calls in his Turkish allies and they go on a field trip through Mesopotamia. And this causes a coup in in Persia and Khosrow is killed.

六、动机学：帝国到底想要什么？

Is there something to be said — a little bit of texture to the motivations of the different empires and the different groups involved? So the East Roman Empire, the Persian Empire, and the Islamic Caliphates — were they after conquering? Were they after expansion? Were they after more like raiding for financial benefits? What was the motivation?

So in the past, the Persian Shahs basically were interested in raiding and they also had an interest in manpower. Uh, so all of those Mesopotamian agricultural estates won't till themselves. So they just — they were kind of insatiable for agricultural workforces and specialized craftsmen and people like that. So they they would go into the empire and get them.

Um, but in this phase, it seems that Khosrow II is taking really to heart the idea that the Persian Empire should really extend to the Mediterranean as it did in like Achaemenid times, right? In in ancient history, the empire that Alexander conquered. So it seems that the the Sasanians — this is this dynasty, this Iranian dynasty that's ruling um the Persian Empire — of course they were aware of the Achaemenids, but sometimes they would have these claims to reviving the former greatness of the Iranian Empire.

Sure.

So, you know, bathing in the Mediterranean Sea is something that uh many Near Eastern monarchs, you know, uh they they did this as a sign of of success.

Anyway, for the Romans, it was sheer survival. Just desperate survival, because they knew that this was a war that could end the Roman state. I mean, they're already on the verge of being ended. Yeah. And here they're defensive wars of survival.

七、阿拉伯扩张：从未被防御的方向

Yeah. So, it's pretty clear that initially the the goal of Arab Muslim warfare is to unify Arabia. That is, that all Arabs should be Muslim and should be unified into this um religious kind of state — whatever that the Prophet and his successors had created. Now then they start striking out um into Rome and into Iran.

Um, and it's not entirely clear like why. Now there are obvious reasons — like, I mean, we don't — it's not like a huge historical mystery why a, you know, recently formed successful army will decide to take on two very weak opponents who are on their doorstep. Like, this is not, you know, complicated.

Yeah, they're pretty effective at military conquest.

Yeah, but they're striking both empires from a direction that those empires had never prepared to defend from. Arabia was just not — "oh, you might get some occasional raiders. You're never going to get an army that that's coming out that can potentially conquer you." So the the defensive orientations are all wrong. And these two empires have gone through this massive war uh that has destabilized them. They're they — low on manpower, low on resources. The outcome is almost kind of inevitable. I don't think there's a great mystery.

And the effect of that is they stripped away the richest provinces of uh the Roman Empire.

Yes. Uh, Egypt in particular. So that means now you can't feed Constantinople. Like we talked about the grain from Egypt, right? That came to Constantinople. You don't have that now. And so that means that the population of Constantinople will decline dramatically over the next few decades.

八、缓慢的重建：数十年的止血

So how does the empire rebuild itself, you know, restabilize? Because it's not — it doesn't collapse there, right?

Yes.

It stays — it survives. There's interesting low points for the empire where it still survives. And that is just interesting — as the survival at the top and the flourishing survival at the bottom is also super interesting. So how does — what happens? How does it kind of stabilize?

Very slowly. This is a painful process and it takes them centuries, you could say. First you got to stop the bleeding — just try to hold the line. It takes them decades to just to hold a line. Like they get pummeled by the Arabs uh for decades in the 7th century until finally, by yeah, 660s, 670, they finally begin to beat them uh beat them on land uh and and hold the line uh on the sea.

Uh and so we now have an investment in fleets. Eventually you get the Greek fire, which is the flamethrowers. Um, and when the Arabs try to take Constantinople, these attempts are defeated, like, twice. And that ensures the survival of this — of the Roman state — in some form.

九、希腊火之谜：国家最高机密

How were the flamethrowers used?

Ah yes, this is an interesting question. We're still kind of debating that.

Oh, it's not fully understood.

Well, it's a state secret. They didn't want this getting out, so they kept it pretty well. The fact that we're debating it will tell you that they were pretty good at keeping secrets. Um, so possibly through a a pressure valve.

Right. So you have this flammable compound whose preparation and components is also a state secret. Um, so there's specialized — there's a corps of engineers that that prepares this and deploys it. Um, and it's possibly through a heated pressurized container with this spigot, let's say, that unleashes this thing uh through a nozzle, and it's lit, uh, and basically spews flaming napalm on other ships. And it will continue to burn even when it's on the water, right on the top. So, like, you can't, like, swim away or anything. So this is very, very destructive weapon.

Terrifying. They used it against Vikings too. This is much later. So once the Vikings show up. Um, like when they would raid — attacked Constantinople in the 940s, then again in the 1040s — um, yeah, the Romans used uh Greek fire fleets to just incinerate them. And that'll teach Vikings.

Yes. If you're in — if you're in that situation, you'd rather serve under the emperors um than attack them.

十、手榴弹形态的希腊火

And so this is this is a kind of nuclear weapon level state secret. They even had it in hand grenade form.

This is a real thing.

Oh yes.

Hand grenade form.

Yes. So it's a — it's like a clay container uh with the compound, and it has like some sort of fuse that you light on the outside and you throw it. Now, you throw it — you can throw it against people, but it's not going to be that effective. Maybe you burn a person, whatever. You just wasted a hand grenade for, you know, one person. It's most effective against things like um siege engines. Uh, so you you throw that on a wooden frame, and, you know…

Yeah, this is incredible. So this is one of the things that's finally stabilized — uh, stop the bleeding from a military perspective.

十一、行政重构：一个更小、更硬的核心

Yeah, eventually. Now, now remember, they've pulled the armies back into Asia Minor, so they're they're beginning to defend and fortify Asia Minor. Now, you have to find ways to pay and support and feed all of these soldiers. And this is a whole administrative — it's a wrenching administrative readjustment, but they go through it. It takes them a long time. Eventually, they emerge with a different kind of strategic logistical system.

So, it's a smaller, harder core of the empire.

That's right. Very militarized. Very militarized.

十二、Leo III 与君士坦丁堡第二次围城

And then in the 8th century, the the second Arab siege of Constantinople that somehow fails in the early 8th century.

Yes, that was the second siege.

Yeah, the early 8th century. So why — why did it fail? Can you tell the story of that siege? That was really the empire hanging by by a thread.

Yes, but it happened to have a very competent man in charge. This is uh Leo III, and he's an emperor who has — he's a usurper. He's just seized power. They knew that this massive Arab attack was coming. And so it's quite possible that the Roman elites, you know, preferred to put someone capable in in power at that moment.

The city was very well prepared. I encourage people in the audience to to go and read the the sources that we have for that. You can find many accounts of it online. It it was just very, very well done. He also had Bulgar allies. So these are the the Bulgars who had just, you know, crossed the Danube just like just a few years earlier, actually, and settled south of the Danube um, you know, where eventually, you know, Bulgaria emerged.

So he had Bulgar allies and he used the the Greek fire very, very strategically. He used his own fleet. He contained the Arabs to certain places where they kind of starved and um wore them down. It's just very well done from a military perspective.

From a military perspective. Well, was there — was it the uh the walls, the navy, the weather, the diplomacy, just good luck that helped Constantinople hold?

I don't see much role for good luck there. I mean, I think he had it all pretty planned out. It was all of the elements that you mentioned and yeah, he he just deployed them very, very astutely. Um, and went on to rule after that for another 20-some years, and um his son continued. Um, they formed a dynasty. We call it the Isaurian dynasty, though they were not from Isauria.

十三、圣像破坏运动：一场"被夸大"的宗教冲突？

And then from that — the fascinating thing that we mentioned about religion — there was a whole stretch of time marked by intense internal religious conflicts over iconoclasm. Maybe you can clarify this. But so iconoclasm, of course, is whether sacred images — icons — should be venerated or destroyed. This had, um, major political and theological stakes. The seemingly smallest things can have the biggest of conflicts.

Yeah. You've been in an Orthodox church.

Mhm.

Have you seen all the icons in the — just painting everywhere? Every every space that you can imagine is covered in either icons or images or iconography of saints. That's a result of this. No, that's not what Christian churches used to be like. At least not in Constantinople.

Um, but uh his dynasty basically tried to limit uh or remove images from churches, and ultimately that policy failed. Um, and the other side — that wanted images, or liked images, let me put differently, thought that images were not only appropriate but helpful in the context of Christian worship — prevailed. And so gradually the churches began to fill up with icons, lots of icons. They're almost like uh, uh, you know, like a — a catchword for Orthodox uh, you know, devotion. Um, I have icons at home. You have a little corner in the house. You — little iconography. Um, anyway, I mean, as a secular Orthodox person, but I — I even I can't get away from —

十四、被少数人书写的"重大冲突"

But that has serious consequence. I mean, there — serious — there was an extremely serious internal religious conflict.

Well, okay. So, uh, one asterisk on that. In my research, I did not find, uh, popular interest in this matter. Like—

Oh, interesting.

Yeah. I — yeah, I don't—

So, this is an overblown—

Yes. Yes. Socially completely overblown.

Oh, this is one of those things where religious folks will write the story of history.

That's it. Got it.

Ah, you got it.

I could not find — there were like a few emperors, and there's some bishops and abbots and monks. Like, I don't know, maybe a 100, 200 people are like seriously invested in this and they're going at it, but these are the people who write the texts. But they never mention, for example, that, "oh, you know, the the people of Constantinople, they rose up and they demanded icons — or not icons — or anything like that."

No, never. So, in my view, this is one of those controversies that's — only a few people really, really cared about it, and, and unfortunately they're the ones who wrote the the the sources. So you always have to uh be very careful to consider the sources when you're reading about anything involving religious events.

Or any kind of events.

But certain — certain things are more intensely — uh, people are more intensely passionate about, and therefore they're going — that bias is going to bleed into the way you report the history.

十五、最终的胜利：图像充满了空间

And not only that — when emperors changed their policy, almost everyone fell into line, like, switched positions. It's like, "No, yeah, whatever. Put the images, take the images, like whatever. We like — this isn't—" they did not see this as central. Some people did and they got very worked up about it.

Anyway, but in the end, the pro-icon position won, and their view just began to fill up the space. I like — it literally was filling up the churches. And it still —

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、"英雄叙事"是历史学的糖精：Heraclius 悖论
大多数人记住 Heraclius，是因为他"拯救"了罗马帝国、夺回真十字架、成为"第一位十字军"。但 Kaldellis 揭开了一层反直觉的真相——**Heraclius 本人正是灾难的合谋者**。他在 608-610 年发动的三年内战，把罗马军队从波斯前线抽离，等于给 Khosrow II 送了一份战略大礼。换句话说，他先制造了危机，再"英勇地"解决它，最终又把一切输给阿拉伯人。

Kaldellis 提到的最有力观点是：罗马-波斯战争没有"赢家"，它只是为第三方——阿拉伯人——腾出了权力真空。这不是简单的"渔翁得利"，而是一种**系统性的自毁耦合（mutually destructiv

item.title: [Lex Clips] Why the Roman Empire collapsed in the East | Anthony Kaldellis and Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反常识的观察：拜占庭其实是"抱怨驱动型"的稳定系统

大多数人以为帝国稳固要靠肃杀与统一口径，而这段对话揭示的却是相反的图景——东罗马之所以能延续千年，恰恰是因为它把"抱怨"制度化了。老百姓一直在抱怨税收，皇帝知道抱怨没有惩罚成本，抱怨甚至能换来让步。抱怨从"体制的裂缝"变成了"体制的减压阀"。

这在系统论上有一个深刻的类比：现代分布式系统的自愈能力，不是来自"消灭故障"，而是来自"允许小故障被观察到"。Netflix 的 Chaos Monkey 就是主动制造故障来验证系统健康。拜占庭的持续抱怨，就是它自己养出来的 Chaos Monkey——让不满在低烈度下被表达、被吸收、被回应，从而永远不会积累到"分离主义"那个临界点。这也许可以解释一个让 Kaldellis 自己都惊讶的数据：1200 年里只有保加利亚一个地区真的想脱离。一个健康的抱怨文化，比一支忠诚的军队更能防止分裂。

跨域连接：Tamerlane 是一个"负熵事件"，也是历史学最少被谈论的黑天鹅

对话里一笔带过的细节其实惊人：帖木儿从中亚横扫过来，把奥斯曼打散，意外地给拜占庭多续了 50 年命。这在演化生物学里有一个精确的对应概念——"捕食者的捕食者"（apex predator suppression）。在生态系统里，狼被消灭会让鹿泛滥，但如果狼有自己的天敌，中间层物种反而能获得更长的稳定期。

拜占庭之所以能到 1453 而不是 1400 灭亡，不是因为它变强了，而是因为"吃它的那位"被更凶的一位咬了一口。这引出了一个让人不适的历史观：很多我们归因于"文明韧性"的延续，其实只是别人打架时溅出的时间红利。 把这个视角推到今天——我们今天所谓的"和平红利"，有多少是结构性的胜利，有多少只是我们尚未看清的"帖木儿之战"在遥远的地方替我们腾出的时间？

修辞的秘密：不是"说得好"，而是"信得深"——从亚里士多德到现代品牌学

Kaldellis 借院长发预算通知的例子讲了一件微妙的事：同样的行动，包装在不同的信念结构里，会产生完全不同的服从与共识。而 Lex 追问的那句才是命门——"你必须真的相信它"。

这里有一个几乎没有人指出的跨领域联系：现代信号理论（signaling theory）里的"高代价信号"和亚里士多德给僭主的建议在结构上是同一件事。亚里士多德说："想让人以为你是仁君？最省事的办法就是真的做一个仁君。" 因为伪装仁慈的边际成本会随时间递增（要撒的谎越来越多、越来越难圆），而真正的仁慈是"零维护成本"的信号。拜占庭皇帝之所以让"我在为你们统治"这句话有效千年，是因为说这句话的成本，被他们真的做过的事情摊薄了——像一笔千年期的信任摊销。

由此我看到一条更让人意外的隐藏线索：东罗马的真正遗产不是版图、不是法律、不是东正教，而是"充分同意（sufficient consent）"这种被低估的政治工艺。它不追求人民爱戴，只追求"替代方案更糟"这一底线感受被牢牢维持。这一心理契约比任何军事同盟都难被外部势力撬动——直到有一天，外部势力大到你连"是否留下"这个问题都不必再问。这或许才是所有长寿组织（无论帝国、宗教、还是苹果这样的公司）的隐秘公分母：它们从不承诺天堂，它们只承诺你离开会更糟。

📝 转录文本

When did the decline begin?

Q: When did the decline of the East Roman Empire begin for you? Throughout this loops, when — if you were to predict — would you be able to say where this is going to be over? And what led to that decline and the collapse? So, of course, the collapse is finally in 1453 AD.

A: That's actually a very good question. In other words, not looking at all of the previous crises that caused loss of territory — like, you know, you lose Egypt, you lose Syria, you lose Palestine, you lose Eastern Asia Minor — but at what point could you say there's no coming back from this?

I would say early 14th century. Somewhere between 1300 and 1350, the sources of resilience begin to dry up. And you have a number of setbacks. They lose Asia Minor to the Turks by 1300, roughly. Which means that you can now no longer draw upon the two wings, as it were — the European side and the Asian side, which they could always do. They're confined to Europe. And then you have a series of civil wars. You have the Serbian expansion. And you have the Black Death. And they never recover from that.

1300: a small state, but not yet doomed

In 1300, you look at it and you think that's a tiny little Balkan state. Okay, it goes from Constantinople to the Adriatic in this sort of ribbon — this corridor that just stretches across the Balkans. And yet, it's not that much smaller than England. And has about as much, if not more, revenue because they're still able to raise cash through taxes. So the emperor is still able to pull in a lot of gold. And his territory is not completely insignificant. It's not what the Roman Empire used to be, but I don't think that it's completely lost.

Then they have some civil wars. Then one rebel makes a deal with the Serbs, surrenders half that territory. Okay — after that, game over.

The Tamerlane accident

What's surprising is it takes the Turks that long. 1453 — that's another century. It didn't need to be. It looks like they might have had it wrapped up like 50 years after — you know, by 1400. But then accidental events happened. This is Timur — Tamerlane — comes from Central Asia, trounces the Turks, breaks up their empire. So they go into a cycle of rebuilding. And so that's why it takes another 50 years. Anyway. But I'd say by the 1340s, game over.

Why did it collapse?

Q: And so, why did it collapse? What are the things on the ground when you zoom out — the thing that ate away at the… I like this term, sources of resilience.

A: Oh, foreign invasions. There's no question.

Q: It's just invasions.

A: It's foreign invasions — those which they could not cope with. They coped with lots of them. But all it takes is to lose to one and you lose some part of your territory. And if you keep losing enough, you won't have anything to fall back on. And that's what happens.

Could it have lasted another thousand years?

Q: So really, if there's no significant external invasions, the Eastern Roman Empire — the fundamentals, the taxation system, the way the policies were developed, the way the politics was done, the way the representation was done, all of that — this empire could have lasted for another thousand years?

A: Absolutely. I'm absolutely convinced of this. Because you never see movements to split away — that is, there's no separatist movements, at least not on the part of any Roman provincials.

There's one separatist movement, which is Bulgaria. You mentioned Bulgaria was conquered, right, in 1018. There's a couple of independentist movements, rebellions — eventually one succeeds in 1185. So just under two centuries. So Bulgaria is the only part of this empire that actively seeks to break away and does so.

But for the rest of the 1200 years, you don't find provincials wanting to leave this system. However much they complain about the taxes, they don't want to leave and they make no move to do so.

You also never have a moment when the center loses its ability to tax its territories. You don't have widespread peasant uprisings or agricultural rebellions or these kinds of things — which happens in every other one of these empires. They also never decide to partition it. The Franks partition theirs, right, after Charlemagne. No — there are no Roman warlords that try to carve out a piece of the territory for themselves and rule it independently. Nope, that doesn't happen either.

So if you're looking at the kinds of factors that cause states to fail, fragment, become ungovernable — these never happen.

The most dangerous neighborhood in the world

Q: This is so incredible. This is truly incredible. In the history of human civilization, just an incredible study of what human societies can form. So what are the whys? Why? Why was it so stable?

A: Well, now look — there are other stable… like for example the British monarchy. That's been around for quite a while. States like Portugal, Japan — they have one thing in common. They're all tucked away at the edges. Like they're off on their little islands or whatever. They're not like…

We're talking about a society that was right in the middle of one of the main corridors of empire building and new religions in the world. This is the most dangerous neighborhood that you can possibly live in. So no wonder it didn't survive. It would have been extraordinary if it had. But absent those forces, I don't see any forces of internal decomposition that would have imperiled it significantly.

Why no internal decomposition?

Q: So — and we've talked about a lot of it, but just returning to it — why was there no internal sources of decomposition? Like why did it work so well? Why did the people feel represented? Yes, they… by the way, you talked about how they complain about the taxes, which is one of the signs of a healthy society. It's like complaining about the weather. The fact that you complain about the taxes actually means that you're happy with the taxes, kind of counterintuitively. It's not too bad. Your complaining is part of the system.

Anyway — but the why, why, why did it work so well? Why did everybody get along? Feel represented? No, not get along — not in a silly way. I mean, sufficiently to where warlords wouldn't rise and break apart the system, and if there were those kinds of forces, they would come back together. Why is it such a great self-healing system?

A: So — you're exactly right. So sufficient is the key word. We don't want to idealize this. They're not just dancing in the streets holding hands. So even when it comes to the tax system, they are complaining all the time. Of course, they're doing so because they know that complaining gets you things. There's no disincentive to complaining. You might not get what you want, or you might get it, but no harm will come to you. So they're complaining all the time. They have all of these civil wars.

A two-part answer: persuasion + identity

I think I would give you a two-part answer, so really quickly, as to why this held together so well.

First is that an extraordinary effort was made by the authorities to persuade their subjects that they were ruling on their behalf. And I think for the most part they actually tended to do so.

Second is that it had a very tightly unified identity as Roman and as Orthodox. In other words, for the most time they knew that they were surrounded by enemies who were not those things. And they did not want to live under the power of non-Romans and non-Christian people.

And so those two factors combined, I think, gave everybody reason to hold it together. The alternatives were worse.

The power of rhetoric — and the necessity of belief

Q: Yeah, that's a really brilliant summary. And I think we should mention that both the rhetoric and the action are important. I think you've articulated that rhetoric is really powerful — stating over and over and over that you represent the people, that you're acting on behalf of the people, and doing the action. But it's not enough to actually do the action. You would think that's all — Machiavelli says that's the only thing that matters. Then, no — the rhetoric actually has a lot of power because it is a uniting idea and ideal that propagates through time, and it pacifies you. It makes you… it puts you at peace. Rhetoric is important.

A: I'll give you an example. As an academic administrator — I was a chair of a department for years and years — there's a difference between a dean who says, "Sorry, bad budget situation, we're just going to have to cut your programs," on the one hand, and a dean who says, "I am fully on board with what you're trying to accomplish with these programs. I am a defender of the humanities. Unfortunately, we have this budget situation and we're going to have to cut some of the programs, but I assure you — I'm in this with you, together — the moment we find the funds, we will restore them."

Those two rhetorical strategies — aiming at the same practical outcome — will produce very different levels of compliance and consensus.

Rhetoric only works if you actually believe it

Q: And I should say that — there's subtlety there. The best rhetoric in that context, and maybe in the East Roman Empire, is: you also have to believe it.

A: Yes. It has to be credible. You can't just say it and then not do it.

Q: One of the things I've learned about humans, just doing this little podcast, is that the best way to say a powerful thing is to really believe it. And so — this is clearly one of the things that made the Roman Empire work: doing the action is the best way to make the rhetoric powerful and effective.

Aristotle's advice to tyrants

A: Yeah. This is sort of what Aristotle says about tyrants. In the Politics, Aristotle's giving essentially advice to tyrants on how to protect themselves — because tyrants are usually hated and are killed in coups. And he says: essentially, how do you manage to secure yourself when you're a tyrant?

Well, you can start by maybe obeying the laws, or making sure that your subjects obey the laws, and maybe work for the interest of your… essentially, he's telling them to kind of become benevolent kings. And the best way to persuade your subjects that you're legitimate benevolent kings is to be that.

Yeah.
